topics.title: Machine Learning vs. Traditional programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAIT404 - FOUNDATION OF MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Machine Learning vs. Traditional programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Traditional Programming (Rule-Based Computing):** A deterministic paradigm in which a human programmer explicitly encodes a set of rules (the *program*) that map input data to output. The machine executes these hand-written instructions to produce the result.
>
> **Machine Learning (Data-Driven Computing):** A subfield of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in which the system **infers the underlying function or rules directly from data**, producing a *model* that can later map new inputs to outputs without being explicitly programmed for that task.

In traditional programming, the developer supplies **both** the data **and** the program, and the computer returns the answer.

In machine learning, the developer supplies the data **and** the desired answers (labels), and the computer **returns the program** (i.e., the learned model/parameters).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Recipe vs. The Chef's Intuition:**
> - *Traditional programming* is like giving a chef a **written recipe** (the rules) plus ingredients (the input). The chef (the computer) just follows the steps to bake a cake (output).
> - *Machine learning* is like giving the chef **1000 cakes and 1000 ingredient lists**, and asking: "Figure out the recipe." After tasting many examples, the chef generalizes a recipe (model) on their own.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Math Exam Style:**
> - *Traditional:* "Here is the formula, here are the numbers, compute the answer." (Formula + Data → Output)
> - *Machine Learning:* "Here are 50 solved examples — derive the formula yourself, then solve the new problem." (Data + Output → Function → New Output)

## 1.3 The Fundamental Inversion

The single most important idea in this module is the **inversion of inputs and outputs** between the two paradigms.

| Paradigm | What the Human Provides | What the Machine Produces |
|---|---|---|
| Traditional Programming | **Data** + **Program (Rules)** | **Output** |
| Machine Learning | **Data** + **Desired Output (Labels)** | **Program (Learned Model)** |

In symbolic form:

- Traditional:  $$f(x) = y, \quad \text{where } f \text{ is hand-coded}$$
- Machine Learning:  $$\hat{f} \approx f, \quad \text{where } \hat{f} \text{ is learned from } \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Traditional Programming

The pipeline is a deterministic, three-stage hand-crafted chain:

1. **Problem Analysis:** The developer studies the problem domain (e.g., tax calculation, sorting).
2. **Rule Formulation:** The developer manually writes every logical condition, branch, and arithmetic operation in a programming language. Every edge case must be anticipated.
3. **Execution:** The compiler/interpreter translates the rules into machine code, and the CPU executes them deterministically on the supplied input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core 'Why':** The system's intelligence is bounded by the **creativity, foresight, and domain expertise of the human programmer**. If a situation not covered by the rules arises, the program fails or returns garbage (the "brittleness" of rule-based systems).

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Machine Learning

The pipeline is a statistical, four-stage data-driven chain:

1. **Data Collection:** Gather a representative dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ of input–output pairs.
2. **Model Selection:** Choose a hypothesis class $\mathcal{H}$ (e.g., linear regression, decision tree, neural network) with tunable parameters $\theta$.
3. **Training (Optimization):** Adjust $\theta$ to minimize a loss function $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ that measures the discrepancy between predictions $\hat{y}$ and true outputs $y$.
4. **Inference (Deployment):** Apply the trained model $\hat{f}_\theta$ to *new, unseen* inputs $x_{\text{new}}$ to obtain predictions $\hat{y}_{\text{new}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core 'Why':** Intelligence emerges from **patterns hidden in large volumes of data**, not from hand-written rules. The system can generalize to cases it has never literally seen, provided those cases resemble the training distribution.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix

| Dimension | Traditional Programming | Machine Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | Data + Hand-coded rules | Data + Labels (or just data) |
| **Output** | Answer / Action | Learned Model (function/parameters) |
| **Logic Source** | Human developer | Statistical inference from data |
| **Data Dependency** | Low to moderate | Very high (data-hungry) |
| **Adaptability** | Static — must be manually updated | Dynamic — can be retrained on new data |
| **Error Handling** | Deterministic (predictable) | Probabilistic (has a measurable error rate) |
| **Transparency** | Fully interpretable (white box) | Often a black box (especially deep nets) |
| **Human Effort** | High in rule design | High in data preparation & feature engineering |
| **Hardware Need** | Modest CPU | High RAM/GPU for large models |
| **Best Suited For** | Well-defined, rule-based, deterministic tasks | Pattern recognition, prediction, perception tasks |
| **Examples** | Calculator, payroll system, compiler | Spam filter, face recognition, recommendation system |
| **Failure Mode** | Crashes / wrong output for unseen cases | Generalization error, data drift, overfitting |

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbolic Form | Plain English |
|---|---|---|
| Traditional paradigm | $y = P(x)$ | Output is a deterministic function of input under program $P$ |
| ML paradigm | $\hat{f} = \mathcal{A}(\mathcal{D})$ | Algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ produces an approximate function from dataset $\mathcal{D}$ |
| Loss function (regression) | $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{f}_\theta(x_i))^2$ | Mean Squared Error — quantifies prediction error |
| Loss function (classification) | $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\big[y_i \log \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{p}_i)\big]$ | Binary Cross-Entropy — log-loss for 2-class problems |
| Generalization gap | $\vert \text{Err}_{\text{test}} - \text{Err}_{\text{train}} \vert$ | How much worse the model is on unseen data |
| Sample complexity | $N = \mathcal{O}(\dots)$ | Number of training samples required to reach a target error |
| Bayes error $\varepsilon^*$ | $\mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim \mathcal{P}}[\mathbf{1}(\hat{f}(x) \neq y)]$ | Irreducible minimum error — the floor no model can beat |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In production systems, both paradigms often **co-exist**. For example, an autonomous car uses *traditional code* for braking logic, sensor fusion arbitration, and CAN-bus signalling, but uses *machine learning* for pedestrian detection, lane segmentation, and traffic-sign classification. KTU examiners love this hybrid viewpoint — state it whenever asked "which is better?"

## 2.5 When to Choose Which Paradigm

- **Choose Traditional Programming** when the rules are **known, finite, and auditable** (e.g., billing, payroll, sorting, compilers, embedded control loops).
- **Choose Machine Learning** when the rules are **unknown, too complex, or constantly changing** (e.g., speech recognition, fraud detection, medical diagnosis from images, stock forecasting).
- **Choose a Hybrid System** when part of the pipeline is rule-based and part requires learned perception (most modern AI products).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> This section is **conceptual-analytical** rather than purely algebraic, because the topic is introductory. We expand the *inversion principle* and the *learning objective* step by step, with full mathematical justification.

## 3.1 Formalizing the Inversion

**Step 1 — Traditional Programming formulation.**

The programmer fixes a function $f: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ in code. For any new input $x \in \mathcal{X}$, the output is computed as:

$$y = f(x)$$

Every branch, threshold, and arithmetic step in $f$ is **explicitly authored**.

**Step 2 — Machine Learning formulation.**

The programmer does *not* know $f$. Instead, they are given a dataset:

$$\mathcal{D} = \{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \dots, (x_N, y_N)\}$$

The goal is to find a hypothesis $\hat{f} \in \mathcal{H}$ that best approximates the unknown $f$ across the data distribution $\mathcal{P}$.

**Step 3 — The Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) objective.**

The most general learning objective is:

$$\hat{f}^{*} = \arg\min_{\hat{f} \in \mathcal{H}} \underbrace{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\big(\hat{f}(x_i),\, y_i\big)}_{\text{Empirical Risk } R_{\text{emp}}(\hat{f})}$$

where $\mathcal{L}$ is a loss function (e.g., squared error, cross-entropy, hinge loss). Expanding for the squared-loss case:

$$\hat{f}^{*} = \arg\min_{\hat{f} \in \mathcal{H}} \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\big(y_i - \hat{f}(x_i)\big)^{2}$$

**Step 4 — From empirical to expected risk.**

A central theorem in learning theory states that the expected (true) risk is bounded by:

$$R(\hat{f}) \leq R_{\text{emp}}(\hat{f}) + \underbrace{\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{VC}(\mathcal{H}) \cdot \log N}{N}}}_{\text{Complexity / Generalization term}}$$

where $\mathrm{VC}(\mathcal{H})$ is the Vapnik–Chervonenkis dimension of the hypothesis class, a measure of model capacity.

**Step 5 — Interpretation of the bound.**

The bound reveals **why** ML differs fundamentally from traditional programming:
- The first term ($R_{\text{emp}}$) rewards *fitting* the training data.
- The second term penalizes *excess complexity* — it shrinks as $N$ grows.
- The optimum $\hat{f}$ is the **trade-off** between the two: a model complex enough to capture $f$, yet simple enough to generalize.

## 3.2 Worked Conceptual Example: Spam Filtering

We use the same task to illustrate both paradigms side by side.

### A. Traditional Approach (Rule-Based)

A developer hand-codes rules like:
- *If* the subject contains the substring **"FREE"** $\rightarrow$ mark as spam.
- *If* the sender is in the blocklist $\rightarrow$ mark as spam.
- *Else* $\rightarrow$ mark as not spam.

Symbolically:

$$\hat{y} = g\big(\mathbf{1}[\text{"FREE"} \in \text{subject}] + \mathbf{1}[\text{sender} \in \text{blocklist}] > 0\big)$$

The function $g$ is **static**; spammers who write "Fr3e" or "F.R.E.E." evade the rules.

### B. Machine Learning Approach (Data-Driven)

A developer feeds the model **10,000 labeled emails** (spam / not spam). The model learns a weight vector $w$ and bias $b$:

$$\hat{p} = \sigma(w^{\top} x + b)$$

where $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ is the sigmoid (logistic) function, and $x$ is a feature vector (word counts, sender reputation, etc.). The decision rule is:

$$\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1, & \hat{p} \geq 0.5 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The parameters are updated by **gradient descent**:

$$w \leftarrow w - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w}, \qquad b \leftarrow b - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b}$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate and $\mathcal{L}$ is the binary cross-entropy loss. The model **adapts** when retrained on new spam patterns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-away:** The traditional approach encodes the developer's *current knowledge*; the ML approach encodes the *collective intelligence of the data*. KTU questions often ask: "Why does the ML approach outperform rule-based filters over time?" — the answer is **continuous adaptability through retraining**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Comparison of Paradigms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TR["TRADITIONAL PROGRAMMING"]
        direction LR
        A1[Input Data X] --> C1[Program: Hand-coded Rules f]
        B1[Rules f authored by Human] --> C1
        C1 --> D1[Output Y]
    end

    subgraph ML["MACHINE LEARNING"]
        direction LR
        A2[Input Data X] --> C2[Learning Algorithm A]
        B2[Desired Output Labels Y] --> C2
        C2 --> D2[Learned Model f-hat approx f]
        D2 --> E2[Output Y-hat for new X]
    end

    classDef inputBox fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef ruleBox fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef algoBox fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef outputBox fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000

    class A1,A2,B2 inputBox
    class B1,C1 ruleBox
    class C2 algoBox
    class D1,D2,E2 outputBox
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — The ML Training–Inference Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OFFLINE["OFFLINE TRAINING PHASE"]
        D[Training Dataset D = x_i, y_i] --> M[Choose Model Class H]
        M --> L[Define Loss Function L]
        L --> O[Optimizer: Minimize L over theta]
        O --> MOD[Trained Model f-hat with params theta-star]
    end

    subgraph ONLINE["ONLINE INFERENCE PHASE"]
        XNEW[New Input x_new] --> MOD
        MOD --> PRED[Prediction y-hat = f-hat x_new]
    end

    PRED --> FB{Monitor Error}
    FB -- Above Threshold --> RET[Retrain on New Data]
    RET --> OFFLINE
    FB -- Acceptable --> END[Deploy to End User]

    classDef data fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef model fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef user fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2,color:#000

    class D,XNEW data
    class M,L,O,RET process
    class MOD,PRED model
    class FB decision
    class END user
```

## 4.3 Decision-Flow Diagram — Choosing the Right Paradigm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Start: Have a New Problem] --> Q1{Are the rules known, finite, and stable?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is the dataset small or unavailable?}
    Q1 -- No --> ML[Use Machine Learning]

    Q2 -- Yes --> TP[Use Traditional Programming]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Does the problem require perception or pattern recognition?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> HYB[Use Hybrid System]
    Q3 -- No --> ML

    HYB --> EX[Example: Autonomous Vehicle - ML for vision, rules for braking logic]
    ML --> EX2[Example: Spam Filter, Face Recognition, Medical Imaging]
    TP --> EX3[Example: Payroll System, Calculator, Sorting Algorithm]

    classDef start fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef outcome fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef example fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000

    class Q start
    class Q1,Q2,Q3 decision
    class TP,ML,HYB outcome
    class EX,EX2,EX3 example
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**
> Differentiate between **traditional programming** and **machine learning** with respect to the inputs, the role of the developer, and the nature of the output produced.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Traditional Programming | Machine Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Inputs** | Data + Hand-written program (rules) | Data + Expected output (labels) |
| **Developer role** | Authors every rule explicitly | Designs features, picks model, supplies data |
| **Output** | Deterministic result for given input | A *learned model/function* that maps inputs to outputs |
| **Logic source** | Human intelligence | Patterns inferred from data |

(1 mark for inputs row, 1 mark for developer role, 1 mark for output row — any equivalent correct framing gets full credit.)

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
> List **any three** real-world problems for which machine learning is a more suitable approach than traditional programming. Justify each choice in one sentence.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Spam email detection** — Spammers constantly mutate text (e.g., "Fr3e", "F.R.E.E."), making hand-written rules brittle; ML adapts by learning from new labeled emails. *(1 mark)*
2. **Face recognition in images** — The mapping from raw pixel intensities to identity is far too complex to hand-code; deep CNNs learn the hierarchy of features automatically. *(1 mark)*
3. **Medical diagnosis from X-ray images** — Subtle, high-dimensional patterns are difficult to specify as rules; a CNN trained on labeled radiographs learns to detect pathologies with expert-level accuracy. *(1 mark)*

(Any three correct, well-justified examples earn full 3 marks.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1, Q1(a) Modified]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the fundamental difference between **traditional programming** and **machine learning** using a suitable block diagram. State clearly what each paradigm takes as input and what it produces as output.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** With the help of the spam-filtering example, demonstrate how the **inversion of inputs and outputs** between the two paradigms is realised in practice. Include the role of the loss function and one optimization step in your answer.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Define Traditional Programming.**
Traditional programming is a deterministic, rule-based paradigm in which the developer writes an explicit program $P$ that maps input data $X$ to output $Y$. The developer's intelligence is encoded line-by-line in the source code.

$$Y = P(X), \quad P \text{ is hand-coded}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Define Machine Learning.**
Machine learning is a data-driven paradigm in which the system infers an approximate function $\hat{f}$ from a dataset of input–output pairs. The developer's role shifts from writing rules to curating data and choosing the model class.

$$\hat{f} = \mathcal{A}\big(\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}\big), \quad \hat{f} \approx f$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Block Diagram comparison.**

```
TRADITIONAL:  [Data X] + [Program P]  --->  [Output Y]
                  ▲           ▲
                  │           │
                Given       Hand-coded
                by user     by developer

MACHINE LEARNING:  [Data X] + [Labels Y]  --->  [Model f-hat]
                       ▲           ▲             │
                       │           │             ▼
                     Given       Given      [Prediction on new X]
                     by user     by user
```

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Key Inversion Statement.**
The crucial inversion is: *what was input becomes output and vice versa*. In traditional programming, the program is input; in ML, the program (model) is output. *(1 mark)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — State the spam-filtering task.**
Goal: classify an incoming email as spam ($\hat{y}=1$) or ham ($\hat{y}=0$). The mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \to \{0, 1\}$ is unknown and shifts over time as spammers evolve.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Traditional rule-based version.**
A developer hand-codes rules, e.g.,
$$\hat{y}_{\text{rules}} = \mathbf{1}\big[\text{"FREE"} \in \text{subject}\big] \lor \mathbf{1}\big[\text{sender} \in \text{blocklist}\big]$$
This is brittle: spammers bypass it via "Fr3e", image-based text, etc. Updating requires manual code edits.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — ML version with loss + one optimization step.**
The model computes a probability via a logistic function:
$$\hat{p} = \sigma(w^{\top} x + b) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(w^{\top} x + b)}}$$
with binary cross-entropy loss over $N$ samples:
$$\mathcal{L}(w, b) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\big[y_i \log \hat{p}_i + (1 - y_i)\log(1 - \hat{p}_i)\big]$$
A single gradient-descent update (e.g., on $w_j$) is:
$$w_j \leftarrow w_j - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_j} = w_j + \eta \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\big(y_i - \hat{p}_i\big) x_{i,j}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Conclusion.**
The ML approach *learns* discriminative word weights directly from labeled data, automatically adapts to new spam patterns on retraining, and quantifies its uncertainty via $\hat{p}$ — none of which the rule-based system provides. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1, Q1(b) Modified]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of machine learning over traditional programming. State at least two engineering domains where traditional programming is still preferred.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** For a **weather-prediction system**, outline (i) a traditional rule-based approach, (ii) a machine-learning approach, and (iii) a hybrid approach. Justify why the hybrid may be the most robust in production.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Advantages of ML over traditional programming (4 marks):**

1. **Handles high-dimensional, non-linear patterns** that are infeasible to hand-code (e.g., image, speech, text).
2. **Improves with more data** — performance scales with $N$, while rule-based systems plateau.
3. **Self-adapts to distribution shift** through periodic retraining, without manual code rewrites.
4. **Discovers latent features** automatically (representation learning), reducing manual feature engineering.
5. **Quantifies uncertainty** through probabilistic outputs (e.g., $\hat{p}$, confidence intervals).

**Limitations of ML (2 marks):**

1. **Data-hungry and bias-prone** — biased or insufficient data produces biased or unreliable models.
2. **Black-box behaviour** — many models (deep nets, ensembles) are hard to interpret, raising concerns in safety-critical and regulated domains.
3. **Computationally expensive** at training time (GPUs, large memory) compared to executing pre-compiled rules.
4. **No guarantee of correctness** — outputs are probabilistic; failures are silent and statistical, not exceptions.

**Domains where traditional programming is still preferred (1 mark):**
- **Embedded control systems** (e.g., ABS braking, fly-by-wire) — hard real-time, fully auditable logic is mandatory.
- **Banking ledger and payroll systems** — strict regulatory auditability demands deterministic, reproducible computation.
- **Compilers and OS kernels** — correctness and predictability outweigh the benefit of any learned component.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**(i) Traditional rule-based approach (2 marks):**
Meteorologists hand-code rules such as:
- *If* atmospheric pressure drops by > 3 hPa in 6 hours *and* humidity > 80% *and* wind shifts clockwise $\rightarrow$ predict storm within 12 hours.
- *If* temperature inversion observed at altitude $h$ *and* surface temperature $< 0^{\circ}\text{C}$ $\rightarrow$ predict frost.

Symbolically, $\hat{y}_{\text{rule}} = g(\Delta P, H, \Delta \theta_{\text{wind}}, T_{\text{surface}}, h)$. *Limitation:* rules are coarse, miss rare-event combinations, and cannot leverage satellite-radar image data.

**(ii) Machine-learning approach (2 marks):**
A regression model is trained on years of historical weather data:
$$\hat{y} = \hat{f}_\theta(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_d)$$
where inputs include temperature, pressure, humidity, wind, satellite imagery features, and outputs are continuous (rainfall mm, max temperature) or categorical (storm / no-storm). The model is optimized via:

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\big(y_i - \hat{f}_\theta(x_i)\big)^{2}$$

*Limitation:* purely data-driven predictions may violate physical laws (e.g., predict negative humidity) and offer no interpretable causal mechanism.

**(iii) Hybrid approach (3 marks):**
A production-grade weather system uses:
- **ML module** to extract features from satellite imagery, radar, and historical patterns and to produce a probabilistic forecast $P(\text{storm})$.
- **Rule-based physics constraints** as a post-processing filter that rejects physically impossible outputs (e.g., humidity $< 0$, temperature outside $[-90^{\circ}\text{C}, 60^{\circ}\text{C}]$).
- **Deterministic alert logic** to trigger public warnings when $P(\text{storm}) > \tau$ **and** a human-confirmed rule (e.g., official meteorological threshold) is met.

The hybrid is most robust because (a) ML captures complex non-linear patterns, (b) physics-based rules enforce domain validity, and (c) deterministic alerting provides regulatory and safety accountability. *(3 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Losing Mistakes**
> 1. **Writing only definitions, no comparison.** A common 2-mark loss: students define ML but never contrast it with traditional programming. Always use a *table* or *side-by-side* comparison.
> 2. **Confusing "AI" with "ML".** KTU strict marking: AI $\supset$ ML $\supset$ Deep Learning. Many students treat them as synonyms and lose the CO1 conceptual clarity mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the role of data.** A definition of ML that omits *learning from data* is incomplete. KTU examiners explicitly look for the phrase "learns from data without being explicitly programmed."
> 4. **Stating ML is "always better".** This is factually wrong and costs 1–2 marks. ML fails on small-data, deterministic-logic problems. KTU loves questions like *"Is ML always superior? Justify."* — the correct answer is **No, not always**.
> 5. **Skipping diagrams in 7-mark sub-parts.** Part (a) of 7-mark questions in KTU 2024 scheme *expects* a labelled diagram. Omitting it costs at least 2 marks.
> 6. **Writing the loss function without defining its variables.** Always state what $x_i$, $y_i$, $\hat{p}_i$, $N$, and $\eta$ denote before plugging in the formula.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Machine Learning vs. Traditional Programming**

- ☐ Traditional programming = **Data + Rules → Output**. ML = **Data + Labels → Learned Model**. *(The "inversion" is the #1 KTU high-weight point.)*
- ☐ Traditional programming is **deterministic and rule-based**; ML is **probabilistic and data-driven**.
- ☐ In ML, the **developer does not write the function**; the algorithm *discovers* it by minimizing a **loss function** $\mathcal{L}$ over parameters $\theta$.
- ☐ Standard regression loss: $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ (MSE).
- ☐ Standard classification loss: $\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum [y_i \log \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{p}_i)]$ (Binary Cross-Entropy).
- ☐ Optimization is typically via **gradient descent**: $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$.
- ☐ ML **outperforms** traditional programming for: high-dimensional data, shifting distributions, perception tasks, pattern recognition.
- ☐ Traditional programming **outperforms** ML for: small-data deterministic tasks, real-time safety-critical systems, regulatory/auditable workflows.
- ☐ Production AI systems are **almost always hybrid** — combine ML perception with rule-based decision logic.
- ☐ Key KTU buzzwords to use verbatim: *"learns from data"*, *"without being explicitly programmed"*, *"generalization"*, *"loss minimization"*, *"empirical risk"*, *"hybrid intelligence system"*.
- ☐ The VC dimension $\mathrm{VC}(\mathcal{H})$ bounds the generalization gap — a model class with higher VC needs more data.
- ☐ ML failure modes to mention in answers: **overfitting, underfitting, data drift, bias, black-box opacity, computational cost**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAIT404 - FOUNDATION OF MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Machine learning paradigms - supervised

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Supervised Learning** is a machine learning paradigm in which a computational model is trained on a *labelled dataset* — i.e., each training example is a pair $(x_i, y_i)$ where $x_i \in \mathcal{X}$ is the input feature vector and $y_i \in \mathcal{Y}$ is the corresponding known target label (or continuous value). The objective is to learn a mapping function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ such that, for unseen inputs, the predicted output $\hat{y} = f(x)$ closely approximates the true label.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PBAIT404, Module 1):**
> "Supervised learning is the machine learning task of inferring a function from *labelled training data* consisting of a set of training examples, where each example is a pair consisting of an input object (typically a vector) and a desired output value (supervisory signal)."

The two principal sub-categories are:

| Sub-Paradigm | Output Type | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Classification** | Discrete categorical label | $y_i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$ |
| **Regression** | Continuous real-valued quantity | $y_i \in \mathbb{R}$ |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Learning with a Teacher**
> Imagine a child learning to identify fruits. The parent (the *supervisor* or *oracle*) holds up an apple and says, "This is an apple." The child observes the *colour* (red), the *shape* (round), the *texture* (smooth) and stores the association. When later shown an unfamiliar fruit, the child can confidently say, "This is a mango," based on the patterns previously taught. Here, the **fruit** = input $x_i$, the **label** ("apple") = $y_i$, the **child's brain** = the model $f$, and the **parent's guidance** = the supervision signal.

The **"supervised"** label itself is the cornerstone: every training sample comes with a *correct answer* provided by an external oracle, exactly like answer sheets checked by an examiner. The model iteratively adjusts its internal parameters so that its predictions align with the answers on the answer sheet.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Vocabulary)

- **Feature vector** $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ — a $d$-dimensional numerical representation of one input sample.
- **Label / Target** $y_i$ — the ground truth supplied by the supervisor.
- **Hypothesis / Model** $f_\theta(x)$ — a parametric function with learnable weights $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^p$.
- **Training set** $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ — the labelled corpus.
- **Loss function** $\mathcal{L}(y, \hat{y})$ — a scalar penalty measuring prediction error.
- **Generalisation** — the model's ability to perform well on *unseen* data drawn from the same distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU-favourite one-liner to memorise: *"Supervised learning is learning a function from labelled input–output pairs such that the learned function can predict outputs for new, previously unseen inputs."*

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of Supervised Learning

The end-to-end workflow taught in PBAIT404 follows six canonical steps. Each step is a potential **2-mark or 3-mark short-answer question** in the KTU ESE.

1. **Data Acquisition & Labelling** — Collect input samples and assign ground-truth labels (the most expensive step, as it often requires human annotators).
2. **Feature Engineering** — Transform raw data into a numerical feature vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ (e.g., scaling, encoding, dimensionality reduction).
3. **Model Selection** — Choose a hypothesis class $\mathcal{F} = \{f_\theta : \theta \in \mathbb{R}^p\}$ (linear, tree-based, kernel, neural, etc.).
4. **Loss Definition** — Select an appropriate loss $\mathcal{L}$ that quantifies disagreement between prediction $\hat{y}$ and truth $y$.
5. **Optimisation / Training** — Minimise the empirical risk:
 $$\hat{\theta} = \arg\min_{\theta \in \mathbb{R}^p} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\big(f_\theta(x_i),\, y_i\big)$$
6. **Evaluation & Deployment** — Measure performance on a held-out test set $\mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}$ and deploy if generalisation is acceptable.

## 2.2 The Empirical Risk Minimisation (ERM) Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ERM is the *theoretical heart* of supervised learning and a guaranteed high-yield topic in the 14-mark question.

The fundamental axiom of supervised learning is that the *expected* (true) risk
 $$R(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim p_{\text{data}}}\big[\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x), y)\big]$$
is unknown (because $p_{\text{data}}$ is unknown), so we approximate it with the *empirical* risk over the training set:
 $$\hat{R}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\big(f_\theta(x_i), y_i\big)$$

Training = minimising $\hat{R}(\theta)$. The *gap* between $\hat{R}(\theta)$ and $R(\theta)$ is the **generalisation error**, bounded by statistical learning theory (e.g., VC dimension, Rademacher complexity).

## 2.3 Classification vs. Regression — The Core Dichotomy

| Aspect | Classification | Regression |
|---|---|---|
| **Output space** | $y \in \{0,1\}$ (binary) or $\{1,\ldots,K\}$ (multiclass) | $y \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| **Decision rule** | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_k P(y=k \mid x)$ | $\hat{y} = f_\theta(x)$ directly |
| **Loss function** | $0$–$1$ loss, cross-entropy / log-loss | Squared error, absolute error |
| **Evaluation metric** | Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, AUC-ROC | MSE, RMSE, MAE, $R^2$ |
| **KTU examples** | Spam detection, disease diagnosis | House-price prediction, temperature forecasting |

## 2.4 Canonical Algorithms in the Supervised Toolbox

The following algorithms are explicitly listed in the KTU PBAIT404 syllabus under "supervised paradigms":

- **$k$-Nearest Neighbours ($k$-NN)** — non-parametric, instance-based, lazy learner.
- **Linear Regression** — parametric, closed-form solution via *normal equations*.
- **Logistic Regression** — linear classifier using the *sigmoid* link function.
- **Decision Trees (ID3 / C4.5 / CART)** — non-linear, interpretable, hierarchical.
- **Support Vector Machines (SVM)** — maximum-margin geometric classifier.
- **Naïve Bayes** — probabilistic generative classifier assuming feature independence.
- **Neural Networks (MLP)** — universal function approximators trained by back-propagation.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Master every row of this table. KTU examiners frequently set "derive / state the formula" questions worth 3 to 5 marks.

| Concept / Algorithm | Formula | Loss / Criterion | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Regression prediction | $\hat{y} = w^{\top} x + b$ | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Closed-form: $w = (X^{\top}X)^{-1}X^{\top}y$ |
| Logistic Regression prediction | $\hat{p} = \sigma(w^{\top}x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-w^{\top}x}}$ | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum\big[y\log\hat{p} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{p})\big]$ | Sigmoid $\sigma(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z})$ |
| $0$–$1$ Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{0\text{-}1} = \mathbb{1}\{\hat{y} \neq y\}$ | Non-differentiable, used for accuracy | Proxy: hinge / log-loss |
| Hinge Loss (SVM) | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{hinge}} = \max(0,\, 1 - y\cdot f(x))$ | Used in max-margin classifiers | $y \in \{-1, +1\}$ |
| Squared Error (regression) | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{SE}} = (y - \hat{y})^2$ | Differentiable, penalises outliers heavily | Convex for linear models |
| Mean Squared Error | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Evaluation metric | Same as training loss for OLS |
| $R^2$ (Coefficient of Determination) | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | Regression metric | $R^2 \in (-\infty, 1]$ |
| Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \frac{1}{N}\sum \mathbb{1}\{\hat{y}_i = y_i\}$ | Classification metric | Misleading on imbalanced data |
| Precision / Recall | $\text{P} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP},\ \text{R} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Classification metrics | Used with confusion matrix |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = 2\cdot\frac{P\cdot R}{P+R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R | Single-number summary |
| Empirical Risk | $\hat{R}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x_i),y_i)$ | Foundation of training | Minimise w.r.t. $\theta$ |
| Regularised Risk (Ridge) | $\hat{R}_{\lambda}(\theta) = \hat{R}(\theta) + \lambda \Vert w\Vert_2^{2}$ | $L_2$ regularisation | Controls overfitting |
| Regularised Risk (Lasso) | $\hat{R}_{\lambda}(\theta) = \hat{R}(\theta) + \lambda \Vert w\Vert_1$ | $L_1$ regularisation | Induces sparsity |
| Bias–Variariance Decomposition | $\mathbb{E}[(y-\hat{f})^2] = \text{Bias}^{2} + \text{Variance} + \sigma^{2}$ | Diagnostic identity | Irreducible error $\sigma^{2}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> In the formula sheet, use `\vert` or `\mid` for norms (e.g., $\Vert w\Vert_2$) — never the bare pipe symbol `|`, which breaks KTU's printable PDF rendering.

## 2.6 Bias–Variance Tradeoff (The Central Diagnostic)

The generalisation error decomposes as:
 $$\mathbb{E}\big[(y - \hat{f}(x))^{2}\big] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^{2}\big(\hat{f}(x)\big)}_{\text{systematic error}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}\big(\hat{f}(x)\big)}_{\text{sensitivity to data}} + \underbrace{\sigma^{2}}_{\text{irreducible noise}}$$

- **High bias** $\rightarrow$ **underfitting** (model too simple, e.g., linear fit on sinusoidal data).
- **High variance** $\rightarrow$ **overfitting** (model memorises training noise, fails on test).
- The goal of supervised learning is the *sweet spot* minimising the sum.

## 2.7 Train / Validation / Test Split

- **Training set** ($\approx 60$–$70\%$) — used to fit parameters $\theta$.
- **Validation set** ($\approx 10$–$20\%$) — used for *hyperparameter tuning* and model selection.
- **Test set** ($\approx 10$–$20\%$) — used **only once** at the end to report unbiased generalisation error.

Cross-validation (especially $k$-fold) is the standard remedy when the dataset is small.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Derivations below are presented in a KTU-friendly "show every step" format. Every algebraic transition is annotated with the underlying logic.

## 3.1 Derivation: Normal Equation for Linear Regression

**Problem statement.** Given training data $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ and $y_i \in \mathbb{R}$, find the weight vector $w \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ minimising the sum of squared residuals.

### Step 1 — Vectorise the data

Stack the input vectors as rows of a matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$ and the targets as a column vector $y \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$. The prediction vector is:
 $$\hat{y} = X w$$
where we absorb the bias $b$ by appending a column of ones to $X$ (the *augmented* design matrix).

### Step 2 — Write the empirical risk

 $$\hat{R}(w) = \frac{1}{N} \Vert y - X w \Vert_{2}^{2} = \frac{1}{N}(y - Xw)^{\top}(y - Xw)$$

### Step 3 — Expand the quadratic form

 $$\hat{R}(w) = \frac{1}{N}\big(y^{\top}y - 2 w^{\top}X^{\top}y + w^{\top}X^{\top}X w\big)$$

The scalar $y^{\top}y$ does not depend on $w$, so the optimisation is governed by the bracketed quadratic.

### Step 4 — Take the gradient w.r.t. $w$ and set to zero

 $$\nabla_{w}\hat{R}(w) = \frac{1}{N}\big(-2 X^{\top}y + 2 X^{\top}X w\big) = 0$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $w$

 $$X^{\top}X w = X^{\top}y$$

Assuming $X^{\top}X$ is invertible (i.e., $X$ has full column rank), we obtain the **Normal Equation**:

 $$w^{*} = (X^{\top}X)^{-1} X^{\top} y$$

This is the *closed-form* solution to ordinary least squares (OLS). It is the derivation KTU examiners most frequently ask under the "supervised learning algorithms" question.

## 3.2 Derivation: Logistic Regression Gradient Update

**Problem statement.** For binary classification with labels $y \in \{0,1\}$, derive the gradient-descent update rule for logistic regression.

### Step 1 — Model definition

 $$p(y=1 \mid x; w) = \sigma(w^{\top}x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-w^{\top}x}}$$

### Step 2 — Likelihood of the dataset

Assuming i.i.d. samples, the likelihood is:
 $$L(w) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p_i^{y_i}(1-p_i)^{1-y_i}$$

### Step 3 — Negative log-likelihood (the loss)

 $$\mathcal{L}(w) = -\log L(w) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N}\big[y_i \log p_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-p_i)\big]$$

This is the **binary cross-entropy** (BCE) loss.

### Step 4 — Compute the gradient

Using $\frac{d\sigma(z)}{dz} = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ and the chain rule, one obtains (each term contributes):

 $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\big(\sigma(w^{\top}x_i) - y_i\big)\, x_i = X^{\top}(\hat{p} - y)$$

### Step 5 — Gradient descent update

With learning rate $\eta > 0$, the parameter update is:

 $$w \;\leftarrow\; w - \eta\, X^{\top}(\hat{p} - y)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The elegant identity in Step 4 — that the gradient is the *prediction error times the input* — is the *exact same form* as for linear regression with squared loss. This is why KTU boards love to ask: *"Compare the gradient of MSE and BCE and explain why they look similar."*

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Mini-Dataset)

Consider the 1-D dataset:
 $$(x_1, y_1) = (1, 2),\quad (x_2, y_2) = (2, 2.5),\quad (x_3, y_3) = (3, 3.5)$$

Fit a linear model $\hat{y} = w_1 x + w_0$ by direct application of the normal equation.

**Construction of $X$ and $y$ (with intercept column):**

 $$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad y = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2.5 \\ 3.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Compute $X^{\top}X$:**

 $$X^{\top}X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 6 \\ 6 & 14 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Compute $X^{\top}y$:**

 $$X^{\top}y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2.5 \\ 3.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 17.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Determinant of $X^{\top}X$:**

 $$\det(X^{\top}X) = 3 \cdot 14 - 6 \cdot 6 = 42 - 36 = 6$$

**Inverse of $X^{\top}X$:**

 $$(X^{\top}X)^{-1} = \frac{1}{6}\begin{bmatrix} 14 & -6 \\ -6 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7/3 & -1 \\ -1 & 1/2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Closed-form solution:**

 $$w^{*} = (X^{\top}X)^{-1} X^{\top}y = \begin{bmatrix} 7/3 & -1 \\ -1 & 1/2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 17.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 56/3 - 17.5 \\ -8 + 8.75 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.1667 \\ 0.75 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Final model:** $\hat{y} = 0.75\, x + 1.167$. This is the kind of step-by-step numeric problem KTU frequently sets as a 7-mark sub-question.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Favourite Table)

| Dimension | Linear Regression | Logistic Regression | $k$-NN | Decision Tree | SVM |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Task type** | Regression | Classification | Both | Both | Both |
| **Parametric?** | Yes (linear) | Yes (linear) | No (instance-based) | No (piecewise constant) | Yes (linear in feature space) |
| **Train cost** | $O(Nd^{2})$ closed-form | Iterative GD | $O(1)$ (lazy) | $O(Nd \log N)$ | $O(N^{2})$ to $O(N^{3})$ |
| **Predict cost** | $O(d)$ | $O(d)$ | $O(Nd)$ | $O(\text{depth})$ | $O(\#\text{support vectors} \cdot d)$ |
| **Loss** | MSE | BCE / log-loss | Distance vote | Gini / entropy | Hinge |
| **Interpretability** | High | Medium | Low | Very high | Low |
| **Handles non-linearity** | No (without basis) | No (without basis) | Yes (locally) | Yes | Yes (with kernel) |
| **Sensitive to scale?** | Yes | Yes | Very yes | No | Very yes |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Supervised Learning Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Acquisition] --> B[Labelling by Oracle / Supervisor]
    B --> C[Preprocessing & Feature Engineering]
    C --> D[Train / Validation / Test Split]
    D --> E[Choose Model Class f_theta]
    E --> F[Define Loss Function L]
    F --> G[Optimiser e.g. Gradient Descent]
    G --> H{Validation Error Acceptable?}
    H -- No --> I[Hyperparameter Tuning or Model Change]
    I --> E
    H -- Yes --> J[Evaluate on Test Set]
    J --> K{Generalisation OK?}
    K -- No --> I
    K -- Yes --> L[Deploy Model f_theta star]
    L --> M[Predict y_hat for New x]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `No` branches from `H` and `K` re-enter the loop at `E` and `I` respectively — capturing the iterative nature of model selection in real-world ML pipelines.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Taxonomy of Supervised Learning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SL[Supervised Learning] --> CL[Classification]
    SL --> RG[Regression]
    CL --> BIN[Binary]
    CL --> MUL[Multiclass]
    CL --> ML[Muli-Label]
    RG --> LIN[Linear Regression]
    RG --> POL[Polynomial Regression]
    RG --> REG[Regularised Regression]
    CL --> LR[Logistic Regression]
    CL --> KNN[k-Nearest Neighbours]
    CL --> DT[Decision Trees]
    CL --> SVM[Support Vector Machines]
    CL --> NB[Naive Bayes]
    CL --> MLP[Neural Networks]
    RG --> TREE[Regression Trees]
    RG --> MREG[Multiple / Multivariate Regression]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Bias–Variance Tradeoff

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Underfit
        A1[High Bias] --> A2[Low Variance]
        A2 --> A3[Training Error High]
        A3 --> A4[Test Error High]
    end
    subgraph Sweet
        B1[Balanced Bias] --> B2[Balanced Variance]
        B2 --> B3[Training Error Low]
        B3 --> B4[Test Error Low]
    end
    subgraph Overfit
        C1[Low Bias] --> C2[High Variance]
        C2 --> C3[Training Error Very Low]
        C3 --> C4[Test Error High]
    end
    Underfit --> Sweet
    Sweet --> Overfit
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Block Architecture of a Generic Supervised Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Input Feature Vector x] --> FE[Feature Extractor / Encoder]
    FE --> PHI[Representation phi of x]
    PHI --> M[Model f theta: Linear or Non-Linear]
    M --> OUT[Raw Output Score]
    OUT --> POST[Post-Processing e.g. Argmax or Sigmoid]
    POST --> YHAT[Final Prediction y hat]
    Y[True Label y] --> LOSS[Loss Function L y hat, y]
    YHAT --> LOSS
    LOSS --> OPT[Optimiser updates theta]
    OPT --> M
```
