topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAIT404 - FOUNDATION OF MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** reinforcement learning. Basics of parameter estimation - maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) and maximum aposteriori estimation (MAP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Reinforcement Learning (RL) — Formal Definition

**Reinforcement Learning (RL)** is a paradigm of Machine Learning in which an autonomous **agent** learns an optimal **policy** of action selection by continuously interacting with a dynamic **environment**. The agent receives a numerical **reward** signal after each transition and must learn a strategy (a mapping from perceived states of the world to actions) that **maximizes the expected cumulative reward** over time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Reinforcement Learning**
> RL is one of the three canonical ML paradigms alongside Supervised and Unsupervised Learning. Unlike supervised learning, RL does **not** require a labelled dataset; the agent learns from scalar reward feedback obtained through trial-and-error interaction.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — Training a Pet Dog 🐕**
> Imagine teaching a puppy to fetch a ball. You do not show it 10,000 labelled images of "ball" vs "no ball" (that would be supervised learning). Instead, you throw the ball and either reward the dog with a treat (positive reward, $r = +1$) when it fetches correctly, or say "no" (negative reward, $r = -1$) when it chews your slippers. Through millions of such trials, the dog learns *which behaviours* maximize the total number of treats. The puppy is the **agent**, your house is the **environment**, the treat is the **reward**, and the dog's behavioural strategy is the **policy** $\pi$.

### 1.1.2 Mathematical Skeleton

The RL problem is formally cast as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** defined by the 5-tuple:

$$
(S, \; A, \; P, \; R, \; \gamma)
$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $S$ | Set of all possible **states** the environment can be in |
| $A$ | Set of all possible **actions** the agent can take |
| $P(s' \mid s, a)$ | **Transition probability** of moving to state $s'$ from $s$ under action $a$ |
| $R(s, a, s')$ | **Reward function** returning a scalar after a transition |
| $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ | **Discount factor** that prioritizes immediate over future rewards |

The agent's goal is to learn a **policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ that maximizes the expected discounted return:

$$
G_t \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} \, r_{t+k+1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Anchor:** KTU 2024 Module 1 treats RL as a foundational paradigm. The components introduced above are tested as **2-mark and 3-mark short-answer questions** in Part A of university examinations.

---

## 1.2 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) — Formal Definition

**Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** is a frequentist parameter-estimation technique. Given an observed dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N\}$ assumed to be drawn i.i.d. from a probability distribution $P(x \mid \theta)$ governed by an unknown parameter vector $\theta$, the MLE finds the parameter value $\hat{\theta}_{MLE}$ that **maximizes the likelihood function** $L(\theta) = P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$:

$$
\hat{\theta}_{MLE} \;=\; \arg\max_{\theta} \; P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) \;=\; \arg\max_{\theta} \; \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(x_i \mid \theta)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Idea — Reverse the Question**
> Intuitively, MLE flips probability on its head. Instead of asking "Given a coin, what is the probability of 5 heads in 10 flips?", we ask: *"Given that we observed 5 heads in 10 flips, what coin bias $\theta$ makes this observation most plausible?"* The bias that maximizes that plausibility is the MLE.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — The Biased Coin Detective 🪙**
> You flip a mysterious coin 100 times and obtain 70 heads. You suspect the coin is biased. MLE says: *for every possible bias $\theta \in [0,1]$, compute the probability of observing exactly 70 heads. Pick the $\theta$ that gives the highest probability.* For a Bernoulli distribution, this turns out to be $\hat{\theta} = 0.70$ — the sample mean. Intuitive, isn't it?

### 1.2.2 Why the Log-Likelihood?

Because products of probabilities (one factor per data point) underflow numerically and are awkward to differentiate, we take the **monotonic logarithm** to obtain the **log-likelihood**:

$$
\ell(\theta) \;=\; \log L(\theta) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(x_i \mid \theta)
$$

Since $\log$ is strictly monotonic, $\arg\max \ell(\theta) = \arg\max L(\theta)$.

---

## 1.3 Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) Estimation — Formal Definition

**Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** is a Bayesian parameter-estimation technique. It finds the parameter value $\hat{\theta}_{MAP}$ that **maximizes the posterior probability** $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$, combining the likelihood with a **prior** distribution $P(\theta)$ that encodes pre-existing beliefs about $\theta$:

$$
\hat{\theta}_{MAP} \;=\; \arg\max_{\theta} \; P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) \;=\; \arg\max_{\theta} \; P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) \, P(\theta)
$$

using Bayes' rule (the evidence $P(\mathcal{D})$ is constant w.r.t. $\theta$ and hence drops out).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Bridge Between MLE and MAP**
> MAP reduces to MLE when the prior $P(\theta)$ is **uniform** (i.e., all values of $\theta$ are equally likely *a priori*). In that limiting case:
> $$P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) \;\propto\; P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) \cdot \text{const} \;\Rightarrow\; \hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \hat{\theta}_{MLE}$$
> This is a high-yield KTU comparison question.

### 1.3.1 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — The Informed Detective 🕵️**
> Using the same coin example: MLE looked at 70 heads in 100 flips and concluded $\hat{\theta} = 0.70$ coldly. MAP, however, also incorporates *prior knowledge* — say, your factory has a long history of producing coins with bias around 0.50. Even after seeing 70 heads, MAP nudges its estimate toward 0.50, yielding perhaps $\hat{\theta}_{MAP} \approx 0.65$. With more data, the prior's influence fades and MAP converges to MLE.

---

## 1.4 Quick Syllabus Map

| Concept | KTU Cognitive Level | Typical Marks |
|---|---|---|
| RL components (Agent, Env, State, Action, Reward) | Remember / Understand | 2 – 3 |
| Markov Property and MDP tuple | Understand | 3 |
| MLE definition & log-likelihood | Understand / Apply | 3 – 7 |
| MAP definition & comparison with MLE | Understand / Apply | 3 – 14 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing "MLE finds the most probable value of the data" — this is **wrong**. MLE finds the parameter $\theta$ that makes the *observed data* most probable. The conditioning direction is $\arg\max P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$, **not** $\arg\max P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reinforcement Learning — Architectural Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 The Agent–Environment Loop

The agent–environment interaction in RL proceeds as a discrete-time loop:

| Time Step | What Happens |
|---|---|
| $t$ | Agent observes current state $s_t \in S$ |
| $t$ | Agent selects action $a_t \in A$ via policy $\pi(a_t \mid s_t)$ |
| $t+1$ | Environment transitions to new state $s_{t+1} \sim P(\cdot \mid s_t, a_t)$ |
| $t+1$ | Environment emits reward $r_{t+1} = R(s_t, a_t, s_{t+1})$ |
| $t+1$ | Loop continues |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Markov Property:** A state $s_t$ is *Markov* if and only if
> $$P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}, \dots, s_0, a_0) \;=\; P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$$
> The future is conditionally independent of the past given the present. This is the structural assumption that makes MDPs tractable.

### 2.1.2 Policy, Value Function, Action-Value Function

A **policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ is the agent's behavioural rule. Two key value functions are defined under a policy $\pi$:

- **State-Value Function** $V^{\pi}(s)$ — expected return when starting in state $s$ and following $\pi$:

$$
V^{\pi}(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1} \,\Big\vert\, s_t = s\right]
$$

- **Action-Value Function** $Q^{\pi}(s, a)$ — expected return when taking action $a$ in state $s$, then following $\pi$:

$$
Q^{\pi}(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1} \,\Big\vert\, s_t = s, \; a_t = a\right]
$$

### 2.1.3 Bellman Optimality Equations

The value functions satisfy recursive **Bellman equations**. For an optimal policy $\pi^{*}$:

$$
V^{*}(s) \;=\; \max_{a \in A} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, V^{*}(s') \right]
$$

$$
Q^{*}(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a') \right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Bellman equations matter in KTU exams:** They transform an infinite-horizon sequential decision problem into a system of linear/fixed-point equations. The **Bellman Expectation Equation** is asked at the 7-mark level; the **Optimality Equation** forms the foundation of value-iteration algorithms.

### 2.1.4 Exploration vs Exploitation Dilemma

The agent faces a fundamental trade-off:

| Strategy | Description | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **Exploitation** | Choose the action that currently appears best (greedy w.r.t. $Q$) | May get stuck in sub-optimal actions |
| **Exploration** | Try seemingly sub-optimal actions to discover better rewards | Short-term reward loss |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Solutions** (tested as 2-mark questions):
> 1. **$\varepsilon$-greedy:** With probability $\varepsilon$, pick a random action; otherwise, greedy action.
> 2. **Softmax (Boltzmann) exploration:** Sample action with probability $\propto e^{Q(s,a)/\tau}$, where $\tau$ is the temperature.
> 3. **Upper Confidence Bound (UCB):** Optimism in the face of uncertainty.

### 2.1.5 Taxonomy of RL Algorithms

| Criterion | Categories |
|---|---|
| Model knowledge | **Model-based** (knows $P$ and $R$) vs **Model-free** (learns from samples) |
| Learning target | **Value-based** (Q-learning), **Policy-based** (REINFORCE), **Actor-Critic** |
| On/Off policy | **On-policy** (SARSA) vs **Off-policy** (Q-learning) |
| State representation | **Tabular** vs **Function approximation** (Deep RL) |

---

## 2.2 Maximum Likelihood Estimation — Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Derivation Workflow

The MLE procedure is a 4-step ritual:

1. **Specify the model** $P(x \mid \theta)$ for the data.
2. **Construct the likelihood** $L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(x_i \mid \theta)$ (i.i.d. assumption).
3. **Take the log** to get $\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(x_i \mid \theta)$.
4. **Optimize** — differentiate w.r.t. $\theta$, set to zero, solve for $\hat{\theta}$:

$$
\frac{\partial \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta} \;=\; 0, \qquad \frac{\partial^{2} \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta^{2}} \;<\; 0 \;\;\text{(maxima check)}
$$

### 2.2.2 Worked Example Skeleton — Bernoulli

Suppose $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ with $P(x_i = 1 \mid \theta) = \theta$. The likelihood is:

$$
L(\theta) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{N} \theta^{x_i} (1 - \theta)^{1 - x_i}
$$

The log-likelihood is:

$$
\ell(\theta) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ x_i \log \theta + (1 - x_i) \log(1 - \theta) \right]
$$

Differentiating and setting to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \theta} \;=\; \frac{\sum_i x_i}{\theta} - \frac{N - \sum_i x_i}{1 - \theta} \;=\; 0
$$

Solving yields the famously intuitive answer:

$$
\hat{\theta}_{MLE} \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \;=\; \bar{x}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility:** MLE is the engine of classical statistics, used in logistic regression coefficients, Gaussian mixture models (via EM), Hidden Markov Models, and the cross-entropy loss in neural networks.

### 2.2.3 Worked Example Skeleton — Gaussian (Unknown Mean, Known Variance)

For $x_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with known $\sigma^2$:

$$
\ell(\mu) \;=\; -\frac{N}{2} \log(2\pi \sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)^{2}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mu} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sigma^{2}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu) \;=\; 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \hat{\mu}_{MLE} \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:** Under regularity conditions, MLE is **consistent** (converges to the true parameter as $N \to \infty$), **asymptotically efficient** (achieves the Cramér–Rao lower bound), and **asymptotically normal**:
> $$\sqrt{N}\,(\hat{\theta}_{MLE} - \theta^{*}) \;\xrightarrow{d}\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \; \mathcal{I}(\theta^{*})^{-1}\right)$$
> where $\mathcal{I}(\theta)$ is the Fisher Information Matrix.

---

## 2.3 Maximum A Posteriori Estimation — Theoretical Analysis

### 2.3.1 Bayesian Foundation

Bayes' rule gives:

$$
P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) \;=\; \frac{P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) \, P(\theta)}{P(\mathcal{D})}
$$

Since $P(\mathcal{D})$ does not depend on $\theta$:

$$
\hat{\theta}_{MAP} \;=\; \arg\max_{\theta} \; \log P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) + \log P(\theta)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** MAP = MLE + (log of prior). When data is plentiful, the likelihood term dominates, and MAP $\to$ MLE.

### 2.3.2 Closed-Form Example — Gaussian Likelihood + Gaussian Prior

Suppose $x_i \mid \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ and the prior is $\mu \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \tau^2)$. Then:

$$
\ell_{MAP}(\mu) \;=\; \log P(\mathcal{D} \mid \mu) + \log P(\mu) \;=\; -\frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}}\sum_i (x_i - \mu)^2 - \frac{1}{2\tau^{2}}(\mu - \mu_0)^2 + \text{const}
$$

Differentiating and solving:

$$
\hat{\mu}_{MAP} \;=\; \frac{\frac{N \bar{x}}{\sigma^{2}} + \frac{\mu_0}{\tau^{2}}}{\frac{N}{\sigma^{2}} + \frac{1}{\tau^{2}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As $N \to \infty$:** The prior term $\mu_0 / \tau^2$ becomes negligible, and $\hat{\mu}_{MAP} \to \bar{x} = \hat{\mu}_{MLE}$. This is the **Bayesian consistency** principle — data eventually overwhelms the prior.

### 2.3.3 Why Use MAP Over MLE?

| Scenario | Preferred Estimator | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Large dataset, weak prior belief | MLE | Prior contributes negligibly |
| Small dataset, strong domain knowledge | MAP | Prior prevents overfitting to noise |
| Need a single point estimate | MLE / MAP | Both are point estimates (not full Bayesian) |
| Need full predictive distribution | Bayesian Posterior Mean | Computes $\mathbb{E}[\theta \mid \mathcal{D}]$, not just the mode |

---

## 2.4 KTU 2024 Formula Sheet — Quick Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is your one-stop revision sheet for the MLE / MAP / RL portion of Module 1.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MLE Objective | $\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \arg\max_{\theta} \prod_{i} P(x_i \mid \theta)$ | i.i.d. samples |
| 2 | Log-Likelihood | $\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(x_i \mid \theta)$ | Monotonic transform of $L$ |
| 3 | MLE Optimality | $\frac{\partial \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = 0$ | First-order necessary condition |
| 4 | Bernoulli MLE | $\hat{\theta} = \bar{x}$ | Sample mean of binary outcomes |
| 5 | Gaussian Mean MLE | $\hat{\mu} = \bar{x}$ | Sample mean of normal data |
| 6 | MAP Objective | $\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\theta} P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) P(\theta)$ | Drop the $P(\mathcal{D})$ denominator |
| 7 | MAP Log-form | $\ell_{MAP}(\theta) = \log P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) + \log P(\theta)$ | Additive log-prior correction |
| 8 | MLE $\equiv$ MAP | When $P(\theta) = $ uniform | Prior is non-informative |
| 9 | MDP Tuple | $(S, A, P, R, \gamma)$ | Defines the RL environment |
| 10 | Discounted Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1}$ | $\gamma \in [0,1)$ |
| 11 | State-Value | $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid s_t = s]$ | Under policy $\pi$ |
| 12 | Action-Value | $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid s_t=s, a_t=a]$ | Under policy $\pi$ |
| 13 | Bellman Optimality for $V^{*}$ | $V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s'\mid s,a)[R + \gamma V^{*}(s')]$ | Optimal policy assumption |
| 14 | Bellman Optimality for $Q^{*}$ | $Q^{*}(s,a) = \sum_{s'} P(s'\mid s,a)[R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s',a')]$ | Foundation of Q-learning |
| 15 | Markov Property | $P(s_{t+1}\mid s_t, a_t) = P(s_{t+1}\mid s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, \dots)$ | Past and future are independent given $s_t$ |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following derivations are exhaustive — every algebraic transition is shown in full. This is calibrated to the KTU 2024 valuation scheme, where partial credit requires showing all intermediate steps.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — MLE for a Bernoulli Distribution

**Problem Setup:** Let $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N$ be i.i.d. samples from a Bernoulli distribution with unknown parameter $\theta \in [0, 1]$:

$$
P(x_i \mid \theta) \;=\; \theta^{x_i} (1 - \theta)^{1 - x_i}, \quad x_i \in \{0, 1\}
$$

Given observed data with $k = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ ones and $N - k$ zeros, derive the MLE for $\theta$.

### Step 1 — Construct the Likelihood

By the i.i.d. assumption, the joint probability of the dataset factors as a product:

$$
L(\theta) \;=\; P(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N \mid \theta) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(x_i \mid \theta) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{N} \theta^{x_i} (1 - \theta)^{1 - x_i}
$$

**Explanation:** Each data point is independent, so probabilities multiply. The exponent $x_i$ selects the $\theta$ factor when $x_i = 1$, and the exponent $1 - x_i$ selects the $(1-\theta)$ factor when $x_i = 0$.

### Step 2 — Simplify the Likelihood

Rearranging the products by collecting like exponents:

$$
L(\theta) \;=\; \theta^{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i} \cdot (1 - \theta)^{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(1 - x_i)} \;=\; \theta^{k} (1 - \theta)^{N - k}
$$

**Explanation:** The exponent of $\theta$ is the count $k$ of ones; the exponent of $(1-\theta)$ is the count $N-k$ of zeros.

### Step 3 — Take the Logarithm

To convert the product into a sum (and to make differentiation tractable), we take the natural log:

$$
\ell(\theta) \;=\; \log L(\theta) \;=\; k \log \theta + (N - k) \log(1 - \theta)
$$

**Explanation:** The constant $N \log 1$ from the count of terms is absorbed; $\log$ of a product = sum of logs. Monotonicity guarantees that maximizing $\ell$ is equivalent to maximizing $L$.

### Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\theta$

$$
\frac{\partial \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta} \;=\; \frac{k}{\theta} - \frac{N - k}{1 - \theta}
$$

**Explanation:** The derivative of $\log \theta$ is $1/\theta$, multiplied by $k$. The derivative of $\log(1-\theta)$ is $-1/(1-\theta)$, multiplied by $(N-k)$.

### Step 5 — Set the Derivative to Zero

$$
\frac{k}{\theta} - \frac{N - k}{1 - \theta} \;=\; 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{k}{\theta} \;=\; \frac{N - k}{1 - \theta}
$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$
k(1 - \theta) \;=\; (N - k)\theta \quad\Rightarrow\quad k - k\theta \;=\; N\theta - k\theta \quad\Rightarrow\quad k \;=\; N\theta
$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $\hat{\theta}_{MLE}$

$$
\boxed{\hat{\theta}_{MLE} \;=\; \frac{k}{N} \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \;=\; \bar{x}}
$$

### Step 7 — Second-Order Check (Verify Maximum)

$$
\frac{\partial^{2} \ell}{\partial \theta^{2}} \;=\; -\frac{k}{\theta^{2}} - \frac{N - k}{(1 - \theta)^{2}} \;<\; 0
$$

**Explanation:** Both terms are strictly negative for $\theta \in (0, 1)$ and $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, N\}$. Hence the critical point is indeed a global maximum on the interior. The boundary cases $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = 1$ are also handled: $L(0) = 0$ if $k > 0$ and $L(1) = 0$ if $N - k > 0$, so neither can be a global max unless the data is degenerate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MLE for a Univariate Gaussian (Mean and Variance)

**Problem Setup:** Let $x_1, \dots, x_N$ be i.i.d. samples from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$. Derive $\hat{\mu}_{MLE}$ and $\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{MLE}$.

### Step 1 — Write the Likelihood

$$
L(\mu, \sigma^{2}) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^{2}}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(x_i - \mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right) \;=\; (2\pi \sigma^{2})^{-N/2} \exp\!\left( -\frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)^{2} \right)
$$

### Step 2 — Take the Log

$$
\ell(\mu, \sigma^{2}) \;=\; -\frac{N}{2} \log(2\pi) - \frac{N}{2} \log \sigma^{2} - \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)^{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Partial Derivative w.r.t. $\mu$

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mu} \;=\; -\frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\partial}{\partial \mu}\left[ (x_i - \mu)^{2} \right] \;=\; -\frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (-2)(x_i - \mu) \;=\; \frac{1}{\sigma^{2}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)
$$

Setting to zero:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu) \;=\; 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i - N\mu \;=\; 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\hat{\mu}_{MLE} \;=\; \bar{x} \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i}
$$

### Step 4 — Partial Derivative w.r.t. $\sigma^2$

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \sigma^{2}} \;=\; -\frac{N}{2\sigma^{2}} + \frac{1}{2(\sigma^{2})^{2}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)^{2}
$$

Setting to zero and substituting $\hat{\mu} = \bar{x}$:

$$
\frac{N}{2\hat{\sigma}^{2}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2(\hat{\sigma}^{2})^{2}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{MLE} \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The MLE estimator for the variance has a divisor of $N$, **not** $N-1$. The $N-1$ form is the *unbiased* estimator. This subtle point is often a 2-mark follow-up in viva voce.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MAP for Gaussian Likelihood + Gaussian Prior

**Problem Setup:** Given the same Gaussian data $x_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ (with known $\sigma^2$) and a Gaussian prior $\mu \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \tau^2)$, derive $\hat{\mu}_{MAP}$.

### Step 1 — Write the Posterior (up to a constant)

By Bayes' rule:

$$
P(\mu \mid \mathcal{D}) \;\propto\; P(\mathcal{D} \mid \mu) \cdot P(\mu) \;\propto\; \exp\!\left( -\frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \sum_i (x_i - \mu)^{2} \right) \cdot \exp\!\left( -\frac{(\mu - \mu_0)^{2}}{2\tau^{2}} \right)
$$

### Step 2 — Take the Log

$$
\ell_{MAP}(\mu) \;=\; -\frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)^{2} - \frac{(\mu - \mu_0)^{2}}{2\tau^{2}} + \text{const}
$$

**Explanation:** The first term is the log-likelihood; the second is the log-prior. Constants independent of $\mu$ are absorbed.

### Step 3 — Expand the Squared Terms

$$
\ell_{MAP}(\mu) \;=\; -\frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i^{2} - 2x_i\mu + \mu^{2}) - \frac{1}{2\tau^{2}}(\mu^{2} - 2\mu\mu_0 + \mu_0^{2}) + \text{const}
$$

Separating $\mu$-dependent terms:

$$
\ell_{MAP}(\mu) \;=\; -\frac{\mu^{2}}{2}\!\left( \frac{N}{\sigma^{2}} + \frac{1}{\tau^{2}} \right) + \mu \!\left( \frac{\sum_i x_i}{\sigma^{2}} + \frac{\mu_0}{\tau^{2}} \right) + \text{const}
$$

### Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\mu$

$$
\frac{\partial \ell_{MAP}}{\partial \mu} \;=\; -\mu \!\left( \frac{N}{\sigma^{2}} + \frac{1}{\tau^{2}} \right) + \left( \frac{\sum_i x_i}{\sigma^{2}} + \frac{\mu_0}{\tau^{2}} \right) \;=\; 0
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $\hat{\mu}_{MAP}$

$$
\mu \!\left( \frac{N}{\sigma^{2}} + \frac{1}{\tau^{2}} \right) \;=\; \frac{\sum_i x_i}{\sigma^{2}} + \frac{\mu_0}{\tau^{2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\hat{\mu}_{MAP} \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{N \bar{x}}{\sigma^{2}} + \dfrac{\mu_0}{\tau^{2}}}{\dfrac{N}{\sigma^{2}} + \dfrac{1}{\tau^{2}}}}
$$

### Step 6 — Asymptotic Behaviour

- **As $N \to \infty$:** $N/\sigma^2$ dominates, so $\hat{\mu}_{MAP} \to \bar{x} = \hat{\mu}_{MLE}$.
- **As $N \to 0$ (no data):** $\hat{\mu}_{MAP} = \mu_0$ — the prior takes over.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This estimator is a **precision-weighted average** of the data-mean $\bar{x}$ and prior-mean $\mu_0$. Precision = $1/$variance. It is the canonical regularized estimator — a Bayesian "shrinkage" of the data mean toward the prior.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bellman Expectation Equation for $V^{\pi}(s)$

**Problem Setup:** Show that the state-value function under a fixed policy $\pi$ satisfies the Bellman expectation equation.

### Step 1 — Unroll the Return One Step

$$
V^{\pi}(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} r_{t+3} + \cdots \,\Big\vert\, s_t = s \right]
$$

Factor out $r_{t+1} + \gamma \cdot (\text{rest})$:

$$
V^{\pi}(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+2} \,\Big\vert\, s_t = s \right]
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Law of Iterated Expectation

$$
V^{\pi}(s) \;=\; \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+2} \,\Big\vert\, s_{t+1} = s' \right] \right]
$$

**Explanation:** We first average over the action $a \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s)$, then over the next state $s' \sim P(\cdot \mid s, a)$. The reward is realized at this transition; the discounted future from $s'$ onwards equals $\gamma V^{\pi}(s')$ by definition.

### Step 3 — Recognize the Inner Expectation

By the Markov property and time-invariance, the inner expectation is exactly $V^{\pi}(s')$. Hence:

$$
\boxed{V^{\pi}(s) \;=\; \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, V^{\pi}(s') \right]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Bellman Expectation Equation**. It expresses the value of a state recursively in terms of the values of its successors. It forms the backbone of *policy evaluation* algorithms.

---

## 3.5 Side-by-Side Comparison Matrix — MLE vs MAP

| Feature | MLE | MAP |
|---|---|---|
| Philosophical school | Frequentist | Bayesian |
| Objective | $\arg\max_{\theta} P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$ | $\arg\max_{\theta} P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) P(\theta)$ |
| Incorporates prior | No | Yes |
| Output | Point estimate $\hat{\theta}$ | Point estimate $\hat{\theta}$ (posterior mode) |
| Requires prior specification | No | Yes |
| Behaviour as $N \to \infty$ | Converges to true $\theta$ | Converges to true $\theta$ |
| Behaviour with small $N$ | High variance, may overfit | Regularized by prior |
| Computational cost | Usually cheaper | Slightly higher (extra prior term) |
| Connection | MAP → MLE when prior is uniform | MLE is a special case of MAP |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **The Mermaid diagrams below visualize the core architectures. They follow the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 node-identifier rules: every node ID is purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters; labels contain no markdown formatting.**

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Agent–Environment Interaction Loop in Reinforcement Learning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    agentA[AGENT]:::agentStyle
    envB[ENVIRONMENT]:::envStyle
    stateC[STATE S_t]:::stateStyle
    actionD[ACTION A_t]:::actionStyle
    rewardE[REWARD R_t+1]:::rewardStyle
    nextStateF[STATE S_t+1]:::stateStyle

    stateC -->|observation| agentA
    agentA -->|selects| actionD
    actionD -->|executes| envB
    envB -->|emits| rewardE
    envB -->|transitions to| nextStateF
    nextStateF -->|next observation| agentA
    rewardE -->|feedback signal| agentA

    classDef agentStyle fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#0a3d62,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef envStyle fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#145214,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef stateStyle fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#a04000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef actionStyle fill:#9467bd,stroke:#4b2e6a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef rewardStyle fill:#d62728,stroke:#7a0a0a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The agent observes a state, picks an action, the environment reacts by transitioning to a new state and emitting a reward, which feeds back to the agent. The loop continues until a terminal condition.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — The MLE Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startN[START: Observed Data D]:::inputStyle
    modelN[STEP 1: Specify Generative Model P of x given theta]:::procStyle
    likeN[STEP 2: Build Likelihood L of theta equals Product of P of x_i given theta]:::procStyle
    logN[STEP 3: Apply Log Transform to get log-Likelihood ell of theta]:::procStyle
    diffN[STEP 4: Differentiate ell with respect to theta]:::procStyle
    solveN[STEP 5: Set Derivative to Zero and Solve for theta hat]:::procStyle
    checkN[STEP 6: Verify Second Derivative is Negative]:::checkStyle
    outN[OUTPUT: MLE Estimate theta hat MLE]:::outputStyle
    failN[STOP: No Closed Form Solution]:::failStyle

    startN --> modelN
    modelN --> likeN
    likeN --> logN
    logN --> diffN
    diffN --> solveN
    solveN -->|analytical solution exists| checkN
    solveN -->|no closed form| failN
    checkN -->|concave verified| outN
    checkN -->|not concave| failN

    classDef inputStyle fill:#3498db,stroke:#1f5582,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef procStyle fill:#f39c12,stroke:#a0580a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef checkStyle fill:#9b59b6,stroke:#5b2c6f,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef outputStyle fill:#27ae60,stroke:#11552b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef failStyle fill:#c0392b,stroke:#73221a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the Diagram:** MLE is a 6-step pipeline. The critical decision point is whether the first-order condition admits a closed-form solution; if not, numerical optimization (e.g., gradient ascent) is invoked.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Bayesian Inference: MLE vs MAP vs Full Posterior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    priorN[PRIOR P of theta]:::priorStyle
    likeN[LIKELIHOOD P of D given theta]:::likeStyle
    mleN[MLE: argmax of P of D given theta]:::mleStyle
    mapN[MAP: argmax of P of D given theta times P of theta]:::mapStyle
    postN[FULL POSTERIOR P of theta given D]:::postStyle
    dataN[OBSERVED DATA D]:::dataStyle

    dataN --> likeN
    priorN --> mapN
    likeN --> mleN
    priorN --> postN
    likeN --> postN
    likeN --> mapN

    classDef priorStyle fill:#8e44ad,stroke:#4a235a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef likeStyle fill:#16a085,stroke:#0b5345,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef mleStyle fill:#2980b9,stroke:#163a5f,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef mapStyle fill:#d35400,stroke:#7a3a00,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef postStyle fill:#c0392b,stroke:#73221a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef dataStyle fill:#34495e,stroke:#1c2833,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the Diagram:** MLE uses only the likelihood. MAP combines the likelihood with the prior. Full Bayesian inference uses the entire posterior distribution (not just its mode), which is needed for predictive uncertainty quantification.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All questions below are modeled on the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern. Part A questions carry 3 marks each; Part B questions carry 14 marks (with internal choice) split as 7 + 7 sub-parts.**

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Differentiate between Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning paradigms. Give one real-world application of each.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Paradigm | Feedback Signal | Data Type | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Supervised** | Labelled target $y$ for each input $x$ | $(x, y)$ pairs | Email spam classification |
| **Unsupervised** | No feedback; only inputs | $\{x_i\}$ | Customer segmentation via clustering |
| **Reinforcement** | Scalar reward $r$ from environment | $(s, a, r, s')$ trajectories | Game-playing AI (e.g., AlphaGo) |

In RL, the agent learns by *trial and error* rather than from explicit supervision, making it suitable for sequential decision-making tasks.

---

### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Define Maximum Likelihood Estimation. State the MLE for the parameter $\theta$ of a Bernoulli distribution given $N$ i.i.d. samples.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
MLE finds the parameter value $\hat{\theta}$ that maximizes the probability (likelihood) of the observed data:

$$
\hat{\theta}_{MLE} \;=\; \arg\max_{\theta} \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(x_i \mid \theta)
$$

For a Bernoulli distribution with $k$ successes in $N$ trials:

$$
\boxed{\hat{\theta}_{MLE} \;=\; \frac{k}{N}}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **agent–environment interaction loop** in Reinforcement Learning. Define the terms: state, action, reward, policy, and value function. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** State and derive the **Bellman Optimality Equation** for the action-value function $Q^{*}(s, a)$. Why is it called a "recursive" formulation? **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

1. **Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the canonical agent–environment loop diagram.)
   - At each time step $t$, the agent observes state $s_t$.
   - The agent selects action $a_t$ via policy $\pi(a_t \mid s_t)$.
   - The environment transitions to $s_{t+1} \sim P(\cdot \mid s_t, a_t)$.
   - The environment emits reward $r_{t+1} = R(s_t, a_t, s_{t+1})$.
   - The pair $(s_{t+1}, r_{t+1})$ becomes the next observation.

2. **Terminology:**
   - **State $s$:** A complete description of the environment's configuration.
   - **Action $a$:** A move the agent can make from state $s$.
   - **Reward $r$:** A scalar signal indicating the immediate desirability of the transition.
   - **Policy $\pi$:** A (possibly stochastic) mapping from states to action probabilities.
   - **Value function $V^{\pi}(s)$:** Expected cumulative discounted reward from state $s$ under $\pi$.

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

The Bellman Optimality Equation for $Q^{*}$ is:

$$
Q^{*}(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a') \right]
$$

**Derivation Sketch:**

Starting from the action-value function definition:

$$
Q^{*}(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s_{t+1}, a') \,\Big\vert\, s_t = s, \; a_t = a \right]
$$

**Step 1:** Condition on the next state $s_{t+1} = s'$ and apply the law of total expectation:

$$
Q^{*}(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \, \mathbb{E}\!\left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a') \,\Big\vert\, s_{t+1} = s' \right]
$$

**Step 2:** Take the reward $r_{t+1} = R(s, a, s')$ outside the inner expectation, as it is fully determined by the triple $(s, a, s')$:

$$
Q^{*}(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a') \right]
$$

**Recursive nature:** The value of $Q^{*}(s, a)$ is expressed in terms of $Q^{*}(s', a')$ for successor states. This recursion allows iterative algorithms (e.g., Value Iteration, Q-learning) to compute optimal values starting from arbitrary initializations.

---

### **Question B-B.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**(a)** Derive the **Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE)** of the mean $\mu$ of a univariate Gaussian distribution $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ from $N$ i.i.d. samples $\{x_i\}$, assuming $\sigma^2$ is known. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** estimation. Derive the MAP estimate for $\mu$ in the same Gaussian model when the prior is $\mu \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \tau^2)$. Comment on the relationship between MLE and MAP. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Likelihood:**

$$
L(\mu) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^{2}}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(x_i - \mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right) \;=\; (2\pi \sigma^{2})^{-N/2} \exp\!\left( -\frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)^{2} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Log-likelihood:**

$$
\ell(\mu) \;=\; -\frac{N}{2} \log(2\pi \sigma^{2}) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate:**

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mu} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sigma^{2}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu) \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i - N\mu = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \boxed{\hat{\mu}_{MLE} \;=\; \bar{x} \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i}
$$

**Step 5 — Second-order check:** $\frac{\partial^{2} \ell}{\partial \mu^{2}} = -\frac{N}{\sigma^{2}} < 0$ confirms a maximum.

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Definition:** MAP estimates the parameter that maximizes the *posterior* $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$, combining likelihood with a prior.

**Step 1 — Posterior up to a constant:**

$$
P(\mu \mid \mathcal{D}) \;\propto\; \exp\!\left( -\frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \sum_i (x_i - \mu)^{2} \right) \cdot \exp\!\left( -\frac{(\mu - \mu_0)^{2}}{2\tau^{2}} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Log-posterior (dropping constants independent of $\mu$):**

$$
\ell_{MAP}(\mu) \;=\; -\frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \sum_i (x_i - \mu)^{2} - \frac{(\mu - \mu_0)^{2}}{2\tau^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand and collect terms in $\mu$:**

$$
\ell_{MAP}(\mu) \;=\; -\frac{\mu^{2}}{2}\!\left( \frac{N}{\sigma^{2}} + \frac{1}{\tau^{2}} \right) + \mu \!\left( \frac{N\bar{x}}{\sigma^{2}} + \frac{\mu_0}{\tau^{2}} \right) + \text{const}
$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate and set to zero:**

$$
-\mu \!\left( \frac{N}{\sigma^{2}} + \frac{1}{\tau^{2}} \right) + \left( \frac{N\bar{x}}{\sigma^{2}} + \frac{\mu_0}{\tau^{2}} \right) \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\hat{\mu}_{MAP}$:**

$$
\boxed{\hat{\mu}_{MAP} \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{N\bar{x}}{\sigma^{2}} + \dfrac{\mu_0}{\tau^{2}}}{\dfrac{N}{\sigma^{2}} + \dfrac{1}{\tau^{2}}}}
$$

**Step 6 — Relationship to MLE:**

- MAP $\to$ MLE as $N \to \infty$ (data dominates prior).
- MAP $\to \mu_0$ as $N \to 0$ (prior dominates).
- When the prior is uniform ($P(\mu) = \text{const}$), MAP = MLE exactly.
- MAP is a *regularized* MLE — the prior term $P(\mu)$ acts as a penalty, preventing overfitting in low-data regimes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Conditioning direction in MLE:** Many students write $\arg\max_{\theta} P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$ instead of $\arg\max_{\theta} P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$. This loses **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting to drop the constant in MAP:** $P(\mathcal{D})$ is independent of $\theta$ and should be ignored. Some students incorrectly include it in the optimization.
> 3. **Confusing MLE variance divisor:** MLE uses $N$ in the denominator; the unbiased estimator uses $N-1$. Examiners reward students who explicitly mention this distinction.
> 4. **Bellman Equation sign error:** The discount factor $\gamma$ must multiply the *future* value $V^{\pi}(s')$, not the reward. A misplaced $\gamma$ can cost 3–4 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up $\arg\max$ and $\arg\min$:** MLE and MAP *maximize* a quantity. Students occasionally minimize the negative log-likelihood but forget to flip the optimization direction in the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist — read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

### Reinforcement Learning
- ✅ RL is a **trial-and-error** learning paradigm where an agent learns by interacting with an environment.
- ✅ The MDP tuple is $(S, A, P, R, \gamma)$ — know the meaning of each symbol.
- ✅ **Policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ defines the agent's behaviour.
- ✅ **Value function** $V^{\pi}(s)$ = expected discounted return from state $s$ under $\pi$.
- ✅ **Action-value** $Q^{\pi}(s, a)$ = expected return from $(s, a)$ under $\pi$.
- ✅ **Bellman equations** express value recursively in terms of successor values.
- ✅ **Markov property** is the structural assumption underlying MDPs.
- ✅ **Exploration vs Exploitation** is the central dilemma; $\varepsilon$-greedy and softmax are common solutions.

### Maximum Likelihood Estimation
- ✅ MLE maximizes $P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$ — the *likelihood*.
- ✅ Log-likelihood: $\ell(\theta) = \sum_i \log P(x_i \mid \theta)$ — always easier to optimize.
- ✅ Bernoulli MLE: $\hat{\theta} = \bar{x}$.
- ✅ Gaussian mean MLE: $\hat{\mu} = \bar{x}$.
- ✅ Gaussian variance MLE: $\hat{\sigma}^{2} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$ (note: $N$, not $N-1$).
- ✅ MLE is **consistent** and **asymptotically efficient** under regularity conditions.
- ✅ The first-order condition $\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \theta} = 0$ is the workhorse equation.

### Maximum A Posteriori Estimation
- ✅ MAP maximizes $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) P(\theta)$.
- ✅ MAP = MLE + log-prior correction.
- ✅ When $P(\theta)$ is uniform, MAP = MLE.
- ✅ Gaussian likelihood + Gaussian prior → closed-form MAP estimate (precision-weighted average).
- ✅ As $N \to \infty$, MAP $\to$ MLE (data dominates).
- ✅ MAP is a *single point estimate* (the mode of the posterior), not the full posterior.

### Quick Cross-Reference
- ✅ **Difference between MLE and MAP:** MAP includes a prior; MLE does not.
- ✅ **Why use log?** Monotonicity + numerical stability + algebraic tractability.
- ✅ **Connection to RL:** Both MLE and MAP are *parameter estimation* tools used to fit the parameters of any probabilistic model — including the transition model $P(s' \mid s, a)$ in an MDP.
- ✅ **Fisher Information** quantifies the curvature of the log-likelihood and sets the asymptotic variance of MLE.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:**
> **M**LE = **M**ax **L**ikelihood = $P(\text{Data} \mid \theta)$ *(Likelihood only)*
> **M**AP = **M**ax **A** **P**osteriori = $P(\theta \mid \text{Data}) \propto P(\text{Data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)$ *(Likelihood + Prior)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bayesian formulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAIT404 - FOUNDATION OF MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bayesian formulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bayesian Formulation** is a statistical and probabilistic framework for machine learning in which **all forms of uncertainty** (about parameters, hypotheses, predictions, and latent variables) are treated as **probability distributions**, and inference is performed by **updating prior beliefs using observed evidence through Bayes' Theorem** to obtain a **posterior distribution**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PBAIT404 – Foundation of Machine Learning, Module 1), Bayesian formulation is the **bedrock paradigm** upon which:

- Bayesian Linear Regression
- Bayesian Classification (e.g., Naive Bayes)
- Bayesian Belief Networks
- Variational Inference and Expectation Maximization
- Gaussian Processes

are all constructed. The formulation explicitly models **what we know (prior)**, **what we observe (likelihood)**, and **what we conclude (posterior)** — making it a *coherent, mathematically rigorous* alternative to the frequentist approach.

### 1.2 The Frequentist vs Bayesian Philosophical Contrast

| Aspect | Frequentist View | Bayesian View |
|---|---|---|
| Probability | Long-run frequency of an event | Degree of belief / personal uncertainty |
| Parameters | Fixed (unknown) constants | Random variables with distributions |
| Data | Random | Fixed (given) |
| Inference | Estimators, Confidence Intervals | Posterior distribution, Credible Intervals |
| Use of Prior | Not used | Central role |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Questions in ESE frequently ask students to *contrast* these two paradigms and to *derive* Bayes' theorem from conditional probability axioms.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Weather-Prior Story"

Imagine you wake up in **Kerala during monsoon season** and wonder:

> *"Will it rain today?"*

- **Prior Belief (Prior):** Based on June–August climatology, it rains on **7 out of 10 days**. So your prior is $P(\text{rain}) = 0.7$.
- **New Evidence (Likelihood):** You look outside and see **dark cumulonimbus clouds** — historically, when such clouds are present, it rains 90% of the time. This is $P(\text{clouds} \mid \text{rain}) = 0.9$.
- **Updated Belief (Posterior):** Using Bayes' theorem, you now combine your prior with the evidence to get a refined probability $P(\text{rain} \mid \text{clouds})$.

This continuous loop — **prior → evidence → posterior → updated prior** — is the *engine* of Bayesian learning. Every new data point refines the belief.

### 1.4 Why Bayesian Formulation Matters in ML

- Handles **small datasets** gracefully (the prior regularizes).
- Provides **uncertainty quantification** for predictions.
- Naturally prevents **overfitting** (acts as regularizer).
- Foundation for **sequential learning** (online/streaming ML).
- Allows **incorporation of domain knowledge** as prior.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric Convention:** Probabilities always lie in the closed unit interval $[0, 1]$, and for any discrete sample space, $\sum_i P(\omega_i) = 1$ (normalization axiom).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Foundational Probability Axioms Used

Before stating Bayes' theorem, recall:

1. **Conditional Probability:**
$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \quad P(B) > 0
$$

2. **Chain Rule (Product Rule):**
$$
P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B)\,P(B) = P(B \mid A)\,P(A)
$$

These two are sufficient to **derive Bayes' theorem in full**.

### 2.2 The Four Canonical Quantities of Bayesian Formulation

| Symbol | Name | Meaning | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P(H)$ | **Prior** | Belief about hypothesis $H$ *before* seeing data | Often $\pi(H)$ or $P(H)$ |
| $P(D \mid H)$ | **Likelihood** | Probability of data $D$ assuming $H$ is true | $L(H \mid D)$ |
| $P(H \mid D)$ | **Posterior** | Updated belief about $H$ *after* seeing data | Target of inference |
| $P(D)$ | **Evidence / Marginal Likelihood** | Total probability of data, summed/integrated over all hypotheses | Normalizing constant |

### 2.3 The Master Equation — Bayes' Theorem

$$
P(H \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(D)}
$$

And the **expanded form using the law of total probability** for the evidence:
$$
P(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(D \mid H_i)\,P(H_i) \quad \text{(discrete case)}
$$

$$
P(D) = \int P(D \mid H)\,P(H)\,dH \quad \text{(continuous case)}
$$

### 2.4 The Posterior as a Product of Prior and Likelihood

A **conceptually clean reformulation** that KTU examiners often reward:
$$
\underbrace{P(H \mid D)}_{\text{Posterior}} \propto \underbrace{P(D \mid H)}_{\text{Likelihood}} \times \underbrace{P(H)}_{\text{Prior}}
$$

The symbol $\propto$ (proportional to) is used because the evidence $P(D)$ is independent of the hypothesis $H$ and merely acts as a **normalizing constant** to ensure the posterior sums to 1.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem (core) | $P(H \mid D) = \dfrac{P(D \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(D)}$ | All Bayesian inference problems |
| 2 | Posterior ∝ Likelihood × Prior | $P(H \mid D) \propto P(D \mid H)\,P(H)$ | When only relative posterior matters |
| 3 | Evidence (discrete) | $P(D) = \sum_{i} P(D \mid H_i)\,P(H_i)$ | Computing normalizer for finite hypotheses |
| 4 | Evidence (continuous) | $P(D) = \int P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)\,d\theta$ | Continuous parameter space |
| 5 | Posterior Predictive Distribution | $P(x_{\text{new}} \mid D) = \int P(x_{\text{new}} \mid \theta)\,P(\theta \mid D)\,d\theta$ | Predicting new data with uncertainty |
| 6 | Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{\theta} P(\theta \mid D) = \arg\max_{\theta} \, P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)$ | Point estimate from posterior |
| 7 | Maximum Likelihood (MLE) | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_{\theta} P(D \mid \theta)$ | Special case of MAP with uniform prior |
| 8 | Conjugate Prior Update Rule | $P(\theta \mid D) \propto P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)$ | Choosing priors for tractability |
| 9 | Odds Form (Bayes Factor) | $\dfrac{P(H_1 \mid D)}{P(H_2 \mid D)} = \dfrac{P(D \mid H_1)}{P(D \mid H_2)} \cdot \dfrac{P(H_1)}{P(H_2)}$ | Model comparison |
| 10 | Log-Posterior (for computation) | $\log P(H \mid D) = \log P(D \mid H) + \log P(H) - \log P(D)$ | Numerical stability in ML |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Conjugate priors (Beta–Binomial, Dirichlet–Multinomial, Gaussian–Gaussian) are *high-frequency* topics. Memorize the **prior → posterior** update pair for Bernoulli likelihood with Beta prior: if prior is $\text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$ and we observe $k$ successes in $n$ trials, posterior is $\text{Beta}(\alpha + k, \beta + n - k)$.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility of Bayesian Formulation

- **Medical Diagnosis Systems:** Compute probability of disease given test results.
- **Spam Filtering (Naive Bayes):** The earliest commercial-grade ML system.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Sensor fusion with uncertainty quantification.
- **A/B Testing in Industry:** Bayesian A/B tests provide *probabilistic* statements about which variant wins.
- **Reinforcement Learning:** Thompson sampling uses Bayesian posteriors for exploration–exploitation.
- **Climate & Weather Modeling:** Updating forecasts with new satellite data.
- **Genomics & Bioinformatics:** Phylogenetic inference uses Bayesian posteriors extensively.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bayes' Theorem from Conditional Probability

We derive Bayes' theorem rigorously from first principles. This is a **guaranteed 7-mark derivation** in KTU ESE.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of conditional probability for $P(H \mid D)$:**
$$
P(H \mid D) = \frac{P(H \cap D)}{P(D)}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the same definition, but the other way around for $P(D \mid H)$:**
$$
P(D \mid H) = \frac{P(D \cap H)}{P(H)}
$$

**Step 3 — Note that intersection is commutative:**
$$
P(H \cap D) = P(D \cap H)
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange the second equation to express the intersection:**
$$
P(D \cap H) = P(D \mid H)\,P(H)
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute this back into the first equation:**
$$
P(H \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(D)}
$$

> This is **Bayes' Theorem**. The derivation is complete, and it relied only on the definition of conditional probability and the commutativity of intersection.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Evidence Term via Law of Total Probability

For the denominator $P(D)$ (the evidence), when hypotheses $\{H_1, H_2, \ldots, H_n\}$ form a **partition** of the sample space (mutually exclusive and exhaustive):

**Step 1 — Write $D$ as union of disjoint intersections with each $H_i$:**
$$
D = (D \cap H_1) \cup (D \cap H_2) \cup \ldots \cup (D \cap H_n)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the additivity axiom for disjoint events:**
$$
P(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(D \cap H_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the product rule to each term:**
$$
P(D \cap H_i) = P(D \mid H_i)\,P(H_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Final expanded form of the evidence:**
$$
P(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(D \mid H_i)\,P(H_i)
$$

Therefore, the **fully expanded Bayes' theorem** with explicit evidence term:
$$
P(H_j \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid H_j)\,P(H_j)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(D \mid H_i)\,P(H_i)}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Classic "Medical Test" Problem (KTU Favourite)

> **Problem (Dec 2023 Pattern):** A disease affects **1%** of a population. A diagnostic test has:
> - **Sensitivity** (true positive rate): $P(T^+ \mid D^+) = 0.95$
> - **Specificity** (true negative rate): $P(T^- \mid D^-) = 0.90$
>
> A person tests positive. What is the probability they actually have the disease?

**Step 1 — Translate the given quantities into Bayesian notation:**

Let $D^+$ = "has disease", $T^+$ = "tests positive".

$$
P(D^+) = 0.01, \quad P(T^+ \mid D^+) = 0.95, \quad P(T^- \mid D^-) = 0.90
$$

From the specificity:
$$
P(T^+ \mid D^-) = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the evidence $P(T^+)$ using the law of total probability:**
$$
P(T^+) = P(T^+ \mid D^+)\,P(D^+) + P(T^+ \mid D^-)\,P(D^-)
$$

$$
P(T^+) = (0.95)(0.01) + (0.10)(0.99)
$$

$$
P(T^+) = 0.0095 + 0.099 = 0.1085
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' theorem:**
$$
P(D^+ \mid T^+) = \frac{P(T^+ \mid D^+)\,P(D^+)}{P(T^+)}
$$

$$
P(D^+ \mid T^+) = \frac{0.95 \times 0.01}{0.1085}
$$

$$
P(D^+ \mid T^+) = \frac{0.0095}{0.1085} \approx 0.0876
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
$$
P(D^+ \mid T^+) \approx 8.76\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-intuitive Insight (Examiner's Favourite):** Even with a 95% accurate test, a positive result only implies an ~8.76% chance of disease! This is because the **base rate** of the disease (1%) is very low, and the false positives from the large healthy population dominate. This is the **Base Rate Fallacy**, and KTU loves to test whether students grasp it.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Bernoulli Likelihood with Beta Prior (Conjugate Update)

This is the **canonical KTU 2024 Module 1 question** for Bayesian parameter estimation.

> **Problem:** Suppose a coin has unknown bias $\theta \in [0, 1]$ (probability of heads). We place a **Beta(2, 2)** prior on $\theta$ and observe **3 heads in 5 tosses**. Compute the posterior distribution and the MAP estimate.

**Step 1 — Specify the prior:**

The Beta distribution has pdf:
$$
P(\theta) = \frac{1}{B(\alpha, \beta)}\,\theta^{\alpha - 1}(1 - \theta)^{\beta - 1}
$$

For $\text{Beta}(2, 2)$:
$$
P(\theta) \propto \theta^{2-1}(1 - \theta)^{2-1} = \theta(1 - \theta)
$$

**Step 2 — Specify the likelihood:**

For $k = 3$ heads in $n = 5$ tosses, the Bernoulli likelihood is:
$$
P(D \mid \theta) = \binom{5}{3}\,\theta^{3}(1 - \theta)^{2} \propto \theta^{3}(1 - \theta)^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply prior × likelihood to get the unnormalized posterior:**
$$
P(\theta \mid D) \propto P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)
$$

$$
P(\theta \mid D) \propto \theta^{3}(1 - \theta)^{2} \cdot \theta(1 - \theta)
$$

$$
P(\theta \mid D) \propto \theta^{3+1}\,(1 - \theta)^{2+1} = \theta^{4}(1 - \theta)^{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the kernel of a Beta distribution:**

The kernel $\theta^{\alpha' - 1}(1 - \theta)^{\beta' - 1}$ matches $\text{Beta}(\alpha', \beta')$, so:
$$
\theta \mid D \sim \text{Beta}(2 + 3,\, 2 + 2) = \text{Beta}(5, 4)
$$

> The general update rule: $\text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$ prior + $(k, n-k)$ successes/failures → $\text{Beta}(\alpha + k, \beta + n - k)$ posterior.

**Step 5 — Compute the MAP estimate:**

For a $\text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$ distribution, the MAP (and mode) is:
$$
\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha + \beta - 2} \quad (\text{for } \alpha, \beta > 1)
$$

$$
\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \frac{5 - 1}{5 + 4 - 2} = \frac{4}{7} \approx 0.571
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with MLE (no prior):**
$$
\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{k}{n} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.600
$$

The Beta(2,2) prior pulled the estimate **slightly toward 0.5** (the prior mean), illustrating the **regularizing effect** of Bayesian formulation.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Bayesian Inference Workflow

| Stage | Frequentist Equivalent | Bayesian Formulation | Key Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Belief before data | None | $P(\theta)$ — Prior | Distribution over parameters |
| Fit to data | Likelihood maximization | $P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)$ | Unnormalized posterior |
| Result of inference | Point estimate $\hat{\theta}$ | Posterior $P(\theta \mid D)$ | Full distribution |
| Point summary | MLE | MAP, Posterior Mean | Single value |
| Prediction for new $x^*$ | Plug-in $P(y^* \mid x^*, \hat{\theta})$ | $P(y^* \mid x^*, D) = \int P(y^* \mid x^*, \theta)\,P(\theta \mid D)\,d\theta$ | Distribution (with uncertainty) |
| Model selection | AIC, BIC | Bayes Factor, Marginal Likelihood | Comparison metric |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Bayesian Inference Cycle (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START Domain Knowledge"] --> B["Prior P of H"]
    B --> C["Observe Data D"]
    C --> D["Likelihood P of D given H"]
    D --> E["Bayes Theorem Computation"]
    E --> F["Posterior P of H given D"]
    F --> G["Decision or Prediction"]
    G --> H{"New Data Available?"}
    H -- Yes --> C
    H -- No --> I["STOP Final Belief"]
```

### 4.2 Decomposition of the Posterior (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Posterior P of H given D"] --> L["Likelihood P of D given H"]
    P --> PR["Prior P of H"]
    P --> E["Evidence P of D"]
    E --> E1["Sum over discrete H"]
    E --> E2["Integral over continuous H"]
    L --> EX["Data Fit Component"]
    PR --> EX2["Domain Belief Component"]
```

### 4.3 Frequentist vs Bayesian Paradigm Comparison (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FREQ ["Frequentist Paradigm"]
        F1["Data is Random"] --> F2["Parameters are Fixed Unknown Constants"]
        F2 --> F3["Use Maximum Likelihood Estimation"]
        F3 --> F4["Output: Point Estimate and Confidence Interval"]
    end
    subgraph BAYES ["Bayesian Paradigm"]
        B1["Data is Fixed Observed"] --> B2["Parameters are Random Variables"]
        B2 --> B3["Use Prior times Likelihood"]
        B3 --> B4["Output: Full Posterior Distribution"]
    end
    F4 --- B4
```

### 4.4 Architecture of a Complete Bayesian Machine Learning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Define Parameter Space Theta"] --> S2["Step 2: Choose Prior Distribution P of Theta"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Model Data via Likelihood P of D given Theta"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Collect Observed Data D"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Compute Posterior P of Theta given D"]
    S5 --> S6A["Point Estimate: MAP or Posterior Mean"]
    S5 --> S6B["Interval Estimate: Credible Interval"]
    S5 --> S6C["Predictive Distribution for New Data"]
    S6A --> S7["Final Inference Output"]
    S6B --> S7
    S6C --> S7
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State and explain Bayes' theorem for discrete hypothesis spaces. What is the role of the evidence term?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Bayes' theorem states that for a partition $\{H_1, H_2, \ldots, H_n\}$ of the sample space and observed data $D$:

$$
P(H_j \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid H_j)\,P(H_j)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(D \mid H_i)\,P(H_i)}
$$

Here, $P(H_j)$ is the **prior probability** of hypothesis $H_j$, $P(D \mid H_j)$ is the **likelihood** of the data given $H_j$, and $P(D)$ is the **evidence**, computed via the law of total probability. The evidence term acts as a **normalizing constant** that ensures the posterior probabilities across all hypotheses sum to 1, making them a valid probability distribution.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) and Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | MLE | MAP |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $\hat{\theta} = \arg\max P(D \mid \theta)$ | $\hat{\theta} = \arg\max P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)$ |
| Prior required? | No | Yes |
| Equivalent to | MAP with uniform prior | MLE with informative prior |
| Output | Single most likely parameter | Most likely parameter *given prior* |
| Overfitting risk | Higher | Lower (prior regularizes) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** *Derive Bayes' theorem from the definition of conditional probability. Clearly state the assumptions made. (7 Marks)*

**(b)** *A factory has two machines M₁ and M₂ producing bolts. M₁ produces 60% of total output with 2% defect rate; M₂ produces 40% with 3% defect rate. A randomly selected bolt is found to be defective. Find the probability it came from M₁. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

Starting from the definition of conditional probability:
$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

Similarly:
$$
P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)} \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

From (2):
$$
P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A)\,P(A)
$$

Substituting into (1):
$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A)\,P(A)}{P(B)}
$$

Replacing $A \to H$ and $B \to D$:
$$
\boxed{P(H \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(D)}}
$$

**Assumptions:**
1. $P(D) > 0$ (so that conditioning is defined).
2. $\{H_i\}$ forms a partition (mutually exclusive and exhaustive hypotheses).
3. Standard Kolmogorov axioms of probability apply.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify the given quantities:**
$$
P(M_1) = 0.60, \quad P(M_2) = 0.40
$$
$$
P(D \mid M_1) = 0.02, \quad P(D \mid M_2) = 0.03
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' theorem:**
$$
P(M_1 \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid M_1)\,P(M_1)}{P(D \mid M_1)\,P(M_1) + P(D \mid M_2)\,P(M_2)}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$
P(M_1 \mid D) = \frac{0.02 \times 0.60}{0.02 \times 0.60 + 0.03 \times 0.40}
$$

$$
P(M_1 \mid D) = \frac{0.012}{0.012 + 0.012} = \frac{0.012}{0.024} = 0.50
$$

**Answer:** $P(M_1 \mid D) = 0.50$ (50%).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** *Explain the components of Bayesian formulation: prior, likelihood, posterior, and evidence. Discuss how conjugate priors simplify Bayesian computation. (7 Marks)*

**(b)** *Consider a coin-tossing experiment where the prior belief about the bias $\theta$ follows a Beta(1, 1) distribution. After observing 7 heads and 3 tails, compute the posterior distribution and the MAP estimate of $\theta$. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

The Bayesian formulation comprises four key components:

1. **Prior $P(\theta)$:** Encodes our belief about the parameter $\theta$ *before* observing any data. It can be **informative** (e.g., Beta(2, 2)) or **non-informative/flat** (e.g., Beta(1, 1), which is the uniform distribution on $[0,1]$).

2. **Likelihood $P(D \mid \theta)$:** The probability of observing the data $D$ given a specific value of $\theta$. For i.i.d. data, it is the product of individual sample likelihoods.

3. **Posterior $P(\theta \mid D)$:** The *updated* belief about $\theta$ after observing data $D$. Computed via Bayes' theorem:
$$
P(\theta \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)}{P(D)}
$$

4. **Evidence $P(D)$:** The marginal probability of the data, obtained by integrating (or summing) the joint distribution over all parameter values. Serves as the normalizing constant.

**Conjugate Priors:**
A prior $P(\theta)$ is **conjugate** to a likelihood $P(D \mid \theta)$ if the resulting posterior belongs to the **same family** as the prior. Examples:

| Likelihood | Conjugate Prior | Posterior |
|---|---|---|
| Bernoulli | Beta | Beta |
| Gaussian (mean, known variance) | Gaussian | Gaussian |
| Multinomial | Dirichlet | Dirichlet |
| Poisson | Gamma | Gamma |

**Why conjugates matter:** They make the posterior analytically tractable (closed-form), avoid numerical integration, and allow recursive/sequential updates — all critical for scalable ML.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Specify the prior:**

Beta(1, 1) is the **uniform distribution** on $[0, 1]$:
$$
P(\theta) \propto \theta^{1-1}(1 - \theta)^{1-1} = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Specify the likelihood:**

For $k = 7$ heads, $n - k = 3$ tails in $n = 10$ tosses:
$$
P(D \mid \theta) = \binom{10}{7}\,\theta^{7}(1 - \theta)^{3} \propto \theta^{7}(1 - \theta)^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the unnormalized posterior:**
$$
P(\theta \mid D) \propto \theta^{7}(1 - \theta)^{3} \cdot 1 = \theta^{7}(1 - \theta)^{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the Beta kernel:**
$$
P(\theta \mid D) \propto \theta^{7}(1 - \theta)^{3} = \theta^{(1+7)-1}(1 - \theta)^{(1+3)-1}
$$

Therefore:
$$
\theta \mid D \sim \text{Beta}(1 + 7,\ 1 + 3) = \text{Beta}(8, 4)
$$

**Step 5 — MAP estimate:**

For Beta($\alpha, \beta$) with $\alpha, \beta > 1$:
$$
\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha + \beta - 2} = \frac{8 - 1}{8 + 4 - 2} = \frac{7}{10} = 0.70
$$

**Step 6 — Posterior mean (additional insight):**
$$
\mathbb{E}[\theta \mid D] = \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta} = \frac{8}{12} \approx 0.667
$$

> The MLE would give $7/10 = 0.7$, identical to MAP in this case because the Beta(1,1) prior is non-informative (uniform).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the evidence term:** Many students write $P(H \mid D) = P(D \mid H)\,P(H)$ without the denominator. KTU deducts **1 full mark** for this.
> 2. **Confusing prior and likelihood:** $P(H \mid D)$ is posterior, not $P(D \mid H)$. Reversing these is the most common conceptual error.
> 3. **Skipping the justification for conjugate choice:** When asked about conjugate priors, you must *state* the likelihood and *verify* the prior lies in the conjugate family. Don't just list the pair.
> 4. **Numerical computation errors:** In the medical test problem, students often forget to compute $P(T^+ \mid D^-) = 1 - \text{Specificity}$. This leads to wrong evidence.
> 5. **Wrong MAP formula for Beta:** Use $\frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha + \beta - 2}$ only when $\alpha, \beta > 1$. Otherwise, the mode is at the boundary.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Bayes' Theorem** is the cornerstone: $P(H \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(D)}$.
- ✅ Four pillars: **Prior, Likelihood, Posterior, Evidence**.
- ✅ The **evidence** $P(D)$ is the *normalizing constant*; in MAP computations it is often ignored (use $\propto$).
- ✅ **Bayesian = Prior × Likelihood → Posterior** (learning as belief updating).
- ✅ **Conjugate priors** keep the posterior in the same family (e.g., Beta–Bernoulli, Dirichlet–Multinomial, Gaussian–Gaussian).
- ✅ **MAP = MLE + Log-prior** in log-space: $\log P(\theta \mid D) = \log P(D \mid \theta) + \log P(\theta) - \log P(D)$.
- ✅ **Posterior Predictive Distribution** integrates over parameter uncertainty: $P(x^* \mid D) = \int P(x^* \mid \theta)\,P(\theta \mid D)\,d\theta$.
- ✅ **Base rate fallacy** dominates low-prevalence scenarios (medical tests, spam detection) — KTU tests this conceptually.
- ✅ **Sequential updating** is a key advantage: yesterday's posterior is today's prior.
- ✅ **Odds form (Bayes Factor)** is the foundation of Bayesian model comparison: $\text{Posterior Odds} = \text{Bayes Factor} \times \text{Prior Odds}$.
- ✅ For $\text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$ prior with $(k, n-k)$ Bernoulli data → Posterior is $\text{Beta}(\alpha + k, \beta + n - k)$.
- ✅ For $\text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$ with $\alpha, \beta > 1$: MAP = $\frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha + \beta - 2}$, Mean = $\frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta}$.
- ✅ **Bayesian formulation provides uncertainty estimates; frequentist only gives point estimates.** This is the KTU exam's most-asked conceptual contrast.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Least Square Estimation (LS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAIT404 - FOUNDATION OF MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Least Square Estimation (LSE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Least Square Estimation (LSE):** A statistical estimation technique used in linear regression that determines the optimal parameters (coefficients) of a linear model by **minimizing the sum of the squared residuals** (the squared differences between the observed and predicted values).

Mathematically, given a linear model:

$$y = X\beta + \epsilon$$

where $y \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ is the observation vector, $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ is the design matrix, $\beta \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times 1}$ is the unknown parameter vector, and $\epsilon \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ is the random error vector with $E(\epsilon) = 0$ and $Var(\epsilon) = \sigma^2 I$, the **Least Square Estimator** $\hat{\beta}$ is defined as the value that minimizes the residual sum of squares:

$$\hat{\beta} = \arg\min_{\beta} \; S(\beta) = \arg\min_{\beta} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - x_i^T \beta)^2 = \arg\min_{\beta} \; \|y - X\beta\|^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Highlight:** LSE forms the foundation of **Linear Regression**, which is a **CO1 (Apply)** level outcome under Module 1 of PBAIT404. The derivation of the **Normal Equation** is a high-frequency exam topic.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **archaeologist** trying to draw the best straight line through a scatter of ancient pottery shard coordinates on a 2D map. There is no single "true" line; instead, you want the line that **comes closest to all points collectively**. 

If a single point lies far from your chosen line, the squared penalty is *enormous* (because we square the distance). This forces the line to bend toward outliers, ensuring it does not ignore extreme observations. **Least Square Estimation is this idea in mathematical clothing** — find the line that distributes the total squared vertical distance between itself and all data points as small as possible.

### 1.3 Geometric Intuition

Geometrically, the vector $y$ (observations) lies in an $n$-dimensional space, but it is approximated by a vector $X\hat{\beta}$ constrained to lie in the **column space** of $X$ (denoted $\mathcal{C}(X)$). The LSE finds the point in $\mathcal{C}(X)$ that is **closest (in Euclidean distance)** to $y$. This closest point is the **orthogonal projection** of $y$ onto $\mathcal{C}(X)$.

### 1.4 Key Notations and Assumptions

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $n$ | Number of observations (samples) |
| $p$ | Number of parameters (features + 1) |
| $X$ | Design matrix of size $n \times p$ |
| $y$ | Response vector of size $n \times 1$ |
| $\beta$ | True parameter vector of size $p \times 1$ |
| $\hat{\beta}$ | Estimated parameter vector |
| $e = y - X\hat{\beta}$ | Residual vector |
| $\sigma^2$ | Variance of the error term |
| $RSS$ | Residual Sum of Squares |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Assumptions of LSE (Gauss-Markov Setup):**
> 1. **Linearity:** The relationship between $X$ and $y$ is linear in parameters.
> 2. **Independence:** Observations are independent of each other.
> 3. **Homoscedasticity:** Error variance is constant: $Var(\epsilon_i) = \sigma^2$.
> 4. **Non-stochastic X:** $X$ is fixed (non-random) and of full column rank: $rank(X) = p$.
> 5. **Zero Mean:** $E(\epsilon) = 0$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Objective Function

The **Residual Sum of Squares (RSS)** is the loss function we minimize:

$$RSS(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - x_i^T \beta)^2 = (y - X\beta)^T (y - X\beta)$$

Expanding the matrix form:

$$RSS(\beta) = y^T y - 2\beta^T X^T y + \beta^T X^T X \beta$$

### 2.2 The Normal Equation (Core Result)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Normal Equation:** Setting $\frac{\partial RSS}{\partial \beta} = 0$ gives the optimal solution:

$$X^T X \hat{\beta} = X^T y$$

Provided $X^T X$ is invertible (i.e., $X$ has full column rank), the **Least Square Estimator** is:

$$\boxed{\hat{\beta}_{LSE} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y}$$

The matrix $P = X(X^T X)^{-1} X^T$ is called the **Hat Matrix** (or Projection Matrix) because it "puts a hat" on $y$:

$$\hat{y} = X\hat{\beta} = X(X^T X)^{-1} X^T y = Py$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Use / Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Objective Function | $J(\beta) = \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Scalar cost to minimize |
| Matrix Form of $J$ | $J(\beta) = (y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta)$ | Compact vector form |
| Gradient | $\nabla_{\beta} J = -2X^T y + 2X^T X \beta$ | First derivative wrt $\beta$ |
| **Normal Equation** | $X^T X \hat{\beta} = X^T y$ | Stationary point condition |
| **LSE Solution** | $\hat{\beta} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ | Closed-form estimator |
| Hat Matrix | $H = X(X^T X)^{-1} X^T$ | Projects $y$ onto $\mathcal{C}(X)$ |
| Predicted Values | $\hat{y} = Hy$ | Fitted values |
| Residuals | $e = y - \hat{y} = (I - H)y$ | Orthogonal to $\mathcal{C}(X)$ |
| RSS at optimum | $RSS = y^T(I - H)y$ | Minimum achievable sum of squares |
| **Variance of LSE** | $Var(\hat{\beta}) = \sigma^2 (X^T X)^{-1}$ | Precision of estimates |
| Unbiasedness | $E(\hat{\beta}) = \beta$ | $X$ fixed, $E(\epsilon)=0$ |
| Estimator of $\sigma^2$ | $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{RSS}{n-p}$ | Unbiased estimator |
| Total Sum of Squares | $TSS = \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2$ | Reference for $R^2$ |
| $R^2$ Coefficient | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{RSS}{TSS}$ | Goodness of fit |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never use the pipe character `|` in markdown table cells. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX, and avoid `\|` clashes with table separators. The vertical bar in $R^2 = 1 - \frac{RSS}{TSS}$ is **mathematical division**, not a pipe.

### 2.4 Properties of the Least Square Estimator

1. **Unbiasedness:** Under the Gauss-Markov assumptions, $E(\hat{\beta}_{LSE}) = \beta$.
2. **Minimum Variance (Gauss-Markov Theorem):** Among all **linear unbiased estimators**, LSE has the **minimum variance** — i.e., it is BLUE (Best Linear Unbiased Estimator).
3. **Consistency:** As $n \to \infty$, $\hat{\beta} \xrightarrow{p} \beta$.
4. **Orthogonality of Residuals:** $X^T e = 0$. Residuals are orthogonal to every column of $X$, including the column of ones if an intercept is fitted.
5. **Sum of Residuals:** $\sum e_i = 0$ when an intercept is included in the model.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Predictive Maintenance:** Estimating remaining useful life of turbine blades from sensor data.
- **Signal Processing:** Channel estimation in wireless communication (MMSE-inspired).
- **Computer Vision:** Facial landmark regression.
- **Econometrics & Finance:** Risk modeling, demand forecasting.
- **Control Systems:** System identification (ARX models use LSE directly).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Normal Equation

We minimize the scalar cost function $J(\beta)$ written in matrix form. We use the matrix differentiation identities:

$$\frac{\partial (A\beta)}{\partial \beta} = A, \quad \frac{\partial (\beta^T A)}{\partial \beta} = A, \quad \frac{\partial (\beta^T A \beta)}{\partial \beta} = (A + A^T)\beta$$

For symmetric $A$ (as is $X^T X$), this simplifies to $2A\beta$.

**Step 1: Write the cost function in expanded matrix form.**

Starting from $J(\beta) = (y - X\beta)^T (y - X\beta)$, let $r = y - X\beta$, so $J = r^T r$.

$$J(\beta) = (y - X\beta)^T (y - X\beta)$$

**Step 2: Expand using $(A - B)^T = A^T - B^T$ and distributive property.**

$$J(\beta) = y^T y - y^T X\beta - (X\beta)^T y + (X\beta)^T (X\beta)$$

**Step 3: Simplify scalar products.** Since $y^T X \beta$ is a scalar, it equals its transpose $(X\beta)^T y$. So the two middle terms combine:

$$J(\beta) = y^T y - 2\beta^T X^T y + \beta^T X^T X \beta$$

**Step 4: Differentiate $J(\beta)$ with respect to $\beta$.**

$$\frac{\partial J(\beta)}{\partial \beta} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \beta}(y^T y) - 2 \frac{\partial}{\partial \beta}(\beta^T X^T y) + \frac{\partial}{\partial \beta}(\beta^T X^T X \beta)$$

$$\frac{\partial J(\beta)}{\partial \beta} = 0 - 2X^T y + 2X^T X \beta$$

**Step 5: Set the gradient to zero (first-order optimality condition).**

$$-2X^T y + 2X^T X \hat{\beta} = 0$$

$$X^T X \hat{\beta} = X^T y$$

This is the **Normal Equation**.

**Step 6: Solve for $\hat{\beta}$.** Assuming $(X^T X)^{-1}$ exists (i.e., $X$ has full column rank):

$$\hat{\beta} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

Consider the following dataset with one independent variable:

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ |
| :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 2 |
| 2 | 4 |
| 3 | 5 |
| 4 | 4 |
| 5 | 5 |

We fit the simple linear regression model: $y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \epsilon_i$.

**Step 1: Construct the design matrix $X$ and observation vector $y$.**

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \\ 1 & 5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 4 \\ 5 \\ 4 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Compute $X^T X$.**

$$X^T X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \\ 1 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$X^T X = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 15 \\ 15 & 55 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute $X^T y$.**

$$X^T y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 4 \\ 5 \\ 4 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2+4+5+4+5 \\ 2+8+15+16+25 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ 66 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compute the inverse of $X^T X$.**

$$\det(X^T X) = (5)(55) - (15)(15) = 275 - 225 = 50$$

$$(X^T X)^{-1} = \frac{1}{50} \begin{bmatrix} 55 & -15 \\ -15 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.1 & -0.3 \\ -0.3 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Compute $\hat{\beta}$.**

$$\hat{\beta} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y = \begin{bmatrix} 1.1 & -0.3 \\ -0.3 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ 66 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\hat{\beta} = \begin{bmatrix} (1.1)(20) + (-0.3)(66) \\ (-0.3)(20) + (0.1)(66) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 22 - 19.8 \\ -6 + 6.6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.2 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

So the fitted regression line is: $\hat{y} = 2.2 + 0.6x$.

**Step 6: Verification using residuals.**

$$\hat{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.2 + 0.6 \\ 2.2 + 1.2 \\ 2.2 + 1.8 \\ 2.2 + 2.4 \\ 2.2 + 3.0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.8 \\ 3.4 \\ 4.0 \\ 4.6 \\ 5.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$e = y - \hat{y} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.8 \\ 0.6 \\ 1.0 \\ -0.6 \\ -0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Check: $\sum e_i = -0.8 + 0.6 + 1.0 - 0.6 - 0.2 = 0$ ✓ (Intercept is fitted)

Check orthogonality: $X^T e = \begin{bmatrix} \sum e_i \\ \sum x_i e_i \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ (1)(-0.8)+(2)(0.6)+(3)(1.0)+(4)(-0.6)+(5)(-0.2) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$ ✓

**Step 7: Compute RSS and $\hat{\sigma}^2$.**

$$RSS = \sum e_i^2 = 0.64 + 0.36 + 1.00 + 0.36 + 0.04 = 2.40$$

$$TSS = \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2, \quad \bar{y} = 20/5 = 4$$

$$TSS = (2-4)^2 + (4-4)^2 + (5-4)^2 + (4-4)^2 + (5-4)^2 = 4 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 1 = 6$$

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{RSS}{TSS} = 1 - \frac{2.40}{6} = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60$$

$$\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{RSS}{n-p} = \frac{2.40}{5-2} = \frac{2.40}{3} = 0.80$$

**Step 8: Variance-covariance matrix of $\hat{\beta}$.**

$$Var(\hat{\beta}) = \hat{\sigma}^2 (X^T X)^{-1} = 0.80 \begin{bmatrix} 1.1 & -0.3 \\ -0.3 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.88 & -0.24 \\ -0.24 & 0.08 \end{bmatrix}$$

Standard errors: $SE(\hat{\beta}_0) = \sqrt{0.88} \approx 0.938$, $SE(\hat{\beta}_1) = \sqrt{0.08} \approx 0.283$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Geometric Projection Interpretation

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of the LSE process — from data ingestion to the projection of $y$ onto the column space of $X$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Data: y in R^n] --> B[Construct Design Matrix X in R^n x p]
    B --> C{Is rank X equal to p?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Compute X-transpose X]
    C -- No --> E[Use Pseudoinverse X-plus]
    D --> F[Compute X-transpose y]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Solve Normal Equation X-transpose X beta-hat equals X-transpose y]
    G --> H[beta-hat equals X-transpose-X-inverse times X-transpose times y]
    H --> I[Compute Fitted y-hat equals X beta-hat]
    I --> J[Compute Residuals e equals y minus y-hat]
    J --> K[Verify Orthogonality: X-transpose e equals 0]
    K --> L[Output: Parameter Estimates and Diagnostics]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow of LSE Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Data] --> B[Feature Engineering]
    B --> C[Form X and y]
    C --> D[Matrix Computations]
    D --> E[Normal Equation]
    E --> F[Closed Form Solution]
    F --> G[Validation and R-squared]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram of LSE-Based Linear Regression System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observations y1 to yn] --> C[Matrix X]
    B[Features x1 to xp] --> C
    C --> D[X-transpose X]
    D --> E[Inverse of X-transpose X]
```
