# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define the Sigmoid Function and State Its Key Properties.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **sigmoid (logistic) function** is defined as:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

**Key Properties:**

1. **Range:** $\sigma(z) \in (0, 1)$ — output is always strictly between 0 and 1, making it ideal for representing probabilities.
2. **Domain:** $z \in (-\infty, +\infty)$ — accepts any real-valued input.
3. **Monotonically Increasing:** As $z$ increases, $\sigma(z)$ strictly increases.
4. **Symmetry:** $\sigma(-z) = 1 - \sigma(z)$.
5. **S-shaped (Sigmoidal) Curve:** Steepest slope at $z = 0$ where $\sigma(0) = 0.5$.
6. **Derivative:** $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\!\left[1 - \sigma(z)\right]$ — a simple, self-referential form.
7. **Decision Point:** The threshold $z = 0$ corresponds to probability $0.5$, which serves as the natural decision boundary for binary classification.

---

### **Question 2: Why is the Log-Loss (Cross-Entropy) Used Instead of Mean Squared Error in Logistic Regression?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The Mean Squared Error (MSE) cost function is **not suitable** for logistic regression for the following reasons:

1. **Non-Convexity:** When MSE is applied with the sigmoid function, the resulting cost function $J(\mathbf{w})$ becomes **non-convex** with respect to the weights. This means gradient descent can get trapped in **local minima**, failing to find the global optimum.

2. **Vanishing Gradients:** For predictions where $\hat{y}$ is near 0 or 1 (saturated sigmoid), the gradient of MSE becomes extremely small, causing **slow convergence**.

3. **Probabilistic Interpretation:** MSE penalizes errors as squared distances, which lacks a clear probabilistic meaning. In contrast, **log-loss** is directly derived from **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** and has a rigorous probabilistic foundation.

4. **Convex Guarantee:** The **log-loss (binary cross-entropy)** function is **strictly convex** when applied to logistic regression. This guarantees that gradient descent will converge to the **unique global minimum**.

5. **Penalization Behavior:** Log-loss heavily penalizes confident wrong predictions (e.g., predicting $\hat{y} = 0.01$ when $y = 1$ produces a very large loss), which is desirable for classification tasks.

**Conclusion:** Log-loss is mathematically principled (derived from MLE), computationally stable (convex), and behaviorally appropriate (heavy penalty for confident errors), making it the correct cost function for logistic regression.

---

## 📗 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Logistic Regression — From Theory to Implementation** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Derive the sigmoid function from the log-odds assumption. Show all algebraic steps clearly.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the probability notation.**

Let $p = P(Y = 1 \mid X = \mathbf{x})$ represent the probability that the output belongs to class 1 given the input features $\mathbf{x}$.

**Step 2 — Define the odds ratio.**

The odds in favor of class 1 are defined as the ratio of probability of success to probability of failure:

$$\text{odds} = \frac{p}{1 - p}$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm to get the log-odds (logit).**

$$\text{logit}(p) = \ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1 - p}\right)$$

**Step 4 — State the logit assumption.**

We assume that the log-odds is a linear function of the input features:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1 - p}\right) = w_0 + w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + \cdots + w_n x_n = z$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate both sides.**

$$\frac{p}{1 - p} = e^{z}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $p$.**

$$p = e^{z} (1 - p)$$

$$p = e^{z} - p \cdot e^{z}$$

$$p + p \cdot e^{z} = e^{z}$$

$$p(1 + e^{z}) = e^{z}$$

**Step 7 — Isolate $p$ and normalize.**

$$p = \frac{e^{z}}{1 + e^{z}}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $e^{z}$:

$$p = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} = \sigma(z)$$

**Step 8 — Conclude with the final form.**

$$\boxed{\; P(Y = 1 \mid X = \mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}}} \;}$$

This proves that the sigmoid function is the natural consequence of assuming a linear relationship between the log-odds and the input features.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Derive the gradient of the log-loss cost function with respect to weight $w_j$ using the chain rule. Show every cancellation step.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the cost function for a single training example.**

$$J^{(i)} = -\left[ y^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Identify the dependency chain.**

The cost depends on $\hat{y}^{(i)}$, which depends on $z^{(i)}$, which depends on $w_j$. By the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial J^{(i)}}{\partial w_j} = \frac{\partial J^{(i)}}{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(i)}}{\partial w_j}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\frac{\partial J^{(i)}}{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}$.**

Differentiating $J^{(i)}$ with respect to $\hat{y}^{(i)}$:

$$\frac{\partial J^{(i)}}{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}} = -\left[ \frac{y^{(i)}}{\hat{y}^{(i)}} - \frac{1 - y^{(i)}}{1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}} \right]$$

Combining over a common denominator:

$$= -\left[ \frac{y^{(i)}(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) - (1 - y^{(i)})\hat{y}^{(i)}}{\hat{y}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})} \right]$$

$$= -\left[ \frac{y^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\hat{y}^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)} + y^{(i)}\hat{y}^{(i)}}{\hat{y}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})} \right]$$

$$= -\left[ \frac{y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\hat{y}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})} \right] = \frac{\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}}{\hat{y}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\frac{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}}$.**

Since $\hat{y}^{(i)} = \sigma(z^{(i)}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z^{(i)}}}$, the derivative is the well-known sigmoid derivative:

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} = \sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\!\left[1 - \sigma(z)\right] = \hat{y}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\frac{\partial z^{(i)}}{\partial w_j}$.**

Since $z^{(i)} = w_0 + w_1 x_1^{(i)} + \cdots + w_n x_n^{(i)}$:

$$\frac{\partial z^{(i)}}{\partial w_j} = x_j^{(i)}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply all three terms together.**

$$\frac{\partial J^{(i)}}{\partial w_j} = \frac{\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}}{\hat{y}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})} \cdot \hat{y}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$$

**Step 7 — Cancel the $\hat{y}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})$ terms.**

$$\frac{\partial J^{(i)}}{\partial w_j} = (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$$

**Step 8 — Average over all $m$ training examples to get the full gradient.**

$$\boxed{\; \frac{\partial J(\mathbf{w})}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)} \;}$$

**Step 9 — State the gradient descent update rule.**

$$w_j := w_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

where $\alpha$ is the learning rate controlling the step size of each update.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Logistic Regression — Cost Function, MLE, and Numerical Computation** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Derive the log-loss (cross-entropy) cost function for logistic regression using the principle of Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE). Show all steps.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the likelihood of a single observation.**

For a single training example $(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ with $y^{(i)} \in \{0, 1\}$ and predicted probability $\hat{y}^{(i)} = P(Y = 1 \mid x^{(i)}; \mathbf{w})$, the probability of observing the correct label is:

$$P(y^{(i)} \mid x^{(i)}; \mathbf{w}) = \begin{cases} \hat{y}^{(i)} & \text{if } y^{(i)} = 1 \\ 1 - \hat{y}^{(i)} & \text{if } y^{(i)} = 0 \end{cases}$$

This can be compactly written as:

$$P(y^{(i)} \mid x^{(i)}; \mathbf{w}) = (\hat{y}^{(i)})^{y^{(i)}} \cdot (1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})^{1 - y^{(i)}}$$

**Step 2 — Form the total likelihood for $m$ i.i.d. samples.**

Assuming all training examples are **independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.)**, the total likelihood is the product of individual likelihoods:

$$L(\mathbf{w}) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} (\hat{y}^{(i)})^{y^{(i)}} \cdot (1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})^{1 - y^{(i)}}$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm to obtain the log-likelihood.**

The logarithm converts the product into a sum, which is easier to differentiate:

$$\ell(\mathbf{w}) = \log L(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Convert to a minimization problem by negating.**

MLE seeks to **maximize** the log-likelihood. For gradient **descent** (which **minimizes**), we negate the objective:

$$-\ell(\mathbf{w}) = -\sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]$$

**Step 5 — Scale by $\frac{1}{m}$ to obtain the average cost per example.**

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]$$

**Step 6 — State the final cost function.**

$$\boxed{\; J(\mathbf{w}) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right] \;}$$

This is the **Binary Cross-Entropy Loss** (also called **Log-Loss**), derived rigorously from Maximum Likelihood Estimation.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Given the training data below, compute one full iteration of gradient descent for logistic regression with $\alpha = 0.1$, $w_0 = 0$, $w_1 = 0$, $w_2 = 0$. Show all intermediate values.

| Example $i$ | $x_1^{(i)}$ | $x_2^{(i)}$ | $y^{(i)}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 1 | 1 |
| 2 | 1 | 3 | 0 |
| 3 | 3 | 2 | 1 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $z^{(i)} = w_0 + w_1 x_1^{(i)} + w_2 x_2^{(i)}$ for each example.**

With all initial weights equal to 0, every $z^{(i)} = 0$:

$$z^{(1)} = 0 + 0(2) + 0(1) = 0$$

$$z^{(2)} = 0 + 0(1) + 0(3) = 0$$

$$z^{(3)} = 0 + 0(3) + 0(2) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\hat{y}^{(i)} = \sigma(z^{(i)}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z^{(i)}}}$.**

Since $z^{(i)} = 0$ for all examples:

$$\hat{y}^{(1)} = \hat{y}^{(2)} = \hat{y}^{(3)} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{0}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Compute prediction errors $(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})$:**

$$e^{(1)} = 0.5 - 1 = -0.5$$

$$e^{(2)} = 0.5 - 0 = +0.5$$

$$e^{(3)} = 0.5 - 1 = -0.5$$

**Step 4 — Compute the gradient for $w_0$ (with $x_0^{(i)} = 1$ for all $i$):**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_0} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(-0.5)(1) + (0.5)(1) + (-0.5)(1)\right] = \frac{-0.5}{3} = -0.1667$$

**Step 5 — Compute the gradient for $w_1$:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_1} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(-0.5)(2) + (0.5)(1) + (-0.5)(3)\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{3}\left[-1.0 + 0.5 - 1.5\right] = \frac{-2.0}{3} = -0.6667$$

**Step 6 — Compute the gradient for $w_2$:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_2} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(-0.5)(1) + (0.5)(3) + (-0.5)(2)\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{3}\left[-0.5 + 1.5 - 1.0\right] = \frac{0.0}{3} = 0.0000$$

**Step 7 — Apply the update rule $w_j := w_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j}$:**

$$w_0^{\text{new}} = 0 - 0.1 \times (-0.1667) = +0.0167$$

$$w_1^{\text{new}} = 0 - 0.1 \times (-0.6667) = +0.0667$$

$$w_2^{\text{new}} = 0 - 0.1 \times (0.0000) = 0.0000$$

**Step 8 — Present the final updated weights:**

$$\boxed{\; w_0^{\text{new}} = 0.0167, \quad w_1^{\text{new}} = 0.0667, \quad w_2^{\text{new}} = 0.0000 \;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Starting from all-zero weights, the first iteration yields small but nonzero updates. With $\alpha = 0.1$, the model takes a conservative first step. In practice, gradient descent would continue for hundreds or thousands of iterations until convergence.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
>
> **1. Confusing Regression with Classification:** Students often state that logistic regression is used for *regression*. It is a **classification** algorithm. The word "regression" refers to its mathematical lineage (modeling log-odds as a linear function), **not** its task. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
>
> **2. Forgetting the Negative Sign in Log-Loss:** When deriving the cost function from MLE, forgetting to negate the log-likelihood (to convert maximization into minimization for gradient descent) is a very common error. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
>
> **3. Not Showing the Chain Rule Cancellation:** In the gradient derivation, the $\hat{y}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})$ cancellation is the **key elegant step**. Skipping it or not explicitly writing the cancellation will cost full marks on the derivation sub-part. (Lose 2–3 marks.)
>
> **4. Using MSE with Logistic Regression:** Stating or implying that MSE can be used with logistic regression is a **critical conceptual error** because it produces a non-convex cost surface. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
>
> **5. Threshold Confusion:** Students sometimes confuse the **decision boundary equation** ($z = 0$, i.e., $P = 0.5$) with the **classification rule** ($P \geq 0.5 \Rightarrow$ Class 1). These are related but distinct concepts; both should be stated. (Lose 1 mark.)
>
> **6. Arithmetic Errors in Numerical Problems:** In the numerical gradient descent problem, a single arithmetic mistake propagates through all subsequent steps. Always **double-check your sigmoid outputs** using a calculator — $e^{-z}$ and $e^{z}$ are easy to confuse. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
>
> **7. Skipping the Sigmoid Symmetry/Derivative:** Examiners may award bonus marks for mentioning $\sigma(-z) = 1 - \sigma(z)$ and $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)[1 - \sigma(z)]$. These are quick marks. (Gain 0.5–1 mark.)

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Logistic Regression**

- ✅ Logistic regression is a **classification algorithm** (not regression), despite its name.
- ✅ The **sigmoid function** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ maps any real number to $(0, 1)$, making it ideal for probability estimation.
- ✅ The sigmoid is derived from the assumption that **log-odds are linear** in the features: $\ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}$.
- ✅ The **logit function** $\text{logit}(p) = \ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)$ is the **inverse** of the sigmoid.
- ✅ The **cost function** is **Binary Cross-Entropy (Log-Loss)**: $J(\mathbf{w}) = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}[y^{(i)}\log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)})\log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})]$.
- ✅ Log-loss is derived from **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** — the negative sign converts maximization to minimization.
- ✅ The **gradient** of the cost function is $\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$ — derived via the chain rule with a beautiful cancellation.
- ✅ The **weight update rule** is $w_j := w_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$.
- ✅ The **decision boundary** is defined by $z = 0$ (i.e., $P(Y=1) = 0.5$), which is a hyperplane in feature space.
- ✅ The **sigmoid derivative** is $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\!\left[1 - \sigma(z)\right]$ — a compact, self-referential form.
- ✅ The **sigmoid symmetry** property $\sigma(-z) = 1 - \sigma(z)$ is useful for simplifying computations.
- ✅ **MSE is not used** in logistic regression because it creates a non-convex cost surface; log-loss guarantees convexity.
- ✅ Logistic regression is the **conceptual building block** of neural networks — a single sigmoid neuron *is* logistic regression.
- ✅ Real-world applications include **medical diagnosis**, **credit scoring**, **spam detection**, **customer churn prediction**, and **sentiment analysis**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Illustrative examples of designing regression models. Decision Trees: Working Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAIT404 - FOUNDATION OF MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Illustrative examples of designing regression models. Decision Trees: Working Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Illustrative Examples of Designing Regression Models

### Formal Definition
**Regression Model Design** is the systematic, iterative process of selecting an appropriate mathematical functional form, identifying relevant independent (predictor) variables, estimating the model parameters, validating the underlying statistical assumptions, and assessing the goodness-of-fit so that the constructed model yields reliable, unbiased, and generalizable predictions of the dependent (response) variable. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBAIT404 - Module 2)**, this process is examined through worked-out numerical illustrations that demonstrate the complete pipeline from raw data to a deployable model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2)**
> Regression design is *not* merely fitting a line. The KTU board expects students to demonstrate:
> (i) Variable selection and model formulation,
> (ii) Parameter estimation via **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)**,
> (iii) Diagnostic checks (R², Adjusted R², F-test, t-test, residual plots),
> (iv) Handling of multicollinearity, and
> (v) Justification of the final model using information criteria.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Engineering" View
Imagine you are a chef trying to recreate a dish you tasted once. You suspect the flavor depends on a few ingredients (salt, sugar, oil, spice). You do not know the exact quantities. Your task is to **experiment, taste, and adjust** until the recipe matches. The dependent variable is the "taste score" (response), the ingredients are the **independent variables (features)**, and the amounts you tweak are the **regression coefficients (β)**. Designing a regression model is the same — you iteratively choose which variables matter (feature selection), quantify their influence (estimation), and verify the recipe works on new taste tests (validation).

### Common Regression Design Scenarios
| Design Type | When to Use | KTU Weightage Hint |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Linear Regression (SLR)** | One predictor, linear trend | Frequently tested (2–7 marks) |
| **Multiple Linear Regression (MLR)** | Multiple predictors, no interaction | High-weightage (7–14 marks) |
| **Polynomial Regression** | Curvilinear relationship (degree ≥ 2) | Moderate (3–7 marks) |
| **Ridge / Lasso (Regularized)** | Multicollinearity or many features | Concept-only (3–5 marks) |

---

## 1.2 Decision Trees — Working Principle

### Formal Definition
A **Decision Tree** is a non-parametric, supervised learning algorithm used for both classification and regression that recursively partitions the feature space into homogeneous sub-regions by selecting, at each internal node, the feature and split-point that maximizes the **purity gain** of the resulting child nodes. The terminal nodes (leaves) carry the predicted class label (classification) or the mean response value (regression).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Definition (Module 2):**
> "A decision tree is a tree-structured classifier in which internal nodes represent tests on attributes, branches denote outcomes of those tests, and leaf nodes represent class labels or continuous values." — *KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Descriptor for PBAIT404*.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Twenty Questions" Game
Think of decision trees as playing the classic guessing game **"20 Questions."** You have a secret object in mind. The opponent asks yes/no questions (e.g., *"Is it alive?"*, *"Does it have four legs?"*, *"Is it a mammal?"*). Each question **splits the remaining possibilities into smaller, more uniform groups**. By the time the opponent has narrowed the candidates down to one, the leaf node is reached. A decision tree does exactly this with data — it asks the *most informative question first* to slice the dataset into pure subsets as quickly as possible.

### The Three Foundational Algorithms
| Algorithm | Splitting Criterion | Tree Type | Year |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ID3** | Information Gain (Entropy-based) | Multi-way, classification | 1986 |
| **C4.5** | Gain Ratio | Multi-way, classification | 1993 |
| **CART** | Gini Index (or MSE for regression) | Binary, classification/regression | 1984 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Steps in Designing a Regression Model

### Step 1 — Problem Formulation
Define the dependent variable $Y$ and the candidate independent variables $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_p$. State the assumed data-generating process (DGP) and the algebraic form of the relationship.

### Step 2 — Data Collection & Pre-processing
Collect $n$ observations, handle missing values, scale/transform variables if required, and split into training (typically **70%**) and test (typically **30%**) subsets. The holdout set is used to estimate the **out-of-sample generalization error**.

### Step 3 — Model Specification
For multiple linear regression, the general form is:

$$Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1i} + \beta_2 X_{2i} + \cdots + \beta_p X_{pi} + \varepsilon_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

The error term $\varepsilon_i$ is assumed to satisfy the **Gauss-Markov assumptions**:
1. $E[\varepsilon_i] = 0$ (zero mean)
2. $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = \sigma^2$ (homoscedasticity)
3. $\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_i, \varepsilon_j) = 0$ for $i \neq j$ (no autocorrelation)
4. $\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ (normality — required only for inference)

### Step 4 — Parameter Estimation (OLS)
Minimize the **Residual Sum of Squares (RSS)**:

$$\text{RSS}(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( Y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j X_{ji} \right)^2$$

The closed-form OLS estimator is:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} \mathbf{Y}$$

provided the matrix $X^{T} X$ is non-singular (i.e., no perfect multicollinearity).

### Step 5 — Goodness-of-Fit & Significance Tests
- **Coefficient of Determination** $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{\text{RSS}}{\text{TSS}}$
- **Adjusted** $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{(1-R^2)(n-1)}{n-p-1}$
- **F-statistic** for overall significance: $F = \dfrac{(R^2 / p)}{((1-R^2) / (n-p-1))}$
- **t-test** for individual coefficient significance: $t_j = \dfrac{\hat{\beta}_j}{\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_j)}$

### Step 6 — Diagnostic Checking
Inspect residual plots, compute **Variance Inflation Factor (VIF)** for multicollinearity:

$$\text{VIF}_j = \frac{1}{1 - R_j^2}$$

where $R_j^2$ is the $R^2$ obtained by regressing $X_j$ on all other predictors. A VIF > **10** signals serious multicollinearity.

### Step 7 — Model Refinement
Apply variable-selection strategies:
- **Forward Selection** (add variables one at a time),
- **Backward Elimination** (remove insignificant variables),
- **Stepwise Regression** (combination of both),
- Or use information criteria: **AIC** = $2k - 2\ln(\hat{L})$ and **BIC** = $k\ln(n) - 2\ln(\hat{L})$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Regression Design

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | OLS Estimator | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} \mathbf{Y}$ | Vector of length $p+1$ |
| 2 | RSS | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2$ | Lower is better |
| 3 | TSS | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2$ | Total variance of Y |
| 4 | $R^2$ | $1 - \text{RSS}/\text{TSS}$ | Range: $[0, 1]$ |
| 5 | Adjusted $R^2$ | $1 - \frac{(1-R^2)(n-1)}{n-p-1}$ | Penalizes extra predictors |
| 6 | F-statistic | $\frac{(\text{TSS}-\text{RSS})/p}{\text{RSS}/(n-p-1)}$ | df: $(p, n-p-1)$ |
| 7 | t-statistic | $\hat{\beta}_j / \text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_j)$ | df: $n-p-1$ |
| 8 | VIF | $1/(1 - R_j^2)$ | Threshold: **VIF > 10** |
| 9 | AIC | $n \ln(\text{RSS}/n) + 2p$ | Lower is better |
| 10 | BIC | $n \ln(\text{RSS}/n) + p \ln n$ | Stronger penalty than AIC |

---

## 2.3 Decision Tree — Theoretical Working Principle

### The Greedy, Top-Down, Recursive Partitioning Paradigm
1. **Start** at the root node containing the entire training set.
2. **Evaluate** every possible feature $X_j$ and every candidate split-point $s$ that divides the node into left ($X_j \le s$) and right ($X_j > s$) subsets.
3. **Select** the feature–split combination $(j^\ast, s^\ast)$ that maximizes the **purity gain** (impurity reduction).
4. **Split** the node into two (CART) or more (ID3) child nodes.
5. **Recurse** on each child until a stopping criterion is met (max depth, min samples per leaf, pure node).
6. **Prune** the fully grown tree to mitigate overfitting (cost-complexity pruning, reduced error pruning).

### Splitting Criteria — The Three Pillars

#### (a) Entropy (Information-Theoretic Impurity)
For a node with class proportions $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_K$ (where $\sum p_k = 1$):

$$H(S) = - \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k \log_2 p_k$$

Entropy is **0** when the node is pure (one class only) and reaches its **maximum** of $\log_2 K$ when classes are uniformly distributed.

#### (b) Information Gain
The reduction in entropy achieved by splitting dataset $S$ on feature $A$:

$$\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

where $S_v$ is the subset of $S$ for which $A = v$, and $\vert S_v \vert$ denotes the cardinality. **ID3** picks the attribute with the highest information gain.

#### (c) Gain Ratio (C4.5's Fix for the Multi-Branch Bias)
$$\text{GainRatio}(S, A) = \frac{\text{IG}(S, A)}{\text{SplitInfo}(S, A)}$$

where the intrinsic split information is:

$$\text{SplitInfo}(S, A) = - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$$

This normalizes IG by the breadth of the split, penalizing features with many distinct values (e.g., a unique ID column).

#### (d) Gini Index (CART's Impurity Measure)
For binary classification with class probability $p$:

$$Gini(p) = 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k^2 = 2p(1-p)$$

The Gini gain of a split is the weighted reduction: $\Delta G = G(\text{parent}) - \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} G(S_v)$.

### KTU Formula Sheet — Decision Tree Splitting

| # | Quantity | Formula | Range / Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Entropy | $H(S) = -\sum_k p_k \log_2 p_k$ | $0 \le H \le \log_2 K$ |
| 2 | Information Gain | $H(S) - \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | $\ge 0$; used by ID3 |
| 3 | Split Information | $-\sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | Penalizes multi-way splits |
| 4 | Gain Ratio | $\text{IG} / \text{SplitInfo}$ | Used by C4.5 |
| 5 | Gini Index | $1 - \sum_k p_k^2$ | $0 \le G \le 1 - 1/K$ |
| 6 | Gini Gain | $G(\text{parent}) - \sum_v w_v G(S_v)$ | Used by CART |
| 7 | Classification Error | $1 - \max_k p_k$ | $0 \le E \le 1 - 1/K$ |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Regression** is the workhorse of predictive analytics in civil engineering (concrete strength prediction), electrical engineering (load forecasting), and biomedical engineering (drug dose–response).
- **Decision Trees** are used in medical diagnosis (chennai's ICUs), credit-risk scoring in banking, fault classification in manufacturing, and rule extraction from opaque deep models via **surrogate models**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Designing a Simple Linear Regression Model

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A researcher collects the following data on the number of hours a student studies (X) and the score obtained (Y):

| Student | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| X (hours) | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
| Y (score) | 50 | 60 | 65 | 80 | 90 |

Fit a simple linear regression model $\hat{Y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$ using the OLS method. Compute $R^2$ and test the significance of $\beta_1$ at $\alpha = 0.05$.

### Step 1 — Compute the Required Sums

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 5 \\
\sum X_i &= 2 + 4 + 6 + 8 + 10 = 30 \\
\sum Y_i &= 50 + 60 + 65 + 80 + 90 = 345 \\
\bar{X} &= 30 / 5 = 6 \\
\bar{Y} &= 345 / 5 = 69
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum X_i^2 &= 4 + 16 + 36 + 64 + 100 = 220 \\
\sum Y_i^2 &= 2500 + 3600 + 4225 + 6400 + 8100 = 24825 \\
\sum X_i Y_i &= (2)(50) + (4)(60) + (6)(65) + (8)(80) + (10)(90) \\
&= 100 + 240 + 390 + 640 + 900 = 2270
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute $S_{XX}$ and $S_{XY}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{XX} &= \sum X_i^2 - \frac{(\sum X_i)^2}{n} = 220 - \frac{30^2}{5} = 220 - 180 = 40 \\
S_{YY} &= \sum Y_i^2 - \frac{(\sum Y_i)^2}{n} = 24825 - \frac{345^2}{5} = 24825 - 23805 = 1020 \\
S_{XY} &= \sum X_i Y_i - \frac{(\sum X_i)(\sum Y_i)}{n} = 2270 - \frac{(30)(345)}{5} = 2270 - 2070 = 200
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the OLS Estimates

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\beta}_1 &= \frac{S_{XY}}{S_{XX}} = \frac{200}{40} = 5 \\
\hat{\beta}_0 &= \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{X} = 69 - (5)(6) = 69 - 30 = 39
\end{aligned}
$$

The fitted regression line is:

$$\hat{Y} = 39 + 5X$$

**Interpretation:** Every additional study hour is associated with a **5-mark** increase in score; a student who does not study at all is predicted to score **39 marks** (the intercept).

### Step 4 — Compute $R^2$

The regression sum of squares is $\text{SSR} = \hat{\beta}_1 \cdot S_{XY} = 5 \times 200 = 1000$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R^2 &= \frac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}} = \frac{1000}{1020} \approx 0.9804
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately **98.04%** of the variation in scores is explained by hours studied.

### Step 5 — t-Test for $\beta_1$

First, the residual sum of squares:

$$
\text{RSS} = \text{SST} - \text{SSR} = 1020 - 1000 = 20
$$

The estimated error variance:

$$
\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{\text{RSS}}{n - 2} = \frac{20}{3} \approx 6.667
$$

Standard error of $\hat{\beta}_1$:

$$
\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_1) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat{\sigma}^2}{S_{XX}}} = \sqrt{\frac{6.667}{40}} = \sqrt{0.1667} \approx 0.4082
$$

t-statistic:

$$
t = \frac{\hat{\beta}_1}{\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_1)} = \frac{5}{0.4082} \approx 12.247
$$

Degrees of freedom: $n - 2 = 3$. The critical value $t_{0.025, 3} = 3.182$. Since $\vert t \vert = 12.247 \gg 3.182$, we **reject $H_0$** and conclude that study hours are a statistically significant predictor of score.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Designing a Multiple Linear Regression (3 Variables)

**Problem:** Predict house price $Y$ (in lakhs) from area $X_1$ (sq.ft), bedrooms $X_2$, and age $X_3$ (years). Given the following summary statistics from $n = 10$ observations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_1 = 1500,\ \bar{X}_2 = 3,\ \bar{X}_3 = 10,\ \bar{Y} = 60 \\
S_{X_1 X_1} = 100000,\ S_{X_2 X_2} = 5,\ S_{X_3 X_3} = 50 \\
S_{X_1 X_2} = 200,\ S_{X_1 X_3} = -300,\ S_{X_2 X_3} = -2 \\
S_{X_1 Y} = 4000,\ S_{X_2 Y} = 25,\ S_{X_3 Y} = -40 \\
\text{RSS}_{\text{full}} = 60
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Build the Sums-of-Squares-and-Cross-Products Matrix (X-Deviation Form)

$$
X^{T}X_{\text{(deviation)}} =
\begin{bmatrix}
100000 & 200 & -300 \\
200 & 5 & -2 \\
-300 & -2 & 50
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Build the Cross-Products Vector

$$
X^{T}\mathbf{Y}_{\text{(deviation)}} =
\begin{bmatrix}
4000 \\ 25 \\ -40
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve the Normal Equations
We need $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (X^{T}X)^{-1} X^{T}\mathbf{Y}$. In a KTU exam, the student is typically given the *inverted* matrix or the answer for the coefficients. Using the **cofactor expansion** (omitted for brevity, expected to be performed on calculator), let us assume the inverted matrix yields:

$$
(X^{T}X)^{-1} \approx
\begin{bmatrix}
1.0 \times 10^{-5} & -4.0 \times 10^{-4} & 6.0 \times 10^{-5} \\
-4.0 \times 10^{-4} & 0.25 & 0.02 \\
6.0 \times 10^{-5} & 0.02 & 0.025
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Multiplying out:

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} =
\begin{bmatrix}
1.0 \times 10^{-5} & -4.0 \times 10^{-4} & 6.0 \times 10^{-5} \\
-4.0 \times 10^{-4} & 0.25 & 0.02 \\
6.0 \times 10^{-5} & 0.02 & 0.025
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
4000 \\ 25 \\ -40
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\hat{\beta}_1 = (1.0 \times 10^{-5})(4000) + (-4.0 \times 10^{-4})(25) + (6.0 \times 10^{-5})(-40) = 0.04 - 0.01 - 0.0024 = 0.0276
$$

$$
\hat{\beta}_2 = (-4.0 \times 10^{-4})(4000) + (0.25)(25) + (0.02)(-40) = -1.6 + 6.25 - 0.8 = 3.85
$$

$$
\hat{\beta}_3 = (6.0 \times 10^{-5})(4000) + (0.02)(25) + (0.025)(-40) = 0.24 + 0.5 - 1.0 = -0.26
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Intercept

$$
\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{X}_1 - \hat{\beta}_2 \bar{X}_2 - \hat{\beta}_3 \bar{X}_3 = 60 - (0.0276)(1500) - (3.85)(3) - (-0.26)(10)
$$

$$
\hat{\beta}_0 = 60 - 41.4 - 11.55 + 2.6 = 9.65
$$

### Step 5 — Final Model and Goodness-of-Fit

$$
\boxed{\hat{Y} = 9.65 + 0.0276 X_1 + 3.85 X_2 - 0.26 X_3}
$$

$R^2$ computation requires SST. Assuming $\text{SST} = 1000$:

$$
R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{RSS}}{\text{SST}} = 1 - \frac{60}{1000} = 0.94
$$

Adjusted $R^2$ with $n=10, p=3$:

$$
R^2_{\text{adj}} = 1 - \frac{(1 - 0.94)(10 - 1)}{10 - 3 - 1} = 1 - \frac{(0.06)(9)}{6} = 1 - 0.09 = 0.91
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Multicollinearity Check (VIF):** The high cross-product $S_{X_1 X_2} = 200$ relative to $S_{X_2 X_2} = 5$ suggests possible multicollinearity between area and bedrooms. Students should compute VIF and recommend dropping $X_2$ if VIF > 10.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Building a Decision Tree (ID3) on the "Play Tennis" Dataset

**Dataset (14 instances, 4 features, binary class {Yes, No}):**

| Day | Outlook | Temp | Humidity | Wind | Play |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sunny | Hot | High | Weak | No |
| 2 | Sunny | Hot | High | Strong | No |
| 3 | Overcast | Hot | High | Weak | Yes |
| 4 | Rain | Mild | High | Weak | Yes |
| 5 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 6 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Strong | No |
| 7 | Overcast | Cool | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| 8 | Sunny | Mild | High | Weak | No |
| 9 | Sunny | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 10 | Rain | Mild | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 11 | Sunny | Mild | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| 12 | Overcast | Mild | High | Strong | Yes |
| 13 | Overcast | Hot | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 14 | Rain | Mild | High | Strong | No |

### Step 1 — Compute the Entropy of the Root Node

Number of "Yes" = 9, "No" = 5, total = 14.

$$
H(S) = -\frac{9}{14}\log_2\frac{9}{14} - \frac{5}{14}\log_2\frac{5}{14}
$$

Computing each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{9}{14}\log_2\frac{9}{14} &= -0.6429 \times (-0.6374) \approx 0.4098 \\
-\frac{5}{14}\log_2\frac{5}{14} &= -0.3571 \times (-1.4854) \approx 0.5305
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
H(S) \approx 0.4098 + 0.5305 = 0.9403 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Information Gain for Each Feature

#### (a) Information Gain for "Outlook"
Outlook has three values: Sunny (5 instances: 2 Yes, 3 No), Overcast (4: 4 Yes, 0 No), Rain (5: 3 Yes, 2 No).

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(\text{Sunny}) &= -\frac{2}{5}\log_2\frac{2}{5} - \frac{3}{5}\log_2\frac{3}{5} = 0.9710 \\
H(\text{Overcast}) &= -\frac{4}{4}\log_2\frac{4}{4} = 0 \\
H(\text{Rain}) &= -\frac{3}{5}\log_2\frac{3}{5} - \frac{2}{5}\log_2\frac{2}{5} = 0.9710
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
H(S \mid \text{Outlook}) = \frac{5}{14}(0.9710) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.9710) = 0.6936
$$

$$
\text{IG}(\text{Outlook}) = H(S) - H(S \mid \text{Outlook}) = 0.9403 - 0.6936 = \mathbf{0.2467}
$$

#### (b) Information Gain for "Humidity"
Humidity has High (7: 3 Yes, 4 No) and Normal (7: 6 Yes, 1 No).

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(\text{High}) &= -\frac{3}{7}\log_2\frac{3}{7} - \frac{4}{7}\log_2\frac{4}{7} = 0.9852 \\
H(\text{Normal}) &= -\frac{6}{7}\log_2\frac{6}{7} - \frac{1}{7}\log_2\frac{1}{7} = 0.5917
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
H(S \mid \text{Humidity}) = \frac{7}{14}(0.9852) + \frac{7}{14}(0.5917) = 0.7885
$$

$$
\text{IG}(\text{Humidity}) = 0.9403 - 0.7885 = \mathbf{0.1518}
$$

#### (c) Information Gain for "Wind"
Wind has Weak (8: 6 Yes, 2 No) and Strong (6: 3 Yes, 3 No).

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(\text{Weak}) &= -\frac{6}{8}\log_2\frac{6}{8} - \frac{2}{8}\log_2\frac{2}{8} = 0.8113 \\
H(\text{Strong}) &= -\frac{3}{6}\log_2\frac{3}{6} - \frac{3}{6}\log_2\frac{3}{6} = 1.0000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
H(S \mid \text{Wind}) = \frac{8}{14}(0.8113) + \frac{6}{14}(1.0) = 0.8922
$$

$$
\text{IG}(\text{Wind}) = 0.9403 - 0.8922 = \mathbf{0.0481}
$$

#### (d) Information Gain for "Temperature"
Temperature has Hot (4: 2 Yes, 2 No), Mild (6: 4 Yes, 2 No), Cool (4: 3 Yes, 1 No).

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(\text{Hot}) &= -\frac{2}{4}\log_2\frac{2}{4} - \frac{2}{4}\log_2\frac{2}{4} = 1.0000 \\
H(\text{Mild}) &= -\frac{4}{6}\log_2\frac{4}{6} - \frac{2}{6}\log_2\frac{2}{6} = 0.9183 \\
H(\text{Cool}) &= -\frac{3}{4}\log_2\frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{4}\log_2\frac{1}{4} = 0.8113
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
H(S \mid \text{Temp}) = \frac{4}{14}(1.0) + \frac{6}{14}(0.9183) + \frac{4}{14}(0.8113) = 0.9111
$$

$$
\text{IG}(\text{Temp}) = 0.9403 - 0.9111 = \mathbf{0.0292}
$$

### Step 3 — Select the Root Split

The Information Gains are:

| Feature | IG (bits) | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| **Outlook** | **0.2467** | 1st (Root) |
| Humidity | 0.1518 | 2nd |
| Wind | 0.0481 | 3rd |
| Temperature | 0.0292 | 4th |

**Outlook** is chosen as the root node. The "Overcast" branch becomes a pure leaf (all Yes).

### Step 4 — Recurse on the "Sunny" Subset (5 instances: 2 Yes, 3 No)
After removing "Outlook", recompute IG for the remaining features on this subset only.

For "Sunny":
- $H(\text{Sunny}) = 0.9710$
- $\text{IG}(\text{Humidity} \mid \text{Sunny})$: Humidity=High (3: 0 Yes, 3 No → $H=0$); Humidity=Normal (2: 2 Yes, 0 No → $H=0$). Both pure!

$$
H(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Humidity}) = \frac{3}{5}(0) + \frac{2}{5}(0) = 0
$$

$$
\text{IG}(\text{Humidity} \mid \text{Sunny}) = 0.9710
$$

Hence on the Sunny branch, **Humidity** is the next splitter, yielding pure leaves (No for High, Yes for Normal).

### Step 5 — Recurse on the "Rain" Subset (5 instances: 3 Yes, 2 No)
- $H(\text{Rain}) = 0.9710$
- $\text{IG}(\text{Wind} \mid \text{Rain})$: Wind=Weak (3: 3 Yes, 0 No → $H=0$); Wind=Strong (2: 0 Yes, 2 No → $H=0$). Both pure!
- Hence **Wind** splits the Rain branch (Yes for Weak, No for Strong).

### Step 6 — Final Tree Structure
The fully-grown decision tree is:

> Root = Outlook
> > Overcast → **Yes**
> > Sunny → Humidity
> > > High → **No**
> > > Normal → **Yes**
> > Rain → Wind
> > > Weak → **Yes**
> > > Strong → **No**

This is the **classic Quinlan ID3 decision tree** that KTU examiners love to test.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** Always tabulate the IG of every feature at every split. Examiners award 1 mark per IG calculation and 1 mark for the final tree diagram.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Regression Model Design Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Problem Definition] --> B[Data Collection and Cleaning]
    B --> C[Exploratory Data Analysis EDA]
    C --> D{Choose Regression Type}
    D -->|1 predictor linear| E[Simple Linear Regression]
    D -->|Multiple predictors| F[Multiple Linear Regression]
    D -->|Curvilinear trend| G[Polynomial Regression]
    E --> H[Estimate OLS Coefficients]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Check Gauss Markov Assumptions]
    I --> J{Assumptions Satisfied?}
    J -->|No| K[Apply Remedies: Transform Variables, Add Regularization]
    K --> H
    J -->|Yes| L[Conduct Significance Tests: F test, t test]
    L --> M[Compute R2 and Adjusted R2]
    M --> N[Multicollinearity Check using VIF]
    N --> O{VIF exceeds 10?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Drop or Combine Predictors]
    P --> L
    O -->|No| Q[Final Model Selected]
    Q --> R[Validate on Holdout Test Set]
    R --> S[Deploy or Iterate]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Tree Working Principle (ID3 Algorithm Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: All Training Samples at Root] --> B[Compute Entropy of Node H of S]
    B --> C[For Every Feature and Split Point]
    C --> D[Compute Weighted Entropy of Children H of S given A]
    D --> E[Compute Information Gain IG]
    E --> F{Select Feature with Maximum IG}
    F --> G[Split Node into Child Nodes]
    G --> H{Is Node Pure or Stopping Criterion Met?}
    H -->|No| I[Recurse on Each Child Node]
    I --> B
    H -->|Yes| J[Assign Leaf Label Majority Class or Mean]
    J --> K[Prune Tree for Generalization]
    K --> L[Final Decision Tree Ready]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Play Tennis Decision Tree (Section 3.3 Result)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Root Outlook] --> B[Overcast]
    A --> C[Sunny]
    A --> D[Rain]
    B --> B1[Yes Play]
    C --> E[Humidity]
    D --> F[Wind]
    E --> E1[High]
    E --> E2[Normal]
    E1 --> E1L[No Play]
    E2 --> E2L[Yes Play]
    F --> F1[Weak]
    F --> F2[Strong]
    F1 --> F1L[Yes Play]
    F2 --> F2L[No Play]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Decision Tree Inducer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1[Training Dataset D]
        A2[Feature Set F1 to Fp]
        A3[Hyperparameters: MaxDepth, MinSamplesLeaf]
    end

    subgraph InductionEngine["INDUCTION ENGINE"]
        B1[Attribute Selector]
        B2[Impurity Calculator: Entropy or Gini]
        B3[Split Point Optimizer]
        B4[Recursive Partitioner]
        B5[Pruning Module: Pre pruning and Post pruning]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        C1[Induced Decision Tree]
        C2[Prediction Function for New Instance]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B4
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
```
