# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Explain the concept of Multicollinearity in multiple regression. How is it detected using the Variance Inflation Factor (VIF)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Multicollinearity occurs when two or more independent variables in a multiple regression model are highly linearly correlated, leading to inflated standard errors, unstable coefficient estimates, and difficulty in isolating the individual effect of each predictor. It is detected using the **Variance Inflation Factor (VIF)** defined as $\text{VIF}_j = \frac{1}{1 - R_j^2}$, where $R_j^2$ is obtained by regressing the $j$-th predictor on all remaining predictors. A **VIF value exceeding 10** indicates severe multicollinearity, prompting remedies such as dropping the offending variable, combining correlated predictors, or applying **Ridge Regression** which introduces a penalty term $\lambda \sum \beta_j^2$ to shrink the coefficients. (1 mark definition, 1 mark VIF formula, 1 mark remedy)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Gini Index and Entropy as impurity measures in decision trees.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Entropy | Gini Index |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $H = -\sum p_k \log_2 p_k$ | $G = 1 - \sum p_k^2$ |
| Used by | ID3, C4.5 | CART |
| Range | $0$ to $\log_2 K$ | $0$ to $1 - 1/K$ |
| Computation | Slower (logarithm) | Faster (no log) |
| Bias | Slightly favors balanced splits | Favors larger partitions |

Both measure node impurity and are 0 for a pure node, but Gini is computationally cheaper. (1 mark for formulas, 1 mark for tabular comparison, 1 mark for application/conclusion)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** With a suitable example dataset of at least 5 observations, design a **simple linear regression model** using the Ordinary Least Squares method. Derive the OLS estimators for $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ and show all the steps of parameter estimation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the procedure to assess the **goodness-of-fit** of a multiple linear regression model. Explain the F-test for overall significance, the t-test for individual coefficients, the coefficient of determination $R^2$, and the **Adjusted $R^2$** criterion. Also state the **Gauss-Markov assumptions**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Worked Example (refer Section 3.1):**
Take the data given in Section 3.1, $n = 5$ observations of (X, Y).
- (1 mark) State model: $\hat{Y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$.
- (2 marks) Derive the OLS estimators by minimizing RSS = $\sum (Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i)^2$:
  - Setting $\partial \text{RSS} / \partial \beta_0 = 0$ and $\partial \text{RSS} / \partial \beta_1 = 0$ yields the **normal equations**:
  $$\sum Y_i = n\beta_0 + \beta_1 \sum X_i \quad \text{and} \quad \sum X_i Y_i = \beta_0 \sum X_i + \beta_1 \sum X_i^2$$
  - Solving simultaneously: $\hat{\beta}_1 = S_{XY}/S_{XX}$, $\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{X}$.
- (2 marks) Plug in the numerical values to obtain $\hat{\beta}_0 = 39, \hat{\beta}_1 = 5$, giving $\hat{Y} = 39 + 5X$.
- (2 marks) State interpretation, compute $R^2 = 0.9804$, and conduct t-test to confirm significance.

**Part (b) — Goodness-of-Fit and Gauss-Markov:**
- (2 marks) $R^2$ definition, range, and limitations (does not penalize complexity).
- (2 marks) **Adjusted $R^2$** formula $1 - \frac{(1-R^2)(n-1)}{n-p-1}$ — penalizes inclusion of irrelevant predictors; the variable should be retained only if adjusted $R^2$ increases.
- (1 mark) **F-test** statistic $F = \frac{(R^2 / p)}{((1-R^2) / (n-p-1))}$ tested with $F_{p, n-p-1}$ degrees of freedom.
- (1 mark) **t-test** statistic $t_j = \hat{\beta}_j / \text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_j)$ with $n-p-1$ df.
- (1 mark) **Gauss-Markov assumptions**: linearity, zero mean errors, homoscedasticity, no autocorrelation, no perfect multicollinearity, normality (for inference).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With reference to **Decision Tree learning**, derive the formula for **Entropy** of a node and **Information Gain** of a split. Apply these formulas to compute the root split of the **Play Tennis dataset** (14 instances, 4 attributes) given in Section 3.3 of your notes. Identify the root attribute with full calculations. **(8 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Gain Ratio** used in the C4.5 algorithm. Why is it preferred over Information Gain, and what problem does it address? Also briefly discuss **pre-pruning** and **post-pruning** techniques used to avoid overfitting in decision trees. **(6 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation and Calculation (refer Section 3.3):**
- (2 marks) Derive Entropy: $H(S) = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k \log_2 p_k$, where $p_k$ is the proportion of class $k$.
- (2 marks) Derive Information Gain: $\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$.
- (1 mark) Compute $H(S) = 0.9403$ for the Play Tennis root.
- (2 marks) Compute IG for each of the 4 attributes (Outlook = 0.2467, Humidity = 0.1518, Wind = 0.0481, Temp = 0.0292).
- (1 mark) Conclude: **Outlook** is the root with the highest IG.

**Part (b) — Gain Ratio and Pruning:**
- (2 marks) **Gain Ratio** formula: $\text{GR}(S, A) = \text{IG}(S, A) / \text{SplitInfo}(S, A)$ where $\text{SplitInfo} = -\sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$.
- (1 mark) C4.5 uses Gain Ratio because plain IG is biased toward features with many distinct values (e.g., a unique ID attribute would have a very high IG trivially). Gain Ratio normalizes this.
- (1.5 marks) **Pre-pruning (early stopping)**: Halt tree growth based on criteria like max depth, minimum samples per leaf, minimum information gain threshold. Pros: Faster; Cons: Greedy — may stop prematurely.
- (1.5 marks) **Post-pruning (reduced-error / cost-complexity pruning)**: Grow the full tree first, then remove subtrees that do not improve validation accuracy. **Cost-complexity pruning** in CART minimizes $R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$ where $R(T)$ is the misclassification rate and $\vert T \vert$ is the number of leaves.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign of coefficients ignored**: Students often forget to interpret negative coefficients (e.g., age $X_3 = -0.26$ in Section 3.2 means *price decreases with age*). — **Loss: 1–2 marks per question.**
> 2. **Degrees of freedom in t/F test**: Many students write "$t_{n, \alpha}$" or "$F_{p, n}$" instead of $t_{n-p-1, \alpha}$ and $F_{p, n-p-1}$. Examiners check this stringently.
> 3. **Decision Tree — Information Gain tabulation**: Examiners award 1 mark for *each* IG calculation. Skipping even one leads to significant mark loss.
> 4. **Entropy of a pure node**: If a child node is pure (all one class), students must explicitly write $H = 0$ — not just leave it blank.
> 5. **Confusing Gain Ratio with Information Gain**: These are *different* criteria. C4.5 uses Gain Ratio; ID3 uses IG. Mixing them up is a frequent error.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **OLS Estimator**: $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (X^{T}X)^{-1} X^{T} \mathbf{Y}$ is the *single most important formula* in regression design.
- ✅ **R² vs Adjusted R²**: Use Adjusted R² for variable selection in MLR; R² alone always increases with more predictors.
- ✅ **F-test** checks if *all* slopes are zero simultaneously; **t-test** checks each slope individually.
- ✅ **VIF > 10** = multicollinearity warning; remedies are dropping, combining, or Ridge regression.
- ✅ **Entropy** = $0$ for pure nodes, $\log_2 K$ for uniform class distribution.
- ✅ **Information Gain** is the *reduction* in entropy; the algorithm (ID3) picks the feature with the *highest* IG.
- ✅ **Gini Index** is the alternative to entropy used by CART; it is computationally cheaper.
- ✅ **Gain Ratio** = IG / SplitInfo; corrects the multi-branch bias of plain IG (used by C4.5).
- ✅ **Decision Tree pipeline**: Compute impurity → choose best split → recurse → prune.
- ✅ **Pre-pruning** = stop early; **Post-pruning** = grow full tree, then trim subtrees.
- ✅ **Root attribute in Play Tennis** is always **Outlook** (highest IG = 0.2467).
- ✅ **KTU exam focus**: Numerical computation of IG, OLS estimation, $R^2$, and significance tests.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for regression assumptions**: **LINE** — **L**inearity, **I**ndependence, **N**ormality, **E**qual variance (homoscedasticity).
- ✅ **Mnemonic for tree algorithms**: **"I-C-G-C"** — **I**D3 (IG), **C**4.5 (Gain Ratio), **G**ini (CART), **C**hampion of classification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decision Tree formation using ID3 Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAIT404 - FOUNDATION OF MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Decision Tree formation using ID3 Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3)** is a greedy, top-down, divide-and-conquer supervised learning algorithm introduced by **Ross Quinlan (1986)** that constructs a **Decision Tree** classifier by recursively partitioning the training dataset along the attribute that yields the **highest Information Gain (IG)** — equivalent to the **largest reduction in Shannon Entropy** — at every internal node.

A **Decision Tree** is a non-parametric, hierarchical, flow-graph model in which:

- **Internal Nodes** represent a *test* on a single feature (attribute).
- **Branches** represent the outcome of that test (attribute value).
- **Leaf Nodes** represent a *class label* (the final decision).

The ID3 algorithm is restricted to **categorical (nominal) attributes**, uses a **multi-way split** (one branch per attribute value), and is the conceptual foundation for its successors C4.5 and CART.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The 20-Questions Game"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of ID3 as an expert playing the game *“20 Questions.”* Instead of asking questions in random order, the expert always asks the question that **splits the remaining possibilities into the most unequal, clean halves** — so the *“Yes”* or *“No”* answer removes the maximum amount of confusion. Information Gain is mathematically that *“cleanliness”* measure: it quantifies how much a question reduces uncertainty.

**Real-world parallel:** A bank officer deciding loan approval does not ask *“What is your height?”* first — that barely narrows the applicants. They ask *“What is your credit score?”* because that single question drastically prunes the candidate pool. ID3 automates exactly this intuition: it picks the most *discriminating* question first.

## 1.3 Why ID3 Matters in Engineering

- **White-box model:** Every decision is human-interpretable (regulatory-friendly for finance/medicine).
- **No feature scaling required** (unlike KNN, SVM, Neural Nets).
- **Handles heterogeneous data** (mix of categorical and numerical — after binarization).
- **Foundation for Random Forests & Gradient Boosting** (XGBoost, LightGBM).

**Standard Metrics Used in ID3:**

- **Shannon Entropy** — measured in **bits** (using $\log_2$).
- **Information Gain** — measured in **bits**.
- **Split Information** (used by C4.5 refinement) — measured in **bits**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ID3 Operational Principle

ID3 builds the tree **recursively**, choosing the *best* attribute at every node using a mathematically principled criterion. The conceptual pipeline is:

1. **Compute the disorder (entropy)** of the target class in the current subset $S$.
2. **For every remaining attribute $A$**, compute the weighted average entropy of $S$ *after* splitting on $A$.
3. The attribute that **maximises the reduction** in entropy (i.e., maximises Information Gain) is selected as the *splitting node*.
4. **Recurse** on each child subset, removing the just-used attribute.
5. **Stop** when (a) all samples in a node belong to one class (pure node), (b) no attributes remain, or (c) further splitting fails an information gain threshold.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind Entropy:** Entropy is the *expected number of yes/no questions* required to identify the class of a randomly drawn sample. A pure node has entropy **0 bits** (no question needed); a 50-50 node has entropy **1 bit** (the maximum ambiguity for a binary class).

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of ID3 Mathematics

### Pillar 1 — Shannon Entropy

Measures the **impurity** (or average information content) of a sample set $S$ with respect to $c$ classes:

$$E(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \, \log_2(p_i)$$

where $p_i$ is the proportion of samples in $S$ that belong to class $i$.
Boundary conditions: $E(S) = 0$ when $|S|$ is pure, and $E(S) = \log_2(c)$ when classes are uniformly distributed.

### Pillar 2 — Conditional Entropy After Splitting on Attribute $A$

For an attribute $A$ with $v$ distinct values, partitioning $S$ into subsets $\{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_v\}$:

$$E(S, A) = \sum_{j=1}^{v} \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} \, E(S_j)$$

This is the **expected entropy** of $S$ after we *know* the value of $A$ — a weighted average of child entropies.

### Pillar 3 — Information Gain

$$IG(S, A) = E(S) - E(S, A) = E(S) - \sum_{j=1}^{v} \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} \, E(S_j)$$

**Selection Rule:** ID3 picks $A^* = \arg\max_{A} \, IG(S, A)$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this table — these four equations account for **~70% of ID3 numerical questions** in KTU ESE papers.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Range | Interpretation |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | Shannon Entropy | $E(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | **bits**, $[0, \log_2 c]$ | Impurity of set $S$ |
| 2 | Weighted Conditional Entropy | $E(S,A) = \sum_{j=1}^{v} \dfrac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} E(S_j)$ | **bits**, $[0, \log_2 c]$ | Average impurity after split on $A$ |
| 3 | Information Gain | $IG(S,A) = E(S) - E(S,A)$ | **bits**, $[0, E(S)]$ | Reduction in uncertainty |
| 4 | Split Info (C4.5 variant) | $SI(S,A) = -\sum_{j=1}^{v} \dfrac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \dfrac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | **bits** | Intrinsic split information |
| 5 | Gain Ratio (C4.5) | $GR(S,A) = \dfrac{IG(S,A)}{SI(S,A)}$ | dimensionless | Normalised gain |

**Special numerical values used in KTU problems:**

$$\log_2(0.5) = -1.0 \quad ; \quad \log_2(0.25) = -2.0 \quad ; \quad \log_2(0.125) = -3.0$$

$$\log_2(2/3) \approx 0.585 \quad ; \quad \log_2(1/3) \approx 1.585 \quad ; \quad \log_2(0.4) \approx 1.322$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis Systems** (Mycin, modern CDSS) — ID3-style rules map symptom → disease.
- **Credit Risk Scoring** in banking — interpretable rules satisfy Basel-III/IV regulatory mandates.
- **Manufacturing Quality Control** — isolate the *root cause* of defects using hierarchical tests.
- **Network Intrusion Detection** — fast, white-box rule generation for SOC analysts.

## 2.5 Limitations of ID3 (Frequently Asked in KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> - **No handling of continuous attributes** (requires pre-discretisation).
- **No pruning mechanism** → severe **overfitting** on noisy data.
- **Multi-way splits** cause **data fragmentation** on high-cardinality attributes.
- **Biased toward attributes with many values** (mitigated by C4.5’s *Gain Ratio*).
- **No native handling of missing values** (handled in C4.5 / CART).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked-Out KTU-Standard Problem:** Construct a Decision Tree using ID3 for the dataset below. Total samples = **10**, Classes = **{Yes, No}**.

### 3.1 The Training Set

| Sample | Age | Income | Buys Product? |
|:------:|:----:|:------:|:-------------:|
| 1 | Young | Low  | No  |
| 2 | Young | Low  | No  |
| 3 | Young | High | Yes |
| 4 | Middle | Low  | Yes |
| 5 | Middle | High | Yes |
| 6 | Middle | High | Yes |
| 7 | Old | Low  | No  |
| 8 | Old | High | Yes |
| 9 | Old | Low  | No  |
| 10 | Old | High | Yes |

**Class Distribution:** Yes = **6**, No = **4**.

---

### 3.2 STEP 1 — Entropy of the Root Set $S$

We compute the impurity of the entire dataset using $p_{\text{Yes}} = 6/10$ and $p_{\text{No}} = 4/10$:

$$
E(S) = -\left(\frac{6}{10}\right)\log_2\!\left(\frac{6}{10}\right) - \left(\frac{4}{10}\right)\log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{10}\right)
$$

$$
E(S) = -(0.6)(-0.7370) - (0.4)(-1.3219)
$$

$$
E(S) = 0.4422 + 0.5288 = 0.9710 \text{ bits}
$$

**Interpretation:** The starting set carries **0.971 bits of uncertainty**.

---

### 3.3 STEP 2 — Information Gain for Attribute **Age**

Age has three values: **Young** (3 samples), **Middle** (3 samples), **Old** (4 samples). For each subset, we tally Yes/No:

- **Age = Young:** 1 Yes, 2 No (3 samples)

$$
E(S_{\text{Young}}) = -\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)\log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) - \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)\log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{3}\right) = 0.5283 + 0.3900 = 0.9183 \text{ bits}
$$

- **Age = Middle:** 3 Yes, 0 No (3 samples)

$$
E(S_{\text{Middle}}) = 0 \text{ bits} \quad \text{(pure node)}
$$

- **Age = Old:** 2 Yes, 2 No (4 samples)

$$
E(S_{\text{Old}}) = -\left(\frac{2}{4}\right)\log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{4}\right) - \left(\frac{2}{4}\right)\log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{4}\right) = 0.5(1) + 0.5(1) = 1.0000 \text{ bits}
$$

**Weighted conditional entropy after Age split:**

$$
E(S, \text{Age}) = \frac{3}{10}(0.9183) + \frac{3}{10}(0) + \frac{4}{10}(1.0000) = 0.2755 + 0 + 0.4000 = 0.6755 \text{ bits}
$$

**Information Gain for Age:**

$$
IG(S, \text{Age}) = E(S) - E(S, \text{Age}) = 0.9710 - 0.6755 = 0.2955 \text{ bits}
$$

---

### 3.4 STEP 3 — Information Gain for Attribute **Income**

Income has two values: **Low** (5 samples) and **High** (5 samples):

- **Income = Low:** 1 Yes (Middle,Low), 4 No (2×Young,Low + 2×Old,Low) (5 samples)

$$
E(S_{\text{Low}}) = -\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)\log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{5}\right) - \left(\frac{4}{5}\right)\log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) = 0.2(2.3219) + 0.8(0.3219) = 0.4644 + 0.2575 = 0.7219 \text{ bits}
$$

- **Income = High:** 5 Yes, 0 No (5 samples)

$$
E(S_{\text{High}}) = 0 \text{ bits} \quad \text{(pure node)}
$$

**Weighted conditional entropy after Income split:**

$$
E(S, \text{Income}) = \frac{5}{10}(0.7219) + \frac{5}{10}(0) = 0.3610 + 0 = 0.3610 \text{ bits}
$$

**Information Gain for Income:**

$$
IG(S, \text{Income}) = E(S) - E(S, \text{Income}) = 0.9710 - 0.3610 = 0.6100 \text{ bits}
$$

---

### 3.5 STEP 4 — Attribute Selection at the Root

$$
IG(S, \text{Income}) = 0.6100 \; > \; IG(S, \text{Age}) = 0.2955
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision:** **Income** is selected as the **root node** because it produces the **largest reduction in entropy** (highest information gain).

---

### 3.6 STEP 5 — Recursive Splitting

**Branch 1 — Income = High:** 5 Yes, 0 No → **Pure leaf (class = Yes)**. Stop.

**Branch 2 — Income = Low:** 1 Yes, 4 No → **Impure**. Recurse on remaining attribute *Age*.

- **(Age = Young, Income = Low):** 2 No → **Pure leaf (class = No)**.
- **(Age = Middle, Income = Low):** 1 Yes → **Pure leaf (class = Yes)**.
- **(Age = Old, Income = Low):** 2 No → **Pure leaf (class = No)**.

---

### 3.7 Final ID3 Decision Tree (Text Representation)

```
                      [ Income ? ]
                     /            \
                High              Low
                 |                |
               (Yes)        [ Age ? ]
                           /    |    \
                        Young Middle  Old
                          |    |      |
                        (No) (Yes)  (No)
```

---

### 3.8 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Attribute | $E(S,A)$ | $IG(S,A)$ | Verdict |
|:---------:|:--------:|:---------:|:-------:|
| Age        | 0.6755 | 0.2955 | 2nd choice |
| **Income** | **0.3610** | **0.6100** | **Root Node ✓** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart of the ID3 Procedure

The following Mermaid block-diagram captures the exact recursive control flow of the ID3 algorithm, including all stopping conditions and recursive calls.

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[START: Load Training Set S] --> calcEnt[Compute Shannon Entropy E S]
    calcEnt --> checkPure{Is S pure or no attributes left?}
    checkPure -->|Yes| makeLeaf[Create Leaf Node: majority class]
    checkPure -->|No| loopAtt[For each candidate attribute A]
    loopAtt --> calcIG[Compute E S sub A and IG S A]
    calcIG --> bestAtt{Select A star = argmax IG}
    bestAtt --> createNode[Create Decision Node on A star]
    createNode --> splitS[Split S into v subsets S1 to Sv by values of A star]
    splitS --> recurse[For each child subset Sj, recursively call ID3 Sj, A minus A star]
    recurse --> attachKid[Attach subtree as child of A star node]
    attachKid --> retTree[Return Root of Subtree]
    makeLeaf --> retTree
    retTree --> stopB[END: Final Decision Tree]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Architecture Mapping:** Each rectangle in the diagram corresponds to a *function call* in the classical ID3 pseudocode. The recursion is represented by the `recurse` block, which mirrors the *divide-and-conquer* nature of the algorithm. The diamond `checkPure` is the **base case** of the recursion.

## 4.2 Resulting Decision-Tree Schematic (Mermaid Tree)

The following diagram is a *graph-theoretic* rendering of the final ID3 tree built in Section 3.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootN[INCOME] --> hN[High]
    rootN --> lN[Low]
    hN --> leafH1((Yes - Pure Leaf))
    lN --> ageN[AGE]
    ageN --> yN[Young]
    ageN --> mN[Middle]
    ageN --> oN[Old]
    yN --> leafY1((No - Pure Leaf))
    mN --> leafM1((Yes - Pure Leaf))
    oN --> leafO1((No - Pure Leaf))
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Rectangular nodes are *decision (test) nodes*; circular nodes `(...)` are *leaf nodes* carrying the final class. Every edge label is a value of the parent attribute. The depth of a node equals the number of tests required to reach a decision.

## 4.3 Information-Gain Comparison Bar (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    gA[IG Age = 0.2955] --- gB[IG Income = 0.6100]
    gB --- winner[ROOT NODE]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. Define Shannon Entropy and Information Gain as used in the ID3 algorithm. State the units.
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Shannon Entropy $E(S)$ quantifies the average impurity (or expected information content) of a dataset $S$ with $c$ classes:

$$
E(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i
$$

where $p_i$ is the proportion of class $i$. It is measured in **bits** and ranges in $[0, \log_2 c]$.

Information Gain $IG(S, A)$ measures the reduction in entropy achieved by partitioning $S$ on attribute $A$:

$$
IG(S, A) = E(S) - \sum_{j} \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} E(S_j)
$$

It is also expressed in **bits**. ID3 selects the attribute with maximum $IG$ as the splitting node.

---

### Q2. List any four limitations of the ID3 algorithm.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **No continuous-attribute handling** — ID3 works only with categorical features; numerical attributes must be pre-discretised.
2. **Overfitting tendency** — the algorithm grows the tree until pure leaves, with no built-in pruning.
3. **Bias toward high-cardinality attributes** — attributes with many values inflate $IG$ spuriously (addressed in C4.5 by *Gain Ratio*).
4. **Data fragmentation** — multi-way splits rapidly create tiny, statistically weak child nodes.

*(Any four of the above. 1 mark per valid limitation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For ID3 numerical questions, students *frequently* lose 2–3 marks for **(i)** forgetting to convert the weighted conditional entropy into a *weighted* average (forgetting the $\vert S_j \vert / \vert S \vert$ factor), **(ii)** using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\log_2$ (yielding units in *decits* not *bits*), and **(iii)** not explicitly stating the **stopping condition** for recursion. Always show the three-term formula and write "Stop: pure node reached."

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the ID3 algorithm with a labelled block diagram. Describe its top-down, greedy, divide-and-conquer strategy and list the stopping conditions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the dataset of Section 3.1 (10 samples, attributes: Age, Income; class: Buys Product), calculate the Information Gain for both attributes. Identify the root node and draw the resulting decision tree.

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 Marks

The **ID3 algorithm**, proposed by **Ross Quinlan (1986)**, builds a classification tree in a **top-down, greedy, divide-and-conquer** fashion.

**Procedure (block-diagram form):**

1. **Input:** Training set $S$ of labelled samples with attributes $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$ and a categorical class label.
2. **Compute** the entropy $E(S)$ of the current node.
3. **For each attribute $A$**, compute the weighted conditional entropy $E(S, A)$ and the information gain $IG(S, A) = E(S) - E(S, A)$.
4. **Select the splitting attribute** $A^* = \arg\max_A IG(S, A)$ and create a *decision node* on $A^*$.
5. **Partition** $S$ along the values of $A^*$ and **recurse** on each child subset, removing $A^*$ from the candidate pool.
6. **Stopping Conditions (base cases):**
   - All samples in the node belong to a single class (pure leaf).
   - No remaining attributes to split on (majority-vote leaf).
   - Information gain falls below a user-defined threshold.
   - Pre-defined maximum tree depth is exceeded.

**Diagrammatic representation:** (Refer to the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1.)

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 Marks

**Class distribution:** Yes = 6, No = 4.

**Step 1 — Root entropy:**

$$
E(S) = -\frac{6}{10}\log_2\!\left(\frac{6}{10}\right) - \frac{4}{10}\log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{10}\right) = 0.9710 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — IG for Age (Young=3, Middle=3, Old=4):**

$$
E(S_{\text{Young}}) = -\tfrac{1}{3}\log_2\!\tfrac{1}{3} - \tfrac{2}{3}\log_2\!\tfrac{2}{3} = 0.9183 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
E(S_{\text{Middle}}) = 0 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
E(S_{\text{Old}}) = -\tfrac{2}{4}\log_2\!\tfrac{2}{4} - \tfrac{2}{4}\log_2\!\tfrac{2}{4} = 1.0000 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
E(S, \text{Age}) = \tfrac{3}{10}(0.9183) + \tfrac{3}{10}(0) + \tfrac{4}{10}(1.0000) = 0.6755 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
IG(S, \text{Age}) = 0.9710 - 0.6755 = \mathbf{0.2955 \text{ bits}}
$$

**Step 3 — IG for Income (Low=5, High=5):**

$$
E(S_{\text{Low}}) = -\tfrac{1}{5}\log_2\!\tfrac{1}{5} - \tfrac{4}{5}\log_2\!\tfrac{4}{5} = 0.7219 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
E(S_{\text{High}}) = 0 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
E(S, \text{Income}) = \tfrac{5}{10}(0.7219) + \tfrac{5}{10}(0) = 0.3610 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
IG(S, \text{Income}) = 0.9710 - 0.3610 = \mathbf{0.6100 \text{ bits}}
$$

**Step 4 — Root selection:** Income (0.6100) > Age (0.2955) ⇒ **Root = Income**.

**Step 5 — Recurse on Income = Low** (samples 1, 2, 4, 7, 9). All sub-branches of Age on this subset are pure (Young→No, Middle→Yes, Old→No). Income = High becomes a pure Yes leaf.

**Final Tree:**

```
[Income?]
   /     \
 High    Low
  |       |
 (Yes)  [Age?]
        /  |  \
    Young Mid Old
      |   |   |
     (No)(Yes)(No)
```

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare ID3 with C4.5 and CART under the following heads: splitting criterion, handling of continuous attributes, multi-way vs binary splits, and pruning mechanism.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a dataset with class distribution Yes = 7 and No = 3, an attribute $A$ partitions the data into three subsets with the following (Yes, No) counts: $S_1 = (3, 1)$, $S_2 = (2, 2)$, $S_3 = (2, 0)$. Compute $E(S)$, $E(S, A)$, and $IG(S, A)$. Hence decide whether $A$ is a good splitter.

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 Marks

| Head | **ID3 (Quinlan 1986)** | **C4.5 (Quinlan 1993)** | **CART (Breiman 1984)** |
|------|-----------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Splitting Criterion** | Information Gain (Entropy-based) | Gain Ratio = IG / Split Info | Gini Index (or Twoing for multi-class) |
| **Continuous Attributes** | Not supported natively; pre-discretisation required | Built-in threshold-based binary split (gain ratio maximised) | Built-in binary threshold split (Gini minimised) |
| **Split Type** | Multi-way (one branch per value) | Multi-way for nominal, binary for continuous | Strictly binary |
| **Pruning** | None (raw overfit) | Error-based post-pruning (pessimistic) | Cost-complexity (CCP) pre- and post-pruning |
| **Output** | Decision Tree | Decision Tree + Rule Sets | Binary Decision Tree |
| **Missing Values** | Not handled | Handled (split on surrogate / probability weights) | Handled via *surrogate splits* |
| **Target** | Classification | Classification | Classification & Regression |

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 Marks

**Class totals:** $N = 10$, Yes = 7, No = 3.

**Step 1 — Root Entropy $E(S)$:**

$$
E(S) = -\tfrac{7}{10}\log_2\!\tfrac{7}{10} - \tfrac{3}{10}\log_2\!\tfrac{3}{10}
$$

$$
E(S) = -(0.7)(-0.5146) - (0.3)(-1.7370) = 0.3602 + 0.5211 = 0.8813 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Entropy of each subset:**

- $S_1$: Yes = 3/4, No = 1/4

$$
E(S_1) = -\tfrac{3}{4}\log_2\!\tfrac{3}{4} - \tfrac{1}{4}\log_2\!\tfrac{1}{4} = 0.75(0.4150) + 0.25(2.0) = 0.3113 + 0.5 = 0.8113 \text{ bits}
$$

- $S_2$: Yes = 2/4, No = 2/4 (perfectly mixed)

$$
E(S_2) = -\tfrac{2}{4}\log_2\!\tfrac{2}{4} - \tfrac{2}{4}\log_2\!\tfrac{2}{4} = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0000 \text{ bits}
$$

- $S_3$: Yes = 2/2, No = 0/2 (pure)

$$
E(S_3) = 0 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Weighted Conditional Entropy $E(S, A)$:**

$$
E(S, A) = \tfrac{4}{10}(0.8113) + \tfrac{4}{10}(1.0000) + \tfrac{2}{10}(0) = 0.3245 + 0.4000 + 0 = 0.7245 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Information Gain $IG(S, A)$:**

$$
IG(S, A) = E(S) - E(S, A) = 0.8813 - 0.7245 = \mathbf{0.1568 \text{ bits}}
$$

**Verdict:** Since $IG(S, A) = 0.1568 > 0$, attribute $A$ *does* reduce uncertainty and is a *valid* splitter. However, the gain is **modest** (only ~18% of the original entropy), so other candidate attributes may yield a *better* split and should be compared before final selection.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Exam Day Quick-Glance):**

- ✅ **ID3** = **Iterative Dichotomiser 3**, by **Ross Quinlan (1986)**; greedy, top-down, multi-way splits; **categorical only**.
- ✅ **Core formula trio:** $E(S)$, $E(S, A)$, $IG(S, A) = E(S) - E(S, A)$.
- ✅ **Always use $\log_2$** — units must be **bits**, not *nats* or *decits*.
- ✅ **Stopping conditions:** pure node, no attributes, low IG, max depth.
- ✅ **Tie-breaker:** if two attributes have equal IG, ID3 picks the **first** encountered (no standard tie rule).
- ✅ **Attribute with highest $IG$** → root; recurse on subsets.
- ✅ **Limitations to memorise:** no continuous, no pruning, no missing values, bias to high-cardinality.
- ✅ **Successor algorithm C4.5** uses **Gain Ratio** = IG / Split Info to remove the multi-value bias.
- ✅ **CART** uses **Gini Index** = $1 - \sum p_i^2$ and **binary splits** only.
- ✅ **Pure-node entropy = 0 bits**; uniform binary entropy = **1 bit**; the maximum-entropy reference.
- ✅ **Always show three numerical values in your answer:** $E(S)$, $E(S, A)$, $IG(S, A)$ — never skip the middle term in KTU valuation.
- ✅ **Draw the final tree using a labelled graph** — KTU examiners award **1–2 marks** for the correct tree diagram even when calculations are slightly off.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Classification and Regression Trees Algorithm (CART). Illustrative examples on designing decision trees. Generalisation and Overfitting: Idea of overfitting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAIT404 - FOUNDATION OF MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Classification and Regression Trees Algorithm (CART). Illustrative examples on designing decision trees. Generalisation and Overfitting: Idea of overfitting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of CART

The **Classification and Regression Trees (CART)** algorithm, formally introduced by **Leo Breiman, Jerome Friedman, Richard Olshen, and Charles Stone in 1984**, is a non-parametric supervised machine learning technique that constructs a **binary decision tree** from training data. CART operates by recursively partitioning the feature space into a hierarchy of binary splits, producing a piecewise constant prediction model.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** CART is a foundational algorithm in the KTU PBAIT404 syllabus. The algorithm is "greedy" — it makes locally optimal splits at each node without backtracking.

### Key Formal Properties of CART:

| Property | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Tree Structure** | Strictly binary (each internal node has exactly two children) |
| **Splitting Criterion (Classification)** | **Gini Impurity** (default) |
| **Splitting Criterion (Regression)** | **Reduction in Variance / RSS** |
| **Output (Classification)** | Discrete class label |
| **Output (Regression)** | Continuous numerical value (mean of leaf node samples) |
| **Stopping Mechanism** | Pre-pruning thresholds (max depth, min samples) |

> [!NOTE]  
> **CART vs. ID3/C4.5:** While ID3 and C4.5 (Quinlan) can produce multi-way splits on categorical features, **CART exclusively produces binary splits**, which gives it a unified, elegant mathematical framework applicable to both classification and regression.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "20 Questions" Game

Imagine playing a strategic guessing game with a friend. They are thinking of an object, and you must identify it by asking yes/no questions. 

- **Bad strategy:** Asking vague, non-discriminating questions like *"Is it related to science?"* (this barely narrows the possibilities).
- **Good strategy:** Asking maximally informative questions like *"Is it a living organism?"* — this splits the universe of objects into two roughly equal, useful halves.

**CART operates exactly like the optimal "20 Questions" player.** At every node, CART asks the **single best binary question** that splits the dataset into the two purest possible subsets. The purity of a subset means that almost all samples inside it belong to the same class (classification) or have nearly identical numerical values (regression).

> [!NOTE]  
> **Intuitive Purity Visualization:** A "pure" node is like a basket containing only red apples. An "impure" node is a basket containing a chaotic mix of red apples, green apples, and oranges. CART's mission is to make every basket as single-fruit as possible through binary partitioning.

---

## 1.3 Overfitting: The "Memorization Trap"

**Overfitting** in the context of decision trees refers to a model that has learned the training data *too well* — including its random noise, outliers, and idiosyncrasies — at the cost of failing to generalize to unseen data.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Overfitting occurs when a learning algorithm generates a hypothesis that perfectly (or near-perfectly) classifies the training data but performs poorly on the validation/test set due to capturing noise rather than the underlying pattern.*

### The Overfitting Analogy: "The Student Who Memorizes vs. The Student Who Learns"

- **The Memorizer (Overfit):** A student crams every past exam paper, including the typos and irrelevant footnotes. In a new exam with different questions, the student fails spectacularly.
- **The True Learner (Generalized):** A student understands the core principles, applies them to unseen problems, and performs consistently across all exams.

An overfit decision tree is one that has grown so deep and complex that it essentially **memorizes** every single training sample as a unique rule (e.g., "If feature₁ = 3.14 AND feature₂ = 'blue' AND feature₃ = 'small', predict Class A"). This rule is meaningless for new data.

> [!CAUTION]  
> **The High-Variance Symptom:** Overfit models exhibit **high variance** — small changes in the training data lead to dramatically different tree structures. This is the **central tradeoff** between bias and variance in tree-based learning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CART Algorithm: Operational Logic Flow

CART operates through a **recursive binary partitioning** procedure. The following is the structured logic that the algorithm executes:

### Stage 1: Initialization
- The entire training dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ is placed at the **root node** of the tree.
- The algorithm considers every feature $X_j$ and every possible threshold value $t$ to create candidate binary splits.

### Stage 2: Split Selection (The "Best Question" Search)
For every candidate split of the form:

$$X_j \leq t \quad \text{vs.} \quad X_j > t$$

CART computes a **goodness measure** (impurity decrease) and selects the split that **maximizes** this measure.

### Stage 3: Recursive Partitioning
- The dataset is divided into two child nodes: the **left child** $\mathcal{D}_{\text{left}}$ (samples satisfying the condition) and the **right child** $\mathcal{D}_{\text{right}}$.
- The algorithm is applied recursively to each child node.

### Stage 4: Termination (Pre-Pruning)
Recursion halts when one of the following conditions is met:
- The node is **pure** (impurity = 0)
- The node has fewer than `min_samples_split` samples
- The tree has reached `max_depth`
- No split provides a meaningful improvement (gain < `min_impurity_decrease`)

### Stage 5: Leaf Node Assignment
- **Classification:** Each leaf is assigned the **majority class** of its contained samples.
- **Regression:** Each leaf is assigned the **mean** of its contained sample target values.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet: Impurity & Splitting Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The following table contains **every essential formula** required to solve CART-based problems in KTU examinations. **Memorize these thoroughly.**

| **Metric** | **Mathematical Formula** | **Range & Use Case** |
|---|---|---|
| **Gini Impurity** | $G(\mathcal{D}) = 1 - \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c^2$ | $[0, 1 - 1/C]$; default CART criterion |
| **Entropy** | $H(\mathcal{D}) = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c \log_2 p_c$ | $[0, \log_2 C]$; ID3/C4.5 criterion |
| **Misclassification Error** | $E(\mathcal{D}) = 1 - \max_c(p_c)$ | $[0, 1 - 1/C]$; used in post-pruning |
| **Gini Gain (Split Score)** | $\Delta G = G(\mathcal{D}) - \frac{N_L}{N}G(\mathcal{D}_L) - \frac{N_R}{N}G(\mathcal{D}_R)$ | Maximized during split selection |
| **Information Gain (ID3)** | $\Delta H = H(\mathcal{D}) - \frac{N_L}{N}H(\mathcal{D}_L) - \frac{N_R}{N}H(\mathcal{D}_R)$ | Maximized by ID3 |
| **Regression SSE (RSS)** | $SSE = \sum_{i \in \text{node}} (y_i - \bar{y}_{\text{node}})^2$ | Minimized for regression splits |
| **Variance Reduction** | $\Delta \text{Var} = \text{Var}(\mathcal{D}) - \frac{N_L}{N}\text{Var}(\mathcal{D}_L) - \frac{N_R}{N}\text{Var}(\mathcal{D}_R)$ | Regression splitting criterion |
| **Leaf Prediction (Reg.)** | $\hat{y}_{\text{leaf}} = \frac{1}{N_{\text{leaf}}} \sum_{i \in \text{leaf}} y_i$ | Output of regression tree |
| **Cost-Complexity Pruning** | $R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$ | Post-pruning objective; $R(T)$ = misclassification rate, $\vert T \vert$ = number of leaves |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Notation Note:** In the formulas above, $p_c$ denotes the proportion (frequency) of class $c$ samples in the node $\mathcal{D}$, and $C$ is the total number of classes. $N$, $N_L$, $N_R$ are the total, left-child, and right-child sample counts respectively.

---

## 2.3 Why Gini Impurity in CART? (The "Why" Behind the Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Conceptual Question:** *Why does CART prefer Gini Impurity over Entropy?*

- **Computational Efficiency:** Gini requires only squaring of probabilities, while Entropy requires logarithmic computation, making Gini faster for large datasets.
- **No Logarithmic Issues:** Gini avoids numerical instability with zero probabilities ($0 \log 0$).
- **Empirical Equivalence:** In practice, Gini and Entropy produce nearly identical trees. Gini tends to slightly favor **larger partitions** (more balanced splits), while Entropy can produce more irregular trees.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of CART

> [!NOTE]  
> **Engineering Applications of Decision Trees:**

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Predicting whether a tumor is malignant based on cell measurements (Wisconsin Breast Cancer Dataset).
- **Credit Risk Scoring:** Banks use CART-like models to classify loan applicants as low/high risk.
- **Manufacturing Quality Control:** Identifying defective products based on sensor readings.
- **Remote Sensing:** Land cover classification from satellite imagery.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Estimating remaining useful life of industrial equipment (regression trees).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Illustrative Example 1: Designing a Classification Tree (CART)

### Problem Statement

We are given the following training dataset of **10 samples** for a binary classification problem (Class = $+$ or $-$). Build a CART decision tree.

| $X_1$ (Feature 1) | $X_2$ (Feature 2) | $Y$ (Class) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 5 | + |
| 2 | 6 | + |
| 3 | 4 | + |
| 4 | 5 | + |
| 5 | 6 | + |
| 6 | 7 | - |
| 7 | 8 | - |
| 8 | 7 | - |
| 9 | 8 | - |
| 10 | 9 | - |

**Objective:** Use the **Gini Impurity** criterion to find the best root-level split.

---

### Step 1: Compute the Gini Impurity of the Root Node

The root node contains **10 samples**: 5 positive ($+$) and 5 negative ($-$) class samples.

The class proportions are:

$$p_{+} = \frac{5}{10} = 0.5, \quad p_{-} = \frac{5}{10} = 0.5$$

Applying the Gini Impurity formula:

$$G(\text{Root}) = 1 - \sum_{c} p_c^2 = 1 - (p_{+}^2 + p_{-}^2)$$

Substituting the values:

$$G(\text{Root}) = 1 - (0.5^2 + 0.5^2) = 1 - (0.25 + 0.25) = 1 - 0.50 = 0.50$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Interpretation:** A Gini value of **0.50** is the **maximum possible impurity** for a 2-class problem ($1 - 1/2 = 0.5$). The root node is maximally impure — a perfect candidate for splitting.

---

### Step 2: Enumerate Candidate Splits for Feature $X_1$

For a binary split, the candidate thresholds for $X_1$ are the midpoints between consecutive values: **1.5, 2.5, 3.5, 4.5, 5.5, 6.5, 7.5, 8.5, 9.5**.

We will evaluate the split at $X_1 \leq 5.5$ (which gives a meaningful separation between the 5 positive samples and the first negative sample).

**Split at $X_1 \leq 5.5$:**

- **Left Child ($\mathcal{D}_L$):** Samples with $X_1 \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ → 5 samples, all class $+$
- **Right Child ($\mathcal{D}_R$):** Samples with $X_1 \in \{6, 7, 8, 9, 10\}$ → 5 samples, all class $-$

**Gini Impurity of Left Child:**

$$G(\mathcal{D}_L) = 1 - (1^2 + 0^2) = 1 - 1 = 0.00$$

**Gini Impurity of Right Child:**

$$G(\mathcal{D}_R) = 1 - (0^2 + 1^2) = 1 - 1 = 0.00$$

**Gini Gain (Decrease in Impurity):**

$$\Delta G = G(\text{Root}) - \frac{N_L}{N}G(\mathcal{D}_L) - \frac{N_R}{N}G(\mathcal{D}_R)$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta G = 0.50 - \frac{5}{10}(0.00) - \frac{5}{10}(0.00)$$

$$\Delta G = 0.50 - 0 - 0 = 0.50$$

---

### Step 3: Enumerate Other Splits for Comparison

Let us check the split at $X_1 \leq 6.5$ for comparison:

- **Left Child:** $X_1 \in \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$ → 5 positives, 1 negative
- **Right Child:** $X_1 \in \{7,8,9,10\}$ → 4 negatives

**Gini of Left:**

$$G(\mathcal{D}_L) = 1 - \left(\frac{5}{6}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{1}{6}\right)^2 = 1 - \frac{25}{36} - \frac{1}{36} = 1 - \frac{26}{36} = \frac{10}{36} \approx 0.278$$

**Gini of Right:**

$$G(\mathcal{D}_R) = 1 - (0^2 + 1^2) = 0.00$$

**Gini Gain:**

$$\Delta G = 0.50 - \frac{6}{10}(0.278) - \frac{4}{10}(0.00) = 0.50 - 0.167 = 0.333$$

Since $\Delta G = 0.50$ for split at $X_1 \leq 5.5$ is **greater than** $\Delta G = 0.333$ for split at $X_1 \leq 6.5$, **the optimal split is at $X_1 \leq 5.5$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Decision Rule:** In CART, the algorithm selects the split that **maximizes the Gini Gain** $\Delta G$. Since the split at 5.5 produces two **pure child nodes** (Gini = 0), no further splitting is needed. The tree has depth = 1.

---

### Final Tree Structure

| Node | Condition | Class Assignment | Gini |
|---|---|---|---|
| Root | $X_1 \leq 5.5$? | — | 0.50 |
| Left Leaf | Yes | Class $+$ | 0.00 |
| Right Leaf | No | Class $-$ | 0.00 |

---

## 3.2 Illustrative Example 2: Designing a Regression Tree (CART)

### Problem Statement

Given the following dataset of 8 samples with feature $X$ and continuous target $Y$, construct a regression tree using **reduction in variance** as the splitting criterion.

| Sample | $X$ | $Y$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1 | 2.0 |
| 2 | 2 | 3.0 |
| 3 | 3 | 4.5 |
| 4 | 4 | 5.0 |
| 5 | 5 | 6.5 |
| 6 | 6 | 7.0 |
| 7 | 7 | 8.5 |
| 8 | 8 | 9.0 |

---

### Step 1: Compute Variance of the Root Node

First, compute the mean of $Y$:

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{2.0 + 3.0 + 4.5 + 5.0 + 6.5 + 7.0 + 8.5 + 9.0}{8} = \frac{45.5}{8} = 5.6875$$

Compute the variance:

$$\text{Var}(\text{Root}) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(Y_i - \bar{Y})^2$$

Calculate each squared deviation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(2.0 - 5.6875)^2 &= 13.6139 \\
(3.0 - 5.6875)^2 &= 7.2227 \\
(4.5 - 5.6875)^2 &= 1.4102 \\
(5.0 - 5.6875)^2 &= 0.4727 \\
(6.5 - 5.6875)^2 &= 0.6602 \\
(7.0 - 5.6875)^2 &= 1.7227 \\
(8.5 - 5.6875)^2 &= 7.9102 \\
(9.0 - 5.6875)^2 &= 10.9727 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing:

$$\sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2 = 43.9853$$

$$\text{Var}(\text{Root}) = \frac{43.9853}{8} \approx 5.498$$

---

### Step 2: Test Split at $X \leq 4.5$

- **Left Child $\mathcal{D}_L$:** Samples $\{1,2,3,4\}$, $Y = \{2.0, 3.0, 4.5, 5.0\}$
- **Right Child $\mathcal{D}_R$:** Samples $\{5,6,7,8\}$, $Y = \{6.5, 7.0, 8.5, 9.0\}$

**Left Mean and Variance:**

$$\bar{Y}_L = \frac{2.0+3.0+4.5+5.0}{4} = 3.625$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(\mathcal{D}_L) &= \frac{(2.0-3.625)^2 + (3.0-3.625)^2 + (4.5-3.625)^2 + (5.0-3.625)^2}{4} \\
&= \frac{2.6406 + 0.3906 + 0.7656 + 1.8906}{4} \\
&= \frac{5.6875}{4} \approx 1.422
\end{aligned}
$$

**Right Mean and Variance:**

$$\bar{Y}_R = \frac{6.5+7.0+8.5+9.0}{4} = 7.75$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(\mathcal{D}_R) &= \frac{(6.5-7.75)^2 + (7.0-7.75)^2 + (8.5-7.75)^2 + (9.0-7.75)^2}{4} \\
&= \frac{1.5625 + 0.5625 + 0.5625 + 1.5625}{4} \\
&= \frac{4.25}{4} = 1.0625
\end{aligned}
$$

**Variance Reduction:**

$$\Delta \text{Var} = \text{Var}(\text{Root}) - \frac{N_L}{N}\text{Var}(\mathcal{D}_L) - \frac{N_R}{N}\text{Var}(\mathcal{D}_R)$$

$$\Delta \text{Var} = 5.498 - \frac{4}{8}(1.422) - \frac{4}{8}(1.0625)$$

$$\Delta \text{Var} = 5.498 - 0.711 - 0.531 = 4.256$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Regression Tree Rule:** Select the split that **maximizes variance reduction** $\Delta \text{Var}$. Each leaf's prediction is the **mean** of its samples: Left leaf predicts **3.625**, right leaf predicts **7.75**.

---

## 3.3 Overfitting: Quantitative Analysis

The expected prediction error of any model can be decomposed as:

$$\mathbb{E}[(Y - \hat{f}(X))^2] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2[\hat{f}(X)]}_{\text{Systematic Error}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}[\hat{f}(X)]}_{\text{Model Sensitivity}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Critical Insight:** As a decision tree grows deeper:
> - **Bias decreases** (the model fits training data more closely)
> - **Variance increases** (the model becomes more sensitive to training data fluctuations)
> - **Total error** has a U-shape — it decreases initially, then **increases** as overfitting sets in.

The optimal tree depth $d^*$ minimizes the total error. Beyond $d^*$, the model is overfit.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CART Algorithm: High-Level Functional Architecture

The following block-level flow illustrates the **operational architecture** of the CART algorithm, mapping out the interaction between the splitting module, impurity computation, recursive partitioning, and overfitting control.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Training Dataset D] --> B[Place D at Root Node]
    B --> C{Is Stopping Condition Met?}
    C -- "Yes (Pure/MinSamples/MaxDepth)" --> D[Assign Leaf: Majority Class or Mean Y]
    C -- "No" --> E[Enumerate All Candidate Binary Splits]
    E --> F[Compute Gini Impurity or Variance for Each Split]
    F --> G[Compute Gain for Each Split]
    G --> H[Select Split with Maximum Gain]
    H --> I[Partition D into D_Left and D_Right]
    I --> J[Recurse on D_Left]
    I --> K[Recurse on D_Right]
    J --> C
    K --> C
    D --> L[Tree Construction Complete]
    L --> M{Overfitting Detected?}
    M -- "Yes" --> N[Apply Pre-Pruning or Post-Pruning]
    M -- "No" --> O[Deploy Tree for Prediction]
    N --> O
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reading the Diagram:** This is a closed-loop recursive architecture. The two "Recurse" arrows loop back to the stopping condition check, indicating the **recursive nature** of CART. The terminal "Apply Pruning" block represents the **overfitting remediation stage**, which can be invoked as either **pre-pruning** (during construction) or **post-pruning** (after full tree is built).

---

## 4.2 Schematic of a Constructed Decision Tree

The following diagram illustrates a typical binary decision tree produced by CART for the classical "Play Tennis" style dataset.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Root: Is X1 <= 5.5?] -->|Yes| B[Left Child: Class = +]
    A -->|No| C[Right Child: Class = -]
    B --> B1[Leaf Node 1: Purity = 100 percent]
    C --> C1[Leaf Node 2: Purity = 100 percent]
    style B1 fill:#90EE90
    style C1 fill:#FFB6C1
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Visual Interpretation:** Green-tinted leaf represents a **pure node** (homogeneous class) on the left, while the pink-tinted leaf represents a **pure node** for the opposite class on the right. In real-world datasets, leaves are rarely perfectly pure; they often contain small impurity percentages.

---

## 4.3 The Bias-Variance Tradeoff in Tree Depth

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Shallow Tree] -->|Underfit| B[High Bias, Low Variance]
    C[Optimal Tree] -->|Balanced| D[Low Bias, Low Variance]
    E[Deep Tree] -->|Overfit| F[Low Bias, High Variance]
    style A fill:#FFB6C1
    style C fill:#90EE90
    style E fill:#FFB6C1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Engineering Design Rule:** The optimal model is the **C point** in the diagram. A well-engineered CART model sits in the green region — not too shallow (underfitting), not too deep (overfitting).

---

## 4.4 Overfitting Remediation Strategies: Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Overfitting Symptoms: High Train Acc, Low Test Acc] --> B{Choose Pruning Strategy}
    B -- "Option 1" --> C[Pre-Pruning: Stop Early]
    B -- "Option 2" --> D[Post-Pruning: Grow Full Tree, then Prune]
    C --> C1[Set max_depth limit]
    C --> C2[Set min_samples_split threshold]
    C --> C3[Set min_impurity_decrease threshold]
    D --> D1[Apply Cost-Complexity Pruning]
    D --> D2[Reduced Error Pruning]
    D --> D3[Validate on Holdout Set]
    style A fill:#FFB6C1
    style B fill:#FFD700
    style C fill:#87CEEB
    style D fill:#87CEEB
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Strategic Insight:** Pre-pruning is computationally cheaper but can stop too early (underfitting). Post-pruning is computationally expensive but generally produces better trees because it evaluates the full structure first.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Short-Answer
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**  
> *Define the Gini Impurity index used in the CART algorithm. How does it differ from the Entropy-based splitting criterion used in ID3?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Gini Impurity Definition (1.5 Marks):**  
The Gini Impurity at a node $\mathcal{D}$ measures the probability of **misclassifying** a randomly chosen sample from that node if it were labeled according to the class distribution within the node. Mathematically:

$$G(\mathcal{D}) = 1 - \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c^2$$

where $p_c$ is the proportion of class $c$ samples in the node. A Gini value of **0** indicates a perfectly pure node, while a value of $1 - 1/C$ indicates maximum impurity.

**Difference from Entropy (1.5 Marks):**

| Aspect | Gini (CART) | Entropy (ID3) |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $1 - \sum p_c^2$ | $-\sum p_c \log_2 p_c$ |
| Computation | Fast (squared probabilities) | Slower (logarithmic) |
| Output Tree | Tends to favor larger partitions | Can produce more irregular trees |
| Empirical Result | Nearly identical to Entropy | Nearly identical to Gini |

---

### Question 2: Conceptual Short-Answer
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**  
> *Briefly explain the concept of overfitting in decision trees. Mention one symptom and one remedy.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**  
Overfitting occurs when a decision tree grows too complex and begins to capture the **noise and random fluctuations** present in the training data, rather than the underlying generalizable patterns. As a result, the model performs exceptionally well on training data but fails to generalize to unseen test data.

**Symptom (1 Mark):**  
A **widening gap between training accuracy and testing accuracy** — the tree achieves near 100% training accuracy while testing accuracy stagnates or decreases. This is a classic high-variance signature.

**Remedy (1 Mark):**  
**Pre-pruning** (also called early stopping) by setting a maximum tree depth limit `max_depth` or a minimum number of samples required to split `min_samples_split`. Alternatively, **post-pruning** using cost-complexity pruning (CART's standard method) can be applied after the full tree is constructed.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**, each carrying **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**  
> *Consider the following binary classification dataset. Build a CART decision tree using the Gini impurity index.*

| $X_1$ | $X_2$ | Class |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 2 | 3 | A |
| 3 | 4 | A |
| 4 | 5 | A |
| 5 | 6 | A |
| 6 | 7 | B |
| 7 | 6 | B |
| 8 | 5 | B |
| 9 | 4 | B |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Root Node Gini (1 Mark):**  
8 samples, 4 of class A, 4 of class B.

$$G(\text{Root}) = 1 - \left(\frac{4}{8}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{4}{8}\right)^2 = 1 - 0.25 - 0.25 = 0.50$$

**Step 2: Test Split at $X_1 \leq 5.5$ (2 Marks):**  
- Left ($X_1 \in \{2,3,4,5\}$): 4 A's, 0 B's
- Right ($X_1 \in \{6,7,8,9\}$): 0 A's, 4 B's

$$G(\mathcal{D}_L) = 1 - 1^2 - 0^2 = 0, \quad G(\mathcal{D}_R) = 1 - 0^2 - 1^2 = 0$$

**Step 3: Compute Gini Gain (1 Mark):**

$$\Delta G = 0.50 - \frac{4}{8}(0) - \frac{4}{8}(0) = 0.50$$

**Step 4: Test Split at $X_2 \leq 5.5$ for Comparison (1.5 Marks):**  
- Left ($X_2 \in \{3,4,5\}$): 3 A's, 1 B
- Right ($X_2 \in \{6,7,6,5\}$): 1 A, 3 B's

$$G(\mathcal{D}_L) = 1 - \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^2 = 1 - 0.5625 - 0.0625 = 0.375$$

$$G(\mathcal{D}_R) = 1 - \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^2 = 0.375$$

$$\Delta G_{X_2} = 0.50 - 0.5(0.375) - 0.5(0.375) = 0.50 - 0.375 = 0.125$$

**Step 5: Decision (1.5 Marks):**  
Since $\Delta G_{X_1} = 0.50 > \Delta G_{X_2} = 0.125$, the optimal root split is **$X_1 \leq 5.5$**. Both children are pure, so the tree terminates at depth 1. Left leaf → Class A, Right leaf → Class B.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> *Explain the concept of overfitting in CART. Describe the cost-complexity pruning algorithm used to mitigate it.*

**Model Solution:**

**Overfitting Explanation (3 Marks):**  
Overfitting in CART occurs when the algorithm continues to split nodes until it perfectly memorizes the training data, including its noise. The resulting tree has excessive depth, high variance, and poor generalization. An overfit model shows:
- Training accuracy approaching 100%
- Validation/test accuracy that is significantly lower and even declining
- Sensitivity to small perturbations in the training data

**Cost-Complexity Pruning Algorithm (4 Marks):**

The cost-complexity criterion (also called weakest-link pruning) is defined as:

$$R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$$

where:
- $R(T)$ is the **misclassification rate** (or total impurity) of tree $T$
- $\vert T \vert$ is the **number of terminal nodes (leaves)** in tree $T$
- $\alpha \geq 0$ is a **complexity parameter** that penalizes tree size

**The Algorithm:**

1. **Step 1:** Grow a very large tree $T_{\max}$ until all leaves are pure (full training fit).
2. **Step 2:** For each internal node $t$, compute the effective alpha at which collapsing the subtree rooted at $t$ becomes beneficial:
   
   $$\alpha_{\text{eff}}(t) = \frac{R(t) - R(T_t)}{\vert T_t \vert - 1}$$
   
   where $R(t)$ is the impurity of node $t$ as a single leaf, and $R(T_t)$ is the total impurity of the subtree $T_t$ rooted at $t$.
3. **Step 3:** Identify the node with the **smallest** $\alpha_{\text{eff}}$ and collapse that subtree into a single leaf.
4. **Step 4:** Repeat Steps 2-3, increasing $\alpha$ each iteration, generating a sequence of trees $T_{\max} \supset T_1 \supset T_2 \supset \ldots \supset \{ \text{root} \}$.
5. **Step 5:** Use **cross-validation** to select the optimal $\alpha^*$ that minimizes validation error.

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently **confuse Gini Gain with Information Gain**. Remember: **CART uses Gini** (not entropy) for split selection. Additionally, in cost-complexity pruning, students often forget that $\alpha$ is **tuned via cross-validation**, not arbitrarily chosen. Skipping this step leads to **2-mark deductions**.

---

### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**  
> *Explain the CART algorithm for regression. How does the splitting criterion differ from classification? Illustrate with a small example involving 6 data points.*

**Model Solution:**

**CART Regression Overview (3 Marks):**  
In CART regression, the target variable $Y$ is **continuous**. The algorithm still constructs a binary tree, but:
- The **splitting criterion** is the **reduction in variance** (or equivalently, reduction in Sum of Squared Errors, SSE).
- The **leaf prediction** is the **mean** of the target values in that leaf.

**Splitting Criterion (2 Marks):**  
For a candidate split dividing node $\mathcal{D}$ into $\mathcal{D}_L$ and $\mathcal{D}_R$:

$$\Delta \text{Var} = \text{Var}(\mathcal{D}) - \frac{N_L}{N}\text{Var}(\mathcal{D}_L) - \frac{N_R}{N}\text{Var}(\mathcal{D}_R)$$

The split that **maximizes** $\Delta \text{Var}$ is selected.

**Worked Example (2 Marks):**  
Consider 6 points: $(1,2), (2,4), (3,6), (4,8), (5,10), (6,12)$.

- Root mean: $\bar{Y} = 42/6 = 7$
- Root variance: $SSE = (2-7)^2 + (4-7)^2 + \ldots + (12-7)^2 = 70$, so $\text{Var} = 70/6 \approx 11.67$

Testing split at $X \leq 3.5$:  
- Left: $(1,2), (2,4), (3,6)$ → mean = 4, $SSE_L = 8$  
- Right: $(4,8), (5,10), (6,12)$ → mean = 10, $SSE_R = 8$

$$\Delta \text{Var} = 11.67 - 0.5(8/3) - 0.5(8/3) = 11.67 - 2.67 = 9.0$$

The tree splits here. Left leaf predicts **4**, right leaf predicts **10**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> *Discuss generalization vs. overfitting in decision trees. How can pre-pruning and post-pruning techniques be used to control tree complexity?*

**Model Solution:**

**Generalization (2 Marks):**  
Generalization refers to a model's ability to perform well on **unseen data** drawn from the same distribution as the training data. A well-generalized model captures the **true underlying pattern** without being misled by noise.

**The Overfitting Crisis in Trees (2 Marks):**  
Without constraints, CART will continue splitting until every leaf contains exactly one training sample, resulting in a tree of arbitrary depth. This "memorization tree" will have **zero training error** but extremely **high test error** because:
- Random noise is treated as a meaningful pattern.
- The decision boundaries become hyper-specific to the training set.
- Small input changes produce drastically different predictions (high variance).

**Pre-Pruning Techniques (1.5 Marks):**
- **`max_depth`:** Limit the maximum depth of the tree.
- **`min_samples_split`:** Require a minimum number of samples in a node to allow splitting (e.g., 20).
- **`min_samples_leaf`:** Require a minimum number of samples in each leaf.
- **`min_impurity_decrease`:** Halt splitting if the gain falls below a threshold.

**Post-Pruning Techniques (1.5 Marks):**
- **Cost-Complexity Pruning (CART's method):** Grow a large tree, then collapse subtrees that increase the cost-complexity metric $R_\alpha(T)$. Use cross-validation to choose $\alpha$.
- **Reduced Error Pruning:** Prune a node if doing so does not worsen validation accuracy.
- **Minimum Description Length (MDL) Pruning:** Prune based on information-theoretic criteria.

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often present pre-pruning and post-pruning as **interchangeable**, but they have fundamentally different philosophies. **Pre-pruning is "eager" (decides early using greedy heuristics)** while **post-pruning is "lazy but reliable" (examines the full tree before deciding)**. In KTU exams, this distinction is worth **at least 1 mark** and is frequently tested.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Module 2 — CART & Overfitting: Quick-Fire Revision Points**

### Core Concepts
- [x] **CART** is a **binary** decision tree algorithm for both **classification and regression**.
- [x] CART was introduced by **Breiman, Friedman, Olshen, and Stone (1984)**.
- [x] **Classification criterion:** **Gini Impurity** (default); CART can also use Entropy/Twoing.
- [x] **Regression criterion:** **Reduction in Variance** (minimizes within-node SSE).
- [x] **Leaf prediction (Classification):** **Majority class**; **Leaf prediction (Regression):** **Mean of targets**.

### Essential Formulas
- [x] Gini: $G = 1 - \sum p_c^2$
- [x] Gini Gain: $\Delta G = G(\mathcal{D}) - \frac{N_L}{N}G(\mathcal{D}_L) - \frac{N_R}{N}G(\mathcal{D}_R)$
- [x] Variance Reduction: $\Delta \text{Var} = \text{Var}(\mathcal{D}) - \frac{N_L}{N}\text{Var}(\mathcal{D}_L) - \frac{N_R}{N}\text{Var}(\mathcal{D}_R)$
- [x] Cost-Complexity: $R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$

### Overfitting Mastery
- [x] **Overfitting** = model memorizes training noise; fails on test data.
- [x] **Symptoms:** High training accuracy + low test accuracy + high variance.
- [x] **Pre-Pruning:** Stop tree growth early using `max_depth`, `min_samples_split`, etc.
- [x] **Post-Pruning:** Grow full tree, then collapse weak subtrees (e.g., cost-complexity pruning).
- [x] **Bias-Variance Tradeoff:** Deeper trees → lower bias, higher variance; optimal depth balances both.

### High-Yield KTU Exam Traps
- [x] **Don't confuse CART's Gini with ID3's Entropy** — these are different algorithms with different criteria.
- [x] **CART is strictly binary** — unlike ID3/C4.5, it never produces multi-way splits.
- [x] **For regression trees, leaf prediction is the mean**, not the median or mode.
- [x] **Cost-complexity parameter $\alpha$ is selected via cross-validation**, not arbitrarily.
- [x] **Pre-pruning risks underfitting** (stopping too early); post-pruning is safer but slower.

### Recommended Practice Problems for KTU 2024
- [x] Compute Gini impurity for a 3-class problem with arbitrary distributions.
- [x] Build a full CART tree for a 10-sample dataset (paper-based).
- [x] Compare pre-pruning vs. post-pruning for a depth-10 overfit tree.
- [x] Compute variance reduction for a regression tree split.
- [x] Trace the cost-complexity pruning sequence for a 4-leaf tree.

> **End of KTU Module 2 Notes — CART & Overfitting**  
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme PBAIT404 — Foundation of Machine Learning*
