topics.title: LASSO and RIDGE regularization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAIT404 - FOUNDATION OF MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** LASSO and RIDGE regularization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Regularization

**Regularization** is a statistical technique used in machine learning to prevent **overfitting** by introducing a penalty term to the model's loss function that constrains the magnitude of the model parameters. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, regularization is essential when the model suffers from high variance due to multicollinearity, high-dimensional feature spaces, or insufficient training samples.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *Regularization is a technique used to address the problem of overfitting in machine learning models by adding a penalty term to the cost function that discourages overly complex models with large parameter magnitudes.*

## 1.2 LASSO Regression (L1 Regularization)

**LASSO (Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator)** is a linear regression technique that performs both **regularization** and **feature selection** by adding a penalty proportional to the **absolute value** of the coefficient magnitudes.

$$J_{LASSO}(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_i - \hat{y}_i\right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert$$

Where:
- $J_{LASSO}(\beta)$ = LASSO cost function
- $\lambda$ = regularization hyperparameter ($\lambda \geq 0$)
- $\beta_j$ = regression coefficient of $j^{th}$ feature

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** LASSO can drive coefficients to **exactly zero**, effectively performing **automatic feature selection** and producing a **sparse model**. This is the most frequently tested LASSO property in KTU exams.

## 1.3 RIDGE Regression (L2 Regularization)

**RIDGE Regression** is a regularized linear regression technique that adds a penalty proportional to the **square of the magnitude** of the coefficients, shrinking them toward zero but never setting them exactly to zero.

$$J_{RIDGE}(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_i - \hat{y}_i\right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** Ridge uses the **L2 norm** (Euclidean norm) of the coefficient vector. It is also called **Tikhonov Regularization** in classical statistics.

## 1.4 Real-World Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Mountain Trek":**
> Imagine you are trekking down a mountain (minimizing loss) on a foggy day (noisy/limited data). Without restrictions, you might follow a wild zigzag path into a small ditch (overfitting to noise). 
> - **RIDGE** = your backpack has a heavy rock weighing you down — you walk steadily in a straight line (small coefficients, all features retained).
> - **LASSO** = you are allowed to abandon heavy items from your backpack entirely — you may drop some trails (features) altogether (zero coefficients).
> - The **weight of the rock ($\lambda$)** is the regularization strength — heavier rock = simpler, more biased model.

## 1.5 Why Regularization is Needed (KTU High-Yield)

- **Overfitting Control:** Multicollinearity in $X^TX$ causes unstable coefficient estimates.
- **Generalization:** A simpler model with smaller coefficients generalizes better to unseen data.
- **Ill-Conditioning:** When $X^TX$ is near-singular, the inverse becomes numerically unstable.
- **Curse of Dimensionality:** When $p \approx n$ or $p > n$, OLS has no unique solution.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation — OLS Recap

The Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) cost function is:

$$J_{OLS}(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j x_{ij}\right)^2$$

In matrix form:

$$J_{OLS}(\beta) = (y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta)$$

The closed-form OLS solution (which is the **baseline** in all regularization problems) is:

$$\hat{\beta}_{OLS} = (X^TX)^{-1}X^Ty$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that OLS fails when $X^TX$ is **non-invertible** (singular). This happens when features are **linearly dependent** (multicollinearity) or when $p > n$. Regularization is the **mathematical fix** for this.

## 2.2 RIDGE Regression — Constrained Optimization View

The Ridge problem can be written in **two equivalent forms**:

### Lagrangian (Penalty) Form:
$$J_{RIDGE}(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j^2$$

### Constrained Form:
$$\min_{\beta} \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j^2 \leq s$$

Both are equivalent via the **Lagrange multiplier duality**.

### Closed-Form Solution:

$$\hat{\beta}_{RIDGE} = (X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** Adding $\lambda I$ to $X^TX$ guarantees invertibility even when $X^TX$ is singular. This is the **mathematical guarantee** of Ridge regression.

## 2.3 LASSO Regression — Constrained Optimization View

### Lagrangian (Penalty) Form:
$$J_{LASSO}(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert$$

### Constrained Form:
$$\min_{\beta} \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert \leq s$$

> [!NOTE]
> **No closed-form solution exists for LASSO** because the absolute value function is non-differentiable at zero. LASSO is solved iteratively using **Coordinate Descent**, **LARS** (Least Angle Regression), or **Subgradient methods**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **KTU Exam Relevance** |
|---|---|---|
| OLS Cost | $J = (y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta)$ | Baseline loss, must be memorized |
| OLS Solution | $\hat{\beta} = (X^TX)^{-1}X^Ty$ | Closed-form, used for comparison |
| Ridge Cost | $J = (y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta) + \lambda \vert \vert \beta \vert \vert_2^2$ | Direct 7-mark derivation question |
| Ridge Solution | $\hat{\beta} = (X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty$ | Most-tested formula in KTU |
| LASSO Cost | $J = (y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta) + \lambda \vert \vert \beta \vert \vert_1$ | Direct 7-mark question |
| LASSO Solution | No closed form — iterative methods | Frequently asked as "explain why" |
| Elastic Net | $J = (y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta) + \lambda_1 \vert \vert \beta \vert \vert_1 + \lambda_2 \vert \vert \beta \vert \vert_2^2$ | Comparative question |
| L1 Norm | $\vert \vert \beta \vert \vert_1 = \sum_j \vert \beta_j \vert$ | Used in LASSO penalty |
| L2 Norm | $\vert \vert \beta \vert \vert_2 = \sqrt{\sum_j \beta_j^2}$ | Used in Ridge penalty |
| Degrees of Freedom (Ridge) | $df(\lambda) = \text{tr}(X(X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^T)$ | Theoretical depth question |

> [!TIP]
> **LaTeX Isolation Rule Applied:** All absolute value notations are written as `\vert \vert \beta \vert \vert` instead of the pipe symbol to prevent breaking the markdown table.

## 2.5 The Role of the Regularization Parameter $\lambda$

| **Value of $\lambda$** | **Effect on Model** |
|---|---|
| $\lambda = 0$ | Recovers OLS — no regularization |
| $\lambda \to 0^+$ | Model nearly identical to OLS, may overfit |
| $\lambda$ moderate | Balanced bias-variance tradeoff — best generalization |
| $\lambda \to \infty$ | All $\beta_j \to 0$ — model predicts the mean (underfitting) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimal $\lambda$** is selected via **Cross-Validation (k-fold)**, **AIC/BIC**, or **Generalized Cross-Validation (GCV)**. This is a frequent KTU sub-question.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Regularization

- **Bioinformatics & Genomics:** LASSO is used to identify disease-associated genes from thousands of features with few samples.
- **Computer Vision:** Ridge regularization stabilizes regression in face recognition systems.
- **Econometrics & Finance:** Ridge handles multicollinear macroeconomic indicators.
- **Signal Processing:** Tikhonov regularization (Ridge) is the foundation of inverse problem solving.
- **Natural Language Processing:** LASSO is used in high-dimensional text classification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Ridge Regression Closed-Form Solution

### Starting Point:
$$J_{RIDGE}(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - x_i^T\beta)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j^2$$

### Step 1: Express in Matrix Form
$$J_{RIDGE}(\beta) = (y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta) + \lambda \beta^T\beta$$

The L2 penalty $\sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j^2$ is written compactly as the dot product $\beta^T\beta$.

### Step 2: Expand the Squared Term
$$\begin{aligned}
(y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta) &= y^Ty - y^TX\beta - (X\beta)^Ty + (X\beta)^T(X\beta) \\
&= y^Ty - 2\beta^TX^Ty + \beta^TX^TX\beta
\end{aligned}$$

We used the identity $(X\beta)^T y = \beta^T X^T y = y^T X \beta$ (scalar) so the cross-terms combine to $-2\beta^T X^T y$.

### Step 3: Substitute Back into $J_{RIDGE}$
$$\begin{aligned}
J_{RIDGE}(\beta) &= y^Ty - 2\beta^TX^Ty + \beta^TX^TX\beta + \lambda \beta^T\beta \\
&= y^Ty - 2\beta^TX^Ty + \beta^T(X^TX + \lambda I)\beta
\end{aligned}$$

We factor $\beta^T(\ldots)\beta$ by combining the L2 penalty $\lambda \beta^T\beta = \beta^T(\lambda I)\beta$ with the OLS quadratic form.

### Step 4: Differentiate with Respect to $\beta$
$$\frac{\partial J_{RIDGE}}{\partial \beta} = -2X^Ty + 2(X^TX + \lambda I)\beta$$

We applied standard matrix calculus rules:
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial \beta}(\beta^TA\beta) = (A + A^T)\beta = 2A\beta$ (since $X^TX$ is symmetric).
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial \beta}(\beta^TAy) = A^Ty$ for constant $A$, $y$.

### Step 5: Set Gradient to Zero (First-Order Optimality)
$$-2X^Ty + 2(X^TX + \lambda I)\beta = 0$$

### Step 6: Solve for $\beta$
$$\begin{aligned}
(X^TX + \lambda I)\beta &= X^Ty \\
\beta &= (X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty
\end{aligned}$$

We multiplied both sides by the inverse of $(X^TX + \lambda I)$, which is **guaranteed to exist** when $\lambda > 0$ because the matrix is **strictly positive definite** under this condition.

### Final Result:
$$\boxed{\hat{\beta}_{RIDGE} = (X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Ridge Estimator Properties

### Property 1: Ridge is a Shrinkage Estimator
Define $Z = X \cdot \text{diag}(1/s_j)$ where $s_j = \sqrt{\sum_i x_{ij}^2}$. After standardizing, the Ridge estimator can be written as:

$$\hat{\beta}_{RIDGE} = (Z^TZ + \lambda I)^{-1}Z^Ty$$

The shrinkage factor for each coefficient is:
$$S_j = \frac{1}{1 + \lambda / d_j^2}$$

where $d_j^2$ is the $j^{th}$ eigenvalue of $Z^TZ$. Coefficients corresponding to **small eigenvalues** (collinear features) are shrunk more aggressively.

### Property 2: Bias-Variance Decomposition
- **Bias:** $E[\hat{\beta}_{RIDGE}] - \beta = -\lambda(X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}\beta \neq 0$ for $\lambda > 0$.
- **Variance:** $\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_{RIDGE}) = \sigma^2(X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^TX(X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}$.

The **Mean Squared Error** is:
$$\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance}$$

By introducing bias, Ridge reduces variance more than it adds bias, **lowering total MSE**.

---

## 3.3 Subgradient Derivation for LASSO

### Starting Point:
$$J_{LASSO}(\beta) = (y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta) + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert$$

### Step 1: Compute the Subgradient

The absolute value $\vert \beta_j \vert$ is non-differentiable at $\beta_j = 0$. The **subgradient** is:

$$\frac{\partial \vert \beta_j \vert}{\partial \beta_j} = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } \beta_j > 0 \\ -1 & \text{if } \beta_j < 0 \\ \in [-1, +1] & \text{if } \beta_j = 0 \end{cases}$$

### Step 2: Set the Subgradient to Zero
$$-2X^T(y - X\beta) + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\beta) = 0$$

where $\text{sign}(\beta)$ is the subgradient vector.

### Step 3: Implication — Sparsity
For the $j^{th}$ coefficient to be exactly zero, the **subgradient at zero must contain zero**:

$$\left| \left[X^T(y - X\hat{\beta})\right]_j \right| \leq \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

This is the **soft-thresholding condition** that produces sparsity in LASSO.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Ridge)

**Problem:** Given $X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$, $y = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}$, compute $\hat{\beta}_{RIDGE}$ for $\lambda = 1$.

### Step 1: Compute $X^TX$
$$X^TX = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 6 \\ 6 & 14 \end{bmatrix}$$

We multiplied row-by-column: $X^TX_{11} = 1+1+1=3$, $X^TX_{12} = 1+2+3=6$, $X^TX_{22} = 1+4+9=14$.

### Step 2: Compute $X^Ty$
$$X^Ty = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 23 \end{bmatrix}$$

Computed as: $2+3+5=10$ and $2+6+15=23$.

### Step 3: Form $(X^TX + \lambda I)$
$$X^TX + \lambda I = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 6 \\ 6 & 14 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 6 \\ 6 & 15 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Inverse
$$\det = (4)(15) - (6)(6) = 60 - 36 = 24$$

$$(X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1} = \frac{1}{24} \begin{bmatrix} 15 & -6 \\ -6 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.625 & -0.25 \\ -0.25 & 0.1667 \end{bmatrix}$$

For a $2 \times 2$ matrix $\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$, the inverse is $\frac{1}{ad-bc}\begin{bmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{bmatrix}$.

### Step 5: Multiply by $X^Ty$
$$\hat{\beta}_{RIDGE} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.625 & -0.25 \\ -0.25 & 0.1667 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 23 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.625 \times 10 + (-0.25) \times 23 \\ (-0.25) \times 10 + 0.1667 \times 23 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 1.333 \end{bmatrix}$$

Computed as: $(0.625)(10) - 0.25(23) = 6.25 - 5.75 = 0.5$, and $-0.25(10) + 0.1667(23) = -2.5 + 3.833 = 1.333$.

### Final Answer:
$$\boxed{\hat{\beta}_{RIDGE} = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{\beta}_0 \\ \hat{\beta}_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 1.333 \end{bmatrix}}$$

The model is $\hat{y} = 0.5 + 1.333 x$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Regularization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Training Data X, y] --> B[Data Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Standardize Features]
    C --> D{Choose Regularization Type}
    D -->|L1 Penalty| E[LASSO Regression]
    D -->|L2 Penalty| F[RIDGE Regression]
    D -->|L1 and L2| G[Elastic Net]
    E --> H[Cross Validation for lambda]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Optimal lambda Selection]
    I --> J[Train Final Model]
    J --> K[Evaluate on Test Set]
    K --> L{Generalization Acceptable?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Deploy Model]
    L -->|No| D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The pipeline begins with raw data, applies standardization (essential for regularization), branches into the three regularization families, and feeds into a hyperparameter-tuning loop driven by cross-validation before deployment.

## 4.2 Geometric Interpretation — L1 vs L2 Constraint Regions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph geom [Geometric Comparison of Constraints]
        direction LR
        L1[L1 Ball Diamond Shape] -->|"corners touch axes"| S1[Sparse Solution at Vertex]
        L2[L2 Ball Circular Shape] -->|"touches circle tangentially"| S2[Non Sparse Interior Solution]
    end
    ELL[Elliptical RSS Contours] --> L1
    ELL --> L2
    ELL -.->|First contact point| O[Optimum]
    S1 --> O
    S2 --> O
```

> [!TIP]
> **Geometric Intuition:** The RSS (residual sum of squares) contours are **elliptical** and concentric. The constraint region is a **diamond (L1)** or **circle (L2)**. The first point of contact is the optimum. For L1, contact happens at a **corner (axis intersection)** → coefficient = 0. For L2, contact is **tangential on the smooth boundary** → coefficient is small but nonzero.

## 4.3 Coefficient Shrinkage Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start lambda = 0 OLS Estimates] --> Q[Increase lambda Gradually]
    Q --> R{Regularization Type}
    R -->|RIDGE L2| S1[Coefficients Shrink Smoothly]
    R -->|LASSO L1| S2[Coefficients Hit Zero and Stay Zero]
    S1 --> T1[All Features Retained]
    S2 --> T2[Some Features Eliminated]
    T1 --> U[End lambda infinity All beta to 0]
    T2 --> U
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visualization:** This flowchart captures the **LASSO shrinkage path** where coefficients become exactly zero (sparse path) versus the **Ridge shrinkage path** where coefficients shrink smoothly toward zero but never reach it.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Cost Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DATA[Input Data X y] --> PRED[Prediction Module y hat = X beta]
    PRED --> RES[Residual y minus y hat]
    RES --> SQ[Squared Error Sum SSE]
    SQ --> ADD[Adder combining loss and penalty]
    PEN[Regularization Penalty lambda times norm] --> ADD
    ADD --> OUT[Total Cost J beta]
    OUT --> OPT[Optimizer Gradient Descent or Coordinate Descent]
    OPT --> UPD[Update Coefficients beta]
    UPD --> PRED
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**Differentiate between LASSO and Ridge regression with respect to their penalty terms and feature selection capability.**

**Model Answer:**

LASSO (Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator) and Ridge are both regularized linear regression techniques but differ fundamentally in the **norm used for the penalty term** and the resulting **sparsity behavior**.

| **Aspect** | **LASSO (L1)** | **Ridge (L2)** |
|---|---|---|
| Penalty Term | $\lambda \sum_j \vert \beta_j \vert$ | $\lambda \sum_j \beta_j^2$ |
| Geometric Shape | Diamond (corners on axes) | Circle (smooth) |
| Coefficient Behavior | Can become **exactly zero** | Shrinks but **never zero** |
| Feature Selection | **Yes — built-in** | No — retains all features |
| Closed-form | **No** — solved via coordinate descent | **Yes** — $(X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty$ |
| Best Use Case | High-dimensional sparse data | Multicollinearity without selection need |

In KTU 2024 Scheme, the L1 penalty produces **sparse models** that automatically eliminate irrelevant features, while L2 only **shrinks** coefficients to small values, retaining all features in the model. LASSO is preferred when **interpretability via feature selection** is the goal.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]
**What is the role of the regularization parameter $\lambda$? Explain the effect of $\lambda = 0$ and $\lambda \to \infty$.**

**Model Answer:**

The regularization parameter $\lambda \geq 0$ controls the **strength of the penalty** applied to the model coefficients. It governs the **bias-variance tradeoff**.

- When $\lambda = 0$: The penalty term vanishes, and the model **recovers the standard Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) solution** $\hat{\beta} = (X^TX)^{-1}X^Ty$. The model has **high variance** and may overfit, especially when $p$ is large or features are collinear.
- When $\lambda \to \infty$: The penalty dominates, and all coefficients are **forced to zero** $\beta_j \to 0$. The model predicts a constant value (the mean of $y$), resulting in **high bias** and underfitting.

An **optimal intermediate $\lambda$** balances bias and variance, minimizing the expected prediction error on unseen data. The optimal value is found via **k-fold cross-validation** by minimizing the validation MSE over a grid of candidate $\lambda$ values.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the closed-form solution for Ridge Regression from the regularized cost function. Show every step of the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of the L2 penalty in reducing multicollinearity. How does Ridge handle the case when $X^TX$ is singular? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1: Write the Ridge Cost Function.**

The Ridge regression cost function combines the standard OLS loss with the L2 penalty:

$$J_{RIDGE}(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - x_i^T\beta)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j^2$$

**Step 2: Convert to Matrix Form.**

Using $\beta^T\beta = \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j^2$ and $(y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - x_i^T\beta)^2$:

$$J_{RIDGE}(\beta) = (y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta) + \lambda \beta^T\beta$$

**Step 3: Expand the Squared Term.**

We use the matrix expansion identity:

$$(y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta) = y^Ty - 2\beta^TX^Ty + \beta^TX^TX\beta$$

The cross-term simplifies because both $X^Ty$ and $\beta^TX^Ty$ are scalars: $y^TX\beta = \beta^TX^Ty = (X^Ty)^T\beta$.

**Step 4: Substitute Back into $J_{RIDGE}$.**

$$J_{RIDGE}(\beta) = y^Ty - 2\beta^TX^Ty + \beta^TX^TX\beta + \lambda \beta^T\beta$$

Factoring out $\beta^T(\ldots)\beta$:

$$J_{RIDGE}(\beta) = y^Ty - 2\beta^TX^Ty + \beta^T(X^TX + \lambda I)\beta$$

**Step 5: Differentiate with Respect to $\beta$.**

Using standard matrix calculus:

$$\frac{\partial J_{RIDGE}}{\partial \beta} = -2X^Ty + 2(X^TX + \lambda I)\beta$$

**Step 6: Set Gradient to Zero (Stationary Point Condition).**

For an interior minimum:

$$-2X^Ty + 2(X^TX + \lambda I)\beta = 0$$

**Step 7: Solve for $\beta$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
(X^TX + \lambda I)\beta &= X^Ty \\
\hat{\beta}_{RIDGE} &= (X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\hat{\beta}_{RIDGE} = (X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Existence of the Inverse:** Since $\lambda > 0$, the matrix $(X^TX + \lambda I)$ is **strictly positive definite** (all eigenvalues $\geq \lambda > 0$), guaranteeing that the inverse exists. This is the **key mathematical reason** Ridge is well-defined even when OLS is not.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Role of L2 Penalty in Reducing Multicollinearity:**

Multicollinearity occurs when two or more predictor variables in $X$ are **highly linearly correlated**. The consequence is that the matrix $X^TX$ has one or more eigenvalues close to zero, making it **ill-conditioned**. This causes:

1. **Inflated coefficient variances** — coefficients become unstable and large in magnitude.
2. **Numerical instability** — small changes in data cause large changes in $\hat{\beta}$.
3. **Loss of interpretability** — signs of coefficients may flip unexpectedly.

The L2 penalty $\lambda \beta^T\beta$ **adds $\lambda$ to every diagonal element of $X^TX$**, which:
- **Lifts small eigenvalues** by $\lambda$, improving the condition number $\kappa(X^TX + \lambda I) \ll \kappa(X^TX)$.
- **Shrinks coefficient magnitudes** toward zero, reducing the variance of $\hat{\beta}$.
- **Distributes the influence** among correlated features instead of assigning arbitrary large weights to one.

**Handling of the Singular Case:**

When $X^TX$ is **singular** (i.e., at least one eigenvalue is exactly zero), OLS has **no unique solution** because $(X^TX)^{-1}$ does not exist. This occurs when:
- Features are **perfectly linearly dependent** (e.g., $x_2 = 2 x_1$).
- The number of features **exceeds** the number of samples ($p > n$), so $X^TX$ is rank-deficient.

Ridge Regression resolves this elegantly. By adding $\lambda I$, the matrix becomes:

$$X^TX + \lambda I$$

Since $\lambda > 0$, every eigenvalue of $X^TX$ is **shifted by $\lambda > 0$**, and previously zero eigenvalues become $\lambda$. The resulting matrix is **strictly positive definite** with full rank, ensuring the **existence and uniqueness** of the Ridge estimator:

$$\hat{\beta}_{RIDGE} = (X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty$$

This makes Ridge regression a **mathematically well-posed** solution even in degenerate cases where OLS fails completely.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Explain LASSO Regression in detail. Why does LASSO produce sparse solutions while Ridge does not? Justify with the subgradient condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dataset has $n = 50$ observations and $p = 80$ features. OLS cannot be applied directly. Explain which regularization technique is more appropriate and justify your choice with mathematical reasoning. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Explanation of LASSO Regression:**

LASSO (Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator), introduced by **Tibshirani (1996)**, is a regularized regression method that adds the **L1 norm** of the coefficient vector as a penalty to the OLS loss:

$$J_{LASSO}(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert$$

The L1 penalty $\sum_j \vert \beta_j \vert$ is non-differentiable at zero but convex elsewhere, making the optimization problem **convex** with a unique global minimum.

**Why LASSO Produces Sparse Solutions (Subgradient Justification):**

The optimality condition for LASSO uses the **subgradient** because $\vert \beta_j \vert$ is non-differentiable at $\beta_j = 0$:

$$-2X^T(y - X\hat{\beta}) + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\hat{\beta}) = 0$$

where:

$$\text{sign}(\beta_j) = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } \beta_j > 0 \\ -1 & \text{if } \beta_j < 0 \\ \in [-1, +1] & \text{if } \beta_j = 0 \end{cases}$$

The **sparsity condition** arises from analyzing the case $\hat{\beta}_j = 0$. Setting $\hat{\beta}_j = 0$ in the optimality condition:

$$\left| \left[X^T(y - X\hat{\beta})\right]_j \right| \leq \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

If the **partial residual correlation** of feature $j$ (left side) is less than or equal to $\lambda/2$, then setting $\hat{\beta}_j = 0$ satisfies the optimality condition. This means **irrelevant features** (with small residual correlation) are **automatically eliminated**.

**Comparison with Ridge:**

For Ridge, the optimality condition is:

$$(X^TX + \lambda I)\hat{\beta} = X^Ty$$

Since $\lambda I$ is added, the system is **never singular**, and the solution is:

$$\hat{\beta}_{RIDGE} = (X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty$$

For **any positive $\lambda$**, all components of $\hat{\beta}_{RIDGE}$ are **non-zero in general** because the matrix inverse produces a smooth, continuous transformation. Ridge **shrinks** coefficients toward zero but never **forces** them to be exactly zero.

**Geometric Reason:**

The L1 constraint region is a **diamond** with corners lying on the coordinate axes. The elliptical RSS contours first contact the constraint at a **corner** (where one or more $\beta_j = 0$). In contrast, the L2 constraint is a **circle** with no corners, so contact happens at a **tangential interior point** where all $\beta_j \neq 0$.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Analysis of the Problem:**

Given:
- Number of observations: $n = 50$
- Number of features: $p = 80$
- $p > n$ (more features than samples)

**Why OLS Fails:**

The OLS estimator requires $(X^TX)^{-1}$ to exist. The matrix $X^TX$ is of size $p \times p = 80 \times 80$, but its rank is at most $\min(n, p) = 50$. Therefore, $X^TX$ is **rank-deficient** (rank 50, not full rank 80), and the inverse **does not exist**. OLS has **infinitely many solutions**, and the system is **underdetermined**.

**Most Appropriate Regularization: LASSO (L1 Regularization)**

**Mathematical Justification:**

**Reason 1 — Sparse Solutions Needed:**

With $p = 80$ features but only $n = 50$ observations, the model is **highly overparameterized**. Most features are likely irrelevant or redundant. LASSO's L1 penalty drives many $\beta_j = 0$, automatically selecting only the **most informative features**. This is critical in high-dimensional low-sample (HDLSS) regimes.

**Reason 2 — Feature Selection Capability:**

LASSO performs **simultaneous regularization and feature selection**. The L1 subgradient condition:

$$\left| \left[X^T(y - X\hat{\beta})\right]_j \right| \leq \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

ensures that features with weak residual correlation are eliminated, yielding a **parsimonious model** with $k \ll p$ nonzero coefficients, where $k$ is chosen by cross-validation.

**Reason 3 — Ridge Cannot Reduce Dimensionality:**

Ridge can compute $\hat{\beta}_{RIDGE} = (X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty$ because $X^TX + \lambda I$ is now full rank (eigenvalues lifted by $\lambda$). However, Ridge **retains all 80 features** with small but nonzero coefficients, which:
- Increases **model complexity**.
- Reduces **interpretability**.
- May **overfit** in the $p > n$ regime.

**Conclusion:**

LASSO is the more appropriate technique for this $p > n$ scenario because it produces a **sparse, interpretable, low-dimensional model** that automatically performs **feature selection**, which is essential when the number of features exceeds the number of samples.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly claim that Ridge can handle $p > n$ "just as well" as LASSO. While Ridge **can compute a solution** (unlike OLS), it does **not** reduce the effective number of features — every $\beta_j$ is nonzero. In high-dimensional problems where **interpretability** and **feature selection** matter, **LASSO is the correct answer**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Regularization** = adding a penalty to the loss function to prevent overfitting.
- **Ridge (L2):** Penalty is $\lambda \sum \beta_j^2$. **Closed-form** solution $\hat{\beta} = (X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty$. Shrinks but **never zeros** coefficients. **Best for multicollinearity.**
- **LASSO (L1):** Penalty is $\lambda \sum \vert \beta_j \vert$. **No closed-form**; solved by coordinate descent. Produces **sparse solutions** with **exact zeros**. **Best for feature selection** in high dimensions.
- **L2 Norm:** $\vert \vert \beta \vert \vert_2^2 = \sum \beta_j^2$. **L1 Norm:** $\vert \vert \beta \vert \vert_1 = \sum \vert \beta_j \vert$.
- **Geometric Intuition:** L1 ball is a **diamond** (corners on axes) → sparsity. L2 ball is a **circle** (smooth) → non-sparse.
- **Ridge Guarantees Invertibility:** $(X^TX + \lambda I)$ is strictly positive definite for $\lambda > 0$.
- **Subgradient Condition for LASSO Sparsity:** $\vert [X^T(y - X\hat{\beta})]_j \vert \leq \lambda/2$ ⟹ $\hat{\beta}_j = 0$.
- **Effect of $\lambda$:** $\lambda = 0$ → OLS (overfit). $\lambda \to \infty$ → zero model (underfit). **Optimal $\lambda$** found via cross-validation.
- **LASSO's Non-Differentiability** at $\beta_j = 0$ is **why no closed-form** exists.
- **Elastic Net** combines L1 + L2 with two hyperparameters $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$; useful when features are **grouped** or highly correlated.
- **Standardization** of features (zero mean, unit variance) is **mandatory** before applying Ridge or LASSO.
- **Bias-Variance Tradeoff:** Regularization **adds bias** but **reduces variance** more than the bias added, lowering total MSE.
- **Degrees of Freedom of Ridge:** $df(\lambda) = \text{tr}[X(X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^T]$, which decreases as $\lambda$ increases.
- **Use Ridge when:** Multicollinearity is present and **all features are believed to be relevant**.
- **Use LASSO when:** Feature selection is desired and **sparse models** are preferred.
- **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution:** Expect 3-mark short questions on L1 vs L2 difference, and 14-mark long questions on Ridge derivation + multicollinearity OR LASSO sparsity justification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Idea of Training
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAIT404 - FOUNDATION OF MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Idea of Training

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Training in Machine Learning**
> *Training* is the **iterative optimization process** by which a parameterized learning model $h(x;\theta)$ discovers the optimal set of internal parameters $\theta^*$ by **minimizing a loss function** $L(\cdot)$ over a finite labeled dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$. The procedure uses an optimization algorithm (e.g., **Gradient Descent**) to repeatedly update the parameters until a convergence criterion is satisfied.

In the KTU 2024 scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), training is treated as the **central learning phase** that converts a generic model architecture (a *hypothesis class*) into a problem-specific predictor. Mathematically, training solves the following **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)** problem:

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta \in \Theta} \; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L\big(h(x_i;\theta),\, y_i\big)$$

where:
- $\theta$ = the **parameter vector** (e.g., weights $w$ and bias $b$)
- $\Theta$ = the feasible parameter space
- $L$ = the per-sample loss function
- $N$ = the number of training samples
- $h(x_i;\theta)$ = the model's prediction on input $x_i$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Student and the Mock Test"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English)**
> Imagine a student preparing for the KTU Semester Exam. The student has a stack of **previous year question papers** (the *training set*). The student:
> 1. **Attempts a paper** (forward pass / prediction)
> 2. **Checks the answer key** to see how many marks were lost (loss / error)
> 3. **Identifies weak topics** and revises them (backpropagation / gradient computation)
> 4. **Tries another paper** with improved strategy (parameter update)
>
> After repeating this loop over hundreds of papers, the student becomes "trained" — the internal knowledge (parameters $\theta$) is now tuned to generalize. Training a machine learning model is **exactly the same loop** applied to numbers instead of humans.

## 1.3 Why Training is the Heart of ML

> [!TIP]
> **Three pillars of any ML training system** (must be memorized for KTU exams):
> 1. **Data** $\mathcal{D}$ — fuel for learning
> 2. **Model** $h(x;\theta)$ — the function family
> 3. **Loss + Optimizer** $(L, \text{Opt})$ — the teacher and the learning rule

Without training, the model parameters are typically initialized **randomly** (e.g., $\theta_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$), and predictions are essentially noise. Training is what converts this randomness into intelligence.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Training Pipeline — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The training procedure can be decomposed into **seven logical stages**. Each stage is mandatory and KTU examiners frequently test the student's understanding of *why* each step exists.

### Stage 1 — Data Acquisition & Splitting
The labeled dataset $\mathcal{D}$ is partitioned into:
- **Training Set** $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$ (typically **70%** of data) — used to update parameters.
- **Validation Set** $\mathcal{D}_{\text{val}}$ (typically **15%**) — used to tune *hyperparameters* (e.g., learning rate).
- **Test Set** $\mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}$ (typically **15%**) — used **only once** at the end to report final accuracy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** The test set **must never be seen** during training. Using test data for parameter updates is called *data leakage* and is the **#1 reason KTU projects lose marks**.

### Stage 2 — Model Selection
Choose a hypothesis class (Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, Neural Network, Decision Tree, etc.). Each class imposes an **inductive bias**.

### Stage 3 — Parameter Initialization
Parameters are initialized to small random values. For Neural Networks, common schemes include **Xavier** and **He initialization**:

$$w \sim \mathcal{U}\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{n_{\text{in}}}},\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{n_{\text{in}}}}\right)$$

where $n_{\text{in}}$ is the number of input neurons.

### Stage 4 — Forward Pass (Prediction)
For each input $x_i$, compute the prediction $\hat{y}_i = h(x_i; \theta)$.

### Stage 5 — Loss Computation
Compare $\hat{y}_i$ with the true label $y_i$ using a loss function $L(\hat{y}_i, y_i)$. The total cost $J(\theta)$ aggregates the loss over all training samples.

### Stage 6 — Backward Pass (Gradient Computation)
Compute the gradient of the cost with respect to every parameter:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta}$$

For deep networks, this is done via the **chain rule** (Backpropagation).

### Stage 7 — Parameter Update
Apply the optimization rule. For **Gradient Descent**:

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \, \nabla_\theta J\big(\theta^{(t)}\big)$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (a positive scalar). Repeat until convergence.

## 2.2 The Three Variants of Gradient Descent

| Variant | Update uses | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Batch GD** | Entire training set | Stable convergence | Very slow for large $N$ |
| **Stochastic GD (SGD)** | One sample per update | Fast, escapes local minima | Noisy convergence |
| **Mini-Batch GD** | A batch of size $B$ (e.g., 32, 64, 128) | Best of both worlds; GPU-friendly | Requires batch size tuning |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table. It covers ~80% of numerical questions in KTU ESE for this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Empirical Risk | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L(\hat{y}_i, y_i)$ | Dimensionless (after averaging) |
| 2 | Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Unit = (target)$^2$ |
| 3 | Mean Absolute Error (MAE) | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Same unit as target |
| 4 | Binary Cross-Entropy | $J = -\frac{1}{N} \sum \big[ y \log \hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y}) \big]$ | Used for classification |
| 5 | GD Update Rule | $\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_\theta J(\theta^{(t)})$ | $\eta$ = learning rate (e.g., 0.01) |
| 6 | SGD Update Rule | $\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_\theta L(\hat{y}_i, y_i)$ | One sample at a time |
| 7 | Momentum Update | $v^{(t+1)} = \beta v^{(t)} + \nabla J(\theta);\;\; \theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta v^{(t+1)}$ | $\beta \in [0, 1)$, typically 0.9 |
| 8 | Epoch | One full pass through $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$ | Integer count |
| 9 | Iteration | One parameter update | Integer count = $\lceil N/B \rceil$ per epoch |
| 10 | Convergence | $\vert \vert \nabla_\theta J(\theta) \vert \vert_2 < \epsilon$ | $\epsilon$ = tolerance (e.g., $10^{-6}$) |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world applications of training principles:**
> - **Computer Vision:** Training CNNs on ImageNet (millions of parameters, weeks of GPU time).
> - **Natural Language Processing:** Fine-tuning BERT/GPT on domain-specific corpora.
> - **Healthcare:** Training diagnostic models on medical imaging — *training data quality directly determines patient safety*.
> - **Finance:** Training fraud detection models on transactional data.
> - **Autonomous Vehicles:** End-to-end training of self-driving policies.

The same mathematical loop — *predict → measure → correct → repeat* — powers all of these systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Gradient Descent Update Rule from First Principles

We begin with the goal: find $\theta^*$ that minimizes $J(\theta)$. Consider a single scalar parameter $\theta$ (extension to vector case is straightforward). Use a **first-order Taylor expansion** of $J(\theta)$ around the current point $\theta^{(t)}$:

$$J(\theta) \approx J(\theta^{(t)}) + (\theta - \theta^{(t)}) \cdot \frac{dJ}{d\theta}\bigg|_{\theta^{(t)}}$$

We want to find $\theta$ that **reduces** $J(\theta)$. To maximally descend, $\theta - \theta^{(t)}$ should be **anti-parallel** to the gradient. Set the step as:

$$\theta - \theta^{(t)} = -\eta \cdot \frac{dJ}{d\theta}\bigg|_{\theta^{(t)}}$$

where $\eta > 0$ is the step size. Solving:

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \frac{dJ}{d\theta}\bigg|_{\theta^{(t)}}$$

> **Line-by-line logic:**
> - Taylor expansion linearizes the cost near the current point.
> - The negative gradient direction is the **steepest descent** direction.
> - $\eta$ controls the **step length**. Too small = slow; too large = divergence.

## 3.2 Derivation B — Closed-Form Training of Linear Regression

Consider the simplest trained model — **Simple Linear Regression**:

$$\hat{y} = w x + b$$

with parameter vector $\theta = (w, b)^T$ and loss:

$$J(w, b) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - (w x_i + b))^2$$

We want the closed-form optimum by setting the gradient to zero:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w} = -\frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i (y_i - w x_i - b) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = -\frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - w x_i - b) = 0$$

Let $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum x_i$, $\bar{y} = \frac{1}{N} \sum y_i$. From the second equation:

$$\bar{y} = w \bar{x} + b \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; b = \bar{y} - w \bar{x}$$

Substitute into the first equation:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i (y_i - w x_i - \bar{y} + w \bar{x}) = 0$$

Expanding:

$$\sum x_i y_i - w \sum x_i^2 - \bar{y} \sum x_i + w \bar{x} \sum x_i = 0$$

Since $\sum x_i = N \bar{x}$, the last two terms cancel:

$$\sum x_i y_i - N \bar{x} \bar{y} = w \left(\sum x_i^2 - N \bar{x}^2\right)$$

Solving for $w$:

$$w = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i y_i - N \bar{x} \bar{y}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2 - N \bar{x}^2} = \frac{\text{Cov}(x, y)}{\text{Var}(x)}$$

> **Conclusion:** For linear regression, "training" produces the well-known **least-squares** solution. This is the only ML model with a closed-form training solution; **all other models require iterative optimization** (i.e., gradient descent).

## 3.3 Derivation C — Iterative Training Trace (Worked Numerical Example)

Let us **manually execute two training iterations** on a toy dataset using Batch Gradient Descent, to build concrete intuition.

**Dataset (1-D):**

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 3 |
| 2 | 2 | 5 |
| 3 | 3 | 7 |

The true relationship is $y = 2x + 1$ (so the optimal $w^* = 2, b^* = 1$). We will start with $w^{(0)} = 0$, $b^{(0)} = 0$, $\eta = 0.05$.

**Loss function (MSE):**

$$J(w, b) = \frac{1}{3} \sum_{i=1}^{3} (y_i - w x_i - b)^2$$

**Gradients:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w} = -\frac{2}{3} \sum x_i (y_i - w x_i - b)$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = -\frac{2}{3} \sum (y_i - w x_i - b)$$

### Iteration $t = 0 \to 1$:

Predictions at $(w, b) = (0, 0)$: $\hat{y}_1 = 0, \hat{y}_2 = 0, \hat{y}_3 = 0$.

Errors: $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i = 3, 5, 7$.

$$\sum x_i e_i = 1(3) + 2(5) + 3(7) = 3 + 10 + 21 = 34$$

$$\sum e_i = 3 + 5 + 7 = 15$$

Gradients:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w} = -\frac{2}{3}(34) = -22.667$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = -\frac{2}{3}(15) = -10.000$$

Updates:

$$w^{(1)} = 0 - (0.05)(-22.667) = 1.133$$

$$b^{(1)} = 0 - (0.05)(-10.000) = 0.500$$

### Iteration $t = 1 \to 2$:

Predictions at $(w, b) = (1.133, 0.500)$:
- $\hat{y}_1 = 1.133(1) + 0.500 = 1.633$
- $\hat{y}_2 = 1.133(2) + 0.500 = 2.766$
- $\hat{y}_3 = 1.133(3) + 0.500 = 3.899$

Errors: $e_1 = 1.367,\; e_2 = 2.234,\; e_3 = 3.101$.

$$\sum x_i e_i = 1(1.367) + 2(2.234) + 3(3.101) = 1.367 + 4.468 + 9.303 = 15.138$$

$$\sum e_i = 1.367 + 2.234 + 3.101 = 6.702$$

Gradients:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w} = -\frac{2}{3}(15.138) = -10.092$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = -\frac{2}{3}(6.702) = -4.468$$

Updates:

$$w^{(2)} = 1.133 - (0.05)(-10.092) = 1.638$$

$$b^{(2)} = 0.500 - (0.05)(-4.468) = 0.723$$

> **Observation after 2 iterations:** $(w, b) = (1.638, 0.723)$ — moving steadily toward the true $(2, 1)$. Loss is decreasing monotonically. **This is exactly what "training" looks like under the hood** — repeated tiny corrections driven by the gradient signal.

## 3.4 The Learning Rate Effect — Convergence Table

| Learning Rate $\eta$ | Behavior | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| $10^{-6}$ | Convergence is glacial; thousands of epochs needed | Rarely used |
| $0.001$ | Slow but stable | Safe default for Adam |
| $0.01$ | Standard choice | Common in SGD |
| $0.1$ | Fast but oscillatory | Risky for deep nets |
| $> 1.0$ | **Divergence** — loss explodes | Catastrophic failure |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The End-to-End Training Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize theta randomly] --> B[Load Training Batch Dbatch]
    B --> C[Forward Pass: Compute yhat = h x, theta]
    C --> D[Compute Loss L yhat, y]
    D --> E{Convergence Reached?}
    E -- No --> F[Backward Pass: Compute gradient of J wrt theta]
    F --> G[Optimizer Updates theta via GD rule]
    G --> B
    E -- Yes --> H[Save Trained Parameters theta_star]
    H --> I[Evaluate on Test Set]
    I --> J[End: Deploy Model]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Taxonomy of Training Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Training Paradigm] --> B[Supervised]
    A --> C[Unsupervised]
    A --> D[Reinforcement]
    B --> B1[Regression: MSE Loss]
    B --> B2[Classification: Cross Entropy Loss]
    C --> C1[Clustering: K Means]
    C --> C2[Dimensionality Reduction: PCA]
    D --> D1[Reward Maximization]
    D --> D2[Policy Gradient Methods]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Loss Landscape & Gradient Descent Trajectory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Parameter Space Theta] --> B[Initialize at theta 0]
    B --> C[Compute Negative Gradient Direction]
    C --> D[Take Step of Size eta]
    D --> E{At Local Minimum?}
    E -- No --> C
    E -- Yes --> F[theta star reached]
    F --> G[Stop Training]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation for KTU viva:** The trajectory in the loss landscape is what differentiates a *good* optimizer from a *bad* one. SGD with momentum will reach a minimum faster than vanilla GD; Adam adapts the learning rate per-parameter; etc. You should be able to **draw and explain** this on the whiteboard.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Data Flow During Training

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DS[(Raw Dataset D)] --> SP[Data Splitting Module]
    SP --> TR[Training Set 70 percent]
    SP --> VA[Validation Set 15 percent]
    SP --> TE[Test Set 15 percent]
    TR --> MD[Model h x, theta]
    MD --> LF[Loss Function L]
    LF --> OP[Optimizer Gradient Descent]
    OP --> MD
    VA --> HP[Hyperparameter Tuning]
    HP --> OP
    TE --> EV[Final Evaluation Report]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "training" in machine learning. List the three essential components required for any training procedure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Training is the iterative optimization process of finding the optimal parameter set $\theta^*$ of a learning model $h(x;\theta)$ by minimizing a loss function $L$ over a labeled training dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.

The three essential components are:
1. **Training Data** $\mathcal{D}$ — the labeled examples that drive learning.
2. **Model / Hypothesis** $h(x;\theta)$ — the parameterized function family.
3. **Loss Function + Optimizer** $(L, \text{Opt})$ — the objective to minimize and the algorithm that performs the minimization.

> **Marking scheme:** Definition (1 mark) + three components (2 marks — 0.5 each + 0.5 for organization).

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between an epoch and an iteration in the context of training a machine learning model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Epoch | Iteration |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | One complete pass of the model over the **entire** training dataset | One single update of the model parameters |
| Count per epoch | 1 | $\lceil N / B \rceil$ where $B$ = batch size |
| Purpose | Measures how many times the model has "seen" all data | Measures the number of gradient updates performed |

Example: For $N = 1000$ samples and batch size $B = 100$, one epoch = 10 iterations.

> **Marking scheme:** 1 mark for each correct definition + 1 mark for the numerical relationship.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> Common mistakes students make on this topic:
> 1. **Confusing parameters with hyperparameters** — parameters are *learned* (weights), hyperparameters are *set by the user* (learning rate).
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the role of the learning rate** — always state that $\eta$ controls step size.
> 3. **Mixing up training set and test set** — data leakage is a **direct loss of 2-3 marks** in practicals and theory alike.
> 4. **Skipping the loss function in derivations** — a gradient descent derivation without explicitly writing $J(\theta)$ is incomplete.
> 5. **Forgetting units/dimensions** in numerical problems.

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)** with its mathematical formulation. Discuss the role of the loss function and the optimizer in the training process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Gradient Descent update rule** for a general cost function $J(\theta)$ using a first-order Taylor approximation. Clearly state the assumptions and explain the significance of the learning rate $\eta$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 marks):** ERM is the principle of training a model by minimizing the *empirical* (i.e., sample-averaged) loss over the training set, in contrast to the *expected* loss over the true data distribution. Mathematically:

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta \in \Theta} \; \underbrace{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L\big(h(x_i;\theta),\, y_i\big)}_{\text{Empirical Risk } \hat{R}(\theta)}$$

**Role of the Loss Function (2 marks):** The loss $L(\hat{y}, y)$ quantifies the discrepancy between the predicted output $\hat{y}$ and the true label $y$ for a single sample. Common choices:
- **MSE** for regression
- **Cross-Entropy** for classification
- **Hinge Loss** for SVMs

It is **differentiable** (or sub-differentiable) so that gradients can be computed.

**Role of the Optimizer (2 marks):** The optimizer (e.g., Gradient Descent, SGD, Adam) is the **algorithmic engine** that navigates the loss landscape. Starting from an initial $\theta^{(0)}$, it iteratively updates $\theta$ in the direction of steepest descent (or an improved variant) until $J(\theta)$ converges to a minimum. Optimizers differ in:
- Convergence speed
- Ability to escape local minima
- Memory and compute cost

**Concluding Statement (1 mark):** ERM provides the *theoretical* foundation of supervised training, but it must be coupled with regularization and proper validation to prevent **overfitting**.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):** We aim to find $\theta^*$ that minimizes a differentiable cost $J(\theta)$. Let $\theta^{(t)}$ be the current parameter value at iteration $t$.

**Step 2 — Taylor Approximation (2 marks):** Expanding $J(\theta)$ to first order around $\theta^{(t)}$:

$$J(\theta) \approx J(\theta^{(t)}) + \left.\frac{dJ}{d\theta}\right|_{\theta^{(t)}} \cdot (\theta - \theta^{(t)})$$

**Step 3 — Minimization Argument (1 mark):** To reduce $J$, we require the linear term to be **negative**:

$$\left.\frac{dJ}{d\theta}\right|_{\theta^{(t)}} \cdot (\theta - \theta^{(t)}) < 0$$

This is achieved by setting the update to be **anti-parallel** to the gradient:

$$\theta - \theta^{(t)} = -\eta \cdot \left.\frac{dJ}{d\theta}\right|_{\theta^{(t)}}$$

**Step 4 — Update Rule (1 mark):** Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta J\big(\theta^{(t)}\big)}$$

For vector parameters $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^d$, the gradient $\nabla_\theta J \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is computed component-wise.

**Step 5 — Assumptions (1 mark):**
- $J(\theta)$ is **differentiable** at $\theta^{(t)}$.
- $\eta$ is **small enough** that the linear approximation remains valid (i.e., the loss landscape is locally flat).
- Cost function is **bounded below** (e.g., $J \geq 0$ for MSE).

**Step 6 — Significance of $\eta$ (1 mark):**
- **Small $\eta$:** Stable but slow convergence; may get stuck in poor local minima.
- **Large $\eta$:** Fast but oscillatory; can **diverge** (loss $\to \infty$).
- **Adaptive $\eta$ (Adam, RMSprop):** Per-parameter learning rates for robust convergence.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **end-to-end training pipeline** of a supervised learning model. Clearly distinguish between the training set, validation set, and test set. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a simple linear regression problem with the following data: $(x_1, y_1) = (1, 2)$, $(x_2, y_2) = (2, 4)$, $(x_3, y_3) = (3, 6)$. Using **one step of batch gradient descent** with learning rate $\eta = 0.1$ and initial parameters $(w, b) = (0, 0)$, compute the updated weights. Use MSE as the loss function. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

```
[Raw Dataset D]
       |
       v
[Split into D_train (70%), D_val (15%), D_test (15%)]
       |
       v
[Initialize parameters theta_0]
       |
       v
[For each epoch: For each mini-batch in D_train]
       |
       v
   [Forward pass: y_hat = h(x; theta)]
       |
       v
   [Compute Loss J(theta)]
       |
       v
   [Compute gradient via Backprop]
       |
       v
   [Update theta using Optimizer]
       |
       v
[Validate on D_val, tune hyperparameters]
       |
       v
[Final evaluation on D_test]
       |
       v
[Deploy model with theta_star]
```

**Description of Each Block (2 marks):** Each block represents a key step — from data loading, splitting, forward inference, loss evaluation, gradient computation, parameter update, and final evaluation. The cycle between forward and backward passes constitutes one **iteration**.

**Distinguishing the Three Splits (2 marks):**
- **Training Set:** Used *only* to update $\theta$ via gradient descent.
- **Validation Set:** Used to *tune hyperparameters* (e.g., $\eta$, regularization strength $\lambda$); *not* used for direct parameter updates.
- **Test Set:** Touched *only once* at the end to report unbiased generalization performance.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Setup (1 mark):** Linear model: $\hat{y} = w x + b$. Cost: $J(w, b) = \frac{1}{3} \sum_{i=1}^{3} (y_i - w x_i - b)^2$.

**Step 1 — Initial Predictions at $(w, b) = (0, 0)$ (1 mark):** $\hat{y}_1 = 0, \hat{y}_2 = 0, \hat{y}_3 = 0$.

**Step 2 — Compute Errors (1 mark):** $e_1 = 2 - 0 = 2$, $e_2 = 4 - 0 = 4$, $e_3 = 6 - 0 = 6$.

**Step 3 — Compute Gradients (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w} = -\frac{2}{3} \sum x_i e_i = -\frac{2}{3}(1 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 4 + 3 \cdot 6) = -\frac{2}{3}(2 + 8 + 18) = -\frac{2}{3}(28) = -18.667$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = -\frac{2}{3} \sum e_i = -\frac{2}{3}(2 + 4 + 6) = -\frac{2}{3}(12) = -8.000$$

**Step 4 — Apply GD Update (2 marks):**

$$w^{(1)} = 0 - (0.1)(-18.667) = 1.867$$

$$b^{(1)} = 0 - (0.1)(-8.000) = 0.800$$

**Final Answer:** After one iteration, $(w, b) = (1.867, 0.800)$, moving toward the true values $(2, 1)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Idea of Training"**

- ✅ **Training** = finding $\theta^* = \arg\min_\theta J(\theta)$ where $J$ is the empirical risk.
- ✅ The **training loop** has 5 steps: forward → loss → backward → update → repeat.
- ✅ **Epoch** = one full pass over $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$; **Iteration** = one parameter update.
- ✅ **Gradient Descent update:** $\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_\theta J(\theta^{(t)})$.
- ✅ **Learning rate $\eta$** is a *hyperparameter*; $\theta$ is a *learned parameter*.
- ✅ Three GD variants: **Batch** (stable/slow), **SGD** (noisy/fast), **Mini-Batch** (balanced — industry default).
- ✅ Data split rule: **70 / 15 / 15** for train / val / test (approximate; can vary).
- ✅ **Test set is sacred** — never use it for training or hyperparameter tuning.
- ✅ **Closed-form solution exists only for Linear Regression**; all other models need iterative optimization.
- ✅ **Loss functions to remember:** MSE (regression), Cross-Entropy (classification), Hinge (SVM).
- ✅ **Convergence criterion:** $\vert \vert \nabla J(\theta) \vert \vert < \epsilon$ or max-epochs reached.
- ✅ **Underfitting** = model too simple / under-trained; **Overfitting** = model too complex / over-trained — both diagnosed by comparing training vs. validation loss curves.
- ✅ For KTU exams: **always draw the loss-vs-epoch curve** when explaining training dynamics — it earns bonus marks.
- ✅ Memorize the numerical GD example above — it is the **single most repeated** numerical question in this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAIT404 - FOUNDATION OF MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Testing" in Machine Learning?

In Machine Learning, **Testing** refers to the systematic process of evaluating a trained model's performance on **unseen data** to estimate its **generalization capability** — that is, how well the model will perform on real-world data it has never encountered during training.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> Testing in ML is the post-training phase in which a learned hypothesis $h$ (the model) is evaluated on an independent test set $D_{test} \subset D$ (where $D$ is the full dataset) using quantitative performance metrics such as **accuracy**, **precision**, **recall**, **F1-score**, and **error rate** to assess generalization, reliability, and robustness.

Unlike traditional software testing (where we check if code matches specifications), ML testing validates whether the **learned patterns** capture true underlying relationships in the data, rather than memorizing noise.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Student Exam" Metaphor

Imagine a student preparing for a final exam:

| ML Concept | Student Analogy |
|------------|-----------------|
| **Training Set** | Practice problems & textbook examples |
| **Validation Set** | Mock tests (used to decide study strategy) |
| **Test Set** | The final exam (never seen before) |
| **Overfitting** | Student memorizes practice problems but fails the real exam |
| **Underfitting** | Student barely studies and fails both practice and real exams |
| **Generalization** | Student truly understands concepts and performs well on new questions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** If a student (or model) sees the final exam questions (test set) while studying (training), we can no longer trust their exam score as a measure of true understanding (generalization). This is why the test set must be **held out** — completely untouched until final evaluation.

---

## 1.3 Why Testing is Crucial — The Three Pillars

1. **Generalization Assessment** — Does the model work on new, unseen data?
2. **Model Comparison** — Which algorithm performs best for the problem?
3. **Trust & Deployment Readiness** — Can we deploy this model in production with confidence?

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** A common mistake students make is reporting **training accuracy** as model performance. The KTU board specifically looks for **test set evaluation** in answers. Always mention that testing is performed on **unseen, held-out data**.

---

## 1.4 Testing in the ML Pipeline

The complete Machine Learning pipeline positions testing at the **end** of the workflow:

$$\text{Data} \rightarrow \text{Preprocessing} \rightarrow \text{Training} \rightarrow \text{Validation} \rightarrow \mathbf{Testing} \rightarrow \text{Deployment}$$

---

## 1.5 Testing vs. Validation vs. Training

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Distinct Phases — Do Not Confuse:**

- **Training:** Model learns parameters (weights) by minimizing a loss function on the **training set**.
- **Validation:** Used to tune **hyperparameters** (e.g., learning rate, tree depth, k in k-NN) and prevent overfitting via the **validation set**.
- **Testing:** Final, unbiased evaluation using the **test set**, which is used **exactly once** before deployment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Data Splitting Strategies — The Foundation of Testing

### A. Holdout Method (Simple Train/Test Split)

The dataset $D$ with $n$ samples is split into:
- Training set: $D_{train}$ (typically 70%)
- Test set: $D_{test}$ (typically 30%)

$$\vert D \vert = \vert D_{train} \vert + \vert D_{test} \vert$$

**Why it works:**
- The model is trained only on $D_{train}$
- $D_{test}$ simulates real-world unseen data
- Test error $\approx$ generalization error

**Limitations:**
- High variance (results depend on the split)
- Wastes data (test set is not used for training)
- Struggles with small datasets

---

### B. k-Fold Cross-Validation (The Gold Standard)

The dataset is divided into $k$ equal-sized, mutually exclusive folds:
$$D = D_1 \cup D_2 \cup \ldots \cup D_k, \quad D_i \cap D_j = \emptyset \text{ for } i \neq j$$

The cross-validation accuracy is computed as:

$$\text{CV Accuracy} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{Accuracy}_i$$

For each fold $i$:
- Train on $D \setminus D_i$ (i.e., $k-1$ folds)
- Test on $D_i$ (the held-out fold)
- Record the performance metric

**Why k-Fold is powerful:**
- Every sample is used for both training and testing
- Reduces variance in performance estimate
- Maximizes use of limited data

---

### C. Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV)

A special case of k-Fold where $k = n$ (number of samples).

- Train on $n-1$ samples
- Test on the 1 remaining sample
- Repeat $n$ times

$$\text{LOOCV Error} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} L\big(y_i, \hat{y}_i^{(-i)}\big)$$

where $\hat{y}_i^{(-i)}$ is the prediction for sample $i$ using a model trained without it.

**Pros:** Maximum training data per iteration (low bias)
**Cons:** Computationally expensive; high variance in estimate

---

### D. Stratified k-Fold Cross-Validation

A refined version of k-Fold that **preserves the class distribution** in each fold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for Imbalanced Datasets:** If 95% of samples are Class A and 5% are Class B, regular k-Fold might produce folds with no Class B samples. Stratified k-Fold guarantees each fold has approximately the same 95/5 ratio.

---

### E. Bootstrap Method

Uses **sampling with replacement** to create $B$ training sets, each of size $n$.

Probability that a given sample is **not** picked in one bootstrap sample:
$$P(\text{not selected}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^n \approx \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$$

So approximately **63.2%** of unique samples appear in each bootstrap training set; the remaining **36.8%** form the out-of-bag (OOB) test set.

$$\text{Bootstrap Accuracy} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \text{Accuracy}_b$$

---

## 2.2 The Confusion Matrix — The Heart of Classification Testing

For a binary classification problem, predictions fall into four categories:

| | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick:** "True/False" answers *Is the prediction correct?* "Positive/Negative" answers *What was predicted?*

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — All Essential Testing Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 KTU Board-Ready Formula Reference Table (CRITICAL — Memorize!)**

| Metric | Formula | Range | Best Value | Real-World Use |
|--------|---------|-------|------------|----------------|
| **Accuracy** | $\frac{TP+TN}{TP+FP+TN+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $\to 1$ | Balanced datasets |
| **Error Rate** | $1 - \text{Accuracy} = \frac{FP+FN}{TP+FP+TN+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $\to 0$ | Complement of accuracy |
| **Precision (PPV)** | $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $\to 1$ | Spam detection (avoid false alarms) |
| **Recall (Sensitivity, TPR)** | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $\to 1$ | Cancer detection (catch all positives) |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $\to 1$ | Medical screening confirmation |
| **F1-Score** | $\frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $\to 1$ | Imbalanced data, single metric |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\frac{FP}{FP+TN} = 1 - \text{Specificity}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $\to 0$ | ROC curve x-axis |
| **Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)** | $\frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | $\to 1$ | Robust metric for imbalance |
| **Cohen's Kappa ($\kappa$)** | $\frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | $\to 1$ | Accounts for chance agreement |

where $p_o$ = observed agreement and $p_e$ = expected agreement by chance.

---

## 2.4 The ROC Curve and AUC

The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** plots:
- **Y-axis:** True Positive Rate (Recall) = $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$
- **X-axis:** False Positive Rate = $\frac{FP}{FP+TN}$

As the classification threshold varies from 0 to 1, we trace out the curve.

**Area Under the Curve (AUC):**
- AUC = 1.0 → Perfect classifier
- AUC = 0.5 → Random guessing (diagonal line)
- AUC < 0.5 → Worse than random (predictions inverted)

> [!NOTE]
> **AUC Interpretation:** AUC equals the probability that the model ranks a random positive example higher than a random negative example.

---

## 2.5 Regression Testing Metrics

For continuous-valued predictions, classification metrics don't apply. Use:

| Metric | Formula | Penalizes |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)** | $\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Equal weight to all errors |
| **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Large errors heavily |
| **Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)** | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ | Large errors; same unit as $y$ |
| **R-squared ($R^2$)** | $1 - \frac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | Variance unexplained |

---

## 2.6 The Bias-Variance Tradeoff

Total expected prediction error decomposes as:

$$\mathbb{E}\big[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\big] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2}_{\text{Systematic Error}} + \underbrace{\text{Variance}}_{\text{Sensitivity to Data}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}}$$

| Term | Meaning | Cause |
|------|---------|-------|
| **Bias²** | How wrong the model's average prediction is | Oversimplified model (underfitting) |
| **Variance** | How much predictions fluctuate across training sets | Overly complex model (overfitting) |
| $\sigma^2$ | Inherent noise in data | Cannot be reduced by any model |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Summary:** Bias = model is *rigid* and consistently wrong. Variance = model is *wiggly* and changes wildly with new data.

---

## 2.7 Overfitting vs. Underfitting — Detecting via Testing

| Symptom | Training Error | Test Error | Diagnosis |
|---------|---------------|------------|-----------|
| Underfitting | High | High | Model too simple → Increase complexity |
| Good Fit | Low | Low | ✅ Ideal scenario |
| Overfitting | Very Low | High | Model too complex → Regularize / Get more data |

The **learning curve** (plot of training and test error vs. dataset size) is a key diagnostic tool.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: F1-Score from Precision and Recall

Starting from the definitions:

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$$

**Step 1:** The F1-score is the **harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall:

$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{\text{Precision}} + \frac{1}{\text{Recall}}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the definitions:

$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{TP+FP}{TP} + \frac{TP+FN}{TP}}$$

**Step 3:** Combine the fractions in the denominator:

$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{(TP+FP) + (TP+FN)}{TP}} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

**Step 4:** Final compact form:

$$\boxed{F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}}$$

> **Why Harmonic Mean (not Arithmetic)?** The harmonic mean **penalizes extreme values**. If either Precision or Recall is very low, F1 will be low — even if the other is high. This is exactly what we want for a balanced metric.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: k-Fold Cross-Validation Expected Error

Let $D_i$ be the $i$-th fold and $\hat{f}_i$ the model trained on $D \setminus D_i$.

**Step 1:** The error on fold $i$ is:

$$E_i = \frac{1}{\vert D_i \vert} \sum_{(x_j, y_j) \in D_i} L\big(y_j, \hat{f}_i(x_j)\big)$$

**Step 2:** The overall cross-validation error is the average:

$$\text{CV Error} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} E_i$$

**Step 3:** Substituting:

$$\boxed{\text{CV Error} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \left[ \frac{1}{\vert D_i \vert} \sum_{(x_j, y_j) \in D_i} L\big(y_j, \hat{f}_i(x_j)\big) \right]}$$

**Step 4:** If all folds are of equal size ($\vert D_i \vert = n/k$):

$$\text{CV Error} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{(x_j, y_j) \in D_i} L\big(y_j, \hat{f}_i(x_j)\big)$$

> **Insight:** The CV error is essentially a Monte Carlo estimate of the **expected generalization error** $E_{D_{train}} \big[ E_{(x,y) \sim P} [L(y, \hat{f}(x))] \big]$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Confusion Matrix and All Metrics

**Problem:** A binary classifier produces the following on a test set of 1000 samples:

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | 150 (TP) | 50 (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | 100 (FP) | 700 (TN) |

**Step 1: Accuracy**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+FP+TN+FN} = \frac{150+700}{1000} = \frac{850}{1000} = 0.85$$

**Step 2: Error Rate**

$$\text{Error Rate} = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15$$

**Step 3: Precision**

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{150}{150+100} = \frac{150}{250} = 0.60$$

**Step 4: Recall (Sensitivity)**

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{150}{150+50} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75$$

**Step 5: Specificity**

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN+FP} = \frac{700}{700+100} = \frac{700}{800} = 0.875$$

**Step 6: F1-Score**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.60 \cdot 0.75}{0.60 + 0.75} = \frac{0.90}{1.35} \approx 0.667$$

**Step 7: False Positive Rate (for ROC)**

$$\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP+TN} = \frac{100}{100+700} = 0.125$$

**Step 8: Cohen's Kappa**

Observed agreement: $p_o = \text{Accuracy} = 0.85$

Expected agreement:
$$p_e = \frac{(TP+FP)(TP+FN) + (FN+TN)(FP+TN)}{n^2}$$

$$p_e = \frac{(250)(200) + (750)(800)}{1000^2} = \frac{50000 + 600000}{1000000} = 0.65$$

$$\kappa = \frac{0.85 - 0.65}{1 - 0.65} = \frac{0.20}{0.35} \approx 0.571$$

> **Interpretation:** The classifier is **substantially better than chance** (Kappa > 0.4 is considered moderate agreement).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: 5-Fold Cross-Validation

**Setup:** Dataset of 500 samples, 5-fold CV, accuracy results per fold:

| Fold | Accuracy |
|------|----------|
| 1 | 0.88 |
| 2 | 0.92 |
| 3 | 0.85 |
| 4 | 0.90 |
| 5 | 0.87 |

**Step 1:** Compute mean accuracy:

$$\bar{A} = \frac{0.88 + 0.92 + 0.85 + 0.90 + 0.87}{5} = \frac{4.42}{5} = 0.884$$

**Step 2:** Compute standard deviation (variance across folds):

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{k-1} \sum_{i=1}^{k}(A_i - \bar{A})^2}$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{(0.004)^2 + (0.036)^2 + (-0.034)^2 + (0.016)^2 + (-0.014)^2}{4}}$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{0.000016 + 0.001296 + 0.001156 + 0.000256 + 0.000196}{4}}$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{0.002920}{4}} = \sqrt{0.000730} \approx 0.027$$

**Step 3:** Report as mean ± standard deviation:

$$\text{CV Accuracy} = 0.884 \pm 0.027$$

> **KTU Board Insight:** Always report CV results as **mean ± std**, not just mean. This shows the **stability** of the model.

---

## 3.5 Bias-Variance Decomposition (Derivation Sketch)

**Step 1:** Expected squared error at point $x$:

$$\mathbb{E}\big[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\big] = \mathbb{E}\big[(y - f(x) + f(x) - \hat{f}(x))^2\big]$$

**Step 2:** Expand using $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$ and noting the cross-term vanishes (since $y$ is independent of training set):

$$= \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[(y - f(x))^2]}_{\sigma^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[(f(x) - \hat{f}(x))^2]}_{\text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance}}$$

**Step 3:** Expand the second term:

$$\mathbb{E}[(f(x) - \hat{f}(x))^2] = (\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x))^2 + \mathbb{E}\big[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2\big]$$

**Step 4:** Identify the terms:

$$= \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2[\hat{f}(x)]}_{\text{squared bias}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)]}_{\text{variance}}$$

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}\big[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\big] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2_{\text{irreducible}}}$$
