|-------------|-------------|-----------|---------------|------------------|
| Euclidean Distance ($L_2$) | $D_E = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - y_i)^2}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Continuous | Most common, scale-sensitive |
| Squared Euclidean | $D_{SE} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - y_i)^2$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Continuous | Faster (no sqrt), amplifies large diffs |
| Manhattan Distance ($L_1$) | $D_M = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Continuous | Robust to outliers, grid movement |
| Minkowski Distance ($L_p$) | $D_p = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Continuous | Generalization; $p$ tunable |
| Chebyshev Distance ($L_\infty$) | $D_C = \max_i \vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Continuous | Max-coordinate difference |
| Cosine Similarity | $S_C = \dfrac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert_2 \, \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert_2}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Sparse vectors | Ignores magnitude, measures angle |
| Cosine Distance | $D_C = 1 - S_C$ | $[0, 2]$ | Sparse vectors | Dissimilarity counterpart |
| Jaccard Similarity | $J(A,B) = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Sets / Binary | Intersection over Union |
| Jaccard Distance | $D_J = 1 - J(A,B)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Sets / Binary | Set dissimilarity |
| Hamming Distance | $D_H = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i \neq y_i)$ | $[0, n]$ | Binary / String | Counts mismatches |
| Mahalanobis Distance | $D_M = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y})}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Correlated features | Scale & correlation invariant |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Information Retrieval (Google Search)**: Cosine similarity ranks web pages by relevance to a query vector in TF-IDF space.
- **Bioinformatics**: Hamming and edit distances compare DNA/RNA/protein sequences for evolutionary analysis.
- **Computer Vision**: Euclidean and Mahalanobis distances in feature embedding space power face recognition (e.g., FaceNet).
- **Recommender Systems**: Cosine similarity finds users with similar movie-rating patterns in collaborative filtering.
- **IoT/Sensor Networks**: Manhattan and Chebyshev distances model grid-based and maximum-deviation sensor readings.
- **GIS & Route Planning**: Manhattan distance models urban grid navigation; Euclidean models aerial distance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feature Scaling is Mandatory!**
> Distance-based measures (Euclidean, Manhattan, Minkowski) are highly sensitive to the **scale of features**. Always apply **Min-Max Normalization** or **Z-Score Standardization** before computing distance, or features with larger ranges will dominate the distance calculation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

### 3.1 Derivation: From Norms to Minkowski Distance

The Minkowski distance is rooted in the concept of **$L_p$ vector norms**. The $L_p$ norm of a vector $\mathbf{v} = (v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n)$ is defined as:

$$\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_p = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert v_i \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$$

The Minkowski distance between $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{y}$ is then simply the $L_p$ norm of their difference vector $(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y})$:

$$D_p(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \Vert_p$$

$$\therefore \quad D_p(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$$

**Special Case 1**: Substituting $p = 1$ recovers Manhattan Distance.

$$D_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^1\right)^{1/1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$$

**Special Case 2**: Substituting $p = 2$ recovers Euclidean Distance.

$$D_2(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^2\right)^{1/2} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - y_i)^2}$$

**Special Case 3**: As $p \to \infty$, the term with the **largest** magnitude dominates the sum, giving Chebyshev Distance.

$$D_{\infty}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \lim_{p \to \infty} \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^p\right)^{1/p} = \max_{i} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Cosine Similarity from Vector Geometry

The cosine of the angle $\theta$ between two non-zero vectors $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{y}$ in $n$-dimensional space is derived from the **dot product formula**:

$$\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y} = \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert_2 \, \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert_2 \cos\theta$$

The dot product is also computed component-wise:

$$\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \, y_i$$

The squared $L_2$ norm is:

$$\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert_2^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2}$$

Substituting back, the cosine similarity is:

$$S_C(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \cos\theta = \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert_2 \, \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert_2} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2} \, \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2}}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1: Distance Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> Given two data points in a 4-dimensional feature space (e.g., a movie profile with features: [Action, Comedy, Drama, Romance]):
> $$\mathbf{x} = (4, 2, 5, 1) \quad \text{and} \quad \mathbf{y} = (1, 3, 2, 0)$$
> Compute the **Euclidean**, **Manhattan**, **Minkowski (p=3)**, and **Chebyshev** distances.

**Step 1: Compute the coordinate-wise differences.**

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $\delta_i = x_i - y_i$ | $\vert \delta_i \vert$ | $\delta_i^2$ | $\vert \delta_i \vert^3$ |
|-----|-------|-------|------------------------|------------------------|--------------|--------------------------|
| 1   | 4     | 1     | 3                      | 3                      | 9            | 27                       |
| 2   | 2     | 3     | -1                     | 1                      | 1            | 1                        |
| 3   | 5     | 2     | 3                      | 3                      | 9            | 27                       |
| 4   | 1     | 0     | 1                      | 1                      | 1            | 1                        |

**Step 2: Apply each formula.**

**Euclidean Distance** ($p=2$):

$$D_E = \sqrt{\sum \delta_i^2} = \sqrt{9 + 1 + 9 + 1} = \sqrt{20} \approx 4.472$$

**Manhattan Distance** ($p=1$):

$$D_M = \sum \vert \delta_i \vert = 3 + 1 + 3 + 1 = 8$$

**Minkowski Distance** ($p=3$):

$$D_3 = \left(\sum \vert \delta_i \vert^3\right)^{1/3} = (27 + 1 + 27 + 1)^{1/3} = 56^{1/3} \approx 3.826$$

**Chebyshev Distance** ($p \to \infty$):

$$D_C = \max_i \vert \delta_i \vert = \max\{3, 1, 3, 1\} = 3$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2: Cosine Similarity

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> Given two document word-count vectors:
> $$\mathbf{d_1} = (1, 1, 2, 1, 0, 0) \quad \text{(Document 1: "machine learning is fun")}$$
> $$\mathbf{d_2} = (1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 1) \quad \text{(Document 2: "machine learning rocks here")}$$
> Compute **Cosine Similarity** between the two documents.

**Step 1: Compute the dot product $\mathbf{d_1} \cdot \mathbf{d_2}$.**

$$\mathbf{d_1} \cdot \mathbf{d_2} = (1)(1) + (1)(1) + (2)(0) + (1)(0) + (0)(1) + (0)(1)$$

$$= 1 + 1 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 2$$

**Step 2: Compute the magnitudes (norms).**

$$\Vert \mathbf{d_1} \Vert_2 = \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 2^2 + 1^2 + 0^2 + 0^2} = \sqrt{1+1+4+1} = \sqrt{7} \approx 2.646$$

$$\Vert \mathbf{d_2} \Vert_2 = \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 0^2 + 0^2 + 1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{1+1+0+0+1+1} = \sqrt{4} = 2$$

**Step 3: Compute Cosine Similarity.**

$$S_C = \frac{\mathbf{d_1} \cdot \mathbf{d_2}}{\Vert \mathbf{d_1} \Vert_2 \, \Vert \mathbf{d_2} \Vert_2} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{7} \times 2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{7}} \approx 0.378$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result.**

Since $S_C \approx 0.378$ is positive and closer to 0 than 1, the documents share some vocabulary (the words "machine" and "learning") but are **not very similar overall**. The angle between them is $\theta = \arccos(0.378) \approx 67.8^\circ$.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 3: Jaccard Similarity

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> Two customers have the following sets of purchased items:
> - Customer A: {Bread, Milk, Eggs, Butter, Cheese}
> - Customer B: {Milk, Eggs, Butter, Yogurt, Juice}
> Compute the **Jaccard Similarity** and **Jaccard Distance**.

**Step 1: Compute the Intersection $A \cap B$.**

$$A \cap B = \{\text{Milk, Eggs, Butter}\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert A \cap B \vert = 3$$

**Step 2: Compute the Union $A \cup B$.**

$$A \cup B = \{\text{Bread, Milk, Eggs, Butter, Cheese, Yogurt, Juice}\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert A \cup B \vert = 7$$

**Step 3: Compute Jaccard Similarity.**

$$J(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert} = \frac{3}{7} \approx 0.4286$$

**Step 4: Compute Jaccard Distance.**

$$D_J(A, B) = 1 - J(A, B) = 1 - 0.4286 = 0.5714$$

**Interpretation:** The two customers share about 42.86% of their purchased items, indicating a moderate overlap in shopping behavior.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example 4: Hamming Distance

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> Given two binary codewords used in error detection:
> $$\mathbf{c_1} = 1011010, \quad \mathbf{c_2} = 1001001$$
> Compute the **Hamming Distance**.

**Step-by-step bit-wise comparison:**

| Position | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|----------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{c_1}$ | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| $\mathbf{c_2}$ | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| Mismatch?  | No | No | **Yes** | No | No | **Yes** | **Yes** |

$$D_H = \sum_{i=1}^{7} (\mathbf{c_1}_i \neq \mathbf{c_2}_i) = 0+0+1+0+0+1+1 = 3$$

The Hamming distance is **3**, meaning 3 bit-flips are required to transform $\mathbf{c_2}$ into $\mathbf{c_1}$. A code with minimum Hamming distance 4 can correct up to 1 error.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Taxonomy of Similarity Measures

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete classification hierarchy of similarity and distance measures covered in the KTU syllabus:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["SIMILARITY AND DISTANCE MEASURES"] --> B["CONTINUOUS VECTOR METRICS"]
    A --> C["ANGULAR DIRECTIONAL METRICS"]
    A --> D["SET AND BINARY METRICS"]
    A --> E["STRING SEQUENCE METRICS"]
    A --> F["PROBABILISTIC METRICS"]

    B --> B1["Euclidean L2 Norm"]
    B --> B2["Manhattan L1 Norm"]
    B --> B3["Minkowski Lp Norm"]
    B --> B4["Chebyshev L Infinity Norm"]
    B --> B5["Mahalanobis Distance"]

    C --> C1["Cosine Similarity"]
    C --> C2["Pearson Correlation"]

    D --> D1["Jaccard Index"]
    D --> D2["Hamming Distance"]
    D --> D3["Sokal Michener"]

    E --> E1["Edit Levenshtein Distance"]
    E --> E2["Longest Common Subsequence"]

    F --> F1["KL Divergence"]
    F --> F2["Jensen Shannon Divergence"]

    B3 --> B3a["p equals 1 gives Manhattan"]
    B3 --> B3b["p equals 2 gives Euclidean"]
    B3 --> B3c["p tends to infinity gives Chebyshev"]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow: Choosing the Right Measure

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1["What is the data type?"] --> Q2["Continuous numeric vectors"]
    Q1 --> Q3["Sparse text or TF IDF"]
    Q1 --> Q4["Binary or sets"]
    Q1 --> Q5["Strings or sequences"]

    Q2 --> Q6["Are features correlated?"]
    Q6 --> Q6a["Yes: use Mahalanobis"]
    Q6 --> Q6b["No: use Euclidean or Manhattan"]

    Q3 --> R1["Use Cosine Similarity"]
    Q4 --> R2["Use Jaccard or Hamming"]
    Q5 --> R3["Use Edit or Hamming"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Distance in KNN

```mermaid
graph LR
    inp["INPUT QUERY POINT x"] --> norm["STEP 1: FEATURE NORMALIZATION"]
    norm --> dist["STEP 2: DISTANCE COMPUTATION"]
    dist --> sort["STEP 3: SORT NEIGHBORS BY DISTANCE"]
    sort --> sel["STEP 4: SELECT TOP K NEAREST"]
    sel --> vote["STEP 5: MAJORITY VOTING"]
    vote --> out["OUTPUT: PREDICTED CLASS LABEL"]

    dist --> d1["Euclidean"]
    dist --> d2["Manhattan"]
    dist --> d3["Minkowski"]
    dist --> d4["Cosine Distance"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Insight:** The choice of distance measure in **STEP 2** is a hyperparameter. A poorly chosen metric silently degrades KNN accuracy. The diagram above shows that normalization (Step 1) is a **prerequisite** to distance computation (Step 2) for any $L_p$ norm-based measure.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**
> **Q1. Define similarity measure and distance measure. State the relationship between them.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **similarity measure** $S(A,B)$ is a scalar function quantifying the degree of resemblance between two data objects, where a **higher value indicates greater similarity**. A **distance measure** $D(A,B)$ quantifies dissimilarity, where a **lower value indicates greater similarity**.
>
> A valid distance measure must satisfy four axioms: non-negativity, identity of indiscernibles, symmetry, and triangle inequality.
>
> **Relationship:** They are **inversely related**. Common conversions include:
> $$S = \frac{1}{1 + D} \quad \text{or} \quad S = \frac{1}{D} \quad \text{or} \quad S = -D \text{ (for bounded distances)}$$

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Q2. Distinguish between Euclidean distance and Manhattan distance. When is Manhattan preferred over Euclidean?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | **Property** | **Euclidean** | **Manhattan** |
> |--------------|---------------|---------------|
> | Formula | $\sqrt{\sum(x_i - y_i)^2}$ | $\sum \vert x_i - y_i \vert$ |
> | Path | Straight line ($L_2$) | Grid path ($L_1$) |
> | Outlier Sensitivity | High (squares amplify) | Low (linear) |
> | Use Case | 2D/3D geometry, dense data | High-dim sparse data, grid movement |
>
> **Manhattan is preferred when:**
> 1. Data is high-dimensional and sparse (e.g., text bags-of-words).
> 2. Outliers are present (Manhattan is more robust).
> 3. The problem involves grid-based movement (e.g., taxi routing, chess rook moves).

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **Q3 (A). (a)** Explain different similarity measures used in machine learning with suitable mathematical formulations. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Given the following data points, compute the Euclidean and Manhattan distances:
> $P = (2, 3, 4)$ and $Q = (5, 1, 7)$. Comment on the results. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Part (a) - Different Similarity Measures (7 marks):**
>
> **1. Euclidean Distance** - The straight-line distance in $n$-dimensional space.
> $$D_E(P, Q) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(p_i - q_i)^2}$$
> Sensitive to feature scale; requires normalization. Widely used in KNN and K-Means.
>
> **2. Manhattan Distance** - Sum of absolute coordinate differences.
> $$D_M(P, Q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert p_i - q_i \vert$$
> Useful in high-dimensional sparse data; robust to outliers.
>
> **3. Minkowski Distance** - A generalization parameterized by $p$.
> $$D_p(P, Q) = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert p_i - q_i \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$$
> Recovers Manhattan ($p=1$), Euclidean ($p=2$), Chebyshev ($p \to \infty$).
>
> **4. Cosine Similarity** - Measures the angle between two vectors.
> $$S_C = \frac{\vec{P} \cdot \vec{Q}}{\Vert \vec{P} \Vert \Vert \vec{Q} \Vert} = \frac{\sum p_i q_i}{\sqrt{\sum p_i^2} \sqrt{\sum q_i^2}}$$
> Range $[-1, 1]$; ignores magnitude. Used in text/document similarity.
>
> **5. Jaccard Similarity** - For sets $A$ and $B$:
> $$J(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$$
> Used in market-basket analysis and binary/categorical data.
>
> **6. Hamming Distance** - Counts bit/character mismatches.
> $$D_H = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(p_i \neq q_i)$$
> Used in error-detection codes and binary string comparison.
>
> **Part (b) - Numerical Computation (7 marks):**
>
> **Given:** $P = (2, 3, 4)$ and $Q = (5, 1, 7)$
>
> **Step 1: Coordinate-wise differences**
>
> | $i$ | $p_i$ | $q_i$ | $p_i - q_i$ | $\vert p_i - q_i \vert$ | $(p_i - q_i)^2$ |
> |-----|-------|-------|-------------|-------------------------|-------------------|
> | 1   | 2     | 5     | -3          | 3                       | 9                 |
> | 2   | 3     | 1     | 2           | 2                       | 4                 |
> | 3   | 4     | 7     | -3          | 3                       | 9                 |
>
> **Step 2: Euclidean Distance**
> $$D_E = \sqrt{9 + 4 + 9} = \sqrt{22} \approx 4.690$$
>
> **Step 3: Manhattan Distance**
> $$D_M = 3 + 2 + 3 = 8$$
>
> **Comment:** The Euclidean distance ($\sqrt{22} \approx 4.69$) is smaller than Manhattan (8) because Euclidean computes a "diagonal" path while Manhattan sums axis-aligned movements. For 3D data, the maximum possible Manhattan distance is $8$ (when each coordinate differs by 3 and 2 and 3). Both indicate the points are reasonably far apart in feature space.

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme - Alternative Choice]**
> **Q3 (B). (a)** What is Cosine Similarity? Derive its mathematical formula. Explain its application in document similarity. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Two customers, A and B, have the following purchases:
> A = {Tea, Coffee, Sugar, Milk, Bread}
> B = {Coffee, Milk, Sugar, Biscuit, Butter}
> Compute Jaccard Similarity and Jaccard Distance. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Part (a) - Cosine Similarity (7 marks):**
>
> **Definition:** Cosine similarity is a measure that quantifies the similarity between two non-zero vectors by computing the **cosine of the angle** between them in vector space. The output ranges from $-1$ (opposite) through $0$ (orthogonal) to $1$ (identical direction).
>
> **Derivation:** From vector geometry, the dot product of two vectors is:
> $$\vec{x} \cdot \vec{y} = \Vert \vec{x} \Vert \Vert \vec{y} \Vert \cos\theta$$
> The dot product in component form is:
> $$\vec{x} \cdot \vec{y} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i$$
> The $L_2$ norm is:
> $$\Vert \vec{x} \Vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2}, \quad \Vert \vec{y} \Vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2}$$
> Substituting and solving for $\cos\theta$:
> $$\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{x} \cdot \vec{y}}{\Vert \vec{x} \Vert \Vert \vec{y} \Vert} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i}{\sqrt{\sum x_i^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum y_i^2}}$$
>
> **Application in Document Similarity:** In NLP, each document is represented as a TF-IDF vector of word frequencies. Cosine similarity is preferred over Euclidean distance because:
> 1. **Magnitude Invariance:** A 1000-word document and a 100-word document with similar word distribution have a high cosine similarity despite different magnitudes.
> 2. **Sparse Data Handling:** Works well with sparse vectors typical in bag-of-words.
> 3. **Semantic Comparison:** Captures topical overlap regardless of document length.
>
> **Part (b) - Jaccard Calculation (7 marks):**
>
> **Step 1: Compute Intersection $A \cap B$**
> $$A \cap B = \{\text{Coffee, Milk, Sugar}\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert A \cap B \vert = 3$$
>
> **Step 2: Compute Union $A \cup B$**
> $$A \cup B = \{\text{Tea, Coffee, Sugar, Milk, Bread, Biscuit, Butter}\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert A \cup B \vert = 7$$
>
> **Step 3: Compute Jaccard Similarity**
> $$J(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert} = \frac{3}{7} \approx 0.4286$$
>
> **Step 4: Compute Jaccard Distance**
> $$D_J(A, B) = 1 - J(A, B) = 1 - 0.4286 = 0.5714$$
>
> **Conclusion:** The two customers share approximately 42.86% of their purchased items, indicating a moderate shopping pattern overlap.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls - Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to take the square root** in Euclidean distance → 1 mark penalty.
> 2. **Using Euclidean distance without normalization** when features have different units → entire answer is logically wrong.
> 3. **Confusing Cosine Similarity ($-1$ to $1$) with Cosine Distance ($0$ to $2$)**: $D_C = 1 - S_C$, not $-S_C$.
> 4. **Misapplying Hamming distance** to continuous (non-binary) data → Jaccard or Euclidean should be used instead.
> 5. **Ignoring the triangle inequality** when claiming a custom formula is a valid metric → 1 mark cut.
> 6. **In Jaccard problems**, students often confuse intersection with union or include duplicates in set cardinality → conceptual mark loss.
> 7. **Skipping unit identification or stating "this distance is in the same unit as features"** when features have different units → -0.5 mark.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist - Similarity Measures (Module 3)**

- **Definition:** Similarity = higher value = more alike; Distance = lower value = more alike. They are inversely related.
- **Four Metric Axioms:** Non-negativity, Identity, Symmetry, Triangle Inequality must hold for any valid distance.
- **Euclidean ($L_2$):** Straight-line distance; sensitive to scale and outliers; needs normalization.
- **Manhattan ($L_1$):** Grid/sum-of-absolute-differences; robust to outliers; good for high-dim sparse data.
- **Minkowski ($L_p$):** Unifying family; tune $p$ to interpolate between metrics.
- **Chebyshev ($L_\infty$):** Maximum coordinate-wise difference; chess king movement; warehouse robotics.
- **Cosine Similarity:** Angle-based; range $[-1, 1]$; ignores magnitude; perfect for TF-IDF text data.
- **Jaccard:** Intersection over Union; range $[0, 1]$; for sets, market baskets, binary attributes.
- **Hamming:** Count mismatches; for binary codes, fixed-length strings, error detection.
- **Mahalanobis:** Accounts for feature correlation via covariance matrix $\Sigma^{-1}$; used in anomaly detection.
- **Feature scaling is mandatory** for all $L_p$ distance-based measures. Use Min-Max or Z-score.
- **Curse of Dimensionality:** In very high dimensions, all pairwise distances converge → similarity measures become less discriminative. Use dimensionality reduction (PCA) or specialized metrics (cosine, Jaccard).
- **Symmetric property:** Always verify $D(A, B) = D(B, A)$. Asymmetric variants exist (e.g., KL divergence) but are not true metrics.
- **Common conversion:** Euclidean → Squared Euclidean (faster, no square root, but not a true metric since triangle inequality fails in trivial cases).
- **Best practice:** Match the metric to the **data type** (continuous/binary/set/string) and the **algorithm** (KNN, K-Means, recommendation).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hierarchical Clustering -Agglomerative Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAIT404 - FOUNDATION OF MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hierarchical Clustering - Agglomerative Clustering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Hierarchical Clustering — Agglomerative Clustering

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical Clustering** is an **unsupervised learning** procedure that builds a nested hierarchy of clusters by successively merging (agglomerative) or splitting (divisive) groups, producing a tree-structured representation called a **dendrogram**.

**Agglomerative Clustering** is a **bottom-up** variant of hierarchical clustering where each observation begins as its own singleton cluster, and at every subsequent step, the two **closest** clusters (according to a chosen *linkage criterion*) are merged. The process continues iteratively until a stopping condition is reached — typically a pre-specified number of clusters **K** or a single all-encompassing cluster.

Formally, given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ in $\mathbb{R}^d$, agglomerative clustering produces a sequence of partitions:

$$P_0 \rightarrow P_1 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow P_{n-1}$$

where $P_0$ has $n$ singleton clusters and $P_{n-1}$ contains a single cluster comprising all points.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Tree of Cities"

Imagine you have a map with 5 cities: **Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, Kolkata, Bengaluru**.

- **Initial State:** Every city is its *own* country (5 clusters).
- **Step 1:** The two cities that are *geographically closest* form a state. Suppose **Chennai** and **Bengaluru** merge first (shortest road distance). 
- **Step 2:** Now look at the distance between "states". **Delhi** is close to **Kolkata** → they form a state. **Mumbai** is left alone.
- **Step 3:** Look again — does the Delhi-Kolkata state border Chennai-Bengaluru state? They get unified into a country.
- **Step 4:** Finally, Mumbai joins the rest.

The **family tree** that records *when* and *at what distance* each merge happened is the **dendrogram** — a vertical tree whose height encodes dissimilarity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Terminology:**
> - **Dendrogram:** Tree diagram showing merge heights
> - **Linkage:** Rule used to measure inter-cluster distance
> - **Cophenetic Distance:** Distance at which two points are first joined in the dendrogram
> - **Agglomerative Coefficient (AC):** Measure of clustering structure preservation (range: 0 to 1)

---

## 1.3 Why Agglomerative Clustering Matters in ML

| Property | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **No prior K** | Unlike K-Means, the number of clusters need not be specified *a priori*; it is chosen post-hoc by cutting the dendrogram |
| **Deterministic** | No random initialization → same dataset always yields the same hierarchy |
| **Captures nested structure** | Reveals sub-clusters within clusters (gene expression, taxonomy, document organization) |
| **Flexible** | Supports multiple distance metrics and linkage criteria for different data shapes |

> [!TIP]
> **When to prefer Agglomerative over K-Means:** Small-to-medium datasets (typically $n \leq 10{,}000$), non-globular cluster shapes, or when hierarchical *relationships* are scientifically meaningful (phylogenetics, taxonomies, organizational hierarchies).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Agglomerative Clustering Algorithm

The procedure is implemented as a deterministic, greedy bottom-up algorithm. Each step performs a single **merge** of the two clusters with the **minimum inter-cluster dissimilarity**.

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Initialization:** Treat each of the $n$ data points as a singleton cluster. Construct an $n \times n$ **proximity matrix** $D$ containing pairwise distances $D[i,j] = d(x_i, x_j)$.
2. **Repeat until a single cluster remains:**
   - **Step A:** Scan the proximity matrix $D$ to locate the smallest off-diagonal entry $D[i^*, j^*] = \min_{i \neq j} D[i,j]$. This pair forms the next merge.
   - **Step B:** Merge clusters $C_{i^*}$ and $C_{j^*}$ into a new cluster $C_{new} = C_{i^*} \cup C_{j^*}$. Record the merge at height $h = D[i^*, j^*]$ in the dendrogram.
   - **Step C:** Update the proximity matrix by computing the distance from $C_{new}$ to every remaining cluster $C_k$ using the chosen **linkage function** $L(C_{new}, C_k)$.
   - **Step D:** Delete rows/columns corresponding to the now-defunct clusters $C_{i^*}$ and $C_{j^*}$.
3. **Output:** A rooted binary tree (dendrogram) of merges and a final flat partition (obtained by cutting at the desired height $h^*$ or extracting $K$ clusters).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computational Complexity:** Naive implementation is $O(n^3)$ time and $O(n^2)$ memory. Optimized variants (e.g., **SLINK** for single linkage) reduce it to $O(n^2)$. For very large datasets, **BIRCH** or **HDBSCAN** are preferred scalable alternatives.

---

## 2.2 The Four Canonical Linkage Criteria

Let $C_r$ and $C_s$ be two clusters with sizes $n_r = \vert C_r \vert$ and $n_s = \vert C_s \vert$, and let $\bar{x}_r, \bar{x}_s$ denote their centroids.

### Single Linkage (MIN)

$$L_{\text{single}}(C_r, C_s) \;=\; \min_{x \in C_r,\; y \in C_s}\; d(x, y)$$

**Intuition:** Distance between the *closest pair* of points. Tends to produce **chaining** (long, straggly clusters).

### Complete Linkage (MAX)

$$L_{\text{complete}}(C_r, C_s) \;=\; \max_{x \in C_r,\; y \in C_s}\; d(x, y)$$

**Intuition:** Distance between the *farthest pair* of points. Tends to produce **compact, spherical** clusters; sensitive to outliers.

### Average Linkage (UPGMA)

$$L_{\text{avg}}(C_r, C_s) \;=\; \frac{1}{n_r \, n_s} \sum_{x \in C_r} \sum_{y \in C_s} d(x, y)$$

**Intuition:** Mean pairwise distance. A **balanced compromise** between single and complete linkage.

### Ward's Linkage (Minimum Variance)

$$\Delta(C_r, C_s) \;=\; \frac{n_r \, n_s}{n_r + n_s}\; \big\Vert \bar{x}_r - \bar{x}_s \big\Vert^2$$

**Intuition:** Measures the **increase in total within-cluster SSE** caused by merging $C_r$ and $C_s$. Produces clusters of roughly equal size and is the most common choice in practice.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lance–Williams Recurrence:** All four linkage rules — and many more — can be expressed through a single recurrence that updates the distance matrix after a merge $(i, j)$:
>
> $$d_{(ij), k} \;=\; \alpha_i \, d_{i,k} \;+\; \alpha_j \, d_{j,k} \;+\; \beta \, d_{i,j} \;+\; \gamma \, \big\vert d_{i,k} - d_{j,k} \big\vert$$
>
> This makes agglomerative clustering a **unified family** of algorithms parameterised by $(\alpha_i, \alpha_j, \beta, \gamma)$.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $d(x_i, x_j)$ | Euclidean: $\sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{d}(x_{ik} - x_{jk})^2}$ | Default for continuous data |
| $d_M(x_i, x_j)$ | Manhattan: $\sum_{k=1}^{d} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| $d_{\cos}(x_i, x_j)$ | Cosine: $1 - \frac{x_i \cdot x_j}{\Vert x_i \Vert \, \Vert x_j \Vert}$ | Text / sparse high-dim data |
| $L_{\text{single}}$ | $\min_{x \in C_r, y \in C_s} d(x,y)$ | Detects elongated clusters |
| $L_{\text{complete}}$ | $\max_{x \in C_r, y \in C_s} d(x,y)$ | Compact, equal-diameter clusters |
| $L_{\text{avg}}$ | $\frac{1}{n_r n_s} \sum \sum d(x,y)$ | Balanced clustering |
| $\Delta_{\text{Ward}}$ | $\frac{n_r n_s}{n_r + n_s} \Vert \bar{x}_r - \bar{x}_s \Vert^2$ | Minimum-variance merging |
| $\text{SSW}(C)$ | $\sum_{x \in C} \Vert x - \bar{x}_C \Vert^2$ | Within-cluster SSE (used by Ward) |
| $h_{\text{merge}}$ | Height in dendrogram at which a merge occurs | Determines cluster granularity |
| $K$ | Number of clusters obtained by cutting the dendrogram | Flat partition after hierarchical build |
| $T$ | Time complexity: $O(n^3)$ naive, $O(n^2)$ with priority queue | Scalability concern |
| $S$ | Space complexity: $O(n^2)$ for distance matrix | Memory requirement |

> [!WARNING]
> **Distance Metric Incompatibility:** Ward's linkage **must be used only with Euclidean distance**. Applying Ward's with cosine or Manhattan distance is mathematically inconsistent and will yield erratic clusters.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Bioinformatics:** Constructing phylogenetic trees from gene-expression or DNA-sequence data.
- **Document Organization:** Hierarchical topic modelling and taxonomy construction in NLP.
- **Image Segmentation:** Multi-resolution region merging in computer vision pipelines.
- **Customer Analytics:** Behavioural segmentation where sub-segments are inherently meaningful.
- **Anomaly Detection:** Points that join the dendrogram at unusually high merge heights are flagged as outliers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Numerical Worked Example — Agglomerative with Single Linkage

Consider the following 2-D dataset of 4 points:

$$\mathcal{D} = \{ A(1,1),\; B(2,1),\; C(4,3),\; D(5,4) \}$$

We will perform agglomerative clustering using **Euclidean distance** and **single linkage**.

### Step 1 — Compute the Initial Pairwise Distance Matrix

For each pair $(i,j)$, compute $d_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_{i1} - x_{j1})^2 + (x_{i2} - x_{j2})^2}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(A,B) &= \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (1-1)^2} = \sqrt{1+0} = 1.000 \\
d(A,C) &= \sqrt{(1-4)^2 + (1-3)^2} = \sqrt{9+4} = \sqrt{13} = 3.606 \\
d(A,D) &= \sqrt{(1-5)^2 + (1-4)^2} = \sqrt{16+9} = \sqrt{25} = 5.000 \\
d(B,C) &= \sqrt{(2-4)^2 + (1-3)^2} = \sqrt{4+4} = \sqrt{8}  = 2.828 \\
d(B,D) &= \sqrt{(2-5)^2 + (1-4)^2} = \sqrt{9+9} = \sqrt{18} = 4.243 \\
d(C,D) &= \sqrt{(4-5)^2 + (3-4)^2} = \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2}  = 1.414
\end{aligned}
$$

The initial **proximity matrix** $D_0$ is therefore:

| Cluster | A | B | C | D |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **A** | 0 | 1.000 | 3.606 | 5.000 |
| **B** | 1.000 | 0 | 2.828 | 4.243 |
| **C** | 3.606 | 2.828 | 0 | 1.414 |
| **D** | 5.000 | 4.243 | 1.414 | 0 |

### Step 2 — First Merge

Scan all off-diagonal entries: minimum value is $d(A,B) = 1.000$.

> **Action:** Merge **A** and **B** into cluster $C_1 = \{A, B\}$ at merge height $h_1 = 1.000$.

**Update rule for single linkage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(C_1, C) &= \min\big(d(A,C),\, d(B,C)\big) = \min(3.606,\, 2.828) = 2.828 \\
d(C_1, D) &= \min\big(d(A,D),\, d(B,D)\big) = \min(5.000,\, 4.243) = 4.243
\end{aligned}
$$

Updated proximity matrix $D_1$:

| Cluster | $\{A,B\}$ | C | D |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **$\{A,B\}$** | 0 | 2.828 | 4.243 |
| **C** | 2.828 | 0 | 1.414 |
| **D** | 4.243 | 1.414 | 0 |

### Step 3 — Second Merge

Minimum off-diagonal entry: $d(C, D) = 1.414$.

> **Action:** Merge **C** and **D** into cluster $C_2 = \{C, D\}$ at merge height $h_2 = 1.414$.

**Update rule:**

$$
d(C_1, C_2) \;=\; \min\big(d(A,C),\, d(A,D),\, d(B,C),\, d(B,D)\big) = \min(3.606,\, 5.000,\, 2.828,\, 4.243) = 2.828
$$

Updated proximity matrix $D_2$:

| Cluster | $\{A,B\}$ | $\{C,D\}$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **$\{A,B\}$** | 0 | 2.828 |
| **$\{C,D\}$** | 2.828 | 0 |

### Step 4 — Final Merge

The only remaining pair merges: $d(\{A,B\}, \{C,D\}) = 2.828$.

> **Action:** Final merge at height $h_3 = 2.828$. The entire dataset is now a single cluster.

---

## 3.2 Comparison Across Linkage Methods (Same Dataset)

Applying **complete**, **average**, and **Ward's** linkage to the same data yields different merge heights. The first two merges are identical ($A \cup B$ and $C \cup D$); only the final merge height changes.

| Linkage Method | Final Merge Height $d(\{A,B\}, \{C,D\})$ | Computation |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Single** | $2.828$ | $\min(3.606, 5.000, 2.828, 4.243)$ |
| **Complete** | $5.000$ | $\max(3.606, 5.000, 2.828, 4.243)$ |
| **Average** | $3.920$ | $\frac{1}{4}(3.606 + 5.000 + 2.828 + 4.243) = \frac{15.677}{4}$ |
| **Ward's** | $7.500$ | $\frac{2 \cdot 2}{2+2} \Vert \bar{x}_{AB} - \bar{x}_{CD} \Vert^2$ |

**Ward's computation in detail** (centroids: $\bar{x}_{AB} = (1.5, 1.0)$, $\bar{x}_{CD} = (4.5, 3.5)$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{\text{Ward}} &= \frac{n_{AB} \cdot n_{CD}}{n_{AB} + n_{CD}} \cdot \Vert \bar{x}_{AB} - \bar{x}_{CD} \Vert^2 \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot 2}{2+2} \cdot \big((1.5-4.5)^2 + (1.0-3.5)^2\big) \\
&= 1 \cdot (9 + 6.25) = 15.25
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Note: Some implementations use Ward's distance = $\sqrt{\Delta}$ to keep units consistent with Euclidean distance; the above is the Lance–Williams form.)*

---

## 3.3 Cophenetic Correlation Coefficient (Model-Fit Diagnostic)

To assess *how faithfully* the dendrogram preserves the original pairwise distances, the **Cophenetic Correlation Coefficient (CCC)** is computed:

$$
\text{CCC} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i<j}(d_{ij} - \bar{d})(c_{ij} - \bar{c})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i<j}(d_{ij} - \bar{d})^2 \cdot \sum_{i<j}(c_{ij} - \bar{c})^2}}
$$

where $d_{ij}$ is the original distance and $c_{ij}$ is the **cophenetic distance** (the height at which $i$ and $j$ first merge in the dendrogram). Values **closer to 1** indicate better hierarchical representation.

---

## 3.4 Determining the Optimal Number of Clusters

Two practical methods for cutting the dendrogram are:

**(a) Elbow on Merge Heights**

Plot merge heights $h_1, h_2, \dots, h_{n-1}$ in increasing order; the **largest jump** indicates the natural cluster count.

**(b) Inconsistency Coefficient** — for each merge, compute:

$$
I(m) \;=\; \frac{h(m) - \bar{h}_{\text{below}}(m)}{\sigma_{\text{below}}(m)}
$$

Merges with $I(m) > 1$ (or a chosen threshold like 2) are statistically significant, and the largest such merge determines $K$.

---

## 3.5 Final Result — Dendrogram of the Worked Example

```
Height
 2.83 ┤              ┌────────────┐
      │              │            │
 1.41 ┤       ┌──────┤            │
      │       │      │            │
 1.00 ┤   ┌───┤      │            │
      │   │   │      │            │
 0.00 ┤   A   B      C            D
```

Reading the dendrogram: cutting horizontally at any height produces a flat partition. For example, cutting at height $1.20$ gives **2 clusters**: $\{A, B\}$ and $\{C, D\}$.

---

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Agglomerative Clustering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Dataset D = x1, x2, ..., xn] --> B[Initialize: n singleton clusters]
    B --> C[Compute pairwise distance matrix D of size n x n]
    C --> D{Minimum distance dmin in D}
    D -- Found --> E[Merge the two closest clusters C_i and C_j]
    E --> F[Record merge height h = dmin in dendrogram]
    F --> G[Update distance matrix using linkage rule L]
    G --> H{More than 1 cluster remains?}
    H -- Yes --> D
    H -- No --> I[Output: Dendrogram tree]
    I --> J[Cut dendrogram at chosen height hstar]
    J --> K[Output: Final flat partition of K clusters]
    D -- Not found --> L[Error: Empty or invalid dataset]
```

## 4.2 Linkage Strategy Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1 [Single Linkage]
        S1[Distance equals MIN of all pairwise distances]
        S2[Forms long, chain-like clusters]
        S3[Sensitive to noise bridges]
    end
    subgraph L2 [Complete Linkage]
        C1[Distance equals MAX of all pairwise distances]
        C2[Forms compact, spherical clusters]
        C3[Sensitive to outliers]
    end
    subgraph L3 [Average Linkage]
        A1[Distance equals MEAN of all pairwise distances]
        A2[Balanced cluster shape]
        A3[Compromise between MIN and MAX]
    end
    subgraph L4 [Wards Linkage]
        W1[Distance equals SSE increase from merging]
        W2[Minimizes total within-cluster variance]
        W3[Requires Euclidean distance only]
    end
    D[Distance Matrix Update] --> L1
    D --> L2
    D --> L3
    D --> L4
    L1 --> O[Updated Proximity Matrix]
    L2 --> O
    L3 --> O
    L4 --> O
```

## 4.3 Dendrogram Interpretation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H0[Height Axis h represents dissimilarity]
    H1[Cutting at LOW height yields MANY small clusters]
    H2[Cutting at HIGH height yields FEW large clusters]
    H3[Vertical line of a leaf represents one data point]
    H4[Height of first common ancestor of two leaves = cophenetic distance]
    H5[Identifying largest vertical gap helps choose optimal K]
    H0 --> H1
    H0 --> H2
    H0 --> H3
    H0 --> H4
    H0 --> H5
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. Define agglomerative clustering. How does it differ from divisive clustering?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Agglomerative clustering** is a *bottom-up* hierarchical clustering technique where each data point initially forms its own singleton cluster, and at every step, the two *closest* clusters (as measured by a linkage criterion) are merged into a single cluster. The process repeats until a stopping condition — typically one cluster or $K$ clusters — is reached.
>
> **Divisive clustering**, in contrast, is a *top-down* approach. It begins with all data points in a single cluster and recursively *splits* the most heterogeneous cluster until each point forms its own cluster. Agglomerative is more widely used in practice because it is computationally simpler, supports a richer family of linkage methods, and is more naturally compatible with dissimilarity matrices (whereas divisive methods typically require a vector-space representation).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2. List and briefly explain any three linkage methods used in agglomerative clustering.**

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Single Linkage (MIN):** $L_{\text{single}}(C_r, C_s) = \min_{x \in C_r, y \in C_s} d(x, y)$. Distance is the *closest pair* between clusters. It is fast to compute and can detect elongated, non-convex shapes, but is sensitive to *chaining* — distant clusters getting merged via a chain of close points.
>
> 2. **Complete Linkage (MAX):** $L_{\text{complete}}(C_r, C_s) = \max_{x \in C_r, y \in C_s} d(x, y)$. Distance is the *farthest pair*. It produces compact clusters of similar diameter but is highly sensitive to outliers.
>
> 3. **Average Linkage (UPGMA):** $L_{\text{avg}}(C_r, C_s) = \frac{1}{n_r n_s} \sum_{x \in C_r} \sum_{y \in C_s} d(x, y)$. Uses the mean pairwise distance. It is less sensitive to outliers than complete linkage and is a good general-purpose default.
>
> *(Optional 4th: **Ward's Linkage** — based on minimum increase in within-cluster SSE, defined as $\Delta = \frac{n_r n_s}{n_r + n_s} \Vert \bar{x}_r - \bar{x}_s \Vert^2$. Best for equal-size, compact clusters.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step working of the agglomerative hierarchical clustering algorithm** with a suitable block diagram. Discuss its **time and space complexity**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Given the dataset $P_1(1,1), P_2(2,1), P_3(5,4), P_4(6,5)$, perform agglomerative clustering using **single linkage** and **Euclidean distance**. Draw the resulting dendrogram and state the cluster assignments if you cut the tree to obtain $K = 2$ clusters. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

> **Step 1 — Initialization:** Treat each of the $n$ points as its own cluster. Compute the $n \times n$ pairwise distance matrix.
>
> **Step 2 — Iterative Merging:** Repeat the following until one cluster remains:
> - Find the pair of clusters $(C_i, C_j)$ with the **smallest** inter-cluster distance (using the chosen linkage).
> - Merge them into a new cluster $C_{ij} = C_i \cup C_j$.
> - Record the merge height $h = L(C_i, C_j)$ for the dendrogram.
> - Update the distance matrix using the chosen linkage rule.
>
> **Step 3 — Termination & Output:** When a single cluster remains, the algorithm halts. The complete history of merges forms the **dendrogram**. The final flat partition is obtained by cutting the dendrogram at a height $h^*$ corresponding to the desired $K$.
>
> **Complexity:**
> - **Time:** Naive implementation is $O(n^3)$ — there are $n-1$ merges, and scanning the matrix at each step is $O(n^2)$. Optimized nearest-neighbour implementations achieve $O(n^2)$.
> - **Space:** $O(n^2)$ to store the distance matrix.
>
> **Key Property:** The algorithm is **deterministic** — for a given dataset, distance metric, and linkage, the dendrogram is *always* identical.

**Model Solution (b):**

> **Compute the initial distance matrix** (Euclidean):
>
> | Cluster | $P_1$ | $P_2$ | $P_3$ | $P_4$ |
> | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
> | **$P_1$** | 0 | 1.000 | 5.000 | 7.071 |
> | **$P_2$** | 1.000 | 0 | 4.243 | 6.325 |
> | **$P_3$** | 5.000 | 4.243 | 0 | 1.414 |
> | **$P_4$** | 7.071 | 6.325 | 1.414 | 0 |
>
> **Iteration 1:** Minimum distance is $d(P_1, P_2) = 1.000$. Merge $P_1$ and $P_2$ at height $h_1 = 1.000$. New cluster $C_1 = \{P_1, P_2\}$.
>
> Updated distances using single linkage:
>
> | Cluster | $C_1$ | $P_3$ | $P_4$ |
> | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
> | **$C_1$** | 0 | 4.243 | 6.325 |
> | **$P_3$** | 4.243 | 0 | 1.414 |
> | **$P_4$** | 6.325 | 1.414 | 0 |
>
> **Iteration 2:** Minimum distance is $d(P_3, P_4) = 1.414$. Merge $P_3$ and $P_4$ at height $h_2 = 1.414$. New cluster $C_2 = \{P_3, P_4\}$.
>
> Updated distance: $d(C_1, C_2) = \min(4.243, 6.325) = 4.243$.
>
> | Cluster | $C_1$ | $C_2$ |
> | :--- | :---: | :---: |
> | **$C_1$** | 0 | 4.243 |
> | **$C_2$** | 4.243 | 0 |
>
> **Iteration 3:** Final merge at $h_3 = 4.243$.
>
> **Dendrogram:**
>
> ```
> Height
>  4.24 ┤           ┌───────────┐
>       │           │           │
>  1.41 ┤      ┌────┤           │
>       │      │    │           │
>  1.00 ┤  ┌───┤    │           │
>       │  │   │    │           │
>  0.00 ┤  P1  P2   P3          P4
> ```
>
> **Cutting for $K = 2$:** The largest vertical gap is between $1.414$ and $4.243$. Cutting at $h^* = 2.5$ gives the partition:
>
> $$C_1 = \{P_1, P_2\}, \qquad C_2 = \{P_3, P_4\}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With a **neat labelled diagram**, explain the structure and interpretation of a **dendrogram**. How is the optimal number of clusters determined from a dendrogram? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **single linkage**, **complete linkage**, **average linkage**, and **Ward's method** in terms of formula, cluster shape produced, sensitivity to outliers, and time complexity. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

> A **dendrogram** is a tree-structured diagram used to visualize the sequence of merges in hierarchical clustering.
>
> **Structure:**
> - The **leaves** (bottom) represent individual data points.
> - The **root** (top) represents the single all-encompassing cluster.
> - Each **internal node** represents a merge, and its **height** on the vertical axis represents the dissimilarity at which the two child clusters were joined.
> - The **horizontal axis** has no quantitative meaning — only the vertical height is informative.
>
> ```
> Height
>  5.0 ┤          ┌──────────────┐
>      │          │              │
>  3.0 ┤    ┌─────┤              │
>      │    │     │              │
>  1.5 ┤  ┌─┤     │              │
>      │  │ │     │              │
>  0.5 ┤  A B     ┌┤              │
>      │          │ │              │
>  0.0 ┤          C  D             E
> ```
>
> **Interpretation:**
> - Two points joined by a **low-height node** are *very similar*.
> - Two points that require going to a **high node** to be joined are *dissimilar*.
> - The **cophenetic distance** between any two points is the height of their lowest common ancestor in the dendrogram.
>
> **Determining Optimal $K$:**
> 1. **Visual Gap Method:** Identify the largest vertical gap in merge heights, and cut just *below* it. Each resulting branch corresponds to a cluster.
> 2. **Elbow on Merge Heights:** Plot $h_i$ vs. merge index $i$ and pick the elbow point.
> 3. **Inconsistency Threshold:** Use the inconsistency coefficient — merges whose height significantly exceeds the average height of recent merges (normalized by their standard deviation) are considered "natural" cluster boundaries.
> 4. **Domain Knowledge:** In applications like taxonomy or document organization, the desired $K$ may be determined by external requirements.

**Model Solution (b):**

| Criterion | Single | Complete | Average | Ward's |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Formula** | $\min d(x,y)$ | $\max d(x,y)$ | $\text{mean } d(x,y)$ | $\frac{n_r n_s}{n_r+n_s}\Vert \bar{x}_r - \bar{x}_s \Vert^2$ |
| **Cluster Shape** | Elongated, chain-like | Compact, spherical | Balanced | Equal-size, compact |
| **Outlier Sensitivity** | High (chaining effect) | Very high | Moderate | Low |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(n^2)$ to $O(n^3)$ | $O(n^2)$ to $O(n^3)$ | $O(n^2)$ to $O(n^3)$ | $O(n^2)$ to $O(n^3)$ |
| **Distance Metric** | Any | Any | Any | Euclidean **only** |
| **Best Use Case** | Non-convex shapes | Compact, equal-diameter | General purpose | Default for continuous data |

> **Practical Recommendation:** Ward's method is the **default choice** for most ML applications on continuous numerical data. Single linkage is reserved for cases where the cluster shape is known to be elongated (e.g., geographic clusters). Complete linkage is used when the data is expected to form tight, well-separated groups.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting matrix symmetry** — when updating the proximity matrix, ensure $D[i,j] = D[j,i]$. Symmetric updates are mandatory; an asymmetric matrix is a direct correctness deduction.
>
> 2. **Confusing single vs. complete linkage formulas** — a single misapplied $\min / \max$ changes every subsequent merge. Examiners specifically test this; they will deduct for the wrong rule even if the procedure is otherwise correct.
>
> 3. **Ward + Non-Euclidean** — applying Ward's method with cosine or Manhattan distance is mathematically invalid. Examiners expect students to know that Ward's method **requires Euclidean distance**.
>
> 4. **Not showing intermediate distance matrices** — a numerical question without intermediate steps loses marks. Always show the matrix *after each* merge.
>
> 5. **Ignoring units in dendrogram height** — explicitly state the linkage criterion when labelling the height axis. Writing only "Height" instead of "Height (Single-Linkage Euclidean Distance)" loses credit.
>
> 6. **Time complexity error** — writing $O(n^2)$ as the time complexity (instead of $O(n^3)$ for naive agglomerative) is a frequent slip. Remember: there are $n-1$ merges, each costing $O(n^2)$ to scan the matrix.
>
> 7. **Drawing a horizontal dendrogram** — the conventional orientation has the **height axis vertical**; horizontal dendrograms are non-standard and may be marked down.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Agglomerative clustering** is a **bottom-up** hierarchical method producing a **dendrogram**.
- **Algorithm skeleton:** Init $n$ singletons → compute $n \times n$ distance matrix → repeat: find min pair → merge → update matrix → record height → until 1 cluster remains.
- **Four core linkage methods:** Single (MIN), Complete (MAX), Average (UPGMA), Ward's (SSE-increase).
- **Lance–Williams recurrence** is a single unified update formula covering all linkage methods.
- **Time complexity:** $O(n^3)$ naive; $O(n^2)$ with priority-queue / nearest-neighbour optimization.
- **Space complexity:** $O(n^2)$ for storing the distance matrix.
- **Single linkage** detects elongated shapes; prone to **chaining**.
- **Complete linkage** gives compact, equal-diameter clusters; **outlier sensitive**.
- **Average linkage** is the **balanced compromise**.
- **Ward's method** minimizes **total within-cluster variance**; works only with **Euclidean distance**.
- **Dendrogram height axis** = dissimilarity at which the merge occurs; cutting horizontally yields a flat partition.
- **Cophenetic distance** = dendrogram height of the **lowest common ancestor** of two leaves.
- **Cophenetic Correlation Coefficient (CCC)** quantifies how faithfully the dendrogram preserves original pairwise distances (closer to 1 = better).
- **Optimal $K$** can be selected via the **largest visual gap** in merge heights, the **elbow rule**, or the **inconsistency coefficient**.
- **Agglomerative is deterministic** (no random initialization unlike K-Means) but **does not scale** to very large $n$ without approximation (BIRCH, HDBSCAN).
- **Practical default for continuous data:** **Ward's linkage + Euclidean distance**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAIT404 - FOUNDATION OF MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** partitional clustering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Partitional Clustering** is a category of unsupervised learning algorithms that divides a dataset of $N$ observations into a pre-specified number $K$ of non-overlapping, non-hierarchical clusters, where each observation belongs to **exactly one cluster** (hard partitioning). Unlike hierarchical clustering, partitional clustering produces a single, flat (non-nested) decomposition of the data.

Formally, given a dataset $\mathcal{X} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ in $\mathbb{R}^d$, partitional clustering seeks to partition $\mathcal{X}$ into $K$ clusters $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_K$ such that:

1. $C_i \neq \emptyset$ for all $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$
2. $C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset$ for all $i \neq j$
3. $\bigcup_{i=1}^{K} C_i = \mathcal{X}$

The most canonical algorithms in this family are **K-Means** and **K-Medoids**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Partitional clustering is fundamentally different from hierarchical clustering. Partitional methods produce a **single-level partition** in one pass (or iteratively), whereas hierarchical methods produce a tree of nested clusters (dendrogram). Partitional clustering is computationally cheaper ($O(NKt)$) compared to agglomerative hierarchical clustering ($O(N^3)$) for large datasets.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **supermarket manager** arranging a large shipment of **500 unsorted fruits** (apples, mangoes, oranges) into exactly **3 large baskets**. The manager does not know the type of any fruit beforehand, but he uses two visible features: **size** and **color intensity**.

**Step 1 (Initialization):** He randomly places 3 empty baskets at 3 spots in the storage area. These are the initial cluster centers.

**Step 2 (Assignment):** He picks each fruit, measures its size and color, and places it into the basket that is **nearest** to it in terms of these two features.

**Step 3 (Update):** After all fruits are placed, each basket now has its own group. He computes the **average size and color** of all fruits in each basket and moves the basket's label to this new average position.

**Step 4 (Re-Assignment):** Now, with the basket positions changed, some fruits may be closer to a *different* basket. He re-sorts them.

**Step 5 (Convergence):** He repeats Steps 3-4 until no fruit changes its basket. At convergence, each basket contains a coherent group of similar fruits.

This iterative *assign–update–reassign* loop is **exactly** how the **K-Means** partitional clustering algorithm operates on numerical data.

> [!NOTE] **Core Distinction: Partitional vs. Hierarchical**
> The supermarket analogy produces a **flat 3-basket arrangement** in one pass. A hierarchical approach would instead repeatedly split baskets into sub-baskets (divisive) or merge the closest fruits one pair at a time (agglomerative), producing a tree. KTU 2024 students must clearly distinguish these paradigms in answers.

## 1.3 Standard Notation & Symbols

| Symbol | Meaning |
|:---|:---|
| $N$ | Total number of data points |
| $K$ | Number of clusters (pre-specified) |
| $x_i$ | The $i$-th data point, $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ |
| $\mu_k$ | Centroid of cluster $C_k$ |
| $c_i$ | Cluster assignment of point $x_i$ |
| $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ | Norm (usually Euclidean L2) |
| $t$ | Current iteration number |

Explicit physical/algorithmic constants worth memorizing: **$K$ (the number of clusters)** and **$t_{max}$ (maximum iterations)** are the two key hyperparameters for K-Means.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The K-Means Algorithm: Step-by-Step Logic

**K-Means (Lloyd's Algorithm, 1957)** is the cornerstone of partitional clustering. It minimizes an objective function called the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**, also known as **distortion** or **inertia**.

### Objective Function

The algorithm seeks to minimize:

$$J(C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_K) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \lVert x_i - \mu_k \rVert^2$$

where the centroid $\mu_k$ is defined as the **mean of all points in cluster $C_k$**:

$$\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$$

### Algorithmic Steps (Why & How)

1. **Initialization:** Choose $K$ initial centroids. Methods include:
   - Random selection of $K$ data points
   - **K-Means++** (smarter, probabilistic seeding — default in scikit-learn)
   - Forgy method (random points from dataset)
   - Random Partition method (randomly assign points, then compute means)

2. **Assignment Step (Expectation-like):** For each data point $x_i$, assign it to the cluster whose centroid is closest (usually Euclidean distance):
   $$c_i = \arg\min_{k \in \{1,\ldots,K\}} \lVert x_i - \mu_k \rVert^2$$
   **Why:** This step greedily minimizes the inner sum for fixed $\mu_k$.

3. **Update Step (Maximization-like):** Recompute each centroid as the mean of all points assigned to it:
   $$\mu_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i : c_i = k} x_i$$
   **Why:** For a fixed assignment $c_i$, the mean $\mu_k$ is the unique point that minimizes $\sum_{x_i \in C_k} \lVert x_i - \mu_k \rVert^2$ (the orthogonal projection of the cluster).

4. **Convergence Check:** Stop if any of these is satisfied:
   - Centroids do not change: $\lVert \mu_k^{\text{new}} - \mu_k^{\text{old}} \rVert < \epsilon$
   - Cluster assignments stabilize: $c_i^{(t)} = c_i^{(t-1)}$ for all $i$
   - WCSS reduction $J^{(t-1)} - J^{(t)} < \epsilon$
   - Maximum iterations $t_{max}$ reached

> [!NOTE] **Convergence Guarantee**
> K-Means is guaranteed to **converge in finite steps** because $J$ is monotonically non-increasing and bounded below (by 0), and the number of possible partitions is finite ($\frac{1}{K!} \sum_{j=0}^{K} (-1)^j \binom{K}{j}(K-j)^N$ — Stirling number of the second kind). However, convergence is to a **local minimum**, not necessarily the global minimum.

## 2.2 K-Medoids (PAM Algorithm)

K-Medoids is a robust variant of K-Means. Instead of using the *mean* (which is sensitive to outliers), it uses an **actual data point** (called a **medoid**) as the cluster center. The most common algorithm is **PAM (Partitioning Around Medoids)**.

**Key Differences from K-Means:**

| Aspect | K-Means | K-Medoids |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Cluster center | Mean $\mu_k$ (synthetic) | Medoid $m_k$ (actual data point) |
| Robustness to outliers | **Poor** (mean is pulled by outliers) | **Excellent** (medoid is a real point) |
| Distance metric | Euclidean (typically) | Any distance (e.g., Manhattan, cosine) |
| Computational cost | $O(NKdT)$ | $O(K(N-K)^2 T)$ — slower |
| Best for | Continuous, low-dimensional data | Mixed data, noise, small datasets |

The objective for K-Medoids is:

$$J_{\text{medoid}} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \mathcal{D}(x_i, m_k)$$

where $\mathcal{D}(\cdot, \cdot)$ is **any** dissimilarity function and $m_k \in C_k$ is a chosen data point.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description |
|:---|:---|:---|
| WCSS Objective | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \lVert x_i - \mu_k \rVert^2$ | Total within-cluster variance to minimize |
| Centroid Update | $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ | Mean of cluster $C_k$ |
| Assignment Rule | $c_i = \arg\min_k \lVert x_i - \mu_k \rVert^2$ | Nearest-centroid rule |
| Euclidean Distance (L2) | $d(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d} (x_j - y_j)^2}$ | Standard choice for K-Means |
| Manhattan Distance (L1) | $d(x, y) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ | Used with K-Medoids |
| Minkowski Distance (L$p$) | $d(x, y) = \left(\sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | Generalization ($p=1$ L1, $p=2$ L2) |
| Cosine Distance | $d(x,y) = 1 - \frac{x \cdot y}{\lVert x \rVert \lVert y \rVert}$ | For sparse/text data |
| K-Means Complexity | $O(N \cdot K \cdot d \cdot t)$ | Per-iteration cost |
| Elbow Method | Plot $J$ vs $K$; pick the "elbow" | Choosing $K$ |
| Silhouette Coefficient | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Validating $K$; range $[-1, 1]$ |
| Number of partitions | $S(N,K) = \frac{1}{K!} \sum_{j=0}^{K} (-1)^j \binom{K}{j} (K-j)^N$ | Upper bound on iterations |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Utility**
> Partitional clustering (especially K-Means) is the **workhorse** of industrial ML pipelines: customer segmentation in marketing, image compression (vector quantization), document clustering, anomaly detection, feature learning, and as a preprocessing step in recommendation systems. Its scalability makes it the de-facto choice for big-data clustering (with Mini-Batch K-Means for very large $N$).

## 2.4 Choosing the Optimal K

Two dominant methods:

1. **Elbow Method:** Plot $J$ (WCSS) against $K$. The "elbow" point (where the rate of decrease sharply changes) is the optimal $K$.
2. **Silhouette Analysis:** For each point $i$, compute:
   - $a(i) = $ mean distance to points in its own cluster (cohesion)
   - $b(i) = $ mean distance to points in the nearest other cluster (separation)
   - $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$
   The mean silhouette score across all points is maximized at the optimal $K$.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Centroid is the Mean

**Claim:** For a fixed cluster $C_k$, the point $\mu$ that minimizes $\sum_{x_i \in C_k} \lVert x_i - \mu \rVert^2$ is the arithmetic mean $\bar{x}_k$.

**Proof:** Let $n_k = \vert C_k \vert$. Consider the objective as a function of $\mu$:

$$f(\mu) = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \lVert x_i - \mu \rVert^2 = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} (x_i - \mu)^\top (x_i - \mu)$$

$$= \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \left( x_i^\top x_i - 2 x_i^\top \mu + \mu^\top \mu \right)$$

Take the gradient with respect to $\mu$ and set to zero:

$$\nabla_\mu f = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \left( -2 x_i + 2 \mu \right) = 2 n_k \mu - 2 \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i = 0$$

Solving for $\mu$:

$$\mu^* = \frac{1}{n_k} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i = \bar{x}_k$$

The second derivative is $2 n_k I \succ 0$, confirming a strict minimum. $\blacksquare$

This is a **favourite KTU question** because it connects calculus with the K-Means update rule.

## 3.2 Complete Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Apply K-Means clustering with $K = 2$ to the following 2D dataset. Use Euclidean distance. Initial centroids are the **first two points**.

$$A(2, 10), \quad B(2, 5), \quad C(8, 4), \quad D(5, 8), \quad E(7, 5), \quad F(6, 4), \quad G(1, 2), \quad H(4, 9)$$

**Initial centroids (Iteration 0):** $\mu_1^{(0)} = A = (2, 10)$, $\mu_2^{(0)} = B = (2, 5)$.

### Iteration 1: Assignment

For each point, compute squared Euclidean distance to $\mu_1^{(0)}$ and $\mu_2^{(0)}$, then assign.

**Point A(2,10):** $d^2(A, \mu_1) = 0$, $d^2(A, \mu_2) = 0^2 + 5^2 = 25$. Assign to **Cluster 1**.

**Point B(2,5):** $d^2(B, \mu_1) = 0^2 + 5^2 = 25$, $d^2(B, \mu_2) = 0$. Assign to **Cluster 2**.

**Point C(8,4):** $d^2(C, \mu_1) = 6^2 + (-6)^2 = 72$, $d^2(C, \mu_2) = 6^2 + (-1)^2 = 37$. Closer to $\mu_2$ → **Cluster 2**.

**Point D(5,8):** $d^2(D, \mu_1) = 3^2 + (-2)^2 = 13$, $d^2(D, \mu_2) = 3^2 + 3^2 = 18$. Closer to $\mu_1$ → **Cluster 1**.

**Point E(7,5):** $d^2(E, \mu_1) = 5^2 + (-5)^2 = 50$, $d^2(E, \mu_2) = 5^2 + 0^2 = 25$. → **Cluster 2**.

**Point F(6,4):** $d^2(F, \mu_1) = 4^2 + (-6)^2 = 52$, $d^2(F, \mu_2) = 4^2 + (-1)^2 = 17$. → **Cluster 2**.

**Point G(1,2):** $d^2(G, \mu_1) = (-1)^2 + (-8)^2 = 65$, $d^2(G, \mu_2) = (-1)^2 + (-3)^2 = 10$. → **Cluster 2**.

**Point H(4,9):** $d^2(H, \mu_1) = 2^2 + (-1)^2 = 5$, $d^2(H, \mu_2) = 2^2 + 4^2 = 20$. → **Cluster 1**.

**Iteration 1 Result:**

- **Cluster 1:** $\{A(2,10), D(5,8), H(4,9)\}$
- **Cluster 2:** $\{B(2,5), C(8,4), E(7,5), F(6,4), G(1,2)\}$

### Iteration 1: Centroid Update

$$\mu_1^{(1)} = \left( \frac{2+5+4}{3}, \frac{10+8+9}{3} \right) = \left( \frac{11}{3}, 9 \right) \approx (3.67, 9)$$

$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \left( \frac{2+8+7+6+1}{5}, \frac{5+4+5+4+2}{5} \right) = \left( \frac{24}{5}, 4 \right) = (4.8, 4)$$

### Iteration 2: Re-Assignment

Recompute distances with new centroids $(3.67, 9)$ and $(4.8, 4)$.

| Point | $d^2$ to $\mu_1^{(1)}$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_2^{(1)}$ | New Cluster |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A(2,10) | $(2-3.67)^2 + 1^2 = 2.79$ | $(2-4.8)^2 + 6^2 = 43.84$ | **1** |
| B(2,5) | $(2-3.67)^2 + 16 = 18.79$ | $(2-4.8)^2 + 1 = 8.84$ | **2** |
| C(8,4) | $(8-3.67)^2 + 25 = 43.79$ | $(8-4.8)^2 + 0 = 10.24$ | **2** |
| D(5,8) | $(5-3.67)^2 + 1 = 2.78$ | $(5-4.8)^2 + 16 = 16.04$ | **1** |
| E(7,5) | $(7-3.67)^2 + 16 = 27.09$ | $(7-4.8)^2 + 1 = 5.84$ | **2** |
| F(6,4) | $(6-3.67)^2 + 25 = 30.43$ | $(6-4.8)^2 + 0 = 1.44$ | **2** |
| G(1,2) | $(1-3.67)^2 + 49 = 56.14$ | $(1-4.8)^2 + 4 = 18.44$ | **2** |
| H(4,9) | $(4-3.67)^2 + 0 = 0.11$ | $(4-4.8)^2 + 25 = 25.64$ | **1** |

**Iteration 2 Result:**

- **Cluster 1:** $\{A, D, H\}$ — **Unchanged from Iteration 1**
- **Cluster 2:** $\{B, C, E, F, G\}$ — **Unchanged**

Cluster assignments are identical → **Convergence reached** in 2 iterations.

### Final WCSS Calculation

**Cluster 1** ($\mu_1 = (3.67, 9)$):

$$\text{WCSS}_1 = (2-3.67)^2 + (10-9)^2 + (5-3.67)^2 + (8-9)^2 + (4-3.67)^2 + (9-9)^2$$
$$= 2.79 + 1.00 + 1.77 + 1.00 + 0.11 + 0 = 6.67$$

**Cluster 2** ($\mu_2 = (4.8, 4)$):

$$\text{WCSS}_2 = (2-4.8)^2 + (5-4)^2 + (8-4.8)^2 + (4-4)^2 + (7-4.8)^2 + (5-4)^2 + (6-4.8)^2 + (4-4)^2 + (1-4.8)^2 + (2-4)^2$$

$$= 7.84 + 1.00 + 10.24 + 0.00 + 4.84 + 1.00 + 1.44 + 0.00 + 14.44 + 4.00 = 44.80$$

**Total WCSS:** $J = 6.67 + 44.80 = 51.47$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (K-Means vs K-Medoids vs Hierarchical)

| Criterion | K-Means | K-Medoids (PAM) | Hierarchical (Agglomerative) |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Structure | Flat partition | Flat partition | Nested dendrogram |
| Number of clusters $K$ | Pre-specified | Pre-specified | Determined post-hoc (cut dendrogram) |
| Cluster representative | Mean (synthetic) | Medoid (real point) | Linkage-based merging |
| Time complexity | $O(NKdT)$ | $O(K(N-K)^2 T)$ | $O(N^3)$ naive, $O(N^2 \log N)$ optimized |
| Space complexity | $O(Nd + Kd)$ | $O(N^2)$ (distance matrix) | $O(N^2)$ |
| Outlier sensitivity | **High** | **Low** | **Medium** |
| Distance flexibility | Euclidean only (typically) | Any dissimilarity | Any linkage + distance |
| Determinism | Stochastic (init-dependent) | Stochastic | Deterministic (mostly) |
| Scalability to big data | **Excellent** | Poor (small/medium $N$) | Poor |
| Convergence | Local optimum in finite steps | Local optimum | Always converges |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Key Engineering Insight**
> K-Means assumes clusters are **isotropic (spherical), equally sized, and have similar density** — this is encoded in the WCSS objective. When clusters are elongated, of varying sizes, or contain outliers, K-Medoids or DBSCAN (density-based) is preferred. KTU 2024 evaluators often test this assumption-awareness in viva and short-answer questions.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 K-Means Algorithm Flowchart (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start K-Means Algorithm]) --> A1[Input Dataset X with N points in R-d]
    A1 --> A2[Choose number of clusters K and max iterations t-max]
    A2 --> A3[Initialize K centroids mu-1 to mu-K using K-Means++ or random]
    A3 --> A4[Set iteration counter t = 1]
    A4 --> A5[ASSIGNMENT STEP: For each point x-i compute distance d x-i to mu-k]
    A5 --> A6[Assign x-i to nearest cluster c-i = argmin d x-i mu-k]
    A6 --> A7[UPDATE STEP: For each cluster k compute new centroid]
    A7 --> A8[mu-k-new = mean of all x-i with c-i = k]
    A8 --> A9[Convergence Check: Has mu changed or c-i stabilized]
    A9 --> A10{Converged or t reaches t-max}
    A10 -->|No| A11[Set t = t + 1 and set mu-k = mu-k-new]
    A11 --> A5
    A10 -->|Yes| A12[Output final clusters C1 to CK and centroids]
    A12 --> A13[Compute final WCSS J = sum sum d-squared]
    A13 --> A14([End Algorithm])
```

## 4.2 K-Means vs K-Medoids Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0([Clustering Problem Input]) --> B1{Is dataset small to medium N under 10000}
    B1 -->|Yes| B2{Does dataset contain outliers}
    B1 -->|No| B3[Use K-Means with Mini Batch for big data]
    B2 -->|Yes| B4[Use K-Medoids PAM algorithm]
    B2 -->|No| B5{Need non Euclidean distance metric}
    B5 -->|Yes| B4
    B5 -->|No| B6[Use K-Means as default choice]
    B3 --> B7{Need probabilistic soft assignments}
    B7 -->|Yes| B8[Use Gaussian Mixture Model GMM]
    B7 -->|No| B6
    B4 --> B9([Final Output Clusters])
    B6 --> B9
    B8 --> B9
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram of Iterative Convergence Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Input Data Points X] --> INIT[Random or K-Means Plus Plus Seeding]
    INIT --> CENT[Initial Centroids mu]
    CENT --> ASSIGN[Distance Computation and Assignment Phase]
    ASSIGN --> CLUST[Cluster Membership Vector c]
    CLUST --> UPDATE[Recompute Centroids as Means]
    UPDATE --> CENT
    UPDATE --> CHECK{Convergence Test}
    CHECK -->|Not Converged| ASSIGN
    CHECK -->|Converged| OUTPUT[Final Cluster Labels and WCSS Value]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Partitional Clustering Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P0([Raw Dataset]) --> P1[Data Cleaning and Normalization Step]
    P1 --> P2[Feature Scaling Standardization Min Max]
    P2 --> P3[Hyperparameter Selection for K via Elbow or Silhouette]
    P3 --> P4{Choose Algorithm Family}
    P4 --> P41[K-Means for Scalable Use Case]
    P4 --> P42[K-Medoids for Outlier Robust Use Case]
    P41 --> P5[Iterative Assignment and Update Loop]
    P42 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Convergence Diagnostic Check]
    P6 --> P7[Cluster Validation Internal and External]
    P7 --> P8([Final Cluster Assignments and Interpretation])
```

These diagrams depict the *control flow* of partitional clustering, which is the standard block-level architecture expected in KTU 2024 answers.
