# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between partitional and hierarchical clustering. State two advantages of K-Means over agglomerative clustering.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Partitional Clustering | Hierarchical Clustering |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Output structure | Single flat partition of $K$ clusters | Dendrogram (nested cluster tree) |
| Number of clusters $K$ | Pre-specified | Determined by cutting dendrogram |
| Computational cost | $O(NKdT)$ — low | $O(N^3)$ naive — high |
| Reversibility | Points can be reassigned later | Once merged/split, cannot be undone |

**Two advantages of K-Means over agglomerative clustering:**

1. **Computational efficiency:** K-Means scales as $O(NKdT)$, while agglomerative clustering requires $O(N^3)$ time (or $O(N^2 \log N)$ with optimization) and $O(N^2)$ memory for the distance matrix. For $N > 10{,}000$, K-Means is the only practical option.
2. **Lower sensitivity to noise in low dimensions and produces tighter, more compact clusters (in terms of WCSS) for spherical Gaussian-like data.** K-Means also allows iterative refinement, while agglomerative methods make greedy merge decisions that cannot be reversed.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is the objective function minimized by the K-Means algorithm? Why is K-Means sensitive to initial centroid placement?**

**Model Answer:**

**Objective Function:** K-Means minimizes the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**, also called the distortion or inertia:

$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \lVert x_i - \mu_k \rVert^2$$

where $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ is the centroid of cluster $C_k$.

**Sensitivity to initialization:** The K-Means objective $J$ is **non-convex**, so the iterative Lloyd's algorithm can converge to different **local minima** depending on where the initial centroids are placed. Bad initialization (e.g., two centroids very close together) can lead to:
- Empty or trivially small clusters
- Suboptimal cluster boundaries
- A higher final WCSS value

This is why the **K-Means++** initialization scheme (Arthur & Vassilvitskii, 2007) was developed — it spreads out initial centroids with probability proportional to $D(x)^2$, where $D(x)$ is the distance to the nearest already-chosen centroid, dramatically reducing the chance of poor local minima.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: each Part B question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. Internal choice must be offered.

### **Question B1 — Option A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the K-Means clustering algorithm. Explain its objective function and discuss why the arithmetic mean is the optimal centroid.

**Model Answer:**

K-Means is an iterative, partitional clustering algorithm proposed by Lloyd (1957) and Stuart Lloyd at Bell Labs, though it was independently discovered by Forgy (1965) and MacQueen (1967). It partitions a dataset $\mathcal{X} = \{x_1, \ldots, x_N\} \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ into $K$ disjoint clusters.

**Step 1 — Initialization:** Choose $K$ initial centroids $\{\mu_1^{(0)}, \ldots, \mu_K^{(0)}\}$ either by random selection of $K$ data points (Forgy method) or by the smarter **K-Means++** probabilistic seeding.

**Step 2 — Assignment:** Each data point is assigned to its nearest centroid:
$$c_i^{(t)} = \arg\min_{k \in \{1, \ldots, K\}} \lVert x_i - \mu_k^{(t)} \rVert^2$$

**Step 3 — Update:** Each centroid is recomputed as the mean of points in its cluster:
$$\mu_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i : c_i^{(t)} = k} x_i$$

**Step 4 — Convergence:** Stop when centroids no longer change, assignments are stable, or maximum iterations are reached.

**Objective Function:** K-Means minimizes WCSS, $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \lVert x_i - \mu_k \rVert^2$.

**Why the arithmetic mean is the optimal centroid:** For a fixed cluster $C_k$ of size $n_k$, we minimize $f(\mu) = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \lVert x_i - \mu \rVert^2$ over $\mu \in \mathbb{R}^d$. Setting $\nabla_\mu f = 0$ yields:

$$\nabla_\mu f = -2 \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i + 2 n_k \mu = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mu^* = \frac{1}{n_k} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$$

The Hessian is $2 n_k I \succ 0$, confirming a strict global minimum. Hence, the arithmetic mean is the *unique* point that minimizes the sum of squared Euclidean distances to all points in the cluster.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss three limitations of K-Means and briefly state how K-Medoids addresses them.

**Model Answer:**

**Limitation 1 — Sensitivity to outliers:** K-Means uses the arithmetic mean as centroid. A single extreme outlier can pull the centroid far from the true cluster center, distorting the cluster. *K-Medoids fix:* The medoid is always an **actual data point** in the cluster, so outliers cannot drag the medoid arbitrarily — they affect the sum of distances but the chosen point is the one minimizing total dissimilarity to other points.

**Limitation 2 — Restricted to Euclidean (mean-defined) distances:** The centroid formula $\mu = \bar{x}$ requires a vector-space mean, which is only meaningful in Euclidean settings. *K-Medoids fix:* K-Medoids uses an arbitrary dissimilarity function $\mathcal{D}(x_i, m_k)$ — Manhattan, cosine, Jaccard, or even non-metric distances — since the medoid is a discrete selection from the data, not an algebraic mean.

**Limitation 3 — Tendency to produce equal-sized, spherical clusters:** The WCSS objective favors isotropic Gaussian-like clusters. When clusters are elongated, of unequal density, or non-globular, K-Means fails. *K-Medoids fix:* Because the medoid is a real data point selected by total dissimilarity, it can conform to arbitrary cluster shapes better, though still imperfect. (For arbitrary shapes, density-based methods like DBSCAN are preferred.)

---

### **Question B1 — Option B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Apply K-Means clustering with $K = 2$ on the dataset: $P_1(1,1)$, $P_2(1,2)$, $P_3(2,1)$, $P_4(8,8)$, $P_5(8,9)$, $P_6(9,8)$. Use initial centroids $P_1$ and $P_4$. Show all iterations and compute the final WCSS.

**Model Answer:**

**Initial centroids:** $\mu_1^{(0)} = P_1(1,1)$, $\mu_2^{(0)} = P_4(8,8)$.

**Iteration 1 — Assignment:**

| Point | $d^2$ to $\mu_1^{(0)}$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_2^{(0)}$ | Cluster |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $P_1(1,1)$ | 0 | 98 | 1 |
| $P_2(1,2)$ | 1 | 85 | 1 |
| $P_3(2,1)$ | 1 | 85 | 1 |
| $P_4(8,8)$ | 98 | 0 | 2 |
| $P_5(8,9)$ | 98 | 1 | 2 |
| $P_6(9,8)$ | 106 | 1 | 2 |

Clusters: $C_1 = \{P_1, P_2, P_3\}$, $C_2 = \{P_4, P_5, P_6\}$.

**Iteration 1 — Centroid Update:**

$$\mu_1^{(1)} = \left(\frac{1+1+2}{3}, \frac{1+2+1}{3}\right) = \left(\frac{4}{3}, \frac{4}{3}\right) \approx (1.33, 1.33)$$

$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \left(\frac{8+8+9}{3}, \frac{8+9+8}{3}\right) = \left(\frac{25}{3}, \frac{25}{3}\right) \approx (8.33, 8.33)$$

**Iteration 2 — Re-Assignment:**

| Point | $d^2$ to $\mu_1^{(1)}$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_2^{(1)}$ | Cluster |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $P_1(1,1)$ | 0.22 | 107.78 | 1 |
| $P_2(1,2)$ | 0.55 | 102.78 | 1 |
| $P_3(2,1)$ | 0.55 | 102.78 | 1 |
| $P_4(8,8)$ | 107.78 | 0.22 | 2 |
| $P_5(8,9)$ | 102.78 | 0.55 | 2 |
| $P_6(9,8)$ | 102.78 | 0.55 | 2 |

Cluster assignments **unchanged** → **Converged in 2 iterations**.

**Final centroids:** $\mu_1 = (1.33, 1.33)$, $\mu_2 = (8.33, 8.33)$.

**Final WCSS Calculation:**

For $C_1$: $0.22 + 0.55 + 0.55 = 1.32$ (using rounded centroids; exact: $\frac{2}{3} + \frac{2}{3} + \frac{2}{3} = 2$)
For $C_2$: $0.22 + 0.55 + 0.55 = 1.32$ (exact: $2$)

**Total WCSS:** $J = 1.32 + 1.32 \approx 2.64$ (exact: $J = 4$)

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Elbow Method and the Silhouette Coefficient as techniques for choosing the optimal number of clusters $K$.

**Model Answer:**

**Elbow Method:** Compute the WCSS $J(K)$ for $K = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, K_{\max}$ and plot $J$ vs $K$. As $K$ increases, $J$ monotonically decreases (more clusters always fit better), but the *rate* of decrease slows. The optimal $K$ is the **"elbow point"** — the value of $K$ where adding another cluster does not give a substantially better model. The intuition is: beyond the elbow, you are over-fragmenting natural clusters.

**Mathematical Insight:** $J(K) = 0$ when $K = N$ (each point is its own cluster), and $J(1) = \sum_i \lVert x_i - \bar{x} \rVert^2$ (total variance). The elbow balances model fit vs. parsimony.

**Silhouette Coefficient:** For each data point $i$, define:
- $a(i) = \frac{1}{\vert C_{c_i} \vert - 1} \sum_{j \in C_{c_i}, j \neq i} d(i,j)$ — mean intra-cluster distance (cohesion)
- $b(i) = \min_{k \neq c_i} \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{j \in C_k} d(i,j)$ — mean distance to nearest other cluster (separation)
- Silhouette score: $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$

**Range of $s(i)$:**
- $s(i) \approx +1$: point is well-matched to its cluster and far from neighbors
- $s(i) \approx 0$: point lies on the boundary between two clusters
- $s(i) \approx -1$: point may be assigned to the wrong cluster

The optimal $K$ maximizes the **mean silhouette score** $\bar{s} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i s(i)$ across all points.

**Comparison:** The Elbow Method is intuitive and fast but subjective (the "elbow" is not always sharp). The Silhouette method is more rigorous and quantitative but computationally heavier (requires $O(N^2)$ pairwise distance computations per $K$).

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the squared Euclidean distance notation** in the assignment step. The rule is $c_i = \arg\min_k \lVert x_i - \mu_k \rVert^2$, not $\arg\min_k \lVert x_i - \mu_k \rVert$ — though both give the same result since $\sqrt{\cdot}$ is monotonic. Examiners want the squared form because it matches the WCSS objective.
> 2. **Not showing centroid recomputation** after every assignment. Half-marks are deducted if only the assignment step is shown.
> 3. **Failing to verify convergence** — KTU expects an explicit statement: "Cluster assignments are unchanged / centroids are unchanged → algorithm converges." Without this, full marks are not awarded.
> 4. **Confusing K-Means with KNN** — K-Means is *unsupervised* clustering; KNN is *supervised* classification. This confusion costs entire questions in the ESE.
> 5. **Forgetting to standardize features** before applying K-Means. K-Means uses Euclidean distance, so features on different scales (e.g., age in years vs. income in lakhs) will dominate the clustering. Always apply StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler first.
> 6. **Stating "K-Means always finds the global minimum"** — this is FALSE. K-Means converges to a *local* minimum, which is why K-Means++ and multiple random restarts are recommended.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Partitional clustering** produces a *single flat partition* of $N$ data points into $K$ clusters, with each point belonging to exactly one cluster (hard partition).
- **K-Means** is the canonical partitional algorithm. It minimizes **WCSS** (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares): $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \lVert x_i - \mu_k \rVert^2$.
- The **centroid update rule** is the arithmetic mean: $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ — derived via calculus ($\nabla_\mu J = 0$).
- The **assignment rule** is nearest-centroid: $c_i = \arg\min_k \lVert x_i - \mu_k \rVert^2$.
- **K-Means complexity** is $O(NKdT)$ — much cheaper than hierarchical $O(N^3)$.
- **K-Means++** initialization is the standard smart seeding technique to avoid poor local minima.
- **Convergence** is in finite steps (monotonic WCSS decrease + finite partitions), but to a *local* minimum, not global.
- **K-Medoids (PAM)** uses actual data points as centroids, is robust to outliers, and supports arbitrary distance metrics.
- **Choosing $K$:** Elbow Method (visual) or Silhouette Coefficient ($s(i) \in [-1, 1]$).
- **Assumptions of K-Means:** clusters are isotropic, equally sized, similar density, and Euclidean distance is appropriate.
- **Limitations:** outlier sensitivity, empty clusters, local minima, $K$ must be pre-specified, cannot handle non-globular shapes.
- **Preprocessing:** Always **standardize features** (zero mean, unit variance) before K-Means.
- **Big data variant:** Mini-Batch K-Means uses random subsamples for faster convergence on massive datasets.
- **Comparison anchor:** Partitional ≠ Hierarchical — flat partition vs. dendrogram. KTU 2024 expects students to articulate this distinction clearly in viva and written exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: K-means clustering. Naive Bayes Classifier - Working Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAIT404 - FOUNDATION OF MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** K-means clustering. Naive Bayes Classifier - Working Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 K-Means Clustering

### Formal Definition
**K-Means Clustering** is an iterative, centroid-based, unsupervised learning algorithm that partitions a dataset of $N$ observations into $K$ non-overlapping clusters, where each observation belongs to the cluster with the nearest centroid (mean). The algorithm aims to minimize the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**, also called the **inertia** or **distortion**.

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$$

where $C_i$ is the $i^{th}$ cluster and $\mu_i$ is the centroid of cluster $C_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** K-Means belongs to the family of **partitional clustering** algorithms. It is a **hard clustering** method (each point belongs to exactly one cluster), as opposed to soft/fuzzy clustering (e.g., Fuzzy C-Means).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you are a school principal organizing **500 students** into **K = 4** houses based on their height and weight. You have **no predefined labels**. Your strategy:
1. Randomly pick 4 students as initial "house captains" (centroids).
2. Every other student joins the house whose captain is physically closest to them.
3. After all students are assigned, recompute each house's captain as the **average** height/weight of the members.
4. Repeat steps 2–3 until captains stop shifting.

This is exactly how **K-Means** operates on data points!

---

## 1.2 Naive Bayes Classifier

### Formal Definition
The **Naive Bayes Classifier** is a probabilistic supervised learning algorithm based on applying **Bayes' Theorem** with a **"naive" assumption of conditional independence** between every pair of features given the class label. It computes the posterior probability of each class and predicts the class with the highest posterior.

$$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(C_k) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Naive" Assumption:** All features $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ are mutually independent given the class $C_k$. This is rarely true in practice, but the algorithm still performs remarkably well!

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine an email arrives. You want to know: **"Is it Spam?"** You observe certain words: *"free, offer, click, money"*. A Naive Bayes classifier assumes the presence of each word is **independent** of the others, given that the email is spam. It then multiplies their individual probabilities (scaled by the prior spam rate) to compute the final spam probability. It is "naive" because, in reality, the words "free" and "offer" often appear together — but the algorithm ignores this correlation.

### Why Use It?

| Algorithm | Type | Key Use Case |
|-----------|------|--------------|
| **K-Means** | Unsupervised | Customer segmentation, document clustering, image compression, anomaly detection |
| **Naive Bayes** | Supervised | Spam filtering, sentiment analysis, medical diagnosis, text classification, real-time prediction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** In Part B questions, examiners frequently ask you to **state the assumption** that makes Naive Bayes "naive." Always write: *"The Naive Bayes classifier assumes that all features are conditionally independent of each other given the class label."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 K-Means Clustering: Operational Logic

The algorithm is an **Expectation-Maximization (EM) style iterative refinement** with two alternating steps:

### Step 1: Cluster Assignment (Expectation Step)
For every data point $x_j$, assign it to the cluster whose centroid is closest (using **squared Euclidean distance**):

$$c^{(t)}_j = \arg\min_{i \in \{1,\ldots,K\}} \Vert x_j - \mu_i^{(t)} \Vert^2$$

### Step 2: Centroid Update (Maximization Step)
Recompute each centroid as the **mean of all points assigned** to its cluster:

$$\mu_i^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_i^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{x_j \in C_i^{(t)}} x_j$$

### Convergence Criterion
The algorithm terminates when one of the following holds:
- Centroid positions stop changing: $\Vert \mu_i^{(t+1)} - \mu_i^{(t)} \Vert < \epsilon$
- The objective function $J$ plateaus: $\vert J^{(t+1)} - J^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$
- A maximum number of iterations is reached

### Why "Why" Works — Intuition
The assignment step **decreases** the WCSS (each point is placed optimally for the current centroids). The update step **further decreases** the WCSS (the mean minimizes squared distance). Monotonic decrease guarantees convergence to a **local minimum**.

### Limitations
- **Sensitive to initialization** → use **K-Means++** for better seeds
- Must pre-specify **K**
- Sensitive to **outliers** and **scaling** → standardize features first
- Assumes **spherical, equal-sized clusters** of similar density
- Converges to **local minima**, not global

---

## 2.2 Naive Bayes Classifier: Operational Logic

### Bayes' Theorem Foundation
$$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(C_k) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ is constant for all classes, we maximize the numerator:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{C_k} P(C_k) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)$$

### Applying the Naive Assumption
With conditional independence:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

So the classification rule becomes:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{C_k} P(C_k) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

### Types of Naive Bayes (Based on $P(x_i \mid C_k)$)

| Variant | Assumption on $P(x_i \mid C_k)$ | Use Case |
|---------|-------------------------------|----------|
| **Gaussian NB** | Features follow a **Normal (Gaussian) distribution** | Continuous data (e.g., iris dataset) |
| **Multinomial NB** | Features are **counts** (multinomial distribution) | Text classification, bag-of-words |
| **Bernoulli NB** | Features are **binary/boolean** | Spam detection (word present/absent) |

### Log-Probability Trick (Avoid Numerical Underflow)
Products of small probabilities → 0 (floating point underflow). Take the **logarithm**:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{C_k} \left[ \log P(C_k) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid C_k) \right]$$

This also converts multiplication into a fast **sum**.

### Laplace (Additive) Smoothing
If a feature value never occurs with class $C_k$ in training data, $P(x_i \mid C_k) = 0$, zeroing out the entire product. **Laplace smoothing** fixes this:

$$P(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i, C_k) + \alpha}{\text{count}(C_k) + \alpha \cdot V}$$

where $V$ is the vocabulary size and $\alpha \geq 1$ (typically $\alpha = 1$).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### K-Means Clustering

| Formula / Quantity | Expression | Notes |
|--------------------|-----------|-------|
| **Objective (WCSS)** | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$ | To be minimized |
| **Cluster Assignment** | $c_j = \arg\min_i \Vert x_j - \mu_i \Vert^2$ | Squared Euclidean |
| **Centroid Update** | $\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{x_j \in C_i} x_j$ | Mean of cluster |
| **Distance Metric** | $d(x, \mu) = \sqrt{\sum_{d=1}^{D} (x_d - \mu_d)^2}$ | Euclidean |
| **Complexity** | $O(N \cdot K \cdot I \cdot D)$ | $I$ = iterations, $D$ = dimensions |

### Naive Bayes Classifier

| Formula / Quantity | Expression | Notes |
|--------------------|-----------|-------|
| **Bayes' Theorem** | $P(C \mid x) = \frac{P(C) \cdot P(x \mid C)}{P(x)}$ | Foundation |
| **Decision Rule** | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_C P(C) \cdot \prod_i P(x_i \mid C)$ | Ignore denominator |
| **Class Prior** | $P(C_k) = \frac{N_k}{N}$ | Frequency-based |
| **Likelihood (Categorical)** | $P(x_i = v \mid C_k) = \frac{N_{v,C_k}}{N_{C_k}}$ | MLE estimate |
| **Gaussian Likelihood** | $P(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_k^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu_k)^2}{2\sigma_k^2}\right)$ | For continuous features |
| **Laplace Smoothing** | $P(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{N_{x_i, C_k} + \alpha}{N_{C_k} + \alpha V}$ | $\alpha = 1$ standard |
| **Log-Form** | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_C \left[ \log P(C) + \sum_i \log P(x_i \mid C) \right]$ | Numerical stability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **K-Means** is the workhorse of **unsupervised learning** in industry — used by Netflix for recommendation preprocessing, by Google for image compression (vector quantization), and in customer analytics.
> - **Naive Bayes** is the **fastest probabilistic classifier** — used as the baseline for spam filters (Gmail, Yahoo), medical diagnosis systems, and real-time sentiment analysis where interpretability and speed matter.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: K-Means on a 2D Toy Dataset (KTU Board Pattern)

**Given Data Points:** $P_1=(2,10),\ P_2=(2,5),\ P_3=(8,4),\ P_4=(5,8),\ P_5=(7,5),\ P_6=(6,4),\ P_7=(1,2),\ P_8=(4,9)$

**Parameters:** $K=2$, initial centroids $\mu_1^{(0)} = (2,10)$, $\mu_2^{(0)} = (5,8)$.

**Task:** Perform 2 iterations of K-Means and find the final cluster assignments.

---

### Iteration 1

**Step 1.1 — Compute Euclidean distance from each point to both centroids.**

Distance formula: $d(P, \mu) = \sqrt{(x - \mu_x)^2 + (y - \mu_y)^2}$

For $P_1 = (2,10)$:
- $d(P_1, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (10-10)^2} = 0$
- $d(P_1, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(2-5)^2 + (10-8)^2} = \sqrt{9+4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$

For $P_2 = (2,5)$:
- $d(P_2, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (5-10)^2} = 5$
- $d(P_2, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(2-5)^2 + (5-8)^2} = \sqrt{9+9} = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.24$

For $P_3 = (8,4)$:
- $d(P_3, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(8-2)^2 + (4-10)^2} = \sqrt{36+36} = \sqrt{72} \approx 8.49$
- $d(P_3, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(8-5)^2 + (4-8)^2} = \sqrt{9+16} = 5$

For $P_4 = (5,8)$:
- $d(P_4, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(5-2)^2 + (8-10)^2} = \sqrt{9+4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$
- $d(P_4, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(5-5)^2 + (8-8)^2} = 0$

For $P_5 = (7,5)$:
- $d(P_5, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(7-2)^2 + (5-10)^2} = \sqrt{25+25} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.07$
- $d(P_5, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(7-5)^2 + (5-8)^2} = \sqrt{4+9} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$

For $P_6 = (6,4)$:
- $d(P_6, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(6-2)^2 + (4-10)^2} = \sqrt{16+36} = \sqrt{52} \approx 7.21$
- $d(P_6, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(6-5)^2 + (4-8)^2} = \sqrt{1+16} = \sqrt{17} \approx 4.12$

For $P_7 = (1,2)$:
- $d(P_7, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (2-10)^2} = \sqrt{1+64} = \sqrt{65} \approx 8.06$
- $d(P_7, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(1-5)^2 + (2-8)^2} = \sqrt{16+36} = \sqrt{52} \approx 7.21$

For $P_8 = (4,9)$:
- $d(P_8, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(4-2)^2 + (9-10)^2} = \sqrt{4+1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$
- $d(P_8, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(4-5)^2 + (9-8)^2} = \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$

**Step 1.2 — Assignment Table**

| Point | $d$ to $\mu_1$ | $d$ to $\mu_2$ | Assigned Cluster |
|-------|---------------|---------------|------------------|
| $P_1$ | 0 | 3.61 | $C_1$ |
| $P_2$ | 5 | 4.24 | $C_2$ |
| $P_3$ | 8.49 | 5 | $C_2$ |
| $P_4$ | 3.61 | 0 | $C_2$ |
| $P_5$ | 7.07 | 3.61 | $C_2$ |
| $P_6$ | 7.21 | 4.12 | $C_2$ |
| $P_7$ | 8.06 | 7.21 | $C_2$ |
| $P_8$ | 2.24 | 1.41 | $C_2$ |

**Step 1.3 — Update Centroids**

- $C_1 = \{P_1\} = \{(2,10)\}$, so $\mu_1^{(1)} = (2, 10)$ *(unchanged)*.
- $C_2 = \{P_2, P_3, P_4, P_5, P_6, P_7, P_8\}$

$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \left( \frac{2+8+5+7+6+1+4}{7}, \frac{5+4+8+5+4+2+9}{7} \right) = \left( \frac{33}{7}, \frac{37}{7} \right) \approx (4.71, 5.29)$$

---

### Iteration 2

**Step 2.1 — Recompute distances with new centroids $\mu_1 = (2,10)$ and $\mu_2 = (4.71, 5.29)$.**

For $P_1=(2,10)$: $d(\mu_1)=0$, $d(\mu_2)=\sqrt{(2-4.71)^2+(10-5.29)^2}=\sqrt{7.34+22.18}=\sqrt{29.52}\approx 5.43$ → $C_1$.

For $P_2=(2,5)$: $d(\mu_1)=\sqrt{0+25}=5$, $d(\mu_2)=\sqrt{(2-4.71)^2+(5-5.29)^2}=\sqrt{7.34+0.084}\approx 2.72$ → $C_2$.

For $P_3=(8,4)$: $d(\mu_1)=\sqrt{36+36}\approx 8.49$, $d(\mu_2)=\sqrt{(8-4.71)^2+(4-5.29)^2}=\sqrt{10.82+1.66}\approx 3.53$ → $C_2$.

For $P_4=(5,8)$: $d(\mu_1)=\sqrt{9+4}\approx 3.61$, $d(\mu_2)=\sqrt{(5-4.71)^2+(8-5.29)^2}=\sqrt{0.084+7.34}\approx 2.72$ → $C_2$.

For $P_5=(7,5)$: $d(\mu_1)=\sqrt{25+25}\approx 7.07$, $d(\mu_2)=\sqrt{(7-4.71)^2+(5-5.29)^2}=\sqrt{5.24+0.084}\approx 2.31$ → $C_2$.

For $P_6=(6,4)$: $d(\mu_1)=\sqrt{16+36}\approx 7.21$, $d(\mu_2)=\sqrt{(6-4.71)^2+(4-5.29)^2}=\sqrt{1.66+1.66}\approx 1.82$ → $C_2$.

For $P_7=(1,2)$: $d(\mu_1)=\sqrt{1+64}\approx 8.06$, $d(\mu_2)=\sqrt{(1-4.71)^2+(2-5.29)^2}=\sqrt{13.76+10.82}\approx 4.96$ → $C_2$.

For $P_8=(4,9)$: $d(\mu_1)=\sqrt{4+1}\approx 2.24$, $d(\mu_2)=\sqrt{(4-4.71)^2+(9-5.29)^2}=\sqrt{0.50+13.76}\approx 3.78$ → $C_1$.

**Step 2.2 — New Assignment**

| Point | Cluster |
|-------|---------|
| $P_1$ | $C_1$ |
| $P_2, P_3, P_4, P_5, P_6, P_7$ | $C_2$ |
| $P_8$ | $C_1$ |

**Step 2.3 — Updated Centroids**

- $C_1 = \{P_1, P_8\} = \{(2,10), (4,9)\}$, so $\mu_1^{(2)} = (3, 9.5)$.
- $C_2 = \{P_2, P_3, P_4, P_5, P_6, P_7\}$

$$\mu_2^{(2)} = \left( \frac{2+8+5+7+6+1}{6}, \frac{5+4+8+5+4+2}{6} \right) = \left( \frac{29}{6}, \frac{28}{6} \right) \approx (4.83, 4.67)$$

**Step 2.4 — Compute WCSS for convergence check**

$$J^{(2)} = \Vert (2,10)-(3,9.5) \Vert^2 + \Vert (4,9)-(3,9.5) \Vert^2 + \sum_{x \in C_2} \Vert x - (4.83, 4.67) \Vert^2$$

If the change $\vert J^{(2)} - J^{(1)} \vert < \epsilon$ (e.g., $\epsilon = 0.001$), the algorithm converges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final answer (after convergence):** $C_1 = \{P_1, P_8\}$ with centroid $(3, 9.5)$ and $C_2 = \{P_2, P_3, P_4, P_5, P_6, P_7\}$ with centroid $(4.83, 4.67)$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Naive Bayes with Laplace Smoothing (KTU Board Pattern)

**Training Data (Play Tennis Dataset — Classic ML Example):**

| Day | Outlook | Temp | Humidity | Wind | Play |
|-----|---------|------|----------|------|------|
| 1   | Sunny   | Hot  | High     | Weak | No   |
| 2   | Sunny   | Hot  | High     | Strong | No |
| 3   | Overcast| Hot  | High     | Weak | Yes  |
| 4   | Rain    | Mild | High     | Weak | Yes  |
| 5   | Rain    | Cool | Normal   | Weak | Yes  |
| 6   | Rain    | Cool | Normal   | Strong | No |
| 7   | Overcast| Cool | Normal   | Strong | Yes  |
| 8   | Sunny   | Mild | High     | Weak | No   |
| 9   | Sunny   | Cool | Normal   | Weak | Yes  |
| 10  | Rain    | Mild | Normal   | Weak | Yes  |
| 11  | Sunny   | Mild | Normal   | Strong | Yes |
| 12  | Overcast| Mild | High     | Strong | Yes |
| 13  | Overcast| Hot  | Normal   | Weak | Yes  |
| 14  | Rain    | Mild | High     | Strong | No  |

**Total:** $N = 14$, Yes = 9, No = 5.

**Test Instance:** $\mathbf{x} = (\text{Outlook=Sunny, Temp=Cool, Humidity=High, Wind=Strong})$ → Predict class.

---

### Step 1: Class Priors

$$P(\text{Yes}) = \frac{9}{14}, \quad P(\text{No}) = \frac{5}{14}$$

### Step 2: Compute Likelihoods with Laplace Smoothing ($\alpha = 1$)

For categorical features, the number of possible values $V$ is:
- Outlook: 3 (Sunny, Overcast, Rain)
- Temp: 3 (Hot, Mild, Cool)
- Humidity: 2 (High, Normal)
- Wind: 2 (Weak, Strong)

#### For Class = Yes (count = 9)

| Feature | Count(Yes) | $P(\cdot \mid \text{Yes})$ with smoothing |
|---------|-----------|------------------------------------------|
| Outlook = Sunny | 2 | $(2+1)/(9+3) = 3/12 = 0.25$ |
| Temp = Cool | 3 | $(3+1)/(9+3) = 4/12 = 0.333$ |
| Humidity = High | 3 | $(3+1)/(9+2) = 4/11 \approx 0.364$ |
| Wind = Strong | 3 | $(3+1)/(9+2) = 4/11 \approx 0.364$ |

#### For Class = No (count = 5)

| Feature | Count(No) | $P(\cdot \mid \text{No})$ with smoothing |
|---------|----------|-----------------------------------------|
| Outlook = Sunny | 3 | $(3+1)/(5+3) = 4/8 = 0.5$ |
| Temp = Cool | 1 | $(1+1)/(5+3) = 2/8 = 0.25$ |
| Humidity = High | 4 | $(4+1)/(5+2) = 5/7 \approx 0.714$ |
| Wind = Strong | 3 | $(3+1)/(5+2) = 4/7 \approx 0.571$ |

### Step 3: Compute Posterior (Numerators)

**For Class = Yes:**

$$P(\text{Yes}) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{9}{14} \times 0.25 \times 0.333 \times 0.364 \times 0.364$$

$$= 0.643 \times 0.25 \times 0.333 \times 0.364 \times 0.364$$

Computing step-by-step:

$$0.643 \times 0.25 = 0.1607$$

$$0.1607 \times 0.333 = 0.0535$$

$$0.0535 \times 0.364 = 0.0195$$

$$0.0195 \times 0.364 = 0.00710$$

So $P(\mathbf{x} \mid \text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Yes}) \approx 0.00710$.

**For Class = No:**

$$P(\text{No}) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{5}{14} \times 0.5 \times 0.25 \times 0.714 \times 0.571$$

Step-by-step:

$$0.357 \times 0.5 = 0.1786$$

$$0.1786 \times 0.25 = 0.0446$$

$$0.0446 \times 0.714 = 0.0319$$

$$0.0319 \times 0.571 = 0.0182$$

So $P(\mathbf{x} \mid \text{No}) \cdot P(\text{No}) \approx 0.0182$.

### Step 4: Normalize (Optional)

$$P(\text{Yes} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{0.00710}{0.00710 + 0.0182} = \frac{0.00710}{0.0253} \approx 0.281$$

$$P(\text{No} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{0.0182}{0.0253} \approx 0.719$$

### Step 5: Decision

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max\{0.281, 0.719\} = \text{No}$$

**Conclusion:** The classifier predicts that tennis will **NOT** be played for the test instance (Sunny, Cool, High humidity, Strong wind) with probability $\approx 71.9\%$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical note:** The denominators $P(\mathbf{x})$ are the same in both cases, so we can compare numerators directly. The exact ratio $0.00710 : 0.0182 \approx 1 : 2.56$ shows the "No" class is about $2.56 \times$ more likely.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 K-Means Clustering: Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Dataset X and K] --> B[Initialize K Centroids]
    B --> B1{Initialization Method}
    B1 -->|Random| B2[Pick K random points as mu1 mu2 ... muK]
    B1 -->|K-Means Plus Plus| B3[Pick first centroid randomly, then weighted by distance squared]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C[Iteration Counter t = 0] --> D[Assignment Step: For each xj compute d xj to mui]
    D --> E[Assign xj to nearest centroid: cj equals argmin d]
    E --> F[Update Step: Recompute mui as mean of all points in cluster Ci]
    F --> G{Convergence Check}
    G -->|Centroids unchanged or J plateau| H[Stop: Output clusters C1 ... CK and centroids]
    G -->|Not converged| I[Increment t to t plus 1]
    I --> D
    H --> J[End]
```

## 4.2 K-Means Convergence: Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        A1[Raw Dataset X of N points with D features]
        A2[Number of clusters K]
        A3[Initialization seeds or method]
    end
    subgraph CORE[Algorithm Core]
        B1[Distance Computation Module]
        B2[Assignment Engine]
        B3[Centroid Update Engine]
        B4[Convergence Monitor]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        C1[Final Cluster Labels C1 ... CK]
        C2[Final Centroids mu1 ... muK]
        C3[WCSS Objective Value J]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 -->|Converged| C1
    B4 -->|Converged| C2
    B4 -->|Converged| C3
    B4 -->|Not Converged| B1
```

## 4.3 Naive Bayes: Bayes' Theorem Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Instance x = x1 x2 ... xn] --> B[Compute Prior P Ck for each class]
    B --> C[Compute Likelihood P xi equals v given Ck]
    C --> D{Feature Type}
    D -->|Categorical| E1[Use Frequency Table with Laplace Smoothing]
    D -->|Continuous| E2[Use Gaussian PDF with mu and sigma from training]
    D -->|Binary| E3[Use Bernoulli Likelihood]
    E1 --> F[Apply Naive Independence: Multiply all P xi given Ck]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Multiply by Prior P Ck]
    G --> H[Take Log for Numerical Stability]
    H --> I[argmax over all classes Ck]
    I --> J[Output Predicted Class y hat]
```

## 4.4 Naive Bayes: Spam Classification Use Case

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRAINING[Training Phase]
        T1[Collect Labeled Emails: Spam and Ham]
        T2[Build Vocabulary from all emails]
        T3[Compute Prior P Spam and P Ham]
        T4[For each word wi compute P wi given Spam and P wi given Ham]
    end
    subgraph INFERENCE[Inference Phase]
        I1[New Email Arrives]
        I2[Extract Words w1 w2 ... wn from email]
        I3[Apply Bayes: Compute P Spam given words and P Ham given words]
        I4[Compare and Pick Maximum]
    end
    subgraph DECISION[Decision Output]
        O1[If P Spam greater than P Ham: Label as SPAM]
        O2[Else: Label as HAM]
    end
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T2 --> T4
    T3 --> I3
    T4 --> I3
    I1 --> I2
    I2 --> I3
    I3 --> I4
    I4 --> O1
    I4 --> O2
```

## 4.5 Comparative Block Diagram: K-Means vs Naive Bayes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph KMEANS[K Means Clustering]
        K1[Unsupervised Learning]
        K2[No Labels Required]
        K3[Output: Group Structure and Centroids]
        K4[Objective: Minimize WCSS]
    end
    subgraph NB[Naive Bayes Classifier]
        N1[Supervised Learning]
        N2[Requires Labeled Training Data]
        N3[Output: Predicted Class Label]
        N4[Objective: Maximize Posterior Probability]
    end
    K1 -.Contrast.- N1
    K2 -.Contrast.- N2
    K3 -.Contrast.- N3
    K4 -.Contrast.- N4
```
