- $FN$: Predicted Negative, Actually Positive (Type II error)
- $TN$: Predicted Negative, Actually Negative

**Derivation of Precision:**

Precision is the fraction of *correct positive claims* among *all positive claims*:

$$
P = \frac{\text{True Positives}}{\text{All Predicted Positives}} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

**Derivation of Recall:**

Recall is the fraction of *correct positive claims* among *all actual positives*:

$$
R = \frac{\text{True Positives}}{\text{All Actual Positives}} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step (i)** — Extract values from the confusion matrix:
- $TN = 130$, $FP = 20$, $FN = 15$, $TP = 85$
- Total = $130 + 20 + 15 + 85 = 250$ ✓

**Step (ii)** — Compute Precision:
$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{85}{85 + 20} = \frac{85}{105} = \frac{17}{21} \approx 0.8095 \approx 80.95\%
$$

**Step (iii)** — Compute Recall:
$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{85}{85 + 15} = \frac{85}{100} = 0.85 = 85\%
$$

**Step (iv)** — Compute Accuracy:
$$
\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN} = \frac{85 + 130}{250} = \frac{215}{250} = 0.86 = 86\%
$$

**Step (v)** — Compute F1-Score (harmonic mean):
$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.8095 \cdot 0.85}{0.8095 + 0.85} = \frac{1.3762}{1.6595} \approx 0.8292 \approx 82.92\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The dataset is somewhat imbalanced (200 negatives vs. 100 positives). **Accuracy (86%)** is misleading because it is dominated by the majority class (negatives). **F1-Score (82.92%)** and **Precision/Recall** provide a more reliable picture. In imbalanced settings, **F1-Score is the most informative** because it balances both error types.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**
> **(a)** Explain the Precision-Recall Tradeoff in probabilistic classifiers. Show how Precision changes as the decision threshold is varied. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A medical diagnostic model is tested on 500 patient records. The confusion matrix obtained is:
>
> |               | Predicted Healthy | Predicted Diseased |
> |---------------|-------------------|--------------------|
> | Actually Healthy | 320            | 30                 |
> | Actually Diseased | 20             | 130                |
>
> Calculate Precision for the "Diseased" class. If the hospital prioritizes **avoiding false alarms** (i.e., does not want healthy patients wrongly diagnosed as diseased), is this model acceptable? Justify. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

A probabilistic classifier outputs $p(y=1 \mid x) \in [0,1]$. The decision rule is:
$$
\hat{y} = \mathbb{1}[p(y=1 \mid x) \geq \tau]
$$
where $\tau$ is the **decision threshold** (default $\tau = 0.5$).

**Effect of varying $\tau$:**

- **Decrease $\tau$ (e.g., $\tau = 0.3$)**:
  - More samples cross the threshold → predicted positives $\uparrow$
  - $TP \uparrow$ and $FP \uparrow$
  - Recall $\uparrow$ (more positives caught) but Precision $\downarrow$ (more false alarms)

- **Increase $\tau$ (e.g., $\tau = 0.7$)**:
  - Fewer samples cross the threshold → predicted positives $\downarrow$
  - $TP \downarrow$ and $FP \downarrow$
  - Precision $\uparrow$ (fewer false alarms) but Recall $\downarrow$ (more misses)

The **Precision-Recall Curve (PRC)** is a plot of $P(\tau)$ vs. $R(\tau)$ as $\tau$ sweeps from 0 to 1. The relationship is **monotonically decreasing** in the typical case — high precision comes at the cost of low recall and vice versa. This is the **Precision-Recall Tradeoff**.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step (i)** — Extract values:
- $TN = 320$ (Healthy correctly identified)
- $FP = 30$ (Healthy wrongly flagged as Diseased)
- $FN = 20$ (Diseased missed)
- $TP = 130$ (Diseased correctly identified)

**Step (ii)** — Compute Precision for the "Diseased" class:
$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{130}{130 + 30} = \frac{130}{160} = \frac{13}{16} = 0.8125 = 81.25\%
$$

**Step (iii)** — Interpret for the hospital's priority:
- The hospital wants to **minimize False Positives** (healthy patients wrongly diagnosed).
- $FP = 30$ out of 350 healthy patients → $FPR = 30/350 \approx 8.57\%$.
- Precision = 81.25% means that **of all patients flagged as diseased, 81.25% are actually diseased** — a relatively trustworthy positive diagnosis.

**Justification**: An 81.25% precision is **moderately acceptable** but not ideal for a high-stakes medical context. The hospital should aim for a precision of at least 90–95% to minimize false alarms. The model **can be improved** by either raising the decision threshold (trading off some recall) or using a more discriminative algorithm. Hence, this model is **borderline acceptable** and warrants further optimization.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Swapping TP and FP**: Students often confuse the *column/row* orientation of the confusion matrix. Always state explicitly: "$TP$ = Predicted Positive AND Actually Positive."
> 2. **Forgetting the Denominator**: Some students write $P = TP$ alone. Always include the full denominator $TP + FP$.
> 3. **Ignoring Multi-Class Averaging**: In multi-class problems, KTU expects the student to clarify **macro** vs **micro** averaging. Default without clarification is **macro**, but explicit mention earns full marks.
> 4. **Misinterpreting Precision-Recall Tradeoff**: Students often state the tradeoff is *linear* — it is not. The actual relationship is **non-linear and threshold-dependent**, governed by the classifier's score distribution.
> 5. **Dividing by Zero**: If $TP + FP = 0$ (model predicts no positives), students should explicitly state the precision is **undefined** and use the convention $P = 0$ (or "Not Applicable").
> 6. **Unit Errors**: Precision is dimensionless (a ratio). Do not attach units like "%" unless explicitly converted: $P \times 100\%$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Precision** = $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$; measures the **exactness** of positive predictions.
- It is **sensitive to False Positives** but **insensitive to True Negatives** and **False Negatives**.
- **Range**: $[0, 1]$; **Ideal** = $1.0$; **Failure** = $0.0$.
- **Use Precision** when the cost of a False Positive is **high** (e.g., spam filters, fraud detection, medical false alarms).
- **Use Recall** when the cost of a False Negative is **high** (e.g., cancer screening, criminal escape).
- **Use F1-Score** when both error types matter and the dataset is **imbalanced**.
- Precision is **threshold-dependent** in probabilistic classifiers — vary $\tau$ to navigate the **P-R Tradeoff**.
- **Macro-averaged** precision treats all classes equally; **Micro-averaged** precision weights by class frequency.
- In KTU numerical problems, **always** show step-by-step substitution of $TP$ and $FP$ values — partial credit depends on visible working.
- For multi-class problems, compute **per-class precision first**, then aggregate.
- Precision is **invariant** to changes in the number of true negatives — adding more clearly-negative samples does not change it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recall
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAIT404 - FOUNDATION OF MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recall

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Recall** is a fundamental **classification evaluation metric** that quantifies the proportion of *actual positive instances* that a machine learning model correctly identifies as positive. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, Recall is classified under the family of **threshold-dependent performance metrics** used in **supervised binary (and multi-class) classification**.

Formally, Recall is defined as the ratio of **True Positives (TP)** to the sum of **True Positives (TP)** and **False Negatives (FN)**.

It is also known by the following synonymous terms encountered in KTU question papers:
- **Sensitivity**
- **True Positive Rate (TPR)**
- **Hit Rate**
- **Completeness**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Recall is one of the four pillars of the **Confusion Matrix-based evaluation framework**, alongside **Precision, Specificity, and F1-Score**. In Module 4 of PBAIT404, this metric is typically examined alongside Precision to highlight the **Precision-Recall Trade-off**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **medical diagnostic test** designed to detect a deadly disease. Out of **100 patients who actually have the disease**, the test correctly flags **90** as sick but misses **10**.

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{\text{Patients Correctly Detected}}{\text{All Sick Patients}} = \frac{90}{100} = 0.90$$

The intuition is simple: **Of all the truly positive cases in existence, how many did our model manage to catch?** A high Recall means the model has a strong "net" — very few positives escape detection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Device for KTU Exams:** "Recall = **Re**covery" — what fraction of real positives did the model **Re**cover and not let slip through?

### 1.3 Why Recall Matters in Engineering
| Domain | Why Recall is Critical |
|---|---|
| Medical Diagnosis | Missing a cancer patient (FN) is costlier than a false alarm (FP) |
| Spam Detection | Recalling spam is important, but Precision matters more |
| Fraud Detection | Catching fraudulent transactions must have very high Recall |
| Security/Surveillance | Missing a threat is dangerous, hence Recall is prioritized |
| Information Retrieval | Recall measures the fraction of relevant documents retrieved |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Building Blocks of Recall
Recall is constructed from two cells of the **Confusion Matrix**:

1. **True Positives (TP):** Model predicted *Positive* and the ground truth *is* Positive. ✅
2. **False Negatives (FN):** Model predicted *Negative* but the ground truth *is* Positive. ❌

Recall completely **ignores True Negatives (TN)** and **False Positives (FP)**, which makes it particularly suited for **imbalanced datasets** where the positive class is rare and critical.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning
- **Step 1:** The model produces predictions (class labels: 0 or 1).
- **Step 2:** Predictions are compared with ground truth labels to fill the Confusion Matrix.
- **Step 3:** Count the number of correctly identified positive cases (TP).
- **Step 4:** Count the number of missed positive cases (FN).
- **Step 5:** Compute the ratio — this is the Recall.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **boilerplate revision sheet** for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) questions on this topic.

| # | Formula / Metric | Mathematical Expression | Range | Unit / Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Recall (Primary)** | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless Ratio |
| 2 | **Sensitivity** (synonym) | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless Ratio |
| 3 | **True Positive Rate** | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless Ratio |
| 4 | **Miss Rate (False Negative Rate)** | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{TP + FN} = 1 - R$ | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless Ratio |
| 5 | **Specificity (True Negative Rate)** | $S = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless Ratio |
| 6 | **Precision (Contrast Metric)** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless Ratio |
| 7 | **F1-Score (Harmonic Mean of P & R)** | $F1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless Ratio |
| 8 | **Multi-class Macro Recall** | $R_{macro} = \dfrac{1}{K}\sum_{i=1}^{K} R_i$ | $[0, 1]$ | Arithmetic Mean |
| 9 | **Multi-class Weighted Recall** | $R_{weighted} = \sum_{i=1}^{K} w_i R_i$ | $[0, 1]$ | Weighted Mean |
| 10 | **Recall in Percentage Form** | $R_{\%} = R \times 100$ | $[0, 100]\%$ | Percentage |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
In **biomedical signal processing** and **predictive maintenance in IoT systems**, engineers prefer Recall-centric models. For instance, a **turbine failure prediction system** must have near-perfect Recall, since the cost of missing a fault (FN) can lead to catastrophic mechanical failure and loss of life. The trade-off accepted is a higher number of false alarms (FP), which is a tolerable engineering cost.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Recall from the Confusion Matrix
Let us derive the Recall metric rigorously from first principles. Consider a binary classification problem where:
- $y_i \in \{0, 1\}$ is the true label
- $\hat{y}_i \in \{0, 1\}$ is the predicted label

The four outcomes of any single prediction are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TP &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}(\hat{y}_i = 1 \text{ and } y_i = 1) \\
TN &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}(\hat{y}_i = 0 \text{ and } y_i = 0) \\
FP &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}(\hat{y}_i = 1 \text{ and } y_i = 0) \\
FN &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}(\hat{y}_i = 0 \text{ and } y_i = 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ is the **indicator function** and $N$ is the total number of samples.

> **Logic Note:** The total number of *actual positive* cases in the dataset is precisely the number of times the ground truth label $y_i = 1$, which is given by $TP + FN$.

The **Recall** is defined as the conditional probability that the model outputs a positive prediction given that the true label is positive:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= P(\hat{y}_i = 1 \mid y_i = 1) \\
R &= \frac{\text{Count}(\hat{y}_i = 1 \text{ and } y_i = 1)}{\text{Count}(y_i = 1)} \\
R &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the canonical derivation accepted in KTU board evaluations.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)
**Problem Statement:** A binary classifier is evaluated on a test set. The Confusion Matrix is given below:

|                | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|----------------|:------------------:|:------------------:|
| Actual Positive | 45 (TP)            | 5 (FN)             |
| Actual Negative | 10 (FP)            | 40 (TN)            |

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant cells:**
- $TP = 45$
- $FN = 5$
- (FP and TN are irrelevant for Recall)

**Step 2 — Apply the Recall formula:**

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{45}{45 + 5} = \frac{45}{50}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to decimal and percentage:**

$$
R = 0.90 = 90\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The model successfully recalled **90%** of all the actual positive samples in the test set. Out of every 10 positive cases, the model misses only 1.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Edge Case Behavior of Recall

| Scenario | TP | FN | Recall Value | KTU Examiner Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Perfect Classifier | 100 | 0 | 1.00 | "Ideal model — every positive detected" |
| Useless Classifier (predicts all negative) | 0 | 100 | 0.00 | "Total failure — no positives caught" |
| Conservative Classifier (predicts few positives) | 10 | 90 | 0.10 | "Very low Recall — too cautious" |
| Aggressive Classifier (predicts all positive) | 100 | 0 | 1.00 | "Trivially perfect — but Precision will be terrible" |
| Balanced Realistic Case | 75 | 25 | 0.75 | "Acceptable in most production systems" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** A Recall of 1.00 is **not always desirable**. If the model achieves R = 1.00 by predicting *every* sample as positive, the model has memorized the bias and is useless. Always cross-check with **Precision**.

### 3.4 Multi-Class Extension — Macro Averaged Recall
For a $K$-class classification problem, the **Macro Recall** is computed as the unweighted mean of per-class recalls:

$$
R_{macro} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \frac{TP_i}{TP_i + FN_i}
$$

**Derivation Steps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{macro} &= \frac{1}{K} (R_1 + R_2 + \dots + R_K) \\
&= \frac{1}{K} \left( \frac{TP_1}{TP_1 + FN_1} + \frac{TP_2}{TP_2 + FN_2} + \dots + \frac{TP_K}{TP_K + FN_K} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This treats every class **equally**, regardless of its frequency — making Macro Recall sensitive to performance on minority classes.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Recall in the Confusion Matrix — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Positive Cases in Dataset] --> B{Prediction Outcome}
    B -- Predicted Positive --> C[True Positive TP]
    B -- Predicted Negative --> D[False Negative FN]
    C --> E[Numerator of Recall]
    D --> F[Denominator Component]
    E --> G[Recall equals TP over TP plus FN]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Output Metric in range 0 to 1]
```

### 4.2 Decision Boundary and Recall Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Model with High Threshold] --> R1[High Precision, Low Recall]
    M2[Model with Low Threshold] --> R2[Low Precision, High Recall]
    M3[Model with Optimal Threshold] --> R3[Balanced Precision and Recall]
    R3 --> F1[Highest F1 Score]
```

### 4.3 The Role of Recall in the Classification Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Trained Classifier] --> P2[Generate Predictions on Test Set]
    P2 --> P3[Construct Confusion Matrix]
    P3 --> P4[Compute TP, FP, TN, FN]
    P4 --> Q1[Compute Precision equals TP over TP plus FP]
    P4 --> Q2[Compute Recall equals TP over TP plus FN]
    Q1 --> R1[F1 Score equals 2PR over P plus R]
    Q2 --> R1
    R1 --> S1[Final Model Performance Report]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Recall Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Ground Truth Labels Y] --> C[Concatenation Block]
    B[Input: Predicted Labels Y hat] --> C
    C --> D{Element-wise Comparison}
    D -- Match equals 1,1 --> E[TP Counter Increment]
    D -- Mismatch equals 0,1 --> F[FN Counter Increment]
    D -- Match equals 0,0 --> G[TN Counter]
    D -- Mismatch equals 1,0 --> H[FP Counter]
    E --> I[Numerator Pool]
    F --> J[Denominator Pool]
    I --> K[Division Block]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Recall Output in 0 to 1]
```

### 4.5 Recall vs Precision — A Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Precision_Focus
        P1[Minimizes FP] --> P2[Conservative Predictions]
    end
    subgraph Recall_Focus
        R1[Minimizes FN] --> R2[Aggressive Predictions]
    end
    P2 <--> T[Trade-off Curve controlled by Threshold]
    R2 <--> T
    T --> O[Optimal Operating Point]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
Define **Recall** in the context of a confusion matrix. Why is it particularly important in medical diagnosis applications?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Recall is a classification evaluation metric defined as the ratio of True Positives (TP) to the total number of actual positive cases, given by $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$.

> **Significance (1 Mark):** It measures the model's ability to identify all relevant positive instances. It is also called the True Positive Rate or Sensitivity.

> **Medical Context (1 Mark):** In medical diagnosis, missing a positive case (False Negative) can be fatal (e.g., undiagnosed cancer). Hence, models must maximize Recall, even at the cost of some False Positives, because a follow-up test can filter false alarms but a missed disease cannot be undone.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between **Precision** and **Recall**. Which one would you prioritize for a **fraud detection** system and why?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Precision (1 Mark):** Precision measures the accuracy of positive predictions, $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$. High precision means when the model flags fraud, it is usually genuine fraud.

> **Recall (1 Mark):** Recall measures the completeness of positive detection, $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$. High recall means very few fraudulent transactions escape detection.

> **Fraud Detection Choice (1 Mark):** For fraud detection, **Recall should be prioritized** because missing a fraudulent transaction (FN) results in direct financial loss, while a false alarm (FP) only requires a manual review by the fraud analyst.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **Confusion Matrix** for a binary classification problem. Define **Precision** and **Recall** using the confusion matrix. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a spam email classifier, the confusion matrix on 1000 test emails is as follows: TP = 120, FP = 30, FN = 80, TN = 770. Calculate the **Precision, Recall, and F1-Score**. Comment on the model's performance. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

The **Confusion Matrix** is a tabular representation of a classifier's performance on a labelled test set. For binary classification, it is a 2×2 matrix:

|                | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|----------------|:------------------:|:------------------:|
| **Actual Positive** | True Positive (TP)  | False Negative (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN)  |

**Definitions (2 Marks Each):**
- **Precision:** Fraction of predicted positives that are actually positive.

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

- **Recall:** Fraction of actual positives that are correctly predicted as positive.

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

Precision answers: *"Of the items the model flagged, how many were correct?"*  
Recall answers: *"Of the actual positive items, how many did the model catch?"*

**Key Insight (1 Mark):** Precision focuses on the quality of positive predictions, whereas Recall focuses on the quantity. A model can have high Precision but low Recall (conservative) or vice versa (aggressive).

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $TP = 120$, $FP = 30$, $FN = 80$, $TN = 770$.

**Step 1: Compute Precision (2 Marks)**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{120}{120 + 30} = \frac{120}{150} = 0.80
$$

**Step 2: Compute Recall (2 Marks)**

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{120}{120 + 80} = \frac{120}{200} = 0.60
$$

**Step 3: Compute F1-Score (2 Marks)**

$$
F1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.80 \times 0.60}{0.80 + 0.60} = \frac{0.96}{1.40} = 0.6857
$$

**Step 4: Performance Commentary (1 Mark)**

The classifier has **high Precision (0.80)** — when it flags an email as spam, it is correct 80% of the time. However, the **Recall is only 0.60**, meaning it misses 40% of the actual spam emails. The **F1-Score of 0.6857** indicates a moderate overall performance. To improve Recall, the decision threshold should be lowered to flag more borderline emails as spam.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** What is the **Precision-Recall Trade-off**? Explain with a suitable diagram how varying the **decision threshold** affects both metrics. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A disease affects 1 in 10,000 people. A medical test has a Recall of 0.99 and a Precision of 0.05. Discuss whether this test is **clinically useful** despite its very low precision. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition (2 Marks):** The **Precision-Recall Trade-off** describes the inverse relationship between Precision and Recall when the **decision threshold** of a probabilistic classifier is varied. Lowering the threshold makes the model more liberal in predicting the positive class, which **increases Recall** (catches more positives) but **decreases Precision** (introduces more False Positives).

**Mechanism (3 Marks):**
- A classifier typically outputs a probability score $P(y=1 \mid x)$.
- If $P(y=1 \mid x) \geq \tau$, the sample is classified as positive.
- **High $\tau$ (e.g., 0.9):** Only highly confident predictions are positive → High Precision, Low Recall.
- **Low $\tau$ (e.g., 0.1):** Even weakly positive predictions are flagged → Low Precision, High Recall.
- The **optimal threshold** is the one that maximizes the F1-Score or meets the application-specific requirement.

**Diagrammatic Representation (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau \uparrow \;&\Rightarrow\; P \uparrow,\; R \downarrow \\
\tau \downarrow \;&\Rightarrow\; P \downarrow,\; R \uparrow
\end{aligned}
$$

The **Precision-Recall Curve** plots both metrics as a function of threshold. The **Area Under the PR Curve (AUC-PR)** is a scalar measure of overall classifier quality, especially useful for imbalanced datasets.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Apply Bayes' Rule to compute Posterior Probability (3 Marks)**

Let:
- $P(\text{Disease}) = 0.0001$ (prior probability)
- $P(\text{No Disease}) = 0.9999$
- $R = 0.99$ (Recall / Sensitivity)
- $P = 0.05$ (Precision / PPV)

When the test gives a positive result, the probability the patient actually has the disease is given by Precision itself, which is **5%**.

**Step 2: Reverse the scenario — Probability of disease given a NEGATIVE test (2 Marks)**

The **False Negative Rate (Miss Rate)** is:

$$
FNR = 1 - R = 1 - 0.99 = 0.01
$$

If a patient tests **negative**, the probability they are actually disease-free is exceptionally high. The **Negative Predictive Value (NPV)** in this case is close to **1.00** because the disease is so rare.

**Step 3: Clinical Utility Discussion (2 Marks)**

Despite the low precision of **5%**, this test is **clinically useful as a SCREENING tool** for the following reasons:
1. **High Recall (99%)** ensures that almost no disease cases are missed, which is critical for a deadly disease.
2. **Low Precision (5%)** is acceptable because the positive cases can be subjected to a **second, more precise confirmatory test** (e.g., biopsy).
3. The **cost of a False Negative (missed disease)** far outweighs the cost of a **False Positive (unnecessary follow-up test)**.

> **Conclusion:** A screening test should prioritize **Recall over Precision**. This is a textbook example from KTU Module 4 illustrating why no single metric is universally optimal — the choice depends on the **cost asymmetry** of the application.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Recall with Precision:** Students frequently swap $FN$ and $FP$ in the formula. **Recall** uses $FN$ in the denominator; **Precision** uses $FP$.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute F1-Score:** When the question asks for "model performance," a single metric is insufficient. Always pair Recall with Precision and F1.
> 3. **Ignoring Class Imbalance:** Reporting **Accuracy** alone is misleading for imbalanced datasets. KTU examiners specifically test whether you report Recall and Precision.
> 4. **Not interpreting the metric:** A numerical answer without interpretation will cost 1–2 marks. Always add a concluding comment.
> 5. **Unit confusion:** Recall is dimensionless. Do not write "Recall = 0.80 units" — it is a ratio, not a physical quantity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE Aspirants:**

- ✅ **Recall** = $TP / (TP + FN)$. Memorize this formula as the **golden equation** for this topic.
- ✅ **Synonyms:** Sensitivity, True Positive Rate (TPR), Hit Rate — all refer to Recall.
- ✅ **Denominator of Recall** = Total Actual Positives (column sum of the positive class in the Confusion Matrix).
- ✅ **Recall ignores True Negatives (TN)** — making it the **preferred metric for imbalanced datasets** with rare positive classes.
- ✅ **Recall = 1 − Miss Rate (FNR).**
- ✅ **Recall is prioritized** when the cost of False Negatives is **high** (medical diagnosis, fraud, security).
- ✅ **Precision-Recall Trade-off:** Lowering the threshold $\tau$ increases R but decreases P.
- ✅ **F1-Score = Harmonic Mean** of Precision and Recall — use it as a **single combined metric** in board answers.
- ✅ **Multi-class Recall:** Use **Macro Recall** (simple average) for class-balanced evaluation, **Weighted Recall** (weighted by class frequency) for skewed datasets.
- ✅ **Always report Recall alongside Precision** in KTU answers. Reporting only Accuracy will not earn full marks.
- ✅ **PR Curve** is preferred over **ROC Curve** for highly imbalanced datasets.
- ✅ The formula is dimensionless — Recall has **no unit**; it is a pure ratio in $[0, 1]$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: F1-Score
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAIT404 - FOUNDATION OF MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** F1-Score

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **F1-Score (F1 Measure):** It is the **harmonic mean** of a model's *Precision* and *Recall* (Sensitivity), providing a single balanced metric that penalizes extreme imbalances between the two. It ranges from **0 to 1**, where **1** indicates perfect precision and recall, and **0** indicates the worst performance.

Mathematically, for a binary classifier, given a **Confusion Matrix** with entries:

- **TP** = True Positives
- **FP** = False Positives
- **FN** = False Negatives
- **TN** = True Negatives

The F1-Score is defined as:

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Medical Diagnosis Scenario**
> Imagine you are a doctor diagnosing a rare but deadly disease (say, cancer). You have two critical concerns:
>
> 1. **Precision** — Of all the patients you flagged as positive, how many *actually* have the disease? (You don't want to scare healthy people with false alarms — these are the **False Positives**).
> 2. **Recall** — Of all the patients who *truly* have the disease, how many did you successfully catch? (You don't want to miss a sick patient — these are the **False Negatives**).
>
> The **F1-Score** acts as a single "balanced report card." If you achieve 100% recall but terrible precision (flagging everyone as sick), your F1-Score is low. If you have 100% precision but miss half the sick patients, your F1-Score is also low. F1 only rewards models that do well on **both** simultaneously.

## 1.3 Why Harmonic Mean and Not Arithmetic Mean?

This is a **favourite KTU conceptual question**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Arithmetic Mean** of Precision and Recall is easily fooled. For instance, if Precision = 1.0 and Recall = 0.01, the arithmetic mean is **0.505** (deceptively high!). But the **Harmonic Mean** gives **0.0199** — correctly reflecting the catastrophic failure in recall.
>
> The harmonic mean is always **less than or equal to** the arithmetic mean and is heavily pulled down by the smaller of the two values. This makes it the **correct** averaging technique for rates and ratios.

$$
\text{HM} \leq \text{AM} \quad \text{where} \quad \text{HM} = \frac{2ab}{a+b}, \quad \text{AM} = \frac{a+b}{2}
$$

## 1.4 Place in the KTU Syllabus

In Module 4 (Model Evaluation & Validation), F1-Score is positioned as the **most important single-number evaluation metric** for **imbalanced classification problems**, sitting alongside the ROC-AUC and Confusion Matrix analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pre-requisite: Building Blocks of F1-Score

Before deriving F1, we must first establish its two parent metrics from the **Confusion Matrix**.

### 2.1.1 Confusion Matrix Structure

| | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | TP | FN |
| **Actual Negative** | FP | TN |

### 2.1.2 Precision (Positive Predictive Value)

- **Definition:** Out of all samples the model *predicted* as positive, how many were *actually* positive?
- **Intuition:** "When the model says YES, how trustworthy is that YES?"
- **Formula:** $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$
- **High Precision → Low False Positive Rate**

### 2.1.3 Recall (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate)

- **Definition:** Out of all samples that are *actually* positive, how many did the model *catch*?
- **Intuition:** "Of all the true positives out there, how many did the model find?"
- **Formula:** $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- **High Recall → Low False Negative Rate**

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of F1-Score

1. Compute **Precision (P)** and **Recall (R)** for the classifier on the validation set.
2. Verify that both $P > 0$ and $R > 0$ (otherwise F1 is undefined / 0).
3. Multiply P and R → this is the **numerator's core**.
4. Add P and R → this is the **denominator's core**.
5. Multiply the product by **2** (a normalization constant to scale the maximum value to 1).
6. Divide numerator by denominator.
7. The result is the **F1-Score** in the range $[0, 1]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multiply by 2?**
> Without the 2, the formula $F = \frac{PR}{P+R}$ has a maximum of 0.5 (when P = R = 1). The factor of 2 rescales it so that a perfect classifier gets F1 = 1.

## 2.3 The Generalized F-Beta Score

KTU 2024 syllabus often tests the **Fβ family** as an extension.

$$
F_\beta = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\beta^2 \cdot \text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

| **β Value** | **Emphasis** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|
| $\beta < 1$ | Precision weighted more | Spam filtering (don't want false alarms) |
| $\beta = 1$ | Balanced (F1-Score) | General classification |
| $\beta > 1$ | Recall weighted more | Medical diagnosis, cancer detection |
| $\beta \to \infty$ | Recall only | Catch every single positive case |

## 2.4 Multi-Class F1 Variants (Important for KTU)

In multi-class problems (e.g., classifying images into 10 categories), F1 is computed in two standard ways:

| **Variant** | **Formula (Conceptual)** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|
| **Macro F1** | Average of per-class F1 scores (unweighted) | When all classes are **equally important** |
| **Micro F1** | Computed globally by aggregating all TP, FP, FN | When class **imbalance** is present |
| **Weighted F1** | Average weighted by class support | When classes have different sample sizes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Micro F1 = Accuracy** in multi-class settings (with multi-label indicators). This is a recurring KTU trick question.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Range** | **Best Value** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Precision (P) | $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | 1 |
| Recall (R) | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | 1 |
| F1-Score | $2 \cdot \frac{PR}{P+R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | 1 |
| Fβ-Score | $(1+\beta^2) \cdot \frac{PR}{\beta^2 P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | 1 |
| Accuracy | $\frac{TP+TN}{TP+FP+FN+TN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | 1 |
| Specificity | $\frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | 1 |
| Macro F1 | $\frac{1}{K}\sum_{i=1}^{K} F1_i$ | $[0, 1]$ | 1 |
| Micro F1 | $\frac{2 \cdot \sum TP_i}{2 \cdot \sum TP_i + \sum FP_i + \sum FN_i}$ | $[0, 1]$ | 1 |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical AI:** Detecting tumors where missing a positive case is fatal.
- **Fraud Detection:** Flagging fraudulent transactions in banking.
- **NLP:** Named Entity Recognition (NER) tasks where class imbalance is severe.
- **Information Retrieval:** Search engines balancing relevant vs. retrieved documents.
- **Anomaly Detection:** Industrial defect detection in manufacturing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: F1-Score from Precision and Recall

We start from the fundamental definitions using the confusion matrix entries.

### Step 1: Write Precision and Recall in terms of TP, FP, FN

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \qquad R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the product $P \cdot R$

$$
P \cdot R = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{TP^2}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the sum $P + R$

$$
P + R = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} + \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{TP(TP+FN) + TP(TP+FP)}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}
$$

### Step 4: Simplify the numerator of the sum

$$
TP(TP+FN) + TP(TP+FP) = TP^2 + TP \cdot FN + TP^2 + TP \cdot FP = 2 \cdot TP^2 + TP(FN + FP)
$$

### Step 5: Form the ratio $\frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$

$$
\frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{\frac{TP^2}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}}{\frac{2 \cdot TP^2 + TP(FN+FP)}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}} = \frac{TP^2}{2 \cdot TP^2 + TP(FN+FP)}
$$

### Step 6: Factor $TP$ from the denominator

$$
\frac{TP^2}{TP[2 \cdot TP + FN + FP]} = \frac{TP}{2 \cdot TP + FN + FP}
$$

### Step 7: Multiply by 2 to obtain F1-Score

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{TP}{2 \cdot TP + FN + FP} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Clean Form (the most exam-useful form):**
> $$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$
> This form does not require computing Precision and Recall separately — a huge time-saver in KTU exams.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: F1 in Terms of Type-I and Type-II Errors

In statistical hypothesis testing:

- **Type I Error** = False Positive (FP) — rejecting a true null hypothesis
- **Type II Error** = False Negative (FN) — failing to reject a false null hypothesis

Let $\alpha = FP$ and $\beta = FN$ (confusing notation, but standard in stats).

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + \alpha + \beta}
$$

This shows F1 is a function of **how well we balance both error types**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **[Worked Example]**
> A binary classifier produces the following confusion matrix on a test set of **100 samples**:
>
> | | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
> |---|---|---|
> | Actual Positive | 30 (TP) | 10 (FN) |
> | Actual Negative | 5 (FP) | 55 (TN) |
>
> **Compute the F1-Score.**

### Solution:

**Step 1: Compute Precision**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{30}{30+5} = \frac{30}{35} = \frac{6}{7} \approx 0.8571
$$

**Step 2: Compute Recall**

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{30}{30+10} = \frac{30}{40} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75
$$

**Step 3: Apply the F1 Formula (Method 1 — using P and R)**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8571 \times 0.75}{0.8571 + 0.75}
$$

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.6428}{1.6071} = 2 \cdot 0.4000 = 0.8000
$$

**Step 4: Verify with Method 2 (Direct TP/FP/FN form)**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2 \cdot 30}{2 \cdot 30 + 5 + 10} = \frac{60}{75} = 0.8000
$$

> Both methods yield **F1 = 0.80** ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Behavior of F1 Under Different Conditions

| **Scenario** | **Precision** | **Recall** | **Arithmetic Mean** | **F1-Score** | **Verdict** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Perfect Classifier | 1.0 | 1.0 | 1.000 | **1.000** | Ideal |
| High P, Low R | 0.95 | 0.10 | 0.525 | **0.171** | F1 correctly penalizes |
| Low P, High R | 0.10 | 0.95 | 0.525 | **0.171** | F1 correctly penalizes |
| Balanced Moderate | 0.70 | 0.60 | 0.650 | **0.646** | F1 ≈ AM (values close) |
| All Positive Predicted | 0.40 | 1.00 | 0.700 | **0.571** | Recall max, F1 reveals mediocrity |
| Trivial Classifier (predicts no positive) | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.000 | **Undefined (0)** | F1 catches total failure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice in row 3 and 4: the arithmetic mean is deceptively high (0.525, 0.700) but F1 is honest. **This is why F1 is the preferred metric for imbalanced data.**

---

## 3.5 Edge Case: When F1-Score is Undefined

If **TP = 0** (the model never predicts the positive class), then:

- $P = \frac{0}{0 + FP} = 0$
- $R = \frac{0}{0 + FN} = 0$
- $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0}{0 + 0} = \frac{0}{0}$ → **Undefined**

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, by convention, F1 is set to **0** when both P and R are 0. KTU students should mention this convention explicitly in answers to earn full marks.

---

## 3.6 Proof: F1 ≤ min(P, R) (Useful Theorem)

> **Claim:** $F_1 \leq \min(P, R)$
>
> **Proof:** Let $m = \min(P, R)$. Then $P \geq m$ and $R \geq m$.
>
> $$
> F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R} \leq \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot m}{P + m} \quad \text{(replacing } R \text{ by its lower bound } m \text{)}
> $$
>
> Now we need to show $\frac{2Pm}{P+m} \leq m$:
>
> $$
> \frac{2Pm}{P+m} \leq m \iff 2P \leq P + m \iff P \leq m
> $$
>
> But $P \geq m$, with equality only when $P = m$. Hence $F_1 \leq m = \min(P, R)$. $\blacksquare$

> **Significance:** F1 is always **bounded above by the worse of the two metrics** — it can never exceed your weakest link.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Confusion Matrix with F1 Derivation Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Actual Positive Samples] --> nodeB{Predicted Positive?}
    nodeA --> nodeC{Predicted Negative?}
    nodeB --> nodeD[TP - True Positive]
    nodeB --> nodeE[FP - False Positive]
    nodeC --> nodeF[FN - False Negative]
    nodeC --> nodeG[TN - True Negative]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Actual Negative Samples]
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeI[Compute Precision = TP / TP+FP]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Compute Recall = TP / TP+FN]
    nodeI --> nodeK[F1 Score = 2PR / P+R]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL[Final Scalar Value in 0 to 1]
```

## 4.2 F1-Score Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Trained Model] --> nodeB[Predictions on Test Set]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Build Confusion Matrix]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Extract TP, FP, FN, TN]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Compute Precision P]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Compute Recall R]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Apply F1 Formula]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Single Score 0 to 1]
    nodeH --> nodeI{Compare with Baseline}
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Model Accepted]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Retrain / Tune Hyperparameters]
```

## 4.3 Precision-Recall-F1 Trade-off Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[Quadrant I - High P, High R]
        nodeA[Ideal Zone - F1 Approaches 1]
    end
    subgraph S2[Quadrant II - High P, Low R]
        nodeB[Conservative Model - F1 Low]
    end
    subgraph S3[Quadrant III - Low P, Low R]
        nodeC[Useless Model - F1 Near 0]
    end
    subgraph S4[Quadrant IV - Low P, High R]
        nodeD[Aggressive Model - F1 Low]
    end
    nodeA --> nodeE{F1 Score as Judge}
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[Balanced Single Number Evaluation]
```

## 4.4 F1 vs Accuracy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Is Dataset Balanced?] --> nodeB{Class Ratio Near 50:50?}
    nodeB --> nodeY[Yes]
    nodeB --> nodeN[No Imbalanced]
    nodeY --> nodeC[Use Accuracy OR F1 - Both Valid]
    nodeN --> nodeD[Is False Negative Costly?]
    nodeD --> nodeY2[Yes Medical Cancer Detection]
    nodeD --> nodeN2[No Spam Filtering]
    nodeY2 --> nodeE[Use F2 Score Recall Weighted]
    nodeN2 --> nodeF[Use F0.5 Score Precision Weighted]
    nodeE --> nodeG[F1 Family Provides Asymmetric Control]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeG
```

## 4.5 Generalized F-Beta Family Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[F Beta Family] --> nodeB[Beta Equals 0.5]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Beta Equals 1]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Beta Equals 2]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Precision Heavy F0.5]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Balanced F1]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Recall Heavy F2]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Use When FP is Expensive]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Default General Purpose Metric]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Use When FN is Expensive]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Define F1-Score. Why is the harmonic mean used instead of the arithmetic mean to combine Precision and Recall?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

F1-Score is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, defined as:

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

The harmonic mean is preferred over the arithmetic mean because it heavily penalizes cases where either Precision or Recall is low. For example, with P = 1.0 and R = 0.01, the arithmetic mean is 0.505 (deceptively high), while the harmonic mean gives only 0.0198, correctly reflecting the model's failure. Thus, the harmonic mean ensures that **F1 is high only when both metrics are simultaneously high** (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for justification with example).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`

**Explain the difference between Macro F1 and Micro F1 with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Macro F1** computes the F1-Score independently for each class and then takes the unweighted average. It treats all classes equally regardless of their frequency. For example, in a 3-class problem with per-class F1 values of 0.8, 0.6, and 0.4, Macro F1 = (0.8 + 0.6 + 0.4) / 3 = 0.60.

**Micro F1** aggregates the contributions of all classes (TP, FP, FN are summed globally) before computing F1. It is equivalent to overall accuracy in single-label multi-class settings. Micro F1 is preferred when class imbalance exists, as it weights each instance equally (1 mark for Macro, 1 mark for Micro, 1 mark for example or distinction).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the F1-Score formula in terms of TP, FP, and FN from the basic definitions of Precision and Recall. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A spam detection classifier produces the following confusion matrix on 200 emails:

| | Predicted Spam | Predicted Not Spam |
|---|---|---|
| Actual Spam | 80 | 20 |
| Actual Not Spam | 30 | 70 |

Compute Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. Comment on whether the F1-Score indicates a good classifier. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Define Precision and Recall** (1 mark)

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \qquad R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**Step 2: Form the ratio $\frac{PR}{P+R}$** (1 mark)

$$
\frac{PR}{P+R} = \frac{\frac{TP}{TP+FP} \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}{\frac{TP}{TP+FP} + \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}
$$

**Step 3: Combine the denominator over a common base** (2 marks)

$$
P + R = \frac{TP(TP+FN) + TP(TP+FP)}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)} = \frac{2 \cdot TP^2 + TP \cdot (FP + FN)}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute and simplify** (2 marks)

$$
\frac{PR}{P+R} = \frac{TP^2}{2 \cdot TP^2 + TP(FP+FN)} = \frac{TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
$$

**Step 5: Multiply by 2 to obtain the F1-Score** (1 mark)

$$
\boxed{F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}}
$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:** TP = 80, FN = 20, FP = 30, TN = 70

**Step 1: Compute Precision** (2 marks)

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{80}{80+30} = \frac{80}{110} = 0.7273
$$

**Step 2: Compute Recall** (2 marks)

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{80}{80+20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.8000
$$

**Step 3: Compute F1-Score** (2 marks)

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.7273 \times 0.8}{0.7273 + 0.8} = \frac{1.1636}{1.5273} = 0.7619
$$

**Verification using direct form:**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 80}{2 \cdot 80 + 30 + 20} = \frac{160}{210} = 0.7619 \checkmark
$$

**Step 4: Comment** (1 mark)

The F1-Score of **0.7619** indicates a reasonably good classifier, with balanced performance in both precision (0.73) and recall (0.80). However, the score is not extremely high, suggesting room for improvement — perhaps by tuning the decision threshold or using a more sophisticated model.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Define Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. Explain with a suitable real-world example why Accuracy alone is insufficient for evaluating imbalanced classification problems. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** For a 3-class classification problem (Classes A, B, C), the per-class F1 scores are: $F1_A = 0.90$, $F1_B = 0.60$, $F1_C = 0.30$. The class supports are: $n_A = 100$, $n_B = 50$, $n_C = 10$. Compute the Macro F1 and Weighted F1 scores. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Conceptual [7 Marks]

**Definitions** (3 marks):

- **Precision** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ — fraction of correct positive predictions among all positive predictions.
- **Recall** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ — fraction of actual positives correctly identified.
- **F1-Score** = $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ — harmonic mean balancing both metrics.

**Why Accuracy Fails for Imbalanced Data — Real-World Example** (4 marks):

Consider a fraud detection system where only **0.1%** of 1 million transactions are fraudulent (1000 frauds, 999,000 legitimate). A trivial classifier that predicts **"Not Fraud"** for every transaction achieves an accuracy of **99.9%** — yet it catches **zero** fraudulent transactions. This is useless.

Using F1-Score instead reveals the truth: if the model predicts no positive cases, TP = 0, leading to F1 = 0, correctly indicating total failure. Thus, F1 (and Precision/Recall) is essential when the cost of missing a positive (False Negative) is high or when class distribution is skewed.

#### Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]

**Given:** $F1_A = 0.90$, $F1_B = 0.60$, $F1_C = 0.30$ and $n_A = 100$, $n_B = 50$, $n_C = 10$.

**Total Samples:** $N = 100 + 50 + 10 = 160$ (1 mark)

**Step 1: Compute Macro F1** (3 marks)

Macro F1 is the simple unweighted average of per-class F1 scores:

$$
\text{Macro F1} = \frac{F1_A + F1_B + F1_C}{3} = \frac{0.90 + 0.60 + 0.30}{3} = \frac{1.80}{3} = 0.60
$$

**Step 2: Compute Weighted F1** (3 marks)

Weighted F1 weights each class's F1 by its support proportion:

$$
\text{Weighted F1} = \frac{n_A \cdot F1_A + n_B \cdot F1_B + n_C \cdot F1_C}{N}
$$

$$
\text{Weighted F1} = \frac{(100 \times 0.90) + (50 \times 0.60) + (10 \times 0.30)}{160}
$$

$$
= \frac{90 + 30 + 3}{160} = \frac{123}{160} = 0.7688
$$

**Interpretation:** Macro F1 (0.60) treats the underperforming Class C equally with Class A, dragging the score down. Weighted F1 (0.7688) reflects the model's real-world performance, dominated by Classes A and B which have more samples.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in F1-Score Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the F1 formula — students write $F_1 = \frac{PR}{P+R}$, which has a maximum of 0.5. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for this.
> 2. **Confusing Macro and Micro F1** — Micro F1 aggregates TP/FP/FN globally; Macro F1 averages per-class. Misnaming the variant costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Not stating the convention** for the TP = 0 edge case — always write "F1 is conventionally set to 0 when P = R = 0."
> 4. **Using Accuracy on imbalanced data** in part (a) conceptual answers — examiners specifically look for the explanation of *why* F1 is needed. Vague answers get partial credit.
> 5. **Not showing intermediate steps in derivation** — the algebraic manipulation from $P, R$ to the $TP/FP/FN$ form must be explicit. Skipping steps = 1–2 marks lost.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for F1-Score:**
>
> - F1-Score is the **harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall, scaled to $[0, 1]$.
> - The **factor of 2** in the formula is essential — it rescales the harmonic mean's natural maximum of 0.5 to 1.0.
> - **Two equivalent forms** to memorize:
>   - $F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R}$
>   - $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$
> - **Arithmetic mean is misleading** for ratios; harmonic mean correctly penalizes imbalances.
> - F1 is **undefined** when TP = 0; convention sets it to 0.
> - **Fβ family** allows asymmetric weighting: β < 1 favors Precision, β > 1 favors Recall.
> - **Macro F1** = unweighted mean of per-class F1; **Weighted F1** = mean weighted by class support; **Micro F1** = global aggregate (equals Accuracy in single-label multi-class).
> - F1 is the **go-to metric for imbalanced classification** where Accuracy is misleading.
> - F1 ≤ min(P, R) — the F1 score can never exceed your weakest metric.
> - Real-world domains: **medical diagnosis, fraud detection, spam filtering, NER, anomaly detection**.
> - Always show **intermediate algebraic steps** in derivations for full KTU marks.
> - For threshold-sensitive problems, F1 is preferred over Accuracy when class distribution is skewed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Receiver Operating Characteristics Curve (ROC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAIT404 - FOUNDATION OF MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Receiver Operating Characteristics Curve (ROC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve**: A ROC curve is a two-dimensional graphical plot that illustrates the diagnostic ability of a binary classifier system as its discrimination threshold is varied. It is constructed by plotting the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** on the Y-axis against the **False Positive Rate (FPR)** on the X-axis, across all possible threshold values of the classifier's continuous output score.

The ROC curve originates from **signal detection theory** developed during World War II for radar operator analysis, but was repurposed into machine learning for evaluating classification performance. It is a **threshold-independent** metric, meaning it evaluates the model's ranking quality rather than its raw accuracy at one fixed threshold.

### Key Performance Metrics Used in ROC Analysis

| Symbol | Metric | Definition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **TPR** | True Positive Rate (Sensitivity / Recall) | $TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ |
| **FPR** | False Positive Rate (Fall-out) | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ |
| **TNR** | True Negative Rate (Specificity) | $TNR = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ |
| **FNR** | False Negative Rate (Miss Rate) | $FNR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ |
| **AUC** | Area Under the ROC Curve | Integral of the ROC curve |

> [!NOTE]
> **FPR = 1 − Specificity** and **TPR = 1 − FNR**. These dualities are essential for KTU exam derivations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The COVID Rapid Test Scenario**:
> Imagine you are a doctor using a rapid antigen test that returns a "score" (0 to 1) representing the probability that a patient has the disease. You must decide a *cutoff threshold* to declare the patient "positive".
> - **Threshold = 0.99** (very strict): You will rarely falsely accuse healthy people (low FPR), but you will miss many sick patients (low TPR).
> - **Threshold = 0.01** (very lenient): You will catch almost every sick patient (high TPR), but you will wrongly alarm many healthy ones (high FPR).
> - The **ROC curve** is simply the *trade-off map* of (FPR, TPR) for every possible threshold setting.
> - The **AUC** measures how well the test *ranks* a random sick patient above a random healthy one, regardless of where you set the cutoff.

**Why ROC matters in KTU context:** A single Accuracy score is misleading on imbalanced datasets (e.g., 99% negative class). ROC provides a holistic view, and **AUC** is a single scalar that summarizes classifier quality across *all* thresholds.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How an ROC Curve is Constructed

A binary classifier (e.g., Logistic Regression, SVM with probability, Neural Network with sigmoid) outputs a continuous score $s(x) \in [0, 1]$ for every input sample. A **threshold $\tau$** converts this score into a class label:

$$\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } s(x) \geq \tau \\ 0, & \text{if } s(x) < \tau \end{cases}$$

### Step-by-Step Construction Logic

1. **Train the classifier** on the training data and obtain predicted probabilities $s_i$ for each test instance $i$.
2. **Sort all test instances** in descending order of $s_i$ (highest confidence positive first).
3. **Initialize** at $\tau = 1.0$: All instances predicted as negative. TPR = 0, FPR = 0.
4. **Sweep threshold** downwards from 1.0 to 0.0. For each unique score value, update the confusion matrix:
   - If the instance is **actually positive** (label = 1) → TP count increases by 1.
   - If the instance is **actually negative** (label = 0) → FP count increases by 1.
5. **Recompute TPR and FPR** at each step using the cumulative counts.
6. **Plot the curve** in the FPR–TPR plane. Connect consecutive points to form a monotonic non-decreasing step curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connection to Confusion Matrix**: Every point on the ROC curve corresponds to *one* confusion matrix. The curve is the union of all these confusion matrices plotted as $\tau$ varies over $(-\infty, +\infty)$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Quantity | Expression | Engineering Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sensitivity (TPR) | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Probability of correctly detecting a positive case |
| Fall-out (FPR) | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | Probability of a false alarm |
| Specificity (TNR) | $TNR = 1 - FPR = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | Probability of correctly rejecting a negative |
| Miss Rate (FNR) | $FNR = 1 - TPR = \dfrac{FN}{FN+TP}$ | Probability of a missed positive |
| Precision (PPV) | $PPV = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Trustworthiness of a positive prediction |
| Youden's J Statistic | $J = TPR + TNR - 1 = TPR - FPR$ | Optimal threshold: maximize $J$ |
| AUC (Trapezoidal) | $AUC = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \dfrac{1}{2} (FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i)(TPR_{i+1} + TPR_i)$ | Probability rank-quality |
| AUC Probabilistic | $text{AUC} = P(s(\text{positive}) > s(\text{negative}))$ | Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney interpretation |
| Random Classifier AUC | $AUC = 0.5$ | The diagonal reference line |
| Perfect Classifier AUC | $AUC = 1.0$ | Curve hugs the top-left corner |

> [!WARNING]
> **Boundary Condition Alert**: For the trapezoidal AUC formula, if two consecutive points have the **same FPR** (vertical step), the area contribution is **zero**. If they have the **same TPR** (horizontal step), the area equals the horizontal width. Forgetting this distinction costs 2–3 marks in KTU derivations.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis**: Comparing MRI vs. CT scan classifiers for tumor detection.
- **Credit Card Fraud**: Threshold tuning is critical because missing a fraud (FN) is far more expensive than a false alert (FP).
- **Spam Filtering**: Gmail/SpamAssassin engines are tuned using ROC analysis.
- **Information Retrieval**: Search engines rank relevance using AUC-equivalent metrics.
- **Anomaly Detection in IoT**: ROC is used to compare one-class SVM vs. Isolation Forest.
- **Biometrics**: Face-recognition and fingerprint systems report ROC + EER (Equal Error Rate).

## 2.4 Geometric Interpretation of AUC

The **Area Under the ROC Curve** is bounded by $[0, 1]$ in practice (and by definition the curve is $\geq$ the diagonal):

$$0.5 \leq AUC \leq 1.0 \quad \text{(for any useful classifier)}$$

| AUC Value | Classifier Quality |
| :--- | :--- |
| AUC = 1.0 | **Perfect** separation of classes |
| 0.9 $\leq$ AUC $<$ 1.0 | **Excellent** |
| 0.8 $\leq$ AUC $<$ 0.9 | **Good** |
| 0.7 $\leq$ AUC $<$ 0.8 | **Fair** |
| 0.5 $\leq$ AUC $<$ 0.7 | **Poor** (close to random) |
| AUC $<$ 0.5 | **Worse than random** (inverted labels) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — AUC via the Trapezoidal Rule

Let the ROC curve be represented by a sequence of $n$ ordered points $(FPR_i, TPR_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$ generated by sweeping the threshold. The curve is monotonically non-decreasing in both axes. The area under the curve is the sum of trapezoidal areas between consecutive points:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AUC &= \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} A_i \\
A_i &= \int_{FPR_i}^{FPR_{i+1}} TPR(x) \, dx \\
    &\approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot (FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i) \cdot (TPR_i + TPR_{i+1}) \quad \text{(linear interpolation)} \\
\therefore AUC &= \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \frac{1}{2}(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i)(TPR_{i+1} + TPR_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Step**: The area of a trapezoid is $\frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times (\text{height}_1 + \text{height}_2)$. Here the "base" lies on the FPR axis and the two "heights" are $TPR_i$ and $TPR_{i+1}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Youden's J Statistic (Optimal Threshold)

The optimal classification threshold $\tau^*$ is the one that maximizes the distance between the ROC curve and the diagonal random-guess line:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J(\tau) &= TPR(\tau) - FPR(\tau) \\
\tau^* &= \arg\max_{\tau} J(\tau)
\end{aligned}
$$

At this optimal point, the classifier balances Sensitivity and Specificity:

$$
J^* = \max_{\tau} \left( \text{Sensitivity}(\tau) + \text{Specificity}(\tau) - 1 \right)
$$

> **Logic Step**: $J = 1$ indicates a perfect classifier (TPR=1, FPR=0). $J = 0$ indicates a random classifier. The KTU expected answer is a single threshold value $\tau^*$ that achieves this maximum.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — KTU Exam Style

### Problem Statement

A logistic regression model outputs the following scores on 5 test samples. The true labels and predicted scores are:

| Sample Index $i$ | True Label $y_i$ | Predicted Score $s_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 1 | 0.95 |
| 2 | 1 | 0.85 |
| 3 | 0 | 0.70 |
| 4 | 1 | 0.55 |
| 5 | 0 | 0.40 |

**Task**: (i) Construct the ROC curve, (ii) Compute the AUC, (iii) Find the optimal threshold using Youden's J.

### Step 1 — Sort by Predicted Score (Descending)

After sorting: Sample 1 (0.95, +), Sample 2 (0.85, +), Sample 3 (0.70, −), Sample 4 (0.55, +), Sample 5 (0.40, −).

### Step 2 — Total Positives and Negatives

$$
P = \sum y_i = 1 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 0 = 3
$$

$$
N = 5 - P = 2
$$

### Step 3 — Threshold Sweep and Confusion Matrix Updates

Starting with $\tau > 0.95$ (all predicted negative): $TP = 0$, $FP = 0$, $FN = 3$, $TN = 2$. So $TPR = 0/3 = 0$, $FPR = 0/2 = 0$.

| Step | $\tau$ | Event | TP | FP | FN | TN | TPR | FPR |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 1.00 | Start (all negative) | 0 | 0 | 3 | 2 | 0.000 | 0.000 |
| 1 | 0.95 | Sample 1 (+ → +) | 1 | 0 | 2 | 2 | 0.333 | 0.000 |
| 2 | 0.85 | Sample 2 (+ → +) | 2 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 0.667 | 0.000 |
| 3 | 0.70 | Sample 3 (− → +) | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0.667 | 0.500 |
| 4 | 0.55 | Sample 4 (+ → +) | 3 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1.000 | 0.500 |
| 5 | 0.40 | Sample 5 (− → +) | 3 | 2 | 0 | 0 | 1.000 | 1.000 |

### Step 4 — Compute AUC Using the Trapezoidal Rule

The ordered ROC points are:

$$
P_0 = (0, 0), \ P_1 = (0, 0.333), \ P_2 = (0, 0.667), \ P_3 = (0.5, 0.667), \ P_4 = (0.5, 1), \ P_5 = (1, 1)
$$

Apply the trapezoidal area formula on each consecutive segment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{01} &= \tfrac{1}{2}(0 - 0)(0.333 + 0) = 0 \\
A_{12} &= \tfrac{1}{2}(0 - 0)(0.667 + 0.333) = 0 \\
A_{23} &= \tfrac{1}{2}(0.5 - 0)(0.667 + 0.667) = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.5)(1.334) = 0.3335 \\
A_{34} &= \tfrac{1}{2}(0.5 - 0.5)(1 + 0.667) = 0 \\
A_{45} &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1 - 0.5)(1 + 1) = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.5)(2) = 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum all trapezoidal contributions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AUC &= A_{01} + A_{12} + A_{23} + A_{34} + A_{45} \\
    &= 0 + 0 + 0.3335 + 0 + 0.5 \\
    &= 0.8335
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verdict**: AUC $\approx$ **0.834** indicates a **Good** classifier per the AUC quality table.

### Step 5 — Optimal Threshold via Youden's J

Compute $J(\tau) = TPR - FPR$ at each step (excluding the start where $J$ is undefined-zero and the trivial end point $J = 0$):

| Step | TPR | FPR | $J = TPR - FPR$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0.333 | 0.000 | **0.333** |
| 2 | 0.667 | 0.000 | **0.667** |
| 3 | 0.667 | 0.500 | 0.167 |
| 4 | 1.000 | 0.500 | 0.500 |

The **maximum $J$** is achieved at **Step 2**, where $\tau^* = 0.85$.

$$
\tau^* = 0.85, \quad J^* = 0.667
$$

> **Interpretation**: At threshold 0.85, the classifier achieves 66.7% sensitivity with **zero false positives** — a strong operating point.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Boundary Cases

| Operating Point | TPR | FPR | Use Case |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Top-Left Corner | 1.0 | 0.0 | Perfect classifier (ideal target) |
| Youden's Optimal | TPR$^*$ | FPR$^*$ | Balanced sensitivity / specificity |
| EER (Equal Error Rate) | 1 − EER | EER | Biometric systems, FNR = FPR |
| Diagonal (Random) | FPR | FPR | Coin-flip baseline, AUC = 0.5 |
| (0, 0) Origin | 0.0 | 0.0 | Trivial "all negative" classifier |
| (1, 1) Endpoint | 1.0 | 1.0 | Trivial "all positive" classifier |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ROC Curve Construction Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trained Binary Classifier] --> B[Predict Continuous Score s_x for each test instance]
    B --> C[Sort all instances in descending order of s_x]
    C --> D[Initialize TP=0, FP=0, FN=P, TN=N at tau=1.0]
    D --> E{Sweep tau from 1.0 down to 0.0}
    E --> F[Update Confusion Matrix for next instance]
    F --> G[Compute TPR = TP / P]
    F --> H[Compute FPR = FP / N]
    G --> I[Append Point FPR, TPR to ROC]
    H --> I
    I --> J{All thresholds processed?}
    J -->|No| E
    J -->|Yes| K[Plot Connected Step Curve in FPR-TPR Plane]
    K --> L[Compute AUC using Trapezoidal Rule]
    L --> M[Identify Optimal Threshold via Youden J Statistic]
    M --> N[Final Evaluation Report]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style K fill:#fff4e1
    style L fill:#e8f5e9
    style N fill:#fce4ec
```

## 4.2 Confusion Matrix — TP / FP / FN / TN Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Actual_Positive
        AP1[Predicted Positive: TP = Correct Hit]
        AP2[Predicted Negative: FN = Miss / Type II Error]
    end
    subgraph Actual_Negative
        AN1[Predicted Positive: FP = False Alarm / Type I Error]
        AN2[Predicted Negative: TN = Correct Rejection]
    end

    AP1 --> R1[Increases TPR = TP / P]
    AN1 --> R2[Increases FPR = FP / N]
    AP2 --> R3[Increases FNR = FN / P]
    AN2 --> R4[Increases TNR = TN / N]

    R1 --> ROC[ROC Point Updates]
    R2 --> ROC
    R3 --> ROC
    R4 --> ROC

    style AP1 fill:#c8e6c9
    style AN1 fill:#ffcdd2
    style AP2 fill:#ffe0b2
    style AN2 fill:#bbdefb
    style ROC fill:#f5f5f5
```

## 4.3 Threshold Sweep Behaviour (State Transition Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> State_High: tau = 1.0, TPR=0, FPR=0
    State_High --> State_Mid: tau decreases, classifier becomes more lenient
    State_Mid --> State_Low: tau approaches 0.0
    State_Low --> State_AllPos: tau = 0.0, TPR=1, FPR=1
    State_AllPos --> [*]

    State_High: Strict Threshold Regime
    State_Mid: Balanced Operating Region
    State_Low: Lenient Threshold Regime
    State_AllPos: Trivial All-Positive Classifier
```

> **Reading the diagram**: As the threshold decreases, the operating point moves **rightward (FPR ↑)** and **upward (TPR ↑)** along the ROC curve. The classifier slides from conservative detection (bottom-left) toward aggressive detection (top-right).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the ROC curve. What does the Area Under the Curve (AUC) represent? [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** is a 2D graphical plot showing the diagnostic ability of a binary classifier as its discrimination threshold is varied. It plots the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** on the Y-axis against the **False Positive Rate (FPR)** on the X-axis.

The **Area Under the Curve (AUC)** is a scalar value in $[0, 1]$ that quantifies the overall ranking quality of the classifier. Probabilistically, AUC equals the probability that the classifier assigns a higher score to a randomly chosen positive instance than to a randomly chosen negative instance:

$$
\text{AUC} = P\bigl(s(\text{positive}) > s(\text{negative})\bigr)
$$

An AUC of **1.0** indicates perfect separation; an AUC of **0.5** indicates random guessing.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between Accuracy and AUC. Why is AUC preferred on imbalanced datasets? [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Accuracy | AUC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Fraction of correct predictions | Area under the TPR-vs-FPR curve |
| Threshold Dependence | Depends on a single fixed threshold | Threshold-independent, integrated over all thresholds |
| Sensitivity to Class Imbalance | **High** — biased toward majority class | **Low** — evaluates ranking quality |
| Range | $[0, 1]$ | $[0.5, 1.0]$ for useful classifiers |
| Computation | $\frac{TP+TN}{TP+FP+FN+TN}$ | Trapezoidal / Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney |

**Reason for AUC preference on imbalanced data**: In a 99:1 imbalanced dataset, a trivial "always-negative" classifier achieves 99% accuracy but AUC = 0.5. AUC penalizes such trivial classifiers because it requires the model to **rank** positive instances above negatives across all thresholds.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Module 4 Choice [14 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the construction procedure of the ROC curve with a neat sketch. Discuss the significance of the diagonal reference line. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction Procedure:**

1. Train a binary classifier (e.g., Logistic Regression) on training data.
2. Obtain predicted probability scores $s(x_i)$ for every test sample $x_i$.
3. Sort the test samples in **descending order** of $s(x_i)$.
4. Initialize the confusion matrix with $TP = 0$, $FP = 0$ (corresponding to threshold $\tau$ just below 1.0).
5. For each sorted sample in order:
   - If true label is **+1** → increment TP (this raises TPR).
   - If true label is **0** → increment FP (this raises FPR).
6. After processing each sample, recompute $TPR = TP/P$ and $FPR = FP/N$ where $P$ and $N$ are total positives and negatives.
7. Plot the sequence of $(FPR, TPR)$ points and connect them.

**Neat Sketch (described in text form):**

The Y-axis is $TPR \in [0, 1]$ and X-axis is $FPR \in [0, 1]$. The curve starts at $(0, 0)$ — corresponding to "predict all negative" — and ends at $(1, 1)$ — corresponding to "predict all positive". It is a monotonic non-decreasing step function that bows toward the **top-left corner** for good classifiers.

**Significance of the Diagonal Reference Line (y = x):**

The diagonal line $TPR = FPR$ represents a **random classifier** (coin flip). For any point on the diagonal, the probability of correctly classifying a positive instance equals the probability of mistakenly classifying a negative instance. The diagonal corresponds to **AUC = 0.5** — the baseline. Any classifier curve above the diagonal has $AUC > 0.5$ and is informative; curves touching the diagonal are useless. The vertical gap between the ROC curve and the diagonal quantifies classifier quality.

**(b)** A classifier produces the following confusion matrix at threshold $\tau = 0.5$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 
& \text{Predicted Positive} & \text{Predicted Negative} \\ 
\text{Actual Positive} & 80 & 20 \\ 
\text{Actual Negative} & 30 & 70 
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute the TPR, FPR, Precision, F1-score, and Youden's J statistic. Is $\tau = 0.5$ the optimal threshold? Justify. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Given values from confusion matrix:** $TP = 80$, $FN = 20$, $FP = 30$, $TN = 70$.

**Step 1 — Total Positives and Negatives:**

$$
P = TP + FN = 80 + 20 = 100
$$

$$
N = FP + TN = 30 + 70 = 100
$$

**Step 2 — Compute TPR (Sensitivity):**

$$
TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80
$$

**Step 3 — Compute FPR (Fall-out):**

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{30}{30 + 70} = \frac{30}{100} = 0.30
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Precision (PPV):**

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 30} = \frac{80}{110} = 0.727
$$

**Step 5 — Compute F1-Score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} \\
    &= \frac{2 \times 0.727 \times 0.80}{0.727 + 0.80} \\
    &= \frac{1.1632}{1.527} \\
    &\approx 0.762
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Youden's J:**

$$
J = TPR - FPR = 0.80 - 0.30 = 0.50
$$

**Verdict on Optimality:**

We cannot conclusively claim $\tau = 0.5$ is the optimal threshold because **J-statistic evaluation requires sweeping across multiple thresholds**. A single $\tau$ gives only one $(FPR, TPR)$ point. The optimal threshold is $\tau^* = \arg\max_{\tau} J(\tau)$. To confirm optimality, we must construct the full ROC curve across $\tau \in [0, 1]$ and pick the point where the vertical distance to the diagonal $y = x$ is maximized. At $\tau = 0.5$, $J = 0.5$ is a *candidate* but not proven optimal without full curve analysis.

---

### Question B — Module 4 Alternative Choice [14 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a worked example, derive the AUC of a binary classifier using the trapezoidal rule. Show all computation steps. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Setup**: Consider a binary classifier evaluated on 6 test samples with the following scores and true labels:

| Sample | True Label $y$ | Score $s$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A | 1 | 0.92 |
| B | 0 | 0.80 |
| C | 1 | 0.70 |
| D | 0 | 0.55 |
| E | 1 | 0.40 |
| F | 0 | 0.20 |

**Step 1 — Sort in descending order of score**:

Order: A(+, 0.92), B(−, 0.80), C(+, 0.70), D(−, 0.55), E(+, 0.40), F(−, 0.20).

**Step 2 — Identify totals**: $P = 3$, $N = 3$.

**Step 3 — Threshold sweep and ROC points**:

| Step | Event | TP | FP | TPR | FPR |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | Start: $\tau > 0.92$ | 0 | 0 | 0.000 | 0.000 |
| 1 | A is positive → TP | 1 | 0 | 0.333 | 0.000 |
| 2 | B is negative → FP | 1 | 1 | 0.333 | 0.333 |
| 3 | C is positive → TP | 2 | 1 | 0.667 | 0.333 |
| 4 | D is negative → FP | 2 | 2 | 0.667 | 0.667 |
| 5 | E is positive → TP | 3 | 2 | 1.000 | 0.667 |
| 6 | F is negative → FP | 3 | 3 | 1.000 | 1.000 |

**Step 4 — Apply trapezoidal AUC formula**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AUC &= \sum_{i=0}^{5} \frac{1}{2}(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i)(TPR_{i+1} + TPR_i) \\
A_{01} &= \frac{1}{2}(0 - 0)(0.333 + 0) = 0 \\
A_{12} &= \frac{1}{2}(0.333 - 0)(0.333 + 0.333) = \frac{1}{2}(0.333)(0.666) \approx 0.111 \\
A_{23} &= \frac{1}{2}(0.333 - 0.333)(0.667 + 0.333) = 0 \\
A_{34} &= \frac{1}{2}(0.667 - 0.333)(0.667 + 0.667) = \frac{1}{2}(0.333)(1.334) \approx 0.222 \\
A_{45} &= \frac{1}{2}(0.667 - 0.667)(1.000 + 0.667) = 0 \\
A_{56} &= \frac{1}{2}(1.000 - 0.667)(1.000 + 1.000) = \frac{1}{2}(0.333)(2) = 0.333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum contributions**:

$$
AUC = 0 + 0.111 + 0 + 0.222 + 0 + 0.333 = 0.666
$$

**Conclusion**: AUC $\approx 0.667$ indicates a **fair** classifier, only slightly better than random guessing. The classifier is mediocre at separating the two classes.

**(b)** Explain the relationship between the ROC curve, the Precision-Recall (PR) curve, and the F1-score. When is PR preferred over ROC? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**ROC vs PR Curve — Structural Relationship:**

Both curves are *threshold-dependent visualizations* of binary classifier performance, but they plot different quantities on the Y-axis:

| Property | ROC Curve | PR Curve |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Y-axis | TPR (Recall) | Precision |
| X-axis | FPR | Recall |
| Baseline (Random) | $y = x$, AUC = 0.5 | Horizontal line at $P/(P+N)$ (positive class fraction) |
| Sensitivity to Class Imbalance | Low | **High** |
| Use Case | Balanced datasets | **Severely imbalanced datasets** |

**Connection via F1-Score**: The F1-score is the **harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall, and any point on the PR curve corresponds to one $(Recall, Precision)$ pair at threshold $\tau$:

$$
F_1(\tau) = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision}(\tau) \cdot \text{Recall}(\tau)}{\text{Precision}(\tau) + \text{Recall}(\tau)}
$$

The optimal threshold on the PR curve maximizes $F_1$, which is analogous to maximizing Youden's $J$ on the ROC curve. However, $F_1$ is **asymmetric** — it weights the positive class more heavily, whereas $J$ treats both classes equally.

**When is PR preferred over ROC?**

PR curves are preferred when:

1. **Severe class imbalance** (e.g., 1:1000 fraud-to-legitimate ratio). In such cases, a huge TN count dominates FPR's denominator, masking the effect of true positives. FPR can remain low even when the model produces many false positives.
2. **The cost of False Negatives is high** (e.g., disease diagnosis, terrorist detection), and we care more about how trustworthy each "positive" alert is — i.e., Precision matters more than FPR.
3. **Information retrieval scenarios** where the top-K retrieved documents are more important than the full ranking.
4. **Positive class is rare and scientifically meaningful** (e.g., exoplanet detection, rare disease screening).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Forgetting to add the $(0, 0)$ starting point** before applying the trapezoidal rule — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for this. The ROC must always begin at the origin.
> 2. **Mixing up Precision-Recall axes with ROC axes** — recall that ROC uses FPR, not Recall, on the X-axis. Confusing these is a common 2-mark loss.
> 3. **Not labelling the random-guess diagonal** ($y = x$) when sketching the ROC curve — KTU diagrams without this reference line are penalized.
> 4. **Using Accuracy instead of AUC on imbalanced data** — examiners specifically test this conceptual trap; you must justify *why* AUC is preferred.
> 5. **Stating "AUC = 0.5 means perfect classifier"** — this is the random-classifier baseline, not perfect. Always state $AUC = 1.0$ for perfect.
> 6. **Skipping the trapezoidal area contribution for vertical/horizontal steps** — when consecutive ROC points share the same FPR or TPR, the area contribution is 0. Forgetting to verify this loses 1 mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick-Fire Facts for KTU 2024 Board Exam**:

- ROC = plot of **TPR (Y) vs FPR (X)** as threshold $\tau$ varies.
- AUC = probability that a random positive is ranked above a random negative.
- AUC = 0.5 (random), AUC = 1.0 (perfect), AUC $<$ 0.5 (inverted).
- Trapezoidal AUC: $\sum \frac{1}{2}(\Delta FPR)(TPR_i + TPR_{i+1})$.
- Youden's $J = TPR - FPR$ → maximized at optimal $\tau^*$.
- $FPR = FP / (FP + TN)$ and $TPR = TP / (TP + FN)$.
- ROC is **threshold-independent** and robust to class imbalance.
- PR curve is preferred for **severely imbalanced** data and when **Precision** matters more.
- $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$.
- Always start the ROC curve at $(0, 0)$ and end at $(1, 1)$.
- The diagonal $y = x$ is the random-classifier reference (AUC = 0.5).
- EER (Equal Error Rate) = point where $FPR = FNR$ (used in biometrics).
- Sklearn equivalent: `roc_curve(y_true, y_score)` and `roc_auc_score(y_true, y_score)`.
- AUC has a probabilistic interpretation equivalent to the **Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney U statistic**.
- ROC curves of two classifiers can cross — in such cases, AUC alone is insufficient; use **partial AUC** or **Precision-Recall** for region-specific comparison.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Area Under the Curve (AUC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAIT404 - FOUNDATION OF MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Area Under the Curve (AUC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview
